Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis

-- 458 of 1282 --

457
• Ask about the duration and onset of tinnitus and whether it started gradually
or suddenly.
• Ask whether the tinnitus affects one ear or both ears.
• Ask the patient to describe the sound and whether it is pulsatile (whether it
seems to beat in time with their heartbeat) Note: Sudden-onset pulsatile
tinnitus requires same-day ENT referral as it may indicate a vascular
abnormality.
• Ask if the tinnitus is continuous or intermittent.
• Ask about any recent upper respiratory tract infections or colds.
• Ask about ear discharge or ear pain.
• Ask if there has been any recent head injury, as tinnitus occurring after a head
injury requires an urgent same-day ENT assessment. This could indicate a
more serious underlying problem, such as a traumatic brain injury.
• Ask about associated symptoms such as hearing loss, dizziness, vertigo,
balance problems, jaw pain or clicking, facial weakness, unilateral limb
weakness, sensitivity to loud noises, stress, visual changes, or headache.
• Ask about any relieving or aggravating factors, for example; background noise
may reduce tinnitus. Stress, lack of sleep, caffeine use, or certain head/jaw
positions may worsen it.
• Ask about any significant noise exposure, either at work or during leisure
activities, such as attending loud music concerts or using headphones at high
volume.
• Ask about use of over-the-counter or prescribed ototoxic drugs (for example
aspirin or NSAIDs).
• Ask about any family history of hearing loss or tinnitus.
• Ask how the tinnitus has affected his sleep, mood, work performance, and
daily activities.
• Ask about social history, including smoking, alcohol intake, and stress levels.
• Explore his ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a diagnosis of noise-induced tinnitus
Example of Explanation to the Patient

-- 459 of 1282 --

458
Alex, thank you for explaining everything so clearly. From what you have told me, it
sounds like you are experiencing something called tinnitus, which is the medical term
for ringing or buzzing in the ears when there is no external sound.
You mentioned that this started after attending a loud rock concert, which makes it
quite likely that the loud music temporarily irritated or damaged the tiny hearing cells
in your inner ear. This is a common cause of tinnitus, and in most cases, it settles
gradually over time as the ears recover.
Would you be happy to come in for me to assess you further? I would like to do a
simple hearing check using a tuning fork and examine your nerves to make sure we
are not missing anything important. The reason for bringing you in is to ensure there
are no red-flag signs that might need an urgent referral.
I would also like to arrange a formal hearing test with the audiology team to check
your hearing levels properly and see if there has been any change or mild noise-
related hearing loss. The referral usually takes around four to five weeks to come
through, just so you are aware. Are you happy for me to make this referral?
In the meantime, try to avoid further exposure to loud noises, for example, concerts
or listening to music at a high volume through headphones. If you cannot avoid noisy
environments, it would really help to use ear protection, such as earplugs.
In terms of things that can help, many people find it useful to have some gentle
background sound, such as quiet music or the sound of a fan, especially at night.
This helps distract the brain from focusing on the ringing. Relaxation techniques or
mindfulness exercises can also help calm your body and mind, as stress can
sometimes make tinnitus feel worse. Would you be open to trying some of these
approaches?
In most cases, tinnitus gradually improves over time. However, if it becomes worse,
or you notice it is only in one ear, starts to beat in time with your heartbeat, or you
develop new symptoms such as dizziness, weakness, headaches, or anything else
that worries you, please contact us straight away.
Also, if the tinnitus or the lack of sleep begins to affect your mood or make you feel
very low, hopeless, or have thoughts of ending your life, please let us know
immediately or contact NHS 111 or 999 if you ever feel unsafe. You do not have to
cope with those feelings alone, and help is always available
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to perform basic hearing tests (Weber and
Rinne tuning fork tests) to help determine whether there is any sensorineural
or conductive hearing loss and carry out a brief neurological examination.

-- 460 of 1282 --

459
• Refer to audiology for a formal hearing assessment to check for noise-
induced hearing loss.
• Explain that if hearing loss is detected, he may be offered hearing aids, which
can often help improve both hearing and the perception of tinnitus.
• Reassure that tinnitus often improves on its own, particularly if caused by
short-term factors like loud noise exposure. However, repeated exposure to
loud sounds can lead to long term hearing issues, so protecting hearing is
essential.
• Advise avoiding further exposure to loud noises, such as concerts, loud
headphones, or prolonged earphone use. Encourage the use of ear protection
(earplugs or noise-reducing headphones) in noisy environments.
• Provide self-help and coping strategies: suggest sound therapy, such as
having low-level background sounds (soft music, the sound of a fan, or noise
from a water feature) to help distract from tinnitus.
• Encourage relaxation techniques, mindfulness, or tinnitus-related CBT, as
these can reduce the distress caused by tinnitus.
• Safety-net advice: advise him to seek medical attention if symptoms worsen, if
tinnitus becomes one-sided, pulsatile, or associated with hearing loss,
weakness, headaches, or balance problems. Also advise him to seek help
urgently if the tinnitus, or the distress it causes, makes him feel anxious, low
in mood, or if he develops any thoughts of suicide.
• Offer a follow-up appointment after the audiology assessment to review the
results and discuss the next steps in management.
Learning Point from this Station
Tinnitus is a symptom rather than a disease. It is defined as the perception of sound
in the absence of an external auditory stimulus. The sound may be described as
ringing, humming, buzzing, hissing, pulsing, clicking, or whistling. It may be unilateral
or bilateral, constant or intermittent, and pulsatile or non-pulsatile.
Classification of Tinnitus:
Subjective tinnitus: Heard only by the affected individual and accounts for most
cases. It is usually caused by an otological or neurological disorder. Common
causes of subjective tinnitus include ear infections (otitis media or otitis externa),
impacted cerumen, Meniere’s disease, noise-induced hearing loss, otosclerosis,
presbycusis, sensorineural hearing loss, and tympanic membrane perforation. It may
also occur as a result of ototoxic medications such as aspirin and NSAIDs, loop
diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide), aminoglycosides (gentamicin), macrolides

-- 461 of 1282 --

460
(erythromycin), antimalarials (quinine, chloroquine), tetracyclines (doxycycline,
minocycline), cytotoxic drugs (cisplatin), and valproate.
Objective tinnitus: Rare, and the sound can also be heard by the examiner. It is
commonly due to vascular or muscular causes, such as aortic stenosis or mitral
regurgitation, arteriovenous malformations or venous hums, carotid or vertebral
artery stenosis, tortuosity, dissection or aneurysm, a high jugular bulb, or vascular
tumours.
Red Flags Requiring Immediate or Urgent Referral:
Immediate (same day) referral if:
• Sudden-onset pulsatile tinnitus (possible vascular abnormality)
• Tinnitus following head trauma
• Sudden-onset hearing loss (within 3 days and in the last 30 days)
• Neurological symptoms or signs (e.g., facial weakness, acute vertigo,
suspected stroke)
• High suicide risk — refer urgently to the crisis mental health team.
Urgent (within 2 weeks) referral if:
• Persistent distress affecting mental wellbeing, despite initial support
• Sudden hearing loss that developed more than 30 days ago or rapidly
worsening hearing over 4–90 days
• Persistent otalgia or otorrhoea not responding to treatment
Management Principles:
Offer audiological assessment to all patients with tinnitus.
Management depends on the underlying cause and may include addressing hearing
loss, discontinuing ototoxic drugs, and providing sound therapy, hearing aids, or
CBT-based tinnitus support for persistent symptoms.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/tinnitus/

-- 464 of 1282 --

463
Marking scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask when the rash started– helps determine acuteness and possible
infectious causes.
• Ask where the rash is located – localised vs generalised can help narrow
differentials.
• Ask if there’s been any contact with someone with a similar rash or viral
illness – to assess for contagious illness.
• Ask if the rash is itchy – helps differentiate between viral and allergic causes.
• Ask if the child has had a fever – viral rashes are often accompanied by fever.
• Ask if pressing on the rash makes it disappear – if it does, it's blanching; if not,
it’s non-blanching (glass test can be used). This helps rule out serious
conditions like meningococcal sepsis.
• Other way to ask for blanching is the glass test- ask them to use a glass and
press gently onto the skin- if it disappears it means it blanches and if it does
not it means non-blanching
• Ask where the child is and what they’re doing now – if sleeping, ask if they’ve
been unusually sleepy today.
• Ask how the child is in general — eating, drinking, and urinating as usual?
• Ask about associated symptoms:
• Headache, neck stiffness, or sensitivity to light — red flags for meningitis.
• Cough, sore throat, runny nose — viral symptoms.
• Vomiting or diarrhoea — common with viral infections.
• Abdominal pain — can be associated with some infections or rashes.
• Joint pain or swelling — think of post-viral or systemic causes.
• Ask about home situation- who lives at home and if anyone else at home is
unwell, any recent travel, school/nursery attendance— helpful for identifying
contagious illness.
• Ask about immunisation status and development (PBIND).
• Ask ICE — what do the parents think is going on, what are their concerns,
and what were they hoping would happen today?
• Give a diagnosis of likely viral rash and explain viral rash to mum.

-- 465 of 1282 --

464
For example:
“Mrs Durov, thank you for speaking with me today. From everything you've told me
so far, it is likely that Nikolai has what we call a viral rash. These kinds of rashes are
common in children, especially when they’re fighting off a mild infection, and they
usually go away on their own within a few days. They are usually not serious or life
threatening. I understand it's worrying to see a rash, but from the information you've
given, I'm not currently seeing anything concerning.
In cases of meningitis, children are typically very unwell, often drowsy or difficult to
wake, with symptoms like a severe headache, stiff neck, sensitivity to light, or a rash
that doesn’t fade when pressed. From everything you've described, Jack doesn’t
have any of these worrying signs
That said, I would really like to see your child properly just to be sure. I know you
mentioned you're unable to bring them in today—would it be possible for you to bring
your child to the clinic tomorrow morning? I’ll make sure we book an appointment for
you.
In the meantime, please keep a close eye on your child and if anything changes or
you notice any worrying symptoms, seek urgent medical help straight away. These
red flag symptoms include:
• A rash that doesn’t fade when pressed with a glass
• Your child becoming very sleepy, drowsy, or difficult to wake
• A high fever that doesn’t come down with paracetamol or ibuprofen
• Severe headache, neck stiffness, or light sensitivity
• Fast breathing or difficulty breathing
• Cold hands and feet or very pale or mottled skin
• Vomiting repeatedly or refusing to drink
If any of these happen, please call 999 or go to A&E immediately and I will write
down these symptoms and send as a text to you now. Otherwise, I’ll see you both
tomorrow. In the meantime, encourage fluids and give paracetamol if needed for
fever or discomfort. You’re doing the right thing by checking in—please don’t hesitate
to call us back if you’re unsure about anything.”

-- 466 of 1282 --

465
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment if the child is able to come in.
• If the mother is unable to bring the child today, offer to book an appointment
for the next day and reassure her that from what she has described, this likely
sounds like a viral rash which is common in children and usually not serious.
• Advise her to monitor the child closely and to keep them well hydrated.
Recommend the use of paracetamol (Calpol) for fever or discomfort
• If using paracetamol, advise that if the child vomits the dose shortly after
taking it, she should not repeat the dose but wait for the next scheduled
one. Ibuprofen can also be given, but ensure the child has no asthma. Both
paracetamol and ibuprofen can be used together, but doses should be
staggered—wait at least 1 hour after giving the first medicine to see if the
second is needed, especially if fever or discomfort persists.
• Reassure the parent that the child can be safely observed at home tonight
provided there are no red flags.
• Safety net thoroughly—advise the parent to seek urgent medical attention if
the child develops a rash that does not fade when pressed with a glass,
becomes unusually drowsy or difficult to wake, has a persistent high fever,
experiences neck stiffness, sensitivity to light, difficulty breathing, cold
extremities, pale or mottled skin, refuses to drink, or is vomiting repeatedly
• Offer follow-up the next day to reassess the child’s condition.
Learning Point from this Station
This station highlights key principles in managing clinical uncertainty, particularly
in children presenting with a rash. While it may be difficult to make a firm diagnosis
over the phone, the absence of red flags, presence of a well child, and parental
ability to monitor justifies a watchful waiting approach with safety netting rather
than unnecessary escalation like a home visit, which would be a misuse of limited
healthcare resources.
Medication advice is crucial. If a child vomits after taking a dose of paracetamol
syrup or tablets, advise the parent not to repeat the dose. This is because it’s
impossible to know how much of the medicine the child has already absorbed. At
therapeutic dose, paracetamol is a safe drug, but with a narrow therapeutic window,
it is easy to accidentally or deliberately overdose. So, repeating the dose for the child
immediately after vomiting risks overdose, which can lead to severe liver damage.

-- 467 of 1282 --

466
Paracetamol poisoning may not show symptoms immediately but can have delayed,
life-threatening effects, hence, a cautious and informed approach is essential.
This station also reinforces clear communication and education: empower parents
with information about red flag symptoms (such as non-blanching rash, drowsiness,
poor feeding, irritability, neck stiffness, and sensitivity to light) and provide a plan for
follow-up and escalation.
Further Reading
https://patient.info/skin-conditions/viral-rashes
https://www.nhs.uk/medicines/paracetamol-for-children/common-questions-about-
paracetamol-for-children/
https://bpac.org.nz/2018/paracetamol.aspx#:~:text=However%2C%20paracetamol%
20has%20a%20narrow,to%20liver%20failure%20and%20death.

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Acknowledge that you can see from his records that he has recently seen
your colleague and had blood tests done, but before discussing the results,
explore his symptoms that led to the tests being arranged.
• Ask about symptoms suggestive of diabetes and its complications, including
blurred vision, increased appetite, increased thirst, shortness of breath, chest
pain, urinary frequency, blood in the urine, tingling or numbness particularly in
the legs, and any non healing wounds or ulcers, especially on the feet.
• Ask about any weight loss or weight gain.
• Explore social history including smoking, alcohol intake, diet, weight,
occupation, and home circumstances.
• Ask about the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Give a diagnosis of type 2 diabetes.

-- 476 of 1282 --

475
Management
• Advise on lifestyle changes including regular exercise, weight loss, and a
healthy balanced diet.
• Advise a healthy balanced diet including low fat dairy products, oily fish, fruits,
and vegetables, and reducing intake of saturated fats. Offer to send him
written information to help tailor his diet for diabetes. If he requires additional
support, offer referral to the in house dietitian.
• Explain that some people can manage their diabetes with lifestyle measures
alone. However, given that he is symptomatic and at higher risk of
complications, recommend starting medication alongside lifestyle changes.
• Offer modified release metformin 500 mg once daily and dapagliflozin 10 mg
once daily.
• Explain that metformin will be started first, and after about two weeks, once it
is confirmed that he is tolerating it well, dapagliflozin will then be introduced.
• Inform him of side effects. Metformin can cause gastrointestinal upset
including diarrhoea and nausea. Dapagliflozin can increase the risk of urinary
tract infections and, in rare cases, a serious infection called Fournier’s
gangrene, which affects the genital and perineal area.
• Explain sick day rules for both medications. If he develops vomiting or
diarrhoea, he should temporarily stop these medications and restart them 24
to 48 hours after he is able to eat and drink normally again.
• Discuss Ramadan. Advise that fasting is possible in many cases but requires
careful planning.
• Explain that if his blood sugar is well controlled and he is at low risk of
complications, fasting can be considered. However, his medications will need
to be adjusted to fit fasting. As these are once daily medications, they can be
taken in the evening after breaking the fast. Reassure him that his blood
sugars will be monitored and medications adjusted accordingly.
• Inform him that he will be offered a follow up appointment before Ramadan to
review his control, adjust medications as needed, and provide specific
personalised advice.
• Inform him that his cholesterol and QRISK score are raised. Offer atorvastatin
20 mg once daily for cardiovascular risk reduction. Explain that this will not be
started immediately, but once he is tolerating metformin, to reduce the risk of
side effects.
• Advise annual diabetic reviews including retinal screening and foot checks.
• Ask him to provide a urine sample for albumin to creatinine ratio, explaining
that diabetes can affect the kidneys and this helps monitor for early changes.
• Offer vaccinations including pneumococcal, influenza, and COVID vaccines.
• Arrange follow up in 3 months to reassess HbA1c and response to treatment.
• Safety net by advising him to seek urgent medical attention if he develops
symptoms suggestive of diabetic ketoacidosis, including feeling very unwell,

-- 477 of 1282 --

476
excessive thirst, passing large amounts of urine, nausea or vomiting,
abdominal pain, fast or deep breathing, confusion, or a fruity smell to the
breath. Advise him to contact the GP surgery or call NHS 111 if out of hours.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Ahmed for coming in today and for explaining everything so clearly. From
your symptoms and your blood test results, I am afraid this confirms that you have
diabetes. I understand this may not be the news you were hoping for. Before we go
further, can I just ask what you already know about diabetes?
Diabetes is a long term condition where the body has difficulty controlling the level of
sugar in the blood. If it is not well controlled over time, it can affect different parts of
the body such as the eyes, heart, and kidneys, and can also lead to problems with
wound healing. In some cases, this can lead to serious complications like
amputation, which I understand is something that happened to your uncle and is a
concern for you.
The good news is that with the right lifestyle changes and medications, we can
control this very well, and many people live normal, healthy lives with diabetes. Are
you happy for us to talk through what we can do to manage this?
Looking at your weight in relation to your height, it is slightly raised, and losing
weight can really help improve your blood sugar control. Things like regular exercise,
for example a daily 30 minute walk and gradually increasing this as you feel able,
can make a big difference. Is that something you feel you could incorporate into your
routine?
You also mentioned that your diet is not ideal. I completely understand that with your
work as a taxi driver, it can be difficult to maintain a healthy routine. However, small
changes can make a big difference. I would recommend a balanced diet with more
fruits and vegetables, low fat dairy products, and oily fish, while reducing saturated
fats and takeaway foods. I can send you some written information to guide you, and
if you would like more support, we have a dietitian who can help tailor this to your
lifestyle. What would you prefer?
Now, some people can manage diabetes with lifestyle changes alone. However,
because you are already having symptoms and your sugar levels are quite high, I
would strongly recommend starting medication alongside these changes. Are you
happy for us to start treatment?
We will begin with a medication called metformin, starting at a low dose of 500 mg
once daily. This helps to lower your blood sugar. After about two weeks, once we are
sure you are tolerating it well, we will add another medication called dapagliflozin,

-- 478 of 1282 --

477
which works in a different way to help control your sugar levels and also has benefits
for your heart and kidneys.
Metformin can sometimes cause side effects such as diarrhoea or feeling sick,
especially at the beginning. Dapagliflozin can increase the risk of urine infections
and, very rarely, a serious infection around the genital area. If you ever notice any
redness, swelling, pain, or ulcers around your genital area, please seek urgent
medical attention. I do not expect this to happen as it is very rare, and most people
tolerate this medication well and safely. Does that sound okay to you?
It is also important to know about sick day rules. If you become unwell with vomiting
or diarrhoea, you should stop these medications temporarily and restart them once
you are eating and drinking normally again after 24 to 48 hours.
You mentioned Ramadan, which is very important to you. Fasting is possible in
many cases, but it does require careful planning. If your blood sugar is well
controlled and you are at low risk of complications, then fasting can be considered
safely.
We can adjust your medications to fit around fasting. As these are once daily
medications, you can take them in the evening after breaking your fast. We will also
continue to monitor your blood sugar and adjust your treatment as needed to keep
you safe.
We will arrange a review before Ramadan to assess your control, make any
necessary adjustments, and provide you with specific personalised advice to support
you during that period.
Your cholesterol was also found to be raised. When this is the case, we use a
medical calculator to estimate your risk of having a heart attack or stroke over the
next ten years, and your result came back as high.
To reduce this risk, the lifestyle changes we discussed are very important. In
addition, we would recommend starting a medication to lower your cholesterol, such
as a statin. This can sometimes cause side effects like mild stomach upset or muscle
aches, but most people tolerate it well.
We will not start this straight away. Once you are comfortable with the metformin, we
can then introduce it gradually. Does that sound okay?
As part of your ongoing care, you will need regular checks, including annual eye
screening and foot checks, as diabetes can affect these areas. I will also ask you to
provide a urine sample to check your kidneys, as diabetes can sometimes affect
kidney function.
We will also offer you vaccinations such as flu, pneumococcal, and COVID, as
diabetes can make infections more serious.

-- 479 of 1282 --

478
I would like to review you again in about three months to see how you are getting on
and to repeat your blood tests.
I do not expect this to happen, but if you feel very unwell, extremely thirsty, pass a lot
of urine, feel sick or start vomiting, have tummy pain, or notice your breathing
becoming fast or deep, or a fruity smell to your breath, these could be signs of very
high blood sugar. If that happens, please seek urgent medical attention by contacting
the surgery or calling NHS 111 if it is out of hours.
Learning Point from This Station
Type 2 diabetes is a common metabolic condition that requires a structured and
patient centred approach to diagnosis and management. In some patients with an
HbA1c in the range of 48 to 53 mmol/mol, particularly if they are asymptomatic and
highly motivated, lifestyle modification alone may be appropriate as an initial
management strategy. However, in patients who are symptomatic or have
significantly elevated HbA1c levels, as in this case, lifestyle changes should be
combined with pharmacological treatment to reduce the risk of complications.
This station also highlights the importance of prioritisation and time management in
the MRCGP SCA. Cases like this can be content heavy, so quick data gathering is
essential to allow adequate time for explanation and management. If time is limited,
it is reasonable to briefly acknowledge additional issues such as raised cholesterol
and arrange a follow up appointment to address this in more detail.
Ramadan has significant religious and cultural importance for Muslims, and
healthcare professionals should understand its impact on people living with diabetes.
Healthy adult Muslims fast from sunrise to sunset during Ramadan. A light meal is
eaten before sunrise, known as Suhoor, before the start of the fasting period. After
sunset, the fast is broken with a larger meal, known as Iftar, which often includes
both savoury and sweet foods.
A pre Ramadan diabetes education session is recommended one to two months
before the fasting period. Participation in a structured diabetes education programme
with a focus on Ramadan has been shown to reduce hypoglycaemic episodes,
support weight loss, and improve overall glycaemic control.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/diabetes-type-2/
https://www.england.nhs.uk/midlands/wp-
content/uploads/sites/46/2020/11/SWBCCG-Summary-Guidance-Diabetes-
Ramadan-V1.pdf

You took over-the-counter Cetirizine (an antihistamine), which helped reduce the

Marking scheme
History
• Explore symptoms of the reaction—Ask about tongue swelling, wheeze, SOB
to rule out anaphylaxis

• Ask about social history
• Give diagnosis of likely angioedema
Explain to patient
Gerald, I agree with you that this reaction sounds like an allergy, and what you're
describing is something called angioedema. It's a kind of swelling that happens
under the skin, often around the lips, eyes, or face, just like you experienced. This
happens when your body reacts to something—like certain foods or medications.
The good news is that this type of reaction usually settles with antihistamines, like
the Cetirizine you took, which helped reduce the symptoms.
From what you’ve told me, it sounds like your reaction may have been triggered by
the food you ate, which contained shellfish. This suggests you may have a shellfish
allergy, and I would strongly advise avoiding shellfish in the future.
In addition to the food allergy, your medication—Ramipril—could also be a
factor, as it is known to cause angioedema in some people. Because of this,
we need to stop Ramipril and switch you to an alternative medication. I will
prescribe Indapamide, which will help manage your blood pressure without the risk
of causing angioedema."
I understand your concern about whether you need an EpiPen. Typically, an EpiPen
is prescribed for people who have a high risk of severe allergic reactions, such as
those with co-existing chest problems like asthma or COPD, heart disease, or for

-- 488 of 1282 --

487
people who cannot easily avoid their allergen triggers. In your case, you don’t have
these risk factors, and since you can avoid shellfish and nuts, an EpiPen would likely
not be needed. However, if you ever experience more severe symptoms, such as
difficulty breathing, dizziness, or swelling that affects your throat, it’s important to
seek urgent medical attention immediately
Management
• Advice to continue taking antihistamines at the same dose, as they have been
effective in reducing symptoms. He can continue this for up to 6 weeks if
needed.
• Advice to stop ramipril –offer to check patient’s blood pressure and start on
indapamide if additional antihypertensive is needed
• No referral is needed, as the likely triggers (ACE inhibitor and shellfish) have
been identified and avoided
• Advise to strictly avoid shellfish and any foods that may contain it to prevent
future reactions
• Advise him to seek immediate medical help (by dialling 999 or attending A&E)
if symptoms progress rapidly or if symptoms of anaphylaxis develop (SOB,
wheeze, dizziness, tongue swelling etc.)

started losing his hair in his 30s, it’s likely that genetics are playing a role here.

-- 491 of 1282 --

Management

• Minoxidil 5% solution (Not available on the NHS, can be gotten over the
counter) but 5% foam (Regaine for Men) is available on NHS.
• Finasteride 1 mg tablets (Propecia®) are not available to prescribe on the
NHS but can be prescribed on a private prescription
• Inform patient that continuous use for 2–4 months is required before evidence
of hair growth can be expected if using topical treatment and continuous use
for 3 6 months if using tablets
• If treatment is stopped, the beneficial effects begin to reverse by 3–6 months
and return to baseline by 9–12 months for both topical and oral tablets
• Inform him that if he is worried about his appearance for his model shoot,
some good hair styling or aesthetic wigs or hair camouflage can help give the
aesthetic appearance you may be looking for. Unfortunately, nothing else may
work as quick as he wants it
• Advise that hair transplants are another way some men may like to explore
but this is not something available under the NHS and it is expensive. Hair
transplants typically take longer to show results. While you may see some
initial growth within a few weeks, it usually takes several months to notice
significant changes.
• Follow up is based on what you prescribe-topical – 2 months, Oral tablets –3
months

Explain to her: Jennifer, based on what you've shared with me and what I've
observed, it's more likely that you're experiencing essential tremor, rather than
Parkinson's disease. Essential tremor is a condition that causes shaking,
particularly when you try to hold or grab things, like you've described. The fact
that the tremor improves with alcohol is also more typical of essential tremor.
It's a common condition, and while it can be frustrating, it doesn't usually
cause the other symptoms that you might see with Parkinson's disease, such
as slowness of movement, stiffness, or problems with balance.
I understand that you’re worried about the possibility of Parkinson's disease,
and I want to acknowledge that concern. It's completely normal to worry about
a diagnosis, especially when you’re noticing changes in your body that affect

-- 497 of 1282 --

your day-to-day life, like your work. However, based on what we know so far,
Parkinson’s disease seems less likely.
Unfortunately, essential tremor doesn’t have a cure, but there are treatments
available that can help manage the symptoms, particularly the tremor.
Management
• Offer face-to-face to examine nerves of the body and tremor

When faced with multiple presenting complaints that seem not associated—start by
asking questions briefly on each of the symptoms

movement, stiffness)?
• Does anything make them better or worse (e.g., stress, caffeine)?

-- 500 of 1282 --

• Can you describe the dizziness? (light-headedness vs. spinning sensation)

concerned about these symptoms, especially since you’ve been noticing
tremors and dizziness that are affecting your work. Based on what you've told
me; I’m initially thinking that these symptoms could be related to the lithium
medication you’re on. Lithium can sometimes cause these side effects,
particularly if the levels in your blood are too high.
Given that you live alone and your symptoms seem to be getting worse and
because lithium toxicity can be serious, I strongly recommend that we arrange
for you to be admitted to the Acute Medical Unit. This will allow the hospital
team to monitor you closely, perform urgent blood tests, and check your
lithium levels, as well as your kidney and thyroid function, which lithium can
sometimes affect.
The goal is to ensure your safety and manage any underlying issues
effectively. I know this might feel like a big step, but it’s important to get the
right care quickly to help you feel better.

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Sarah, thank you for explaining everything so clearly. I can see how difficult the last
few months have been for you, especially with the forgetfulness, difficulty
multitasking, and the changes in your mood. Before we go any further, are you
happy for me to talk through what may be causing these symptoms?
From what you have told me, your symptoms are very typical of something called
perimenopause, which is the stage leading up to menopause. During this time,
hormone levels start to fluctuate, and this can cause a wide range of symptoms.
These are not just hot flushes and changes to your periods, but can also affect
concentration, memory, irritability, and your ability to manage several tasks at once.
Many women describe it exactly the way you have. Are you with me so far?
A blood test is not helpful for diagnosing menopause at your age, because hormone
levels naturally rise and fall day to day, which makes the result unreliable. The safest
and most accurate way to diagnose this is through your symptoms, and your
symptoms fit very well. Does that make sense?
We can talk about treatment options, and there are hormonal and non-hormonal
choices.
Hormone Replacement Therapy, or HRT, is the most effective treatment. It can
improve hot flushes, mood, sleep, energy levels, and even concentration. It also
helps protect your bones and reduces the risk of fractures.
Before starting it, I would just need to check that it is safe for you by asking a few
questions. Is that alright?
• Have you ever had breast cancer, or is there any family history of breast
cancer?
• Have you noticed any new breast lumps?
• Have you experienced any abnormal vaginal bleeding?

-- 505 of 1282 --

504
• Is there any possibility that you might be pregnant?
• Have you ever had a blood clot in your legs (deep vein thrombosis) or lungs
(pulmonary embolism), or does this run in your family?
• Have you had a heart attack or stroke within the last twelve months?
• Do you suffer from migraines? (This does not stop you from using HRT, but it
means we may need to be a bit more cautious.)
HRT is generally very safe for most women and the benefits usually outweigh the
risks, especially around your age. However, like any treatment, there are some risks
that you need to be aware of.
Some types of HRT can slightly increase the risk of breast cancer, although this risk
goes back down again after stopping treatment. There is also a small increased risk
of blood clots in the legs or lungs, and a very small increased risk of stroke. These
risks are still low overall, but it is important that you know about them so you can
make an informed choice.
If you decide you want to start HRT, we would arrange a face-to-face appointment
with the healthcare assistant to check your blood pressure first. How does that sound
so far?
If you prefer not to take HRT, that is absolutely fine as well. There are very good
non-hormonal options we can use. For example, for vaginal dryness, moisturisers
and lubricants can help a great deal. There is also menopause-specific cognitive
behavioural therapy, which has been shown to help with hot flushes, sleep, mood,
and overall quality of life. Many women find this very helpful.
These non-hormonal options avoid the risks associated with HRT, but they may not
be as effective in relieving symptoms like hot flushes, night sweats, and difficulties
with concentration or mood. Are you with me so far?
You mentioned herbal remedies; There’s some evidence that natural remedies like
isoflavones and black cohosh might help with symptoms like hot flushes. However,
the quality and safety of these products can vary, as they’re not always well-
regulated. This means that what’s in the preparation might differ between brands, so
it’s important to be cautious if you’re considering trying them.

-- 506 of 1282 --

505
Management
• Explain to her that her symptoms are most likely due to early menopause or
perimenopause.
• Decline blood tests to confirm menopause and explain that menopause is
diagnosed based on symptoms, and that hormone levels can fluctuate at her
age, making blood tests unreliable.
• Explain the benefits and risks of hormone replacement therapy and also
discuss the benefits and risks of non hormonal options.
• If she wants to proceed with HRT, ask about any contraindications such as a
new breast lump, abnormal vaginal bleeding, a family or personal history of
breast cancer, or a family or personal history of thromboembolism, including
deep vein thrombosis or pulmonary embolism. Also ask if she experiences
migraine, which is not a strict contraindication but requires caution. Then
advise her to attend a face-to-face appointment with the healthcare assistant
to have her blood pressure checked before HRT can be prescribed.
• If she prefers not to take HRT, reassure her that symptoms can still be
managed without it. Offer non hormonal treatments such as vaginal
moisturisers and lubricants to help with vaginal dryness and reduce
discomfort during intercourse.
• Offer menopause-specific cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT). Explain that
this can help by addressing unhelpful thought patterns and behaviours,
teaching coping strategies for symptoms such as hot flushes, night sweats,
and mood changes, and improving overall quality of life.
• If she mentions herbal remedies, explain that some herbal remedies, such as
isoflavones or black cohosh, may help with hot flushes, but the quality and
safety of these products can vary and their effects are not always predictable.
• Offer to send her leaflets and reliable links, such as from Menopause Matters,
so she can read more and make an informed decision.
• Arrange a follow-up in 2–4 weeks to review her symptoms and discuss which
treatment option she has decided on.

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Mary, thank you for explaining how things have been for you.
Your results show two important things. First, the level of inflammation in your body
is higher than normal. Second, your haemoglobin is slightly low, which means you
have mildanaemia.
Haemoglobin is the part of your blood that carries oxygen around your body. When
your haemoglobin level is a little low, like yours, it means your blood is not carrying
as much oxygen as usual. This kind of mild anaemia is something we often see
when the body is dealing with inflammation. Are you following me so far?
When we put these results together with your symptoms, especially the shoulder
pain and stiffness in the morning that lasts for about an hour, it strongly fits with a
condition called polymyalgia rheumatica, or PMR. Have you heard of this before?

-- 511 of 1282 --

510
Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) is a condition that causes pain and stiffness around
the shoulders. We do not know exactly what causes it, but the good news is that it
usually responds very well to treatment. Does that make sense so far?
Now, the good news is that PMR responds very well to a type of medication called
steroids. Most people notice a big improvement within a few days. What we usually
do is start a low dose of steroid tablets and review you after one week to see how
you have responded. If you feel much better, that supports the diagnosis. Over time,
we then slowly reduce the dose, but treatment is usually needed for one to two
years. Are you with me so far?
Because you have diabetes, steroids can make your blood sugar rise, so we will
check your HbA1c and keep a closer eye on your sugar levels every three months.
Your healthy diet and regular walking will help keep this controlled, and if needed,
we can adjust your diabetes medications.
Steroids can also thin the bones over time, so if you remain on them for several
months, we will arrange a bone scan and discuss bone protection. We will also give
you medication to protect your stomach called omeprazole, because steroids can
sometimes cause indigestion.
Another thing to keep in mind is that steroids can slightly weaken your immune
system, which means you may be more likely to pick up infections. To help protect
you, we can arrange vaccines such as the pneumococcal, flu, and COVID vaccines.
If you have never had chickenpox, shingles, or measles before, you will not have
natural immunity, so it is important to avoid close contact with anyone who has these
infections and to let us know urgently if you are exposed.
In terms of your daily function, once your symptoms start to improve and the
inflammation settles, physiotherapy can help you rebuild strength in the shoulders so
you can gradually return to doing things like driving and bowling again.
I want to reassure you that you do not need an X-ray of the shoulder. An X-ray would
not diagnose this condition or change the treatment plan.
We will review you in one week to see how well you are responding. If you notice
any new symptoms such as a new headache, pain in your jaw when chewing, or any
change in your vision, please seek help urgently by calling the GP urgent line or 111
out of hours. These symptoms can be signs of a related condition called giant cell
arteritis. This condition causes inflammation in the blood vessels in the head and can
lead to sudden and permanent loss of vision if not treated quickly. If this were to
happen, you would need urgent treatment with higher-dose steroids to protect your
sight.

-- 512 of 1282 --

511
Management
• Offer Prednisolone 15 mg once daily and arrange a review in one week.
• Inform her that if her symptoms improve with steroids, this would support a
diagnosis of polymyalgia rheumatica. Explain that steroids would then need to
be continued long term, with the dose gradually reduced according to her
symptoms. Advise her that treatment typically lasts for one to two years and
reassure her that she will be closely monitored throughout.
• Inform the patient about the side effects of steroids, including the possibility of
raised blood glucose levels. Advise her that you will check her HbA1c and
monitor her blood sugar more closely during treatment, ideally every three
months, as she has diabetes. Encourage her that maintaining a healthy diet
and continuing her regular walks will help with glucose control. Reassure her
that if her blood sugar does rise, her diabetes medication can be adjusted
accordingly.
• Explain that steroids can thin the bones over time, so we will offer her a bone
scan if she has been on steroids for a while, for example around three
months, and we will continue to assess her risk as treatment continues.
• Explain that steroids increase the risk of infection. Offer vaccinations such as
pneumococcal, influenza, and COVID.
• Advise her to avoid close contact with anyone who has chickenpox, shingles,
or measles if she has never had these illnesses, as she would not have
natural immunity. Tell her to seek urgent medical advice immediately if she is
exposed.
• Inform her that steroids can cause stomach irritation, so you will prescribe a
medication to protect her stomach lining, such as omeprazole 20 mg once
daily.
• Explain that steroids must not be stopped suddenly and provide a blue steroid
card.
• Explain that an X-ray is not required, as it would not help confirm the
diagnosis and would expose her to unnecessary radiation.
• Explain to her that physiotherapy can be trialled to help rebuild strength in the
shoulders and support a return to her usual level of function once she is
stabilised on steroid treatment.
• Provide safety-netting about symptoms of giant cell arteritis, such as new
headache, visual disturbance, or jaw pain, and advise her to seek urgent help
through the GP urgent line or 111 if these occur.
• Arrange review in one week.

-- 513 of 1282 --

512
What are other options for patient who refuse Steroids?
Other options would be to start them on NSAIDs to control pain in the interim and
refer to Rheumatology to consider biologics like methotrexate etc.
Learning Point from This Station
This station highlights the importance of recognising the typical presentation of
polymyalgia rheumatica and understanding the key role of inflammatory markers in
its diagnosis. PMR commonly presents with bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain,
marked morning stiffness lasting more than 45 minutes, and significant functional
limitation. A rapid and sustained response to steroids further supports the diagnosis.
It is important to remember that mild normocytic, normochromic anaemia is a
common finding in inflammatory conditions (or chronic disease) such as PMR. This
type of anaemia occurs because inflammation affects the body’s ability to use iron
effectively and reduces red blood cell production, even when iron stores are normal.
Therefore, a low haemoglobin with normal MCV and MCHC should prompt clinicians
to consider chronic inflammatory disorders as part of the differential diagnosis.
Starting long-term steroid therapy requires careful counselling, including discussing
potential risks such as raised blood glucose, bone thinning, increased infection risk,
stomach irritation, and adrenal suppression.
It is essential to provide a steroid blue card and emphasise that steroids must never
be stopped abruptly, especially after prolonged use (more than three weeks). Abrupt
withdrawal can lead to adrenal insufficiency or adrenal crisis, which is a medical
emergency.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/polymyalgia-rheumatica/

something more serious. You also want to know if there are ways to improve your
mood and adjust better to your new life.
You do not have genuine friends as most of your friends are from different

you still do your school activities well

Concerns: You are worried that your mood might get worse or that it might turn into
something more serious, such as depression.

Marking scheme

-- 516 of 1282 --

515
Explain to patient
Ayesha, I understand that you’ve been feeling low since moving to the UK, and it’s
completely natural to feel this way during such a big change. From what you’ve
described, it sounds like you may be experiencing something called adjustment
disorder. This is a condition that happens when someone has trouble adapting to a
new situation or environment, especially when there’s a major life change, like
moving to a new country.
Moving to a new place, adjusting to a different culture, and being away from family
can all be really challenging. It’s very common for people in similar situations to feel
a bit low or out of sorts as they navigate these changes. The good news is that
adjustment disorder is usually temporary and often improves as you get more settled
and accustomed to your new surroundings.
I also agree with your brother that vitamin D deficiency could be playing a role in how
you're feeling. Vitamin D is really important for mood regulation, and a deficiency can
contribute to feelings of low energy or even sadness. It’s a good idea to get a blood
test to check your vitamin D levels. If they’re low, we can work on correcting that,
which may help improve how you’re feeling.
Management
• Offer bloods for Vitamin D , thyroid function, LFTs, FBC , U+E’s Bone profile,
CRP
• Offer CBT
• Recommend seeking out cultural or community groups in the UK that may
offer support—you can also signpost to social prescriber who can help with
organizing this
• Safety netting: Explain that if her mood does not improve or if she
experiences more severe symptoms, like persistent feelings of hopelessness,
guilt, or suicidal thoughts, she should seek help immediately.
• Advise her to return for follow-up in a few weeks to review her progress
Learning point from this station
This patient clearly has adjustment disorder. Adjustment disorder and depression
can be very difficult to differentiate. However, few things stand out
Depression: this usually affects sleep, appetite , loss of interest in things they love
doing, including daily activities and symptoms are constant and sometimes without a
trigger

-- 517 of 1282 --

516
Adjustment disorder, there is a trigger or a specific life stressor or change, such as
moving to a new country, starting a new job, or experiencing a significant life
transition (e.g., leaving family and friends behind).
If Ayesha were experiencing depression, her low mood would likely be more
persistent and may include additional symptoms such as a loss of interest in
activities, significant changes in sleep or appetite, or feelings of hopelessness
that are not necessarily related to the stress of her move.
Depression would also be more likely to interfere with her ability to engage in daily
tasks, and her symptoms could last much longer if not treated.

progression. His performance status has declined significantly, and further disease-
modifying treatment is not feasible.
Mr. Aldershot has been transitioned to a palliative care pathway, with a focus on
symptom management and maintaining comfort. He is fully aware of his poor
prognosis, which is estimated to be in the range of weeks to a few months. He
understands that his care is now focused on maximizing quality of life rather than
pursuing curative treatments. He is now discharged back to your able hands for end-
of-life care. Please do not hesitate to contact our team if there are any concerns.
Kind regards,
Dr Peter Obi , MBBS, MRCP (UK), FRCR, MSc (Oncology)
Consultant clinical and radiation oncologist

Explanation
Adam, I want to reassure you that based on what you've described, it’s very unlikely
to be anything as serious as penile cancer. What you’re experiencing sounds much
more like a condition called Peyronie’s disease.
Peyronie’s disease is a condition where scar tissue forms inside the penis, causing it
to bend or curve during an erection. While some curvature of the penis is normal,
this condition can make the bend more noticeable and sometimes uncomfortable.
The scar tissue develops for reasons we don’t fully understand. It can happen after
an injury to the penis—like during sex or a physical activity—or sometimes without
any clear cause
Peyronie’s disease is actually quite common, and in some cases, it can improve on
its own over time.
However, since the pain and curvature are significantly affecting your erections and
your intimacy with your partner, it’s important to get some further help. I’m going to
refer you to a urologist—a specialist in this area—who will be able to assess things
in more detail. They might discuss treatments like using a vacuum device to improve
blood flow, or in more severe cases, they could suggest surgery to correct the
curvature.
It is also important to see you face to face for an examination to ensure there is
nothing sinister –although, unlikely.

medications like ibuprofen, which may help reduce both the discomfort and any

your partner—to provide emotional support and strategies to cope.

Explain to patient
Oliver, based on your symptoms and history, it sounds like you’re experiencing a
condition called Eustachian tube dysfunction which is caused by your recent cold
symptoms. The Eustachian tube connects your middle ear to the back of your nose
and throat. After a cold, this tube can sometimes get blocked, causing the ear to feel
full and affecting your hearing.
The good news is that this type of hearing loss is usually temporary and tends to
resolve once the Eustachian tube starts functioning normally again.
However, since you’re regularly exposed to loud music, we should also check for any
signs of noise-induced hearing damage to ensure there’s no permanent impact.
Management
• Offer face to face to examine ear with otoscope, examine nerves of the body
(cranial nerve exam-facial nerve) and to also perform some hearing test
(Rinne’s and Weber's)
• Offer to refer to audiology for formal hearing test/audiometry. You can also
wait and review in 2 weeks before offering audiometry as this is also
reasonable
• Limit exposure to loud music where possible. Encourage the use of ear
protection, like noise-cancelling earplugs, during band practice to prevent
further damage.
• Re-assure that eustachian tube dysfunction usually resolves within 6 weeks
for most people
• Safety Netting: If the hearing loss doesn’t improve in next 2-3 weeks or
worsens, return for review.
• Advice to seek urgent medical attention if he develops severe pain, dizziness,
or discharge from the ear, which could indicate an infection.
Important things to note here
• Sudden onset Sudden-onset (developing within 72 hours) unilateral or
bilateral hearing loss, which has occurred within the past 30 days and cannot
be explained by external or middle ear causes will need urgent referral (Within
24hrs) to ENT for consideration of starting steroids

-- 529 of 1282 --

528
• If this patient has any focal neurology—they also need same day assessment
to rule out stroke
• If they have sudden-onset (developing within 72 hours) unilateral or bilateral

period of 4–90 days), which cannot be explained by external or middle ear
causes. They need to be referred to ENT or an audio-vestibular medicine

• Eustachian tube dysfunction is very common cause of hearing loss following a
viral infection. It can also be caused by altitude change (When travelling by
airplane) , smoking, sinusitis/rhinitis/hay fever/glue ear.
• Symptoms include; Dulled or muffled hearing, sensation of popping in the ear,
ear pressure or mild pain, ringing or buzzing in the ear (tinnitus), mild
dizziness may occur.

"Samira, I can see why you’re concerned about your symptoms, especially

tuberculosis (TB), which is a bacterial infection that affects the lungs. TB is
still relatively common in Bangladesh, and it’s something we should
investigate. Your work in the factory may have exposed you to silica dust,
which can increase the risk of lung problems, including TB. Silica exposure
can weaken the lungs, making them more vulnerable to infections like TB, and
in rare cases, it can also increase the risk of other lung conditions.
I also understand your concern about cancer, especially with the coughing up
blood. While lung cancer is less likely at your age and with no smoking
history, it’s important to take your symptoms seriously and rule out any
serious conditions.
There are other possible causes for your symptoms, like chronic infections or
non-cancerous lung diseases, so the next step will be to run some tests, such
as a chest X-ray and sputum tests, to find out what’s going on and guide the
right treatment."

• Inform her that as TB is suspected, she will be referred urgently to a

• Advice that if TB is confirmed, she will be given antibiotics for about 6 months
to eradicate it
• Advice that TB is spread from person to person through the air when
someone with active TB in their lungs coughs, sneezes, or even talks. The

bacteria can become airborne and be inhaled by people nearby. If she is

Patient’s Story (Role Player’s Brief)
You are Justina Coleman, a 28-year-old female.
Opening Statement: Hi doctor, I am calling because I keep having problems with
my hands. They sometimes change colour and it has been getting worse.
If Asked to Explain Further:
You have booked this appointment because you have noticed problems with your
hands for about 4 years, but they have become worse in the last 6 months. Your
hands first go white, then turn blue, and finally red. When your hands are white or
blue, they feel numb. When they turn red, they become painful.
You have realised that the episodes tend to occur when you play the clarinet. You
usually have to stop playing, and the symptoms settle after about 15 minutes.
You are a professional musician and play the clarinet at special events such as
weddings and exclusive dinners.
Social History: You smoke around 15 cigarettes per day and have done so for the
last 5 years. You live with your partner. You do not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs.
Family History: Unknown, as you were adopted.
Ideas: You are unsure what this condition might be.
Concerns: You have an important show on a big stage in 4 weeks and worry this
problem will affect your performance.

-- 535 of 1282 --

534
Expectations: You would like advice on what this could be and what can be done to
help manage it.
Role-player instruction:
If the doctor offers you a face-to-face appointment to examine your hands, ask:
“What will this change, since I do not have the symptoms now and this only happens
when I play the clarinet?”
Say NO to any other questions/symptoms outside of the details already provided in
the scenario. Accept anything offered to you by the doctor.
Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about onset and duration of symptoms
• Ask if symptoms affect both hands or just one.
• Ask about colour changes in the fingers (white, blue, red) and how long it
takes to return to normal
• Ask if there is associated pain in the hands or fingers during an episode
• Ask about abnormal sensations such as tingling, numbness, or pins and
needles
• Ask about known triggers such as cold exposure, repetitive finger use (e.g.
clarinet playing in the above patient), emotional stress, or vibrating tools
• Ask if other areas such as nose tip, earlobes, tongue, or nipples are affected
• Ask about any ulcers on the fingers or thickening/tightening of the skin
(sclerodactyly), which may suggest secondary Raynaud’s
• Ask about weakness or reduced grip strength in the hands
• Ask about itchiness, red or purple lesions with swelling on the fingers after
cold exposure, which may suggest chilblains. Please note: Chilblains are
usually triggered by cold alone. Raynaud’s, on the other hand, can be
triggered not only by cold but also by stress, vibrating tools, or repetitive finger
use.
• Ask about symptoms of CREST syndrome — a limited form of systemic
sclerosis: Calcinosis (hard lumps under the skin), Raynaud’s phenomenon,
Oesophageal dysmotility (difficulty swallowing), Sclerodactyly (tightening
of the skin on fingers), and Telangiectasia (visible small blood vessels on the
skin).
• Ask about the impact of symptoms on the patient’s life and activities (e.g.
playing clarinet)
• Ask about smoking, alcohol use, and occupational exposures (cold or
vibrating tools)
• Ask about family history of Raynaud’s or autoimmune conditions

-- 536 of 1282 --

535
• Ask about patient’s ideas, concerns and expectations.
• Make a diagnosis of Raynaud’s Phenomenon.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Justina, thank you for explaining what has been happening with your hands.
From what you have told me, your symptoms sound very much like a condition
called Raynaud’s phenomenon. Is this something you have heard of before?
This is when the small blood vessels in the fingers temporarily narrow. That can
cause the fingers to change colour, go numb, and then become painful as the blood
flow returns. In many people it is triggered by cold or stress, but in your case, it does
sound like repetitive finger use when you are playing the clarinet is acting as the
trigger. Does that make sense so far?
I know you do not have the symptoms right now, but I would like to see you in
person. This would allow me to check the blood flow in your hands by feeling the
pulses, and also to measure your blood pressure. That is important before we
consider starting any treatment. Would you be comfortable with that?
In addition, would it be okay if I arranged some blood tests for you, just to make sure
there is no other condition causing your symptoms? Sometimes Raynaud’s happens
on its own, but other times it can be linked to other conditions, which we call
secondary Raynaud’s.
In future, it would also help if you could take photographs of your fingers during an
episode. That way, we can see exactly what happens. How would you feel about
doing that?
In terms of treatment, there is a medicine we can use called Nifedipine. It works by
relaxing the blood vessels, which improves circulation and helps prevent the
symptoms from happening. Most people get on well with this medication, but it can
sometimes cause light-headedness because it lowers blood pressure. If you notice
any side effects, please let us know straight away. Are you happy to try this
medication?
Alongside medicines, there are also things you can do that can make a big
difference. Stopping smoking is really important, as smoking reduces circulation and
makes Raynaud’s worse. Do you feel this is something you might be able to
consider? We have excellent stop-smoking services that can support you if you
would like help.
Also, taking short breaks when you play, and using padded finger supports or small
finger cushions on the clarinet, may help reduce the strain on your fingers. Stress
can sometimes be a trigger as well, so simple techniques like deep breathing or
relaxation exercises may make a difference. How do those ideas sound to you?

-- 537 of 1282 --

536
Keeping your hands warm before and during performances can also help, for
example, by using gloves, hand warmers, or even warming your hands in water
beforehand.
With these approaches, most people notice their symptoms improve and are able to
carry on with their normal activities, including work. What are your thoughts on this
plan?
Management
• Offer a face-to-face assessment to examine the hands for pulses, check for
ulcers, and measure blood pressure
• Explain to the patient that although she does not have symptoms currently,
examination is important to assess blood flow by checking her pulse and to
check blood pressure before starting any treatment
• Advise the patient to take photographs of the affected fingers during an
episode if possible
• Arrange blood tests including autoimmune screen, CRP, ESR, and FBC. Note
that these are important to check for secondary Raynaud’s. Secondary
Raynaud’s can be linked to occupational factors such as repetitive hand
movements (like playing the clarinet) or the use of vibrating tools, and it is less
likely to have a family history compared to primary Raynaud’s
• Offer Nifedipine (immediate release) 5 mg three times a day, explaining that
this medication can lower blood pressure and may cause dizziness, but most
people tolerate it well; advise the patient to report any troublesome side
effects.
• Strongly advise smoking cessation, as smoking worsens circulation and can
aggravate Raynaud’s symptoms
• Suggest and explore strategies for managing during performances, such as
taking breaks, warming the hands between sessions, and considering padded
finger supports or ergonomic adjustments to the instrument to reduce strain
and repetitive pressure
• Advise that stress can be a trigger, and encourage stress-management
techniques such as deep breathing or relaxation exercises
• Encourage the patient to keep hands warm before and during playing, for
example by using gloves, hand warmers.

-- 538 of 1282 --

537
Learning Point from this Station
Raynaud’s phenomenon describes the symptoms of the condition, while Raynaud’s
disease (also called primary Raynaud’s) refers to cases where it occurs on its
owwithout an underlying cause. Secondary Raynaud’s phenomenon occurs when
the symptoms are caused by another health condition, medication, or external factor.
In this patient’s case, repetitive finger use from playing the clarinet is the likely
trigger, pointing towards secondary Raynaud’s. Secondary Raynaud’s is also more
common if onset is after the age of 30 years, if episodes are painful or asymmetrical,
and if they progress to complications such as digital ischaemia, ulceration, infection,
or even tissue necrosis and loss of digits.
Investigations should include full blood count (FBC), inflammatory markers (CRP and
ESR), thyroid function tests, and anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) titres to screen for
secondary causes.
In terms of treatment, Nifedipine can be used daily for both primary and secondary
Raynaud’s. However, in primary Raynaud’s, nifedipine can also be used
intermittently, meaning the patient only takes it when they know they will be
exposed to cold, rather than taking it every day. For this patient involved, intermittent
use could be considered once secondary causes have been excluded.
Referral to a rheumatologist should be made if:
• Symptoms are frequent and severe, or
• There is evidence of secondary Raynaud’s phenomenon, such as a positive
ANA on blood tests or an associated connective tissue disease (for example
rheumatoid arthritis, CREST syndrome, or systemic lupus erythematosus).
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/raynauds-phenomenon/

-- 539 of 1282 --

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset of symptoms (when they first started and how they have
changed)
• Ask about frequency and intensity of symptoms (continuous or with specific
triggers such as before presentations)
• Ask if the patient’s symptoms are constant worry and tension most days (this
points to generalised anxiety disorder) or if the symptoms come as sudden
strong attacks that peak quickly and then settle (this points to panic
disorder).
• Ask about associated symptoms such as tingling around the lips or mouth,
restlessness, shortness of breath, palpitations, or chest pain
• Ask about the impact of symptoms on daily life and work (especially new job
performance)
• Ask about mood and general emotional wellbeing
• Screen for suicide or self-harm risk
• Ask about sleep quality and appetite
• Ask about smoking, alcohol, or illicit drug use
• Ask about any personal or family history of anxiety, panic attacks, or other
mental health conditions
• Explore patient’s Ideas, Concerns, and Expectations (ICE)
• Give working diagnosis of possible generalised anxiety disorder.
Example of Explanation to the patient
Abisola, thank you for sharing all of this with me. From what you have told me; I do
agree with you that you are experiencing anxiety. The type you describe fits with
something we call generalised anxiety disorder. This means that the mind and body
become overly worried about everyday things, more than the actual risk, and it starts

-- 541 of 1282 --

540
to interfere with daily life. The feelings you have such as your heart racing,
restlessness, and being on edge are very typical of this. Are you following me so far?
I can understand why you were hoping for diazepam. Diazepam can sometimes help
people feel calm for a short while, but it is not a safe long-term treatment because it
can cause dependence and addiction, and in some people, it can make anxiety
worse over time. What I can do is give you a very short course to help you through
this particularly stressful period and your upcoming presentation, but I would not be
able to continue it after that. Would that sound reasonable to you?
Looking beyond this week, the best way to manage anxiety is with longer-term
treatments. One option is talking therapy, such as what we call Cognitive
Behavioural Therapy (CBT). This helps people recognise and manage unhelpful
thought patterns, and most people find it very helpful. Is this something you would
like to explore?
Another option is a medication called sertraline. It is not addictive, and it works very
well for many people, although it usually takes about 4 to 6 weeks before you feel
the full benefit. Sometimes in the first week, anxiety can feel a little worse before it
starts to improve, which is why I would suggest starting this after your presentation.
How would you feel about that?
I also want to be open with you that, in a small number of people, the medication
sertraline can increase suicidal thoughts when they are first started. Because of this,
we always arrange to review you after one week, to make sure you are coping well.
If at any point you ever felt unsafe or developed thoughts of harming yourself, I
would want you to seek urgent help straight away, either from us, by calling 111, or
by going to A&E.
There are also self-help approaches that can make a difference. Things like
breathing exercises, relaxation techniques, mindfulness, good sleep, and regular
physical activity. These are not a replacement for treatment, but they can really
support recovery and reduce the overall anxiety burden.
Management
• Offer a very short course of diazepam (for example, 2 mg three times daily for
up to 3 days), but emphasise this is for short-term use only and will not be re-
prescribed, due to the risks of tolerance, dependence, and addiction
• Explain the importance of long-term management of anxiety rather than
relying on diazepam
• Offer Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT) as first-line talking therapy

-- 542 of 1282 --

541
• Discuss the option of starting an SSRI such as sertraline for long-term control.
Explain that it usually takes around 4 to 6 weeks to feel the full benefit of the
medication, and that symptoms of anxiety can temporarily worsen in the first
week. Inform patient about other common side effects such as mild
gastrointestinal upset.
• If the patient is happy to start long-term management with an SSRI, explain
that since her presentation is in 5 days, it would be best to begin the SSRI
after the presentation. This is because SSRIs can sometimes make anxiety
feel worse in the first week before improvement is noticed.
• Advise that there is a small increased risk of suicidal thinking in some people
when starting SSRIs and arrange a follow-up review within 1 week of starting
the medication.
• Advise patient on self-help strategies like relaxation techniques, breathing
exercises, mindfulness, lifestyle adjustments such as regular sleep and
exercise.
• Safety-net: advise the patient to seek urgent help if she develops suicidal
thoughts, self-harm risk, or worsening symptoms.
Learning Point from this Station
Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD) is characterised by excessive worry about
everyday issues that is disproportionate to the actual risk. It is important to note that
GAD can sometimes be confused with other conditions such as panic disorder, and
both can also occur together.
In panic disorder, the patient typically describes sudden episodes of intense fear or
discomfort that peak within minutes. Symptoms may include palpitations, sweating,
trembling, shortness of breath, chest tightness, dizziness, or a feeling of impending
death or loss of control.
In generalised anxiety disorder, the patient usually describes persistent anxiety
present most days for at least six months. They often report constant worry, feeling
on edge, restlessness, poor sleep, muscle tension, irritability, and difficulty
concentrating.
Although the conditions present differently, their management is similar, with both
benefiting from psychological therapies such as Cognitive Behavioural Therapy and
the use of SSRIs when medication is required.

-- 543 of 1282 --

542
Please note: NICE does not recommendpropranolol for the treatment of anxiety
or panic disorders. However, the BNF states that propranolol is licensed for the
physical symptoms of anxiety, such as palpitations, sweating, and tremor.
For exam and practical purposes, it is advisable not to prescribe propranolol, as
there is a growing trend towards discouraging its use. This is due to its toxicity in
overdose and the potentially under-recognised risk of harm, which prescribers
should carefully consider before initiating treatment.
Diazepam can be offered as a short-term measure during acute crises or for
temporary relief. Treatment should not exceed 2–4 weeks, and a typical dose is 2
mg three times a day.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/generalized-anxiety-disorder/
https://patient.info/doctor/mental-health/generalised-anxiety-disorder-pro

Dear GP,
Re: Richard Holmes | 53 years old | male
Thank you for your referral of Mr Richard Holmes, who presented with fatigue and
joint pain. His initial blood tests revealed elevated serum ferritin and transferrin
saturation levels. Genetic testing confirmed homozygosity for the HFE C282Y
mutation, consistent with hereditary hemochromatosis.
He has commenced therapeutic venesection, which has successfully reduced his
ferritin levels into the target range. He reports some improvement in symptoms and
has tolerated the procedure well, without complications.
Mr Holmes will require regular venesection therapy to maintain ferritin levels
between 50–100 μg/L and transferrin saturation below 50%. This will be arranged
through the hospital phlebotomy service/ haematology clinic. We have advised him
to avoid iron fortified foods, vitamin C supplements, and alcohol to reduce liver
strain. Liver function tests and imaging (including a recent ultrasound) have shown
no evidence of cirrhosis or hepatocellular carcinoma at this stage. However, ongoing
monitoring will be necessary, and I have copied Dr Sharma (Consultant
Hepatologist) to this letter who will ensure this monitoring. I have advised that he
discusses family screening of his condition with his GP.
Mr Holmes will continue to attend the haematology clinic for venesection and
monitoring, and we will keep you updated on his progress. Should he develop new
symptoms or if there are any concerns, please do not hesitate to contact us.

Patient’s Story (Role Player’s Brief)
You are Richard Holmes, a 53-year-old man.
Opening Statement: Hi doctor, I was recently diagnosed with something called
haemochromatosis. I have been having treatment at the hospital where they take
blood out regularly, and although I am feeling better, I am quite worried about what
this means for my family.
You were diagnosed with haemochromatosis, a condition where the body stores too
much iron. You have been having treatment at the hospital, where they remove
blood at intervals (what the hospital called venesection). This treatment has helped
and you feel physically better, but you now have concerns about your family.
Concerns: You were told by the consultant, and have also read online, that this
condition is genetic. You are worried that you may have passed it on to your
children. You have a 25-year-old daughter and a 20-year-old son, and you want to
know if they should be tested. Your children do not have any symptoms at present.
Expectations: You would like the GP to explain more about the condition and to
