Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about current symptoms: For example, Richard, the letter says your
symptoms have improved, just wanted to check if that is still the case. Any
persistent fatigue, joint pain, abdominal discomfort, erectile dysfunction, or
skin changes
• Ask about family history of haemochromatosis or other iron-related conditions
• Ask about children’s health: any symptoms of fatigue, joint problems, or
unexplained illness
• Ask about partner: any symptoms or family history of similar conditions
• Ask about social and dietary habits, including alcohol, smoking, red meat,
iron-fortified foods, and occupation
• Ask about low mood and/or any anxiety related to the diagnosis
• Explore patient’s understanding of haemochromatosis
• Explore patient’s concerns and expectations
Example of Explanation to the patient
Richard, you are absolutely right. Haemochromatosis is a condition where too much
iron builds up in the body over time. The excess iron tends to get stored in organs
such as the liver, heart, and pancreas, and if it is not managed properly, it can
eventually cause damage to these organs. The good news is that with treatment and
regular monitoring, we can usually prevent these complications."
"This condition does run in families. Most people develop it when they inherit two
copies of the faulty gene, one from each parent. This means your children could
either inherit two copies, one copy, or no copies at all. If they have two copies, they
may develop the condition like you. If they have one copy, they would usually just be
carriers and not have symptoms, although in rare cases even carriers can build up
some extra iron.
Are you following me so far? Does that explanation make sense, or would you like
me to go over any part again?
You mentioned that you are not sure about your wife’s family history. Even without
that information, because carriers can sometimes build up extra iron, it is important
that your children who are over the age of 16 get screened. The screening usually
starts with a simple blood test to check their iron levels, and if needed, they may also
be referred for genetic counselling. I would suggest that you encourage both of your
children to arrange this with their GP. Does that make sense?
In terms of your diet, you don’t need to completely avoid foods that naturally contain
iron, like red meat or spinach, because they’re part of a healthy diet and provide

-- 547 of 1282 --

546
important nutrients. You can take them in moderation. Making big changes such as
avoiding all foods that contain iron will not give you extra benefit since you are
already having treatment.
What will help is avoiding foods with added iron, such as some breakfast cereals or
meal replacement drinks. These are often labelled ‘iron-fortified,’ so it is worth
checking the packaging when shopping.
Management
• Explain haemochromatosis in simple terms, including the genetic basis and
why iron builds up in the body
• Advise that his children should be tested. They can arrange this through their
own GP, who will start with blood tests such as serum ferritin and transferrin
saturation, and may refer for genetic testing if needed
• Advise keeping alcohol intake within recommended limits to reduce the risk of
liver damage
• Advise the patient that they do not need to completely avoid foods with natural
iron. These foods are safe if eaten in moderation because treatment keeps
iron levels under control. The key advice is to avoid foods with added iron,
such as iron-fortified cereals or iron supplements.
• Advise avoiding iron-containing vitamin supplements, iron-fortified foods (such
as some breakfast cereals), and vitamin C supplements, as these can
increase iron absorption
• Advise to monitor for and report any new symptoms such as worsening
fatigue, joint pain, abdominal pain, or skin changes
• Advise the patient to contact the GP promptly if he develops any new
concerns or symptoms.
Learning Point from this Station
Hereditary haemochromatosis (HHC) is most commonly inherited in an autosomal
recessive pattern, meaning both copies of the gene must be altered for the condition
to develop. An exception is type 4 haemochromatosis, which is inherited in an
autosomal dominant pattern, where only one altered gene copy is sufficient.
The condition occurs due to increased intestinal absorption of iron, leading to iron
accumulation in tissues, particularly the liver, which may result in organ damage if
untreated.
Early diagnosis can be difficult because HHC is often asymptomatic in its early
stages. Symptoms typically begin between ages 40–60 in males, and after the

-- 548 of 1282 --

547
menopause in females. Clinical features include fatigue, weakness, joint pain
(arthropathy), nonspecific abdominal discomfort, erectile dysfunction, and in
advanced disease, bronzing of the skin.
Screening: HHC is an adult-onset condition, therefore carrier testing is not
recommended in healthy children.
Young children are very unlikely to be clinically affected. Unless there are symptoms,
genetic testing is not indicated until the child is over 16 years old and able to
understand the implications.
Screening is offered to first-degree relatives over the age of 16 of affected
individuals.
Further Reading
https://patient.info/doctor/haematology/hereditary-haemochromatosis

Example of Explanation to the Patient
James, thank you for being open about what has been happening. From what you
have told me; you are experiencing erectile dysfunction. This is a condition where a
man either cannot get an erection or cannot keep an erection for long enough to
have sex. It is actually quite common in men, especially if they have conditions such
as diabetes, high blood pressure, or raised cholesterol. Sometimes medications or
lifestyle factors, like alcohol or drug use, can also play a part. Are you following me
so far?
Moving forward, would it be okay for me to arrange a face-to-face appointment so
that I can examine you? This would involve checking your penis and testes to look
for any changes such as a bend in the penis or lumps in the testicles. Because you

-- 552 of 1282 --

551
are over 50, I would also check your prostate, as some prostate problems can affect
erections. I would also check your blood pressure and pulse to make sure everything
is in order. Would that be okay with you?
I would also like to arrange some blood tests. These would include your early
morning hormone level (testosterone), cholesterol, thyroid function, liver function,
and a prostate blood test (PSA) given your age. These tests will help us check for
underlying causes and make sure nothing important is being missed. Does that
sound alright to you?
You mentioned using poppers. I need to explain that these can sometimes affect
erections themselves, and more importantly, they must never be used together with
sildenafil, the tablet often used for erectile dysfunction. The combination can cause a
very sudden drop in blood pressure, which can be life-threatening. If you are able to
stop using poppers, sildenafil would be an option for you.
You mentioned that you were interested in trying sildenafil. Sildenafil, sometimes
called Viagra, works by improving blood flow to the penis. The usual starting dose is
50 mg, taken about an hour before sex. Some men do buy it over the counter, but
before I prescribe it, I would like to ask you a few questions first to make sure it is
safe for you.
Do you ever get chest pain, feel short of breath, or struggle with exercise? I also
need to ask if there is any family history of rare eye conditions, such as retinitis
pigmentosa.
Common side effects of sildenafil include headache, flushing, and dizziness, but
most men tolerate it well. One important safety point is that if you ever have an
erection lasting longer than three to four hours, you must seek urgent medical help
straight away. Once more, it is very important that you do not take sildenafil at the
same time as poppers, as this combination can cause a dangerous drop in blood
pressure.
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to examine the genitalia (penis and testes)
and perform a prostate examination, as the patient is over 50. At the same
visit, check blood pressure and pulse.
• Arrange blood tests including HbA1c, early-morning testosterone (between 9–
11 am), lipid profile, prostate-specific antigen (PSA for men over 50), thyroid
function tests (TFTs), and liver function tests (LFTs).

-- 553 of 1282 --

552
• Offer to calculate QRISK score to assess cardiovascular risk, as an organic
cause is suspected
• Explain to the patient that using poppers can also affect erections and may
contribute to the erection difficulties he is experiencing.
• Inform the patient that sildenafil must not be used with poppers. Taking them
together can cause a dangerous drop in blood pressure, which in some cases
can be life-threatening. If the patient agrees to stop using poppers, then
sildenafil may be safely offered.
• Offer sildenafil 50 mg if the patient agrees to stop using poppers. Explain that
it can also be purchased over the counter without prescription. Instruct to take
it one hour before sexual activity.
• Before prescribing, ask about any history of chest pain, shortness of breath,
or difficulty with exercise, as this helps to quickly assess cardiac risk before
offering PDE5 inhibitors like sildenafil. Also ask about any personal or family
history of eye/retinal disease, such as retinitis pigmentosa. Inform the patient
about common side effects of sildenafil, including headache, flushing, and
dizziness, and re-assure him that most people tolerate the medication well.
• Advise and safety net regarding priapism which is a rare side effect of
sildenafil: explain that if he develops an erection lasting longer than 3 to 4
hours, he must seek urgent medical help
• Offer the patient a referral for psychosexual counselling, if he feels this would
be helpful.
• Provide lifestyle advice: healthy eating, regular exercise and weight loss (BMI
is raised)
• Offer follow-up in 6 weeks
Learning Point From this Station
Erectile dysfunction is very common, particularly in men over the age of 40. It may
be due to an organic cause, a psychogenic cause, or a combination of both, with
most cases being of mixed aetiology.
Organic causes:
• Vascular: cardiovascular disease, hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, peripheral
arterial disease, diabetes mellitus, smoking, obesity
• Neurological: multiple sclerosis, Parkinson’s disease, stroke, spinal cord or
central nervous system disease
• Neurogenic/metabolic: diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, chronic
liver disease, pelvic or urological surgery
• Anatomical: Peyronie’s disease, prostate cancer

-- 554 of 1282 --

553
Psychogenic causes:
• Disorders of arousal or intimacy
• Relationship problems
• Stress, anxiety, depression
Drug-related causes:
• Antihypertensives, diuretics, antidepressants, hormonal treatments
• Recreational drugs such as poppers, which are sometimes used by gay
men for sexual pleasure, can also worsen erectile problems.
Poppers (amyl nitrite) are liquid substances that some people inhale to experience
euphoria, relaxation, or to enhance sexual activity. They are commonly used among
gay men. Poppers act as muscle relaxants, causing relaxation of smooth muscles
such as those around the anus. However, they can worsen erectile problems. More
importantly, poppers must never be taken together with sildenafil or other PDE5
inhibitors, as this can cause a sudden and dangerous drop in blood pressure, which
may be life-threatening.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/erectile-dysfunction/
https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/324000

needs steroid cream.
Say NO to any other questions/symptoms asked outside of the details already
provided in the scenario. Accept anything offered to you by the doctor.
Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about eczema history: onset, frequency of flares, and treatments used so
far
• Ask if the child is currently experiencing a flare

-- 557 of 1282 --

556
• Ask if flares occur after specific triggers (e.g. foods, environmental factors,
infections, soaps, temperature changes)
• Explore symptoms suggestive of milk allergy: Any vomiting, diarrhoea, or
abdominal pain after consuming milk? Any hives/raised rashes, or swelling
after milk or certain foods? Any wheezing or breathing difficulties after milk or
certain foods?
• Ask about current feeding and diet: types of foods eaten and milk consumed
• Ask about other associated allergic conditions in Kwame, such as asthma or
hay fever
• Ask about family history of atopic conditions, such as asthma, eczema, or hay
fever.
• Ask about the impact of eczema on daily life (e.g. does it affect sleep, feeding,
or play)
• Ask about the home situation: who lives at home, whether there are any other
children, and if they also suffer with skin problems or milk allergy.
• Ask about pregnancy, birth, immunisation, and developmental history.
• Explore mother’s Ideas, Concerns, and Expectations (ICE)
• Give a diagnosis of recurrent flare of eczema, with possible cow’s milk protein
allergy as a contributing factor.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Mrs Mensah, I completely understand your worries about Kwame’s eczema and the
possibility of a milk allergy. You are right that some children with eczema can also
have food allergies, including milk. In Kwame’s case, he does not seem to have the
usual signs of milk allergy, such as tummy pain, vomiting, diarrhoea, raised rashes
(hives), or breathing problems. His growth and general health are also good, which is
reassuring.
That said, it is still important that we look into whether milk could be playing a part in
his eczema. For this reason, I will arrange a referral to a paediatrician/paediatric
dietitian (children’s specialist). They will assess him in more detail, and if needed,
they may place him on a special milk. At Kwame’s age, this type of prescription is
best managed by specialists to make sure he continues to get all the nutrition he
needs and avoids any deficiencies. Are you happy for me to make this referral?
In the meantime, it is reasonable to stop cow’s milk from Kwame’s diet, since he is
on complementary feeding and will still receive the nutrition, he needs from his
regular foods. This will also help us to see whether his eczema improves without
cow’s milk.
It would be very helpful to keep a diary of when his flares happen and any possible
triggers, like certain foods, temperature changes, or soaps and detergents. This can

-- 558 of 1282 --

557
really help us and the specialists to work out if something is contributing to his
eczema.
Please continue with his emollients as you have been doing, you are doing a great
job there.
You mentioned that you would like Kwame’s steroid cream to be on repeat
prescription, and I completely understand why. In general, we do not usually put
steroid creams on repeat for eczema, because the condition can change over time
and it is important for a healthcare professional to check the skin regularly to make
sure the treatment is still the right one.
However, because Kwame’s flares are happening quite often, I think it would be
reasonable to put the steroid on repeat prescription, but with a clear plan. I would like
to start something called weekend therapy. This means applying a very thin layer of
the steroid cream on two days (e.g. Saturday and Sunday) each week to the areas
most likely to flare, even if the skin looks normal. The aim is to help stop the eczema
from coming back so often. We would still arrange regular reviews every three
months, so we can be sure the treatment is safe and still working well for him. How
does that sound to you?
I would like to explain a little about steroid creams. If they are used for a long time,
they can sometimes cause side effects such as the skin becoming thinner, slight
changes in colour, a bit of burning or stinging, or in rare cases, stretch marks on the
skin. I want to reassure you that not every child gets these problems. Our plan is to
manage Kwame’s eczema so that the flares become less frequent, meaning he will
need the steroid cream less often. This way, the risk of side effects stays very low.
Does that make sense? Would you be happy for me to go ahead with this
prescription?
Management
• Acknowledge mum’s concerns and reassure her that she is right, milk allergy
can sometimes be linked to eczema. However, explain that eczema is very
common in young children and often improves as they grow older.
• Inform mum that specialised milk, such as extensively hydrolysed formula
(EHF) or amino acid formula (AAF), for children at this age would usually only
be prescribed on the advice of a paediatrician or paediatric dietitian.
• Refer to paediatrics/paediatric dietitian for further assessment and support.
• Advise mum that cow’s milk can be excluded from her son’s diet while
awaiting specialist review. Note: he is on complementary feeding and will
obtain sufficient nutrition from regular foods, so temporary exclusion is
reasonable until specialist advice is given on suitable alternatives.

-- 559 of 1282 --

558
• Advise keeping a symptom diary to record when flares occur and note
possible triggers, such as foods, extreme temperatures, soap products, or
detergents.
• Encourage the patient’s mother to continue regular emollient use as she is
currently doing.
• Advise mum that because her son’s flares are frequent, it is important to start
long-term management with topical steroids using a regime called weekend
therapy. This involves applying the usual steroid thinly on two consecutive
days (e.g. Saturday and Sunday) each week to the areas most prone to flare,
even if the skin looks normal, to help prevent recurrence.
• For the above reason, the topical steroid can be placed on repeat
prescription, but with regular monitoring (e.g. every 3 months).
• Inform mum about the possible long-term effects of steroid creams, such as
thinning of the skin, mild depigmentation, temporary burning or stinging, and
permanent stretch marks. Reassure her that not everyone develops these
side effects, and by working to reduce Kwame’s flares, we can also reduce
how often steroid creams are needed, which keeps the risk low.
• Safety netting: Advise to seek medical review if eczema worsens, fails to
respond to treatment, or shows signs of infection (weeping, crusting,
spreading rash, or fever).
Learning Point from this Station
Cow’s milk allergy is one of the most common food allergies in early childhood, and
most cases present before the age of 1 year. Onset after 12 months is rare,
especially for non-IgE–mediated allergy, but it can still occur, particularly with
delayed reactions.
Food allergy should be suspected in:
• Children with atopic eczema who have had a reaction after eating a food,
especially if symptoms occur immediately.
• Infants and young children with moderate or severe atopic eczema that does
not improve with optimal management.
• Children with eczema associated with gut problems (such as colic, vomiting,
or altered bowel habit) or failure to thrive.
Specialist formulas:
For children under 1 year with eczema that does not respond to emollients and
mild topical corticosteroids, a trial of extensively hydrolysed formula (EHF) or, if
necessary, amino acid formula (AAF) can be considered in primary care.

-- 560 of 1282 --

559
For children over 1 year with eczema not controlled by emollients and mild topical
corticosteroids, specialist advice from a paediatrician or paediatric dietitian should be
sought before prescribing formula, as not all EHFs are appropriate at this age.
• Examples: Aptamil Pepti Junior (EHF, suitable for 6 months–3 years),
Neocate Junior (AAF, suitable for 1–10 years).
Plant-based options such as soya protein diets may also be considered in children
over 1 year of age, but only under specialist guidance, as they carry additional
allergy risks.
Topical steroids:
• Repeat prescriptions are not generally advised in new cases of eczema
because the condition can change, and the skin needs reassessment.
• In long-standing or frequent eczema, maintenance treatment may be required.
One option is weekend therapy, where the usual topical corticosteroid is
applied thinly on two consecutive days each week to the areas most likely to
flare, even if the skin looks normal.
• In such cases, steroids can be placed on repeat prescription but must be
reviewed every 2–3 months to ensure ongoing suitability.
• Always warn parents about the potential side effects of long-term steroid use.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/eczema-atopic/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/cows-milk-allergy-in-children/

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset and duration of current symptoms.
• Ask how many episodes she has had in the last year (note: recurrent candida
is defined as 4 or more episodes per year).
• Ask about specific local symptoms: itching, redness, discharge, soreness,
pain on passing urine.
• Ask about urinary symptoms: frequency, dysuria, haematuria.
• Explore hygiene practices, including use of soaps, shower gels, bubble baths,
or douching.
• Ask about symptoms suggestive of diabetes: excessive thirst, excessive
urination, unexplained weight loss or gain.
• Ask about systemic symptoms such as fever or abdominal pain.
• Sensitively ask about any concerns regarding inappropriate contact or sexual
abuse, including changes in behaviour (for example, is she being withdrawn
or avoiding social interaction?).
• Ask about the home situation: who lives at home, family support.
• Ask about school and general wellbeing.
• Ask about general health, including nutrition and diet.
• Explore grandmother’s ICE (Ideas, Concerns, Expectations).
• Give a working diagnosis of likely vulvovaginal candidiasis
Example of Explanation to the Patient’s Grandmother
Mrs Kovalenko, thank you for sharing your concerns with me. From what you have
described, I do agree with you that this sounds very much like thrush, which is a
yeast infection. It causes the itching, redness, and whitish discharge you have
noticed in Anastasia. Are you following me so far?
You mentioned being worried that she is too young to have this. I understand your
concern. Thrush can happen in children, especially if certain factors are at play, like
using soaps, bubble baths, or even tight clothing. These things can sometimes upset
the natural balance of bacteria and yeast in that area, which makes thrush more
likely. Does that make sense?
Because this is not Anastasia’s first episode, I would like to bring her in for a face-to-
face review. That would allow me to check her gently and take a small swab from the
outside, just to confirm it is thrush. I would also suggest some simple blood tests to
rule out other causes such as diabetes or low iron. Would that be okay with you?

-- 564 of 1282 --

563
For now, I can prescribe the cream you mentioned, Canesten, as it usually helps
relieve the symptoms. Alongside this, small changes like using plain cotton
underwear, avoiding scented soaps or bubble baths, and making sure the area is
dried properly after washing can make a real difference. Are you happy to try this?
We will review her in about a week once the swab results are back, to make sure
she is improving. If she develops any new problems such as fever, tummy pain, or
unusual discharge, please seek help straight away.
Management
• Offer a face-to-face assessment to examine the genital area and take a low
vaginal swab (not HVS due to age) for culture to confirm candida.
• Arrange blood tests including FBC, ferritin, and diabetes screen (HbA1c or
fasting glucose).
• Prescribe topical clotrimazole 2% cream, applied three times daily for 7 days.
• Offer hygiene advice, including wearing cotton underwear instead of synthetic
fabrics, avoiding tight clothing, using non-scented soaps and avoiding bubble
baths or perfumed products, keeping vulval hygiene gentle, drying the genital
area properly after washing, and avoiding wet wipes on the genital skin.
• Arrange a follow-up in 7 days to review swab results and assess progress.
• Safety-netting: Advise to seek urgent medical help if she develops fever,
abdominal pain, spreading redness, or unusual discharge.
Learning Point from this Station
Vulvovaginitis is a general term that means inflammation of the vulva and vagina. It
can be caused by many things, such as yeast infections, bacterial infections,
irritation from soaps or bubble baths, poor hygiene, or even something like a small
foreign body.
Vaginal candidiasis (thrush) is a specific type of vulvovaginitis caused by a yeast
infection. Thrush is much more common in adult women and after puberty. It is
uncommon in young girls before puberty because the female hormone oestrogen
plays an important role in its development. Oestrogen makes the vaginal
environment more favourable for the Candida fungus to grow. Since
prepubescent girls have much lower oestrogen levels, the fungus is less likely to
thrive, which makes thrush less common in this age group.
However, young girls before puberty are more likely to get vulvovaginitis from
bacteria or irritation rather than thrush. This is because their vaginal tissues are

-- 565 of 1282 --

564
thinner, less protected, and the vagina is not acidic yet, which makes it easier for
bacteria to grow.
Even though thrush is less common in young girls, it can still happen, especially if
there are risk factors such as recent antibiotics, diabetes, dampness, bubble baths,
or tight clothing. In Anastasia’s case, thrush is likely because she had whitish
discharge and her symptoms improved with Canesten cream.
Recurrent vaginal thrush is defined as four or more episodes in a year. If a child
has frequent or unusual episodes, we must always think about other possible
causes, including, very importantly, the possibility of sexual abuse, and this should
never be missed.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/candida-female-genital/

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask when the weight loss and diarrhoea were first noticed.
• Ask about frequency of diarrhoea (episodes per day) and stool characteristics
(bloody or with mucus).
• Ask about other associated gastrointestinal symptoms such as vomiting,
abdominal pain (for example if he appears to be in pain, crying or drawing his
legs up to his tummy), and abdominal distension
• Ask about symptoms of allergy and anaphylaxis, including rash (particularly
immediately or within hours after feeding), shortness of breath, wheeze, or
swelling of the mouth and tongue.
• Explore appetite: what foods he eats and how often.
• Ask about fluid intake.
• Ask if he is passing urine normally (wet nappies).
• Check if symptoms have changed since the last consultation 5 weeks ago (if
unchanged, no urgent need for face-to-face review).
• Ask about recurrent infections (sore throats, chest infections, UTIs). Recurrent
chest infections can point towards cystic fibrosis.
• Ask about family history of bowel disease, especially coeliac disease.
• Ask about PBIND history: pregnancy, birth, immunisation, nutrition, and
development. Specifically confirm newborn screening (to help rule out cystic
fibrosis).
• Ask about other medical conditions such as asthma or eczema (to consider
possible food allergy).
• Ask about who lives at home (siblings, father, support).
• Explore mum’s ICE: why she thinks this could be food allergy, what she is
worried about, and what she expects.
• Give a working diagnosis of likely faltering growth / failure to thrive with
coeliac disease as a potential cause.
Example of Explanation to the Mother
Emily, thank you for sharing your concerns. I can see how worried you are about
Lucas. From what you have told me, it does sound like Lucas has what we call
faltering growth. This means that his weight, or the rate at which he is gaining
weight, is much lower than we would normally expect for a child of his age, sex, and
background. Are you following me so far?
There are a number of possible reasons why this can happen. You are right that
sometimes food allergies can play a role, but in Lucas’s case I am also concerned
about another condition called coeliac disease. This is a condition where the body

-- 569 of 1282 --

568
reacts to a protein called gluten, found in foods such as bread, pasta, and cereals.
When someone with coeliac disease eats gluten, it can damage the lining of the gut,
making it harder to absorb nutrients properly. This can lead to problems like
diarrhoea, poor weight gain, and, over time, weight. Does that make sense so far?
I want to reassure you that we can investigate this further with some blood tests and
stool tests, and whatever the outcome, we will make sure Lucas gets the right
support. Are you happy for me to arrange these tests?
It is very important that you do not stop giving him foods containing gluten, like bread
or pasta, until these tests are done. If you take gluten out of his diet too soon, the
test may come back falsely negative, and we could miss the diagnosis.
In the meantime, please keep feeding Lucas normally and make sure he stays well
hydrated. If he is not wetting his nappies, becomes very sleepy, or develops blood in
his stools, you must seek urgent medical help. I will also arrange close follow-up with
you and the health visitor so that we can keep monitoring his weight.
If the tests confirm coeliac disease, the treatment is simply removing gluten from his
diet, and dietitians will support you fully with this. If the tests are negative, we will still
refer you to a specialist, because sometimes further checks are needed.
Management
• Arrange further blood tests: coeliac screen, U&Es, folate, vitamin B12, vitamin
D, ferritin, FBC, thyroid function, and liver function tests.
• Request a stool sample for routine culture to rule out infection.
Note:Calprotectin testing is generally not recommended for children
under the age of 5. This is because inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is less
common in this age group, and baseline faecal calprotectin levels are
naturally higher, making the results more challenging to interpret. However,
very early-onset IBD (VEO-IBD) can occur in children less than 5, so it is
sensible to offer this to this child a calprotectin test, if coeliac screen or other
investigations return as normal.
• No face-to-face review required at this stage, as symptoms have not changed
since the last consultation.
• Advise mum not to stop gluten-containing foods until investigations are
complete, as this may affect test accuracy.
• Explain that if the coeliac screen is positive, referral to a specialist (paediatric
gastroenterologist) will be arranged. If negative, referral will still be made, as
false negatives can occur.

-- 570 of 1282 --

569
• If coeliac disease is confirmed, the main treatment is a lifelong gluten-free
diet. Explain that gluten-free products are available on prescription, and
dietitians will provide ongoing support.
• Encourage mum to continue offering regular meals but not to force feed.
• Liaise with health visitors for regular monitoring of weight and growth.
• Safety-net: advise urgent review if signs of dehydration develop (no wet
nappies, lethargy, unwell appearance) or if blood appears in the stools.
• Arrange follow-up in 1 week to review results and reassess progress.
Learning Point from This Station
Faltering growth describes a slower rate of weight gain in children than expected for
their age and sex. It is important to consider a wide range of causes, including
feeding difficulties, chronic infections, metabolic disorders, food allergies, and
gastrointestinal conditions such as coeliac disease.
Other contributing factors include parental stress, poor mealtime interactions, or
rarely, child maltreatment.
Coeliac disease is an autoimmune condition triggered by gluten, leading to
inflammation and damage of the small intestine. This prevents proper absorption of
nutrients and can present with diarrhoea, abdominal pain, weight loss, or poor
growth. Diagnosis requires blood tests (coeliac serology) while the child remains on
a gluten-containing diet. Gluten should not be withdrawn until tests are complete and
diagnosis is confirmed by a specialist, as this can lead to false negative results.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/coeliac-disease/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/faltering-growth/

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset of bleeding and clarify the source: is it from the nose,
vomiting blood (haematemesis), or coughing blood (haemoptysis).
• Ask whether the blood is bright red or dark (bright red suggests a fresh bleed
from the upper airway, while darker blood may indicate older blood or a
source further down)
• Ask about the frequency of the bleeding, how often it occurs, and the volume
of blood lost.
• Ask if there is any active bleeding from the nostrils or mouth, and when the
most recent episode of blood-stained mucus occurred.
• Ask about symptoms of sinus infection: nasal dripping, blocked nose, recent
flu, or a previous history of sinus infections.
• Ask about associated symptoms: cough, difficulty swallowing, heart
burn/abdominal pain, hoarse voice, neck lumps, changes in appetite,
unilateral nasal obstruction, or unilateral facial pain and visual symptoms
(which may suggest sinonasal/nasopharyngeal cancer).
• Ask about weight loss and night sweats.
• Ask how long he has been on Apixaban and whether he has been taking it
regularly.
• Ask about anaemia red flags: palpitations, chest pain, shortness of breath,
dizziness, light-headedness, headaches, and tiredness.
• Ask about social history, including smoking and alcohol intake.
• Ask about patient’s ICE (ideas, concerns and expectations).
• Give a diagnosis of possible sinonasal cancer
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Mr O’Toole, thank you for sharing your concerns with me. You are quite right that
apixaban can increase the risk of bleeding, and that could explain some of what
you’ve been noticing. It is also possible that a sinus problem or irritation at the back
of your nose could be contributing.
What does concern me is the weight loss you have described. I want to make sure
we do not miss anything serious. One of the conditions we need to rule out is a
cancer that can develop at the back of the nose or throat. Are you following me so
far?
I understand this may sound worrying, but I want to reassure you that I am not
saying you have cancer. It is just that your symptoms and smoking history mean we
cannot ignore the possibility. To investigate this properly, I will refer you urgently to
an ear, nose and throat specialist. They will see you within two weeks and examine

-- 574 of 1282 --

573
your nose and throat more closely to work out what is going on. Are you happy for
me to make this referral?
You asked about whether you should stop apixaban. You are correct that it can
increase bleeding risk. However, given that the bleeding you’ve had is only streaks in
the mucus and not heavy, and you have had no signs of major blood loss such as
dizziness or a racing heart, I think it is much safer for you to continue it. The reason
is that apixaban is very important for preventing a stroke, which is a serious
complication of atrial fibrillation. At the moment, the benefits of staying on the
medication outweigh the risks. Are you following me so far?
Of course, we will continue to monitor you closely, and if there are any changes we
will reassess. In the meantime, it is important to keep an eye on your symptoms. If
you notice the bleeding becomes heavier, if you cough up large amounts of blood, if
your stools turn black or tarry, if you feel faint, or notice unusual bruising, you must
seek urgent medical help straightaway. Does that sound clear to you? Are you happy
with this plan?
Meanwhile, would you be happy to come in today so that I can have a proper look? I
would like to examine your nose with a light, listen to your chest, and check you
over. It will also be important to arrange some blood tests, both to check your blood
count and make sure you are not losing blood, and to look at how your blood is
clotting and how well your kidneys are working, as these can contribute to your
symptoms. Does that make sense so far?
In addition, I would like to arrange a chest X-ray to have a closer look at your lungs,
just to be certain we are not missing anything there. Does that sound okay to you?
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to examine the patient. This should include
listening to the chest, using an otoscope to inspect the nostrils, and checking
blood pressure and pulse.
• Arrange blood tests including renal function, full blood count, and clotting
profile.
• Offer to make a two-week wait referral to ENT for further assessment.
• Inform the patient that the ENT specialists are likely to carry out further tests,
such as a nasal endoscopy to look directly at the back of the nose and throat,
possibly with a biopsy if needed, and imaging such as a CT scan.
• Offer an urgent chest X-ray (to be performed within 2 weeks) to assess for
lung cancer (NICE recommends offering an urgent chest X-ray in people aged

-- 575 of 1282 --

574
over 40 years who have ever smoked and report weight loss, to assess for
lung cancer).
• Inform the patient that apixaban should not be stopped at this stage, as the
bleeding is only streaks and not heavy. Stopping apixaban would significantly
increase the risk of stroke, and at present the benefits of continuing outweigh
the risks.
• Offer smoking cessation advice and support.
• Safety netting: Advise that if the patient has not heard from the specialist
within two weeks, he should contact the GP surgery. If bleeding worsens or
he develops dizziness, light-headedness, or palpitations, he should seek
urgent medical help.
Learning Point from this Station
This case highlights the importance of managing uncertainty and balancing risks
versus benefits. The patient presented with blood-stained mucus that he describes
as draining from the back of his throat. It is essential to carefully establish the source
of bleeding, which may be from the nose, throat, lungs, or gastrointestinal tract.
Blood-streaked mucus with associated weight loss in a long-term smoker raises
concern for a serious underlying cause such as sinonasal cancer. Other symptoms
of sinonasal cancer include a persistent blocked nose (often one-sided), recurrent
nosebleeds, reduced sense of smell, facial pain or numbness, and visual symptoms
like partial loss of vision or double vision.
It is also important to consider the impact of anticoagulation. Apixaban increases the
risk of bleeding and may partly explain his symptoms. However, as the bleeding is
currently mild and not uncontrolled, the benefits of continuing apixaban outweigh the
risks. Stopping it suddenly would significantly increase his risk of stroke due to atrial
fibrillation. If he were to develop an active or severe bleed, a temporary pause in
apixaban might be appropriate, but this would need to be carefully weighed against
the stroke risk, with clear safety-netting advice provided.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/head-neck-cancers-recognition-referral/
https://www.nhsinform.scot/illnesses-and-conditions/cancer/cancer-types-in-
adults/nasal-and-sinus-cancer/

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Alex, thank you for explaining what has been happening. From what you have told
me, your symptoms are most likely due to a sexually transmitted infection. In the UK,
the most common one is chlamydia, but it could also be something else such as
gonorrhoea.
The positive news is that this is something that can be treated effectively. At the
same time, it is important to confirm the diagnosis and to check for other infections.
Does that make sense so far?
Ideally, I would like you to come in today for a urine test and full STI screening.
However, as you are travelling to Dubai and cannot attend right now, the safest step
is to start you on an antibiotic called doxycycline. This should treat the most likely
infection and help with your symptoms. Are you happy with that plan?

-- 578 of 1282 --

577
Can I just confirm that you have no allergies, as noted in your records?
This medication can sometimes cause side effects such as tummy upset, diarrhoea,
or increased sensitivity to sunlight. However, most people tolerate it well. If you do
develop any troublesome side effects, please let us know straight away. Does that
sound okay to you?
Even though we are starting treatment, it is still important that you are tested for
other infections. You could arrange this while in Dubai if it is convenient, or we can
arrange it immediately when you return. Screening is important not only for your own
health but also to protect any current or future partner. Does that make sense so far?
Until you and any partner have completed the course of treatment, it is important to
avoid sexual intercourse, including oral sex, as the infection can still be passed on.
For the future, we normally recommend the use of condoms as they provide reliable
protection against many sexually transmitted infections. Is this something you would
consider?
Management
• Offer a face-to-face consultation for assessment, including urinalysis and a
urine sample for nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) for chlamydia.
• If unable to attend, treat empirically with doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7
days
• Inform the patient about common side effects of doxycycline, including
diarrhoea, vomiting, and photosensitivity, which is particularly relevant as he
is travelling to Dubai where it will be sunny.
• As the patient is travelling abroad, advise that having health insurance is
essential to enable access to medical care or testing overseas if required, and
to seek help promptly if he becomes unwell while away.
• Emphasise the importance of formal STI testing. Explain that this can be
arranged while he is in Dubai or as soon as he returns to the United Kingdom.
• On his return, advise attendance at a genitourinary medicine (GUM) clinic,
where consultations are confidential, full STI screening will be provided, and
partner notification can be arranged.
• Advise that sexual intercourse, including oral sex, should be avoided until
both the patient and partner(s) have completed treatment.
• Provide non-judgemental education on the consistent use of condoms to
reduce the risk of future infections.
• Safety net by advising to seek urgent medical attention if symptoms worsen,
such as fever, urethral discharge, or feeling generally unwell.
• Arrange follow-up in 2 weeks to review resolution of symptoms and confirm
attendance at the GUM clinic.

-- 579 of 1282 --

578
Learning Point from this Station
In young men, particularly those who are sexually active, a sexually transmitted
infection is more likely than a urinary tract infection. Urinary tract infections are less
common in men than in women, as the male urethra is longer and therefore provides
more protection against ascending infection. However, in men over the age of 65
years, urinary tract infections become more common than sexually transmitted
infections.
Genital chlamydia infection is the most common sexually transmitted infection in the
United Kingdom. In situations where immediate testing is not possible, it is
reasonable to treat empirically for the most likely sexually transmitted infection, while
advising that testing is arranged as soon as possible.
This case also highlights the role of health promotion in clinical practice. Using a
sensitive and non-judgemental approach, clinicians should promote condom use to
reduce the risk of reinfection, encourage attendance at a genitourinary medicine
(GUM) clinic for comprehensive screening, and support partner notification. These
measures not only safeguard the individual patient but also contribute to wider public
health protection.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/chlamydia-uncomplicated-genital/

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about hypothyroid symptoms: tiredness, cold intolerance, skin changes,
hair loss, weight gain, neck swelling, neck pain, mood changes, menstrual
irregularities, constipation
• Ask about compliance with levothyroxine and how she is taking her
medication
• Ask about the use of other medications or supplements, including
multivitamins
• Ask about simultaneous intake of levothyroxine with other medications (e.g.
multivitamins), food or drink such as milk, coffee, grapefruit juice, soya
products, or papaya, which may impair absorption of levothyroxine.
• Ask about gastrointestinal symptoms such as bloating, diarrhoea, abdominal
pain, or vomiting, which may indicate malabsorption conditions such as
coeliac disease, Helicobacter pylori gastritis, atrophic gastritis, or
inflammatory bowel disease.
• Ask about weight gain, which may increase levothyroxine requirements
• Ask how her symptoms have affected her day-to-day activities, including her
ability to carry out her job
• Ask about her mood and any impact on psychological wellbeing
• Ask about her sleep and any difficulties, as poor sleep can contribute to
tiredness.
• Ask about social history including smoking, alcohol intake, occupation, and
home situation
• Explore her ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Give a diagnosis of uncontrolled hypothyroidism, likely due to poor absorption
of levothyroxine from simultaneous intake of multivitamins.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Sarah, thank you for explaining everything and for booking this appointment to go
through your results today. The blood tests show that your thyroid is still underactive,
which means your body is not getting enough thyroid hormone. This explains why
you are still feeling tired and experiencing some of your symptoms. Does that make
sense so far?
You mentioned that you take multivitamins with your levothyroxine in the morning.
That is likely to be the reason why your levels are not improving. Multivitamins,
especially those that contain calcium or iron, can interfere with how well
levothyroxine is absorbed into your body. Are you following me so far?

-- 583 of 1282 --

582
I would recommend that you take your levothyroxine on an empty stomach with
water, ideally first thing in the morning. You should then wait at least 4 hours before
taking your multivitamins, supplements, or any other medication. How does that
sound to you?
At this stage, I do not think we need to increase your dose, because once you start
taking it correctly, it is likely that your levels will improve. We will repeat your thyroid
blood tests in about 6 to 10 weeks to see how things are going. If your results are
still abnormal, then we may look at adjusting your dose further. Is that clear?
I understand that your symptoms have been making it difficult for you to manage at
work. Sometimes a fit note that recommends lighter or amended duties can help
employers understand the situation better and give you some flexibility while we
work on getting your thyroid levels under control. Would you find that helpful?
Management
• Advise the patient to take levothyroxine on an empty stomach, either at least
60 minutes before any meal or at least 4 hours before taking any other
medication or supplement. This will improve absorption and may remove the
need to increase the dose further at this stage.
• Offer a blood test for lipid profile to support Q-Risk calculation, as part of
health promotion in hypothyroid patients.
• Offer a fit note with amended or lighter duties at work, which may help the
employer understand her situation while symptoms remain troublesome.
• Arrange follow-up in 6–10 weeks to review symptoms and repeat thyroid
function tests. At that stage, the levothyroxine dose may be increased if
symptoms have not improved, thyroid results remain abnormal, and she is
taking the medication correctly. Additional blood tests for malabsorption, such
as screening for coeliac disease, can also be considered at that point if there
is still no improvement.
• Provide a patient information leaflet on hypothyroidism to reinforce education
and self-management.
• Safety net by advising to seek urgent medical attention if symptoms
significantly worsen, particularly if there is confusion, drowsiness, or seizures,
which could indicate severe hypothyroidism (myxoedema coma).

-- 584 of 1282 --

583
Learning Point from this Station
When managing hypothyroidism, thyroid function tests may remain abnormal and
patients may continue to experience symptoms despite treatment. If this occurs, it is
important to assess for possible causes and address them appropriately.
These include:
• Non-compliance with prescribed levothyroxine treatment.
• Drug interactions that reduce levothyroxine absorption or increase its
requirements, such as multivitamins and other over-the-counter medication.
Patients should be advised to leave at least a four-hour interval between
levothyroxine and the interacting medication.
• Gastrointestinal conditions that impair absorption, including coeliac disease,
Helicobacter pylori gastritis, atrophic gastritis or pernicious anaemia,
giardiasis, and inflammatory bowel disease.
• Simultaneous intake of levothyroxine with food or drinks such as milk, coffee,
grapefruit juice, soya products, or papaya, which can impair absorption.
If you find a reason why the thyroid treatment is not working properly (for example,
the patient is not taking levothyroxine correctly, or something is interfering with its
absorption), then the first step is to address that problem. Once the underlying issue
has been managed, the levothyroxine dose can then be adjusted if it is still needed.
Simply raising the levothyroxine dose without addressing the cause will not correct
the issue and may even risk overtreatment once the problem is fixed.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/hypothyroidism/

586
Say NO to any other questions/symptoms asked outside of the details already
provided in the scenario. Accept anything offered to you by the doctor.
Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the episode of the fall: when it happened, mechanism of the fall,
whether it was witnessed, what led to it, and whether she was able to get
herself up.
• Ask about pre-fall symptoms such as dizziness, palpitations, chest pain, or
weakness in any part of the body to suggest a stroke
• Ask if any injury or fracture was sustained. Use the ambulance report in
questioning, for example: “I can see from the ambulance records that there
were no injuries, is that correct?” to demonstrate use of available information.
• Ask if she lost consciousness during or after the fall.
• Ask if she ever feels dizzy when standing up from a sitting position, to assess
for postural hypotension.
• Ask if anything is affecting her mobility, such as arthritis or joint pain, or
symptoms suggestive of Parkinson’s disease (tremor, slow gait).
• Ask if she has recently been more forgetful, or if anyone has noticed changes
in her behaviour or concerns about her memory, to help rule out cognitive
decline or dementia.
• Ask about her eyesight and any visual problems that could contribute to falls.
• Ask about urinary symptoms, including frequency, urgency or incontinence.
• Ask about her bowel habits and whether there are any issues.
• Ask about her diabetic control, whether she has any upcoming follow-ups, and
when she last had a blood test.
• Ask about hazards in the home that may have contributed to the fall, such as
loose rugs, mats, poor lighting, wet bathroom floors, or unstable fittings like
handrails.
• Ask about her home situation, including whether she lives alone, how she is
coping, and whether she has carers or family members who help with
shopping, cooking, or daily activities.
• Ask about her mood and whether the fall has affected her confidence or
caused any feelings of low mood or anxiety.
• Ask about diet and nutrition, as poor intake can lead to conditions such as
hypoglycaemia or hyponatraemia, which can increase fall risk
• Review her current medications, such as furosemide and aspirin, which do not
have clear indications from her notes, and ask if she knows why she is taking
them, to assess appropriateness.

-- 588 of 1282 --

587
• Ask about social history, including alcohol use and smoking.
• Explore her ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Give a diagnosis of likely mechanical fall, possibly caused by polypharmacy
and side effects of medications such as furosemide and antihypertensives.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Mary, thank you for sharing what happened with your fall. I can see from the
ambulance report that your observations and checks were reassuring, and thankfully
there was no injury. From what you have described, this sounds like a mechanical
fall, meaning you most likely tripped or lost balance rather than it being caused by
something more serious like a heart problem or a stroke. Does that make sense so
far?
You mentioned that you think you may have tripped because your legs felt wobbly.
One possible reason for this is the tablets you are taking, particularly the water tablet
called furosemide. This medicine can cause you to pass urine more often and
sometimes make you a little dehydrated, which can make your legs feel weak. It is
no longer needed now that your leg swelling has improved, so I would recommend
stopping it. How do you feel about that?
I also notice that you are taking aspirin. This is usually only used for people who
have had a heart attack, a stroke, or problems with their blood vessels. From your
records, you do not seem to have had these conditions, so there is no clear reason
for you to be on aspirin. Continuing it when it is not needed can increase the risk of
side effects like bleeding. I would therefore advise we stop this too. Is that okay with
you?
In addition, the ambulance team recorded your blood pressure and it was within a
good range. You are currently on more than one type of blood pressure tablet, and
sometimes this combination can make people feel dizzy or unsteady. I would
suggest stopping one of them, ramipril, and then reviewing your blood pressure in a
few weeks to make sure it is still in the normal range. How do you feel about that?
You told me you are worried about having another fall. That is a very valid concern,
and we can definitely work on prevention. I will arrange for physiotherapy to support
you with balance and strength exercises, which will also help rebuild your
confidence. I will also ask occupational therapy to look at your home and see if any
changes, such as handrails or removing loose rugs, might make things safer. In
addition, there are falls alarms available, these are watches or pendants that can
detect if you fall and alert someone straight away. Would you find that reassuring?
You also said that what you really want is advice on how to prevent another fall. So,
alongside the physiotherapy and occupational therapy, I would also recommend
keeping well hydrated, wearing supportive footwear indoors, and letting us know if

-- 589 of 1282 --

588
you notice any dizziness, chest pain, weakness, or confusion. If anything like that
happens, or if you fall again, please seek urgent medical help.
Management
• Offer discontinuation of furosemide, as it can lead to dehydration, contribute
to feeling wobbly, and is no longer required since her leg swelling has
resolved
• Offer review and reduction of antihypertensives, for example discontinuing
ramipril, to reduce the risk of postural hypotension
• Stop aspirin, as she has no clear indication for it and it is not recommended
for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease. Aspirin should only be
continued if there is a clear secondary prevention indication, such as a
previous TIA, stroke, myocardial infarction, or peripheral arterial disease.
• No immediate need for a face-to-face assessment, as she has recently been
reviewed by the ambulance crew with vital signs, ECG, and cardiovascular
examination. Therefore, do not offer a face-to-face appointment or home visit
at this stage.
• You can offer bloods to check for electrolyte abnormality due to furosemide
• Offer referral to occupational therapy to assess her home environment and
identify adjustments that may reduce her risk of falls
• Offer referral to community physiotherapy for strength and balance training,
which can improve confidence and help prevent further falls.
• Offer a falls alarm, which can be arranged through social prescribers,
occupational therapy, community physiotherapy, or frailty practitioners. These
devices use motion sensors to detect a fall and automatically alert a
monitoring team, who can arrange emergency help if required.
• Consider anti-fall stockings, which may provide better traction on smooth
floors and reduce fall risk.
• Encourage good fluid intake to prevent dehydration, which can contribute to
falls.
• Arrange follow-up in 1–2 weeks to recheck blood pressure and reassess
overall progress.
• Safety net by advising that if she develops dizziness, chest pain, weakness in
any part of the body, another fall, or any other concerning symptoms, she
should seek urgent medical advice immediately.

-- 590 of 1282 --

589
Learning Point from this Station
Falls are common in older adults, especially those aged 65 years and over, and the
risk increases with age. A history of falls is one of the strongest predictors of future
falls. The risk is usually multifactorial, so prevention involves assessing and
addressing multiple contributing factors.
Key risk factors for falls include:
• Conditions that affect mobility or balance, such as arthritis, diabetes,
incontinence, stroke, syncope, or Parkinson’s disease
• Muscle weakness, poor balance, visual impairment, cognitive impairment,
depression, or alcohol misuse
• Polypharmacy, particularly the use of fall-risk increasing drugs
• Home hazards such as loose rugs, poor lighting, wet bathroom floors, or
unstable fittings like handrails
In this station, recognising polypharmacy as a contributing factor is crucial.
Deprescribing medications that are no longer needed or inappropriate is an essential
part of safe primary care. For example, aspirin should only be used for secondary
prevention of cardiovascular disease (such as after myocardial infarction,
percutaneous coronary intervention, stroke, or TIA) and should not be prescribed
routinely for primary prevention.
Falls prevention requires a multidisciplinary approach:
• Occupational therapy: home hazard assessment and interventions, adapting
the environment, modifying activities, and providing adaptive equipment
• Physiotherapy: strength, balance, and mobility training to improve physical
function and restore confidence
• Medication review: assessing appropriateness and reducing polypharmacy
where possible
• Vision checks: addressing visual impairment when present
This case highlights the importance of comprehensive falls assessment, targeted
interventions, and the GP’s role in rationalising medication to improve safety and
maintain independence in older adults.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/falls-assessment/

-- 596 of 1282 --

595
Mr. Brown, from everything you've told me about your symptoms—feeling breathless
when you exert yourself, but improving with rest—I am concerned that this could be
a condition called angina. I’d like to explain what that means and what we can do
about it. Angina happens when the heart muscle doesn’t get enough oxygen-rich
blood, usually because of narrowing in the blood vessels that supply the heart.
This can cause symptoms like chest tightness, discomfort, or even breathlessness,
especially during activities like walking or climbing stairs.
Management
• Offer face to face to see and assess- listen to heart sounds, listen to chest,
check BP, etc
• Offer ECG, bloods for BNP, FBC, Cholesterol, Chest Xray
• Offer GTN spray and Aspirin 75mg OD- heavily safety net- To use GTN spray
during an episode of SOB, take a second dose after 5 minutes if the pain has
not eased, Call 999 for an ambulance if the pain has not eased 5 minutes
after the second dose, or earlier if the pain is intensifying or the person is
unwell
• Offer referral to Rapid access chest pain clinic
• Safety net re unstable angina – if worsening SOB at rest, chest pain,
palpitations, dial 999
Lesson from this case: Shortness of breath (SOB) can be the only symptom of
angina, often misattributed to COPD. Key clues for angina include exertional
onset, intermittent, relief with rest/GTN, and absence of cough/wheeze. COPD-
related SOB is persistent, relieved by inhalers, and often accompanied by
cough/sputum. Always assess for underlying cardiac causes!

Patient’s story

experiencing intense, one-sided headaches around your left eye and temple. The
pain feels sharp, stabbing, and unbearable, almost like someone is pressing a knife
into your head.
Each episode lasts around 30–90 minutes and occurs multiple times per day (Up to
6 episodes). You’ve noticed your left eye becomes red and watery during attacks,
and your nose gets blocked or runs on the same side. You also get facial sweating
You have suffered this type of headache 2 months ago and it settled on its own
You’ve tried paracetamol and ibuprofen, but they haven’t helped at all. You feel
restless during attacks and cannot sit still—pacing or moving seems to distract you.
You’re frustrated because the pain is severe, and you’ve never had anything like this
before. You’re worried it might be something serious, like a brain tumour or bleed in
the brain. SAY NO TO ANY OTHER SYMPTOMS ASKED

Non-smoker, occasional alcohol.
No history of recent head trauma.
You live with your girlfriend
Ideas and concerns; You are worried about brain tumour or bleed in the brain
Expectations: You would like the GP to help with a stronger painkiller and to get a
scan of your head

-- 598 of 1282 --

597
History
• Ask about onset of headache
• Ask about headache characteristics like location of headache, duration – how
long does each attack last? (Short, 30-90 minutes), nature of headache-
stabbing, dull ache, frequency – how many times per day , Triggers – Any
specific triggers (alcohol, stress, sleep changes)
• Ask about associated symptoms- photophobia – Does the patient find that
bright light makes headache worse? Or are they more sensitive to light than
usual?
• Other associated symptoms- nausea, vomiting , teary eyes, blocked nose
/running nose, facial sweating,
• Ask for Red flags- neck stiffness (Think meningitis/ sub arachnoid
haemorrhage), fever (Thinks meningitis-either viral/bacteria-also encephalitis),
rash, weakness/numbness, visual disturbances (think stroke, tumour), recent
trauma (think subdural hematoma), confusion
• Ask about social history and how this has affected patient’s life
• Elicit ICE
• Give diagnosis of potential cluster headache
Explain to patient
"Mr. Cooper, I completely understand why you're worried about something serious
like a brain bleed or a tumour. It’s natural to think that when you have a severe
headache that won’t go away.
From everything you’ve described, the one-sided, severe stabbing pain near your
eye, the teary eye, the runny nose, and the fact that painkillers haven’t worked, this
sounds very much like a condition called Cluster Headache.
Cluster headaches are a type of severe headache that comes in cycles or “clusters”
over a few weeks. Unlike migraines, which often cause nausea and light sensitivity,
cluster headaches cause intense pain around one eye, along with symptoms like a
teary eye and a runny nose on the same side. Many people describe them as the
worst headache they’ve ever had."
The good news is, while they are extremely painful, they are not dangerous, and we
have very effective treatments.
Your headache is very unlikely to be a brain bleed because it wasn’t a sudden,
explosive pain, and you don’t have serious symptoms like weakness, speech issues,
or confusion. A brain tumour is also unlikely because your headaches are episodic

-- 599 of 1282 --

598
rather than progressively worsening over weeks or months, and you don’t have red-
flag symptoms like persistent vomiting, vision changes, or memory problems.
However, because this is your first time experiencing these symptoms, guidelines
recommend that we refer you to a neurology specialist to confirm the diagnosis."
The specialist will likely arrange a brain scan, not because we suspect anything
dangerous, but to rule out other possible causes of headache and ensure we’re
treating the right condition.
Additionally, they can discuss preventive medications to help reduce how often these
headaches happen and make them less severe.
Management
As he is presenting for the first time with cluster headache- refer to specialist for
confirmation of diagnosis, neuroimaging and likely initiation of prophylaxis. You can
do an urgent advice and guidance or urgent referral.
• Offer Face to face to examine nerves of the body (Neurological exam),
fundoscopy and blood pressure.
• For acute attack- Offer Sumatriptan intranasal spray - inform patient- Start
with a dose of 10–20 mg in one nostril. If the headache comes back after at
least 2 hours, you can take another 10–20 mg. Don't take a second dose for
the same headache if the first dose didn’t help. The maximum amount you
can take in one day is 40 mg.
• Sumatriptan subcutaneous injection can also be considered
• Short-burst oxygen therapy can also be considered for acute attacks
• If severe, then we can seek specialist advice to start on prophylaxis with
verapamil
• Advice to keep symptoms diary to elicit triggers so as to avoid them
• Heavily safety net on neurological symptoms /worsening symptoms
Note: NICE says we should NOT offer paracetamol, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory
drugs (NSAIDs), opioids, ergots, or oral triptans for the acute treatment of cluster
headache. The basis for recommendation is, Cluster headaches are extremely
intense and short-lasting, so treatments need to work quickly and effectively to
provide relief. Oral triptans have slow onset of action. Paracetamol and NSAIDs
work too slowly, and they are not powerful enough to relieve the severe pain of
cluster headaches.

Explain to her about down syndrome
Sarah, Down syndrome is a condition where a baby is born with an extra copy of
genetic instructions that affect their growth and learning. It happens naturally and
isn’t caused by anything you did or didn’t do. The chances of having a baby with
Down syndrome increase as you get older, and at 42, your risk is higher than when

-- 602 of 1282 --

601
you were younger. Since you have already had a baby with Down syndrome, your
risk is also slightly higher compared to someone your age who hasn’t had a child
with the condition before. That’s why we offer tests to check if your baby might have
it.
There are two main types of tests. Screening and diagnostic test. The screening
test tells us if your baby is at a higher chance of having Down syndrome. The NHS
offers this at 11 to 14 weeks, where we do a scan and a blood test to estimate the
risk.
You also have the option of a second screening test called Non-Invasive
Prenatal Testing (NIPT). This is a blood test that provides a more accurate
screening result, helping you decide whether you want to proceed with a diagnostic
test or not.
If the screening test shows you have a lower-chance result; you will not be offered a
further test.
If a screening test shows a high chance, we then offer diagnostic tests, which
confirm whether the baby has Down syndrome or not. These tests involve taking a
small sample from the placenta or the fluid around the baby and are done from 11
weeks onwards, but they carry a small risk of miscarriage.
If the test confirms Down syndrome, we will fully support you in making the best
decision for you and your baby. Some parents choose to prepare for a child with
special needs, while others consider different options. Whatever you decide, we will
be here to guide you. Does this all make sense, or would you like me to go over any
part again?
Management
• Explain down syndrome
• Explain test available for her and the week which screening begins. Two tests
are available for down syndrome testing in pregnancy. Screening and
diagnostic test
• Screening test are of three types--two of them are done in early
pregnancy - Combined Screening (11–14 weeks) OR NIPT from 10 weeks.
• If patient booked later in pregnancy- quadruple test can be offered at 14-20
weeks - While it is useful, it is less accurate than the earlier screening tests.
• Inform patient that if diagnostic test confirms downs syndrome- Options
include- Continue the pregnancy and prepare for the baby- The parents
can use the information to plan for the baby’s care and support. Continue the
pregnancy and consider adoption- If the parents are unable to raise the

-- 603 of 1282 --

602
child, adoption is an option, and there are families willing to care for children
with special needs. End the pregnancy (termination)- Some parents choose
not to continue with the pregnancy.
• Ensure she is taking folic acid (400mcg or 5mg if high risk) and vitamin D.
• Advise her to register for antenatal care if not done
• Offer pregnancy vaccination- Flu vaccine and COVID19 vaccine at anytime in
pregnancy, whooping cough vaccine from 16 weeks pregnancy and RSV
vaccine from 28 weeks pregnancy
• Offer leaflets to read more about down syndrome
• Advise patient to book another appointment after the screening results are
out, if she wishes to discuss further

Marking scheme
History
• Ask about hypothyroid symptoms: Fatigue, cold intolerance, skin/hair
changes, weight gain, mood changes, constipation.
• Ask if symptoms have worsened since she last saw the nurse practitioner

below? Any nausea or vomiting

• Ask about home situation and support at home

• Ask ICE
Explain blood test results, informing of subclinical hypothyroidism
Emma, your blood test shows that your thyroid function is borderline underactive, a
condition we call subclinical hypothyroidism. This means your thyroid is working a
little slower than expected, but not enough to be considered full hypothyroidism.
The thyroid is a gland that sits in front of the neck which is responsible for converting
food into energy (Metabolism), basically regulating energy levels. When the thyroid is
underactive, even slightly, it can slow things down, leading to symptoms like
tiredness, feeling cold, weight changes, and constipation.
During pregnancy, your body relies on your thyroid to produce enough hormones to
support both you and your baby’s development. Even when thyroid levels are only
slightly off, it can contribute to symptoms like fatigue and, if left untreated, could
affect the pregnancy. That’s why we monitor and manage it early

-- 607 of 1282 --

606
Management
• Offer further blood test to check for thyroid peroxidase antibodies (TPOAbs) -
Please note that pregnant women with positive TPOAbs will usually benefit
from treatment with levothyroxine
Offer levothyroxine 50mcg OD – but offer to discuss with a specialist
(Endocrinologist) FIRST
NOTE: The general guide is; pregnant women or women seeking to be pregnant
who have subclinical hypothyroidism should be treated only if they have positive
TPOAbs.
However, NICE has noted on their basis for recommendation under Hypothyroidism
Preconception or pregnant advice that treatment of subclinical hypothyroidism in
pregnancy with Levothyroxine (LT4) may help lower the risk of pregnancy
complications, especially in women who test positive for thyroid peroxidase
antibodies (TPOAbs). They have advised to seek specialist advice before initiation
In conclusion, both those who have positive TPOAbs and negative TPOAbs
should be offered levothyroxine because this will improve pregnancy
outcomes and prevent complications, however, please seek specialist advice.
Levothyroxine is completely safe in pregnancy
• Advice patient that levothyroxine should be taken on an empty stomach at
least 30-60 minutes before food and at least 4 hours apart from iron or
calcium supplements (which can reduce its absorption)
• Offer follow up to repeat thyroid blood tests in 4 weeks to check if TSH is
within the pregnancy target range (<2.5 mU/L in the first trimester).
• Ensure she is taking folic acid (400mcg or 5mg if high risk) and vitamin D.
• Offer pregnancy vaccination- Flu vaccine and COVID19 vaccine at anytime in
pregnancy, whooping cough vaccine from 16 weeks pregnancy and RSV
vaccine from 28 weeks pregnancy
• Safety Netting & advice- If she develops worsening fatigue, weight gain, cold
intolerance, or other symptoms, seek urgent medical advice ASAP.

-- 612 of 1282 --

611
Your diet is not great and you rarely exercise – you know it is somewhat ironic that a

Marking scheme
History- From head to toe
• Ask about headache
• Ask about blurry vision
• Ask about weakness in one part of the body (Stroke)
• Ask about chest pain, palpitations, SOB (Hypertensive heart disease)
• Ask about waterworks and if any blood in urine
• Ask about ankle swelling
• Ask about family history of hypertension, heart attack, stroke.
• Ask about Smoking, alcohol, diet, occupation, caffeine, stress
• Make a diagnosis of possible hypertension
Explain to the patient
Mr Okonkwo, I understand your concerns, and you’ve done the right thing by
checking your blood pressure. Based on the readings you’ve taken, it’s possible that
you have high blood pressure, but we cannot diagnose hypertension from three
readings alone.
Blood pressure fluctuates throughout the day due to factors like stress, exercise,
caffeine, or even the way it’s measured. A single set of high readings doesn’t always
mean hypertension. To diagnose high blood pressure accurately, we need more
data."
There are two ways we can confirm whether you truly have hypertension:
Ambulatory Blood Pressure Monitoring (ABPM) – A small device that measures your
BP over 24 hours while you do your daily activities.
Home Blood Pressure Monitoring (HBPM) – Checking your BP twice daily for 7 days
and recording the readings.

-- 613 of 1282 --

612
Management
• Offer Face to Face to check blood pressure, blood investigations include:
FBC, U+E, HBA1c, Lipid profile,
• Other investigations include Urine ACR (first void urine –early morning
sample) ECG and Fundoscopy
• Offer ABPM or HBPM to confirm high blood pressure
• Inform patient that if this confirms hypertension, then he will be offered
medication to help control his blood pressure
• Discuss lifestyle, exercise, diet, low salt, discourage caffeine, etc.
• Arrange follow up in 1 – 2 weeks to discuss results of blood test and ABPM or
HBPM
• Safety net on red flag symptoms (severe headaches, chest pain,
breathlessness, swelling, vision problems) – seek urgent medical help if they
occur
Learning point from this station
Do not rush to diagnose hypertension – confirm with ABPM or HBPM.
Reassure the patient while acknowledging his concerns
Provide strong lifestyle advice – diet, salt reduction, exercise, stress management.
Ensure full cardiovascular risk assessment – blood tests, ECG, kidney function

Patient’s story (Role Player’s Brief)
You are Tatenda Ndlovu, a 25-year-old female. You have noticed a lump in your
tummy that has been gradually increasing in size over the past few months.
The lump first appeared two years ago, approximately three months after you had a
deep cut to your tummy following a road traffic accident in Harare, Zimbabwe. The
wound was sutured, and you recovered well, but the lump gradually formed
afterward.
The lump feels rubbery and soft. It is not painful
You are worried because the lump has become more noticeable, and in recent
months, it has increased in size.
SAY NO TO ANY OTHER SYMPTOMS ASKED
ICE (Ideas, Concerns, Expectations)
Idea: You do not know what the lump is but feel it is related to the past injury.
Concern: You are a model and worry about the cosmetic appearance. Wearing
bikinis is important to your career, and this lump makes you feel less confident about
your body.
Expectation: You want a solution, ideally a surgical removal or any other treatment
that can make your tummy look normal again.
Social History: Occupation: Model, No Smoking/Alcohol. You live with your parents
History
• Ask about onset of lump
• Ask for any history of trauma prior to onset of lump (Classical of
Keloid/hypertrophic scars)
• Ask if lump has increased in size
• Ask if lump feels soft, firm or hard
• Ask for any associated tenderness or pain
• Ask for any discharge, redness or skin changes over the lump
• Ask for any weight loss, loss of appetite
• Ask for lumps and bumps anywhere else in the body
• Ask for any family history of keloid scars or similar lumps

-- 616 of 1282 --

615
• Ask how this has affected patient’s life
• Ask social history: Occupation
• Ask about ICE
• Give diagnosis of Keloid
Explain to patient
Tatenda, I understand your concerns, and you’ve done the right thing by seeking
medical advice.
Based on what you've described, the lump could be due to scar tissue called Keloid
A keloid is a type of raised scar that grows larger than the original wound. It happens
when the body produces too much collagen during healing.
In your case, the lump may have formed after your injury from the accident. Keloids
are not dangerous, but they can keep growing and affect appearance.
Management
• Offer routine referral to Dermatology for treatment such as intralesional
steroid injections given every 2 -6weeks until improvement
• Inform patient about side effects of the steroid injection- it can cause
temporary skin changes in the treated area. These may include: Lightening or
darkening of the skin (pigment changes), which is usually more noticeable in
darker skin tones. Tiny visible blood vessels (telangiectasias), making the skin
look slightly reddish or thin. Thinning of the skin or slight denting
(subcutaneous atrophy), but this often improves over time.
• Other treatments include steroid impregnated tapes for example Haelen tape
(fludroxycortide tape) and Betesil medicated plaster (betamethasone valerate
2.25mg). This is applied for 12 hours per day which can flatten keloids.
• If the patient asks about surgical excision- inform the patient that - If we
remove the keloid with surgery alone, there is a high chance it may grow
back, and sometimes the new scar can be even bigger than the original one
• Another treatment is Cryotherapy; This is a treatment that uses extreme cold
(usually liquid nitrogen) to freeze and shrink the keloid.
• Advice patient as she is at risk of Keloid scars - Avoid body piercings, tattoos,
or unnecessary cosmetic surgery, especially in areas like the chest,
shoulders, and earlobes, where keloids are more likely to develop.

-- 617 of 1282 --

616
• If she ever need surgery, she should inform the surgeons about her history of
keloid, so they can take extra precautions, like using special dressings or
steroid injections, to reduce the risk of keloid formation.
• Safety net - If the lump changes rapidly, becomes painful, red, or warm, or
causes other new symptoms, seek urgent medical attention.
• Offer leaflets to read more about keloids
Learning point from this station
Keloid scars grow beyond the original wound boundary, continue enlarging over
time, and do not regress on their own. They are more common in darker skin tones
and can appear months after the injury.
Hypertrophic scars remain within the wound boundary, are smaller, appear soon
after the injury, may improve over time, and are often linked to high tension on the
wound during healing
