-- 620 of 1282 --

619
Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about symptoms of chronic kidney disease, including nausea, vomiting,
shortness of breath, itching, fatigue, muscle cramps, blood in the urine,
reduced urine output, and other urinary symptoms.
• Ask about symptoms suggestive of diabetes and its complications, including
blurred vision, increased appetite, increased thirst, shortness of breath, chest
pain, urinary frequency, tingling or numbness particularly in the legs, and any
non healing wounds or ulcers, especially on the feet.
• Ask about medication use, including over the counter medications such as
NSAIDs and supplements such as creatine.
• Ask about family history of kidney disease, for example, polycystic kidney
disease.
• Ask about social history including smoking, alcohol intake, diet, occupation,
and stress levels.
• Ask about adherence to medications, including antidiabetic, antihypertensive,
and cholesterol lowering treatments.
• Make a diagnosis of chronic kidney disease, likely secondary to a combination
of diabetes and hypertension.
Management
• Stop metformin and offer alternative antidiabetic medications, as metformin
should not be used if eGFR is below 30.
• Offer to start an SGLT2 inhibitor such as dapagliflozin 10 mg once daily and a
DPP-4 inhibitor such as linagliptin 5 mg once daily.
• Explain that both medications will not be started at the same time.
Dapagliflozin will be initiated first, and once tolerance is confirmed, linagliptin
will be introduced after one to two weeks.
• Inform him of side effects. Dapagliflozin can increase the risk of urinary tract
infections and, in rare cases, a serious infection called Fournier’s gangrene
affecting the genital and perineal area. Linagliptin may cause side effects
such as cough, although most people tolerate it well.
• Explain sick day rules for dapagliflozin. If he develops vomiting or diarrhoea,
he should temporarily stop the medication and restart it 24 to 48 hours after
he is able to eat and drink normally again.
• Offer to calculate his 5-year risk of requiring renal replacement therapy using
the 4 variable Kidney Failure Risk Equation. If the risk is greater than 5%, he

-- 621 of 1282 --

620
will be referred to a specialist. If the risk is below 5%, we will continue
monitoring him in primary care.
• Offer vaccinations including pneumococcal, influenza, and COVID vaccines.
• Advise lifestyle changes including avoiding NSAIDs, maintaining regular
exercise, and following a healthy balanced diet.
• Provide safety net advice for diabetic ketoacidosis. Advise him to seek urgent
medical attention if he develops symptoms such as feeling very unwell,
excessive thirst, passing large amounts of urine, nausea or vomiting,
abdominal pain, fast or deep breathing, confusion, or a fruity smell to the
breath. He should contact the GP surgery or call NHS 111 if out of hours.
• Also advise him to seek urgent medical attention if he develops symptoms of
worsening kidney function such as severe swelling of the legs or face, very
little or no urine output, severe tiredness, nausea, confusion, shortness of
breath, or chest pain.
• Arrange follow up in 3 months to reassess symptoms, blood results, and
response to treatment.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you George for taking the time to speak with me today. I understand that you
are worried about your kidneys, especially given what happened to your friend, and
that fear of needing dialysis is understandably concerning. I will go through
everything with you clearly.
From your blood results, your kidney function is reduced, and this is what we call
chronic kidney disease. This means that your kidneys are not working as well as
they should over a long period of time. There is no cure for this condition, but the
most important thing is that we can slow it down and protect your kidneys by
managing your blood pressure, blood sugar, and lifestyle. Does that make sense so
far?
I want to reassure you that not everyone with reduced kidney function goes on to
need dialysis. To give you a clearer idea of your risk, I will use a medical calculator
to estimate your chance of needing dialysis or a kidney transplant over the next five
years. If your risk is low, we will continue to monitor you closely and optimise your
treatment to slow things down. If the risk is higher, we would involve a kidney
specialist earlier to support your care. Are you with me so far?
In terms of your treatment, one important change is that we will need to stop your
metformin, as it is not suitable at your current level of kidney function. Instead, we
will start alternative medications called dapagliflozin and linagliptin.
Dapagliflozin will be started first, as it helps to control your blood sugar and also
offers protection for your kidneys. After one to two weeks, once we are sure you are
tolerating it well, we can then introduce the second medication, linagliptin.

-- 622 of 1282 --

621
Like all medications, they can have side effects. Dapagliflozin can sometimes
increase the risk of urine infections and, very rarely, a serious infection around the
genital area. If you notice any redness, swelling, pain, or ulcers in that area, you
should seek urgent medical attention. Linagliptin can occasionally cause a cough.
However, I want to reassure you that these medications are generally safe, and most
people tolerate them well. Are you happy to try these medications?
It is also important to follow sick day rules. If you become unwell with vomiting or
diarrhoea, you should stop dapagliflozin temporarily and restart it once you are
eating and drinking normally again after 24 to 48 hours.
Alongside medication, lifestyle is very important. Continue your good habits with diet
and exercise, and avoid medications like ibuprofen, as these can worsen kidney
function.
We will also offer you vaccinations such as flu, pneumococcal, and COVID, as these
help reduce the risk of infections, which can affect your kidneys further.
I do not expect this to happen, but if you develop symptoms such as feeling very
unwell, severe tiredness, swelling in your legs or face, passing very little urine,
shortness of breath, chest pain, or confusion, you should seek urgent medical
attention. Also, if you develop symptoms of very high blood sugar such as excessive
thirst, passing a lot of urine, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, or fast breathing, you
should contact us or call NHS 111 if it is out of hours.
We will review you again in three months to repeat your blood tests and monitor your
progress.
Learning Point from This Station
This station highlights the importance of recognising and managing chronic kidney
disease in primary care, particularly in patients with comorbid conditions such as
diabetes and hypertension. A key principle is understanding how declining renal
function impacts medication choices. For example, metformin should be stopped
when eGFR falls below 30 due to the risk of accumulation and adverse effects.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/diabetes-type-2/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/chronic-kidney-disease/

Questions for the doctor
If the doctor mentions heart failure, act so surprised and ask him/her if you are going
to die and also ask the doctor the cause of the heart failure
Marking scheme
History
• Ask patient about symptoms that led her to have blood test in the first place
• Ask about SOB- Exertional, at rest, or lying flat
• Ask about waking up suddenly gasping for air (Paroxysmal nocturnal
dyspnoea)
• Ask about associated symptoms- cough, chest pain, palpitations, ankle
swelling, distended neck veins, fatigue
• Ask about family history of heart disease
• Ask about smoking, alcohol,
• Ask about home situation and how she is coping at home
• Ask if the furosemide given by the previous doctor made any difference and if
she feels better
• Ask if she is compliant with her medication
• Explain blood test results and Chest Xray findings
Make a diagnosis of heart failure
Mrs Johnson, your blood test showed a raised level of a heart marker, this is a
chemical that tells us how hard your heart is working. When this level is higher than
normal, it suggests that the heart is under some strain. Your chest X-ray also
showed that your heart is slightly larger than usual, which can happen when the
heart isn’t pumping as strongly as it should. Taken together, these results suggest
that your symptoms are due to a condition where your heart might not be pumping
efficiently called heart failure.

-- 626 of 1282 --

625
I know the term ‘heart failure’ can sound scary, but I want to reassure you, it does
not mean that your heart is stopping. It simply means that your heart needs some
extra support to pump blood around your body more effectively.
Mrs Johnson. In your case, the most likely cause of your heart working harder than
usual is high blood pressure over time. When blood pressure is high for many years,
the heart has to pump harder, and over time, this can make it weaker or stiffer,
leading to fluid buildup in the lungs and legs.
The good news is that now we know what’s happening, we can take steps to support
your heart, reduce the strain, and help you feel better.
Management
• As her symptoms are not better on 20mg of Furosemide, offer to increase this
to 40mg OD and recheck renal functions in1-2 weeks time
• Offer referral to Cardiology for Echocardiography and confirmation of
diagnosis – referral should be between 6 weeks. Remember as per NICE- If
the NT-pro-BNP level is above 2000 ng/L (236 pmol/L), refer urgently for
specialist assessment and echocardiography to be seen within 2 weeks. If the
NT-pro-BNP level is between 400–2000 ng/L (47–236 pmol/L), refer for
specialist assessment and echocardiography to be seen within 6 weeks.
• Advise her that the cardiologist may start her on some new medications,
depending on what they find on echocardiography (If reduced ejection fraction
or preserved)
• Advise her to avoid excessive salt intake. Adult daily intake should not exceed
5 g of salt per day.
• Offer vaccinations- pneumococcal, influenza, COVID
• Follow up after seen by cardiology clinic
• Advise to seek urgent help if: severe breathlessness (unable to speak in
sentences), chest pain, sudden worsening swelling in the legs or face,
confusion or dizziness.

Ideas; You think it might be a chest infection or an asthma flare-up.
Concerns; You are due to return to work in 4 days. If you do not improve, you need
a sick note to apply for leave early.
Expectations; You expect the GP to prescribe steroids to help with your symptoms.
Marking scheme
History
• Ask about onset, duration, and progression of SOB—is SOB intermittent or
constant? Is it worse with movement or activity? Is it worse when laying down
flat (Orthopnea)
• Ask about other respiratory symptoms: cough (If present, ask for
haemoptysis) , sputum production, wheezing, fever, recent infections/flu like
symptoms.
• Ask about pleuritic chest pain (worse on inspiration), palpitations.
• Ask about asthma control: Frequency of inhaler use? Night-time awakenings?
• Previous exacerbations or hospital admissions?
• Ask about smoking history (frequency and duration).
• Ask about job/occupation - (air hostess—long haul flights –high risk).
• Ask about use of over-the-counter medications or any other medications –
COCP for example
• Ask for red flags- dizziness, worsening SOB
• Ask ICE (Ideas, Concerns, and Expectations)
• Make a diagnosis of potential Pulmonary embolism
Example of explanation to the patient
Charlotte, I can hear that you’re really concerned about your symptoms, and I want
to explain what might be happening. You mentioned that you've had shortness of
breath that hasn’t improved with your inhaler, along with a racing heart and chest

-- 629 of 1282 --

628
pain that gets worse when you breathe in. These symptoms, combined with the fact
that you are an air hostess who flies long distances and that you smoke
occasionally, raise a concern about a condition called Pulmonary Embolism.
A Pulmonary Embolism, or PE, happens when a blood clot forms in the lungs,
blocking blood flow. This can make breathing difficult and painful and put extra strain
on the heart, which may explain why your heart is racing. It’s a serious condition that
needs urgent medical attention because, if left untreated, it can become life-
threatening.
I don’t want to alarm you, but I do want to stress that it is important we get you into
the hospital. I will organize and get you admitted via the Acute medical unit, where
they can do urgent tests, including a scan of your lungs, to check for a clot. If a clot is
found, they can start treatment immediately with blood-thinning medication to
dissolve it and prevent further complications
Management
• Offer same day referral to acute medical unit
• Inform her that initial investigations will include a blood test to assess the
likelihood of a pulmonary embolism (PE), along with a chest X-ray and an
ECG. If the blood test result suggests PE, she will be offered a further scan
called a CTPA to look more closely at the lungs.
• Inform her that if PE is confirmed, she should not fly for 4 weeks-offer her a fit
note if needed
• Advise her to stop taking the combined oral contraceptive pill for now. In the
meantime, she can use condoms as an alternative method. Once she has
been discharged from hospital, she should arrange a follow-up appointment to
discuss longer-term contraception options.
• Offer smoking cessation advice
• For future flights, advise her to take preventive measures to reduce the risk of
blood clots. These include wearing class 2 compression stockings, staying
well hydrated, and moving around the cabin regularly, especially on long-haul
journeys. Simple leg exercises while seated can also help maintain
circulation.

-- 630 of 1282 --

629
Learning Point from this station
In line with NICE guidance, patients with suspected pulmonary embolism (PE) who
are haemodynamically unstable, pregnant, or recently postpartum (within 6 weeks)
should be admitted urgently via the emergency department.
For all other cases, a structured clinical assessment using the Wells Score should be
performed:
• If the Wells Score is greater than 4, PE is likely. Arrange urgent admission for
CTPA (CT Pulmonary Angiogram). If immediate imaging is not available, start
interim anticoagulation (e.g., apixaban or rivaroxaban).
• If the Wells Score is 4 or less, PE is less likely. Offer a D-dimer test within 4
hours. If this is not possible, start interim anticoagulation while awaiting
results.
• If D-dimer is positive, refer for imaging. If D-dimer is negative, PE is unlikely
and no further investigation is needed.
In primary care, if a patient's history strongly suggests pulmonary embolism (PE),
immediate action is required. For example, in this patient, who is a smoker, recently
took a long-haul flight, and is on the combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP), the
clinical picture points clearly to PE as the most likely diagnosis.
In such high-risk cases, you do not need to bring the patient in for face-to-face
assessment or calculate the Wells Score. The safest and most appropriate action is
to arrange same-day hospital referral for urgent assessment and imaging (CTPA)
without delay.
Face-to-face evaluation in general practice should only be considered if the history is
unclear or PE is not the leading differential. When PE is suspected, do not delay, act
quickly to protect the patient.

-- 634 of 1282 --

633
Management
• If symptoms have changed from when child was previously seen by your
colleague, or you are concerned about child you may want to offer a face-to-
face examination, otherwise no need for this as your colleague has seen and
child has no red flags.
• Offer a 1–2-week trial of smaller, more frequent feeds (while maintaining an
appropriate total daily feed volume) AND/ OR ;
• Offer a 1–2-week trial of thickened formula - either they get a pre-thickened
formula (Formula which is already thick) or they get a thickener (For example-
Instant Carobel ) and add to the child’s formula.
NOTE: pre- thickened formular or thickeners should not be used with Gaviscon.
This is because both Gaviscon Infant and pre-thickened formulas or formula
thickeners (e.g., Carobel) work by thickening the feed. Using both together can make
the milk too thick, leading to difficulty sucking, swallowing, or even choking risk. Also,
there is increased risk of constipation: Gaviscon already has a tendency to cause
constipation, and adding another thickener can worsen this.
• So, if you are considering offering a trial of smaller, more frequent feeds along
with a thickener, you should discontinue Gaviscon.
• You can also continue Gaviscon with trial of smaller but more frequent feeds
• If mum has tried smaller but more frequent feeds or she is not willing to try,
you can stop Gaviscon and offer omeprazole trial for 4 weeks
• Inform mum of possibility of CMPA but however, his symptoms are not fully
suggestive of this now.
• Keep baby upright for at least 20-30 minutes after feeds.
• Review in 1- 2 weeks
• Safety netting – to seek urgent medical help earlier if symptoms persists or
she notices any red flag symptoms: Projectile vomiting (possible pyloric
stenosis), Blood in vomit or stool, Signs of dehydration (fewer wet nappies,
sunken fontanelle), Refusing feeds or losing weight etc.

636
Example of explanation to the patient
Alfred, based on what you've told me, it sounds like your asthma is not well
controlled at the moment. You're using your blue inhaler too frequently, waking up at
night coughing, and experiencing tightness in your chest, which all suggest that your
preventer inhaler (Clenil) may not be working as effectively as it should.
A big factor could be your new living environment, especially with your roommate
smoking indoors, as smoke is a known trigger for asthma.
Alfred, since your asthma is not well controlled, I think the best approach would be to
change your inhaler to a combination inhaler. This will mean you’ll have just one
inhaler that works to both relieve your symptoms and control your asthma in the long
term.
The inhaler I’m prescribing is called a Maintenance and Reliever Therapy (MART)
inhaler. It contains both a preventer medication (steroid) to reduce inflammation and
a long-acting reliever to open up your airways. The advantage of this is that it helps
keep your asthma under control while also providing quick relief when needed.
Management
• Bring in for face to face to examine chest, check weight/height, BP and pulse
etc.
• Offer MART inhalers as per New Asthma guideline (Symbicort, Fostair etc.)
• Advice that smoking from his room mate maybe a potential trigger -
Encourage talking to his roommate or speaking to accommodation services to
request a smoke-free environment.
• Offer referral to asthma nurse for full asthma review
• Asthma nurse will also discuss - Personalised Asthma plan- This is a plan that
talks about the patient’s asthma, the list of medications they are taking, their
normal peak flow readings, triggers, how to recognise a flare and what to do
when they have a flare
• Offer vaccinations- Influenza, COVID
• Safety netting advice to return if worsening symptoms / if he is an acute flare
• Follow up in 4 weeks

639
• Ask about lifestyle—any stress from school/exams, social pressures, bullying,
any big life changes recently. You can ask personal questions--is patient is in

• Ask about medical history—any history of anemia, thyroid problems, digestive

• Make diagnosis of eating disorder
Example of explanation to patient
Ava, I want to reassure you that this is a safe space, and my goal is to help you feel
your best. From what we've discussed, it sounds like your low weight and eating
habits may be affecting your body in ways you might not have realized. Your BMI is
quite low, and when the body doesn’t get enough energy, it can pause functions that
aren’t essential for survival, this includes your periods stopping and feeling tired
more often.
I also want to mention that some of the things you’ve described, being concerned
about weight, eating less, and using certain behaviors to control weight, can
sometimes be signs of an eating disorder. I know this might not be something you’ve
thought about before, but it’s really important that we look after both your physical
and mental health.
The next step is to see you face-to-face so we can check your current weight, do
some urgent blood tests, and also an ECG, which is a simple test that checks how
your heart is working. Because low weight can sometimes affect the heart, we want
to make sure everything is safe.
I would also like to refer you to a specialist eating disorder clinic, where they have a
team that can support you in understanding what’s going on and help you feel better.
This doesn’t mean anything is wrong with you, it just means we’re making sure you
have the right support.
How do you feel about all of this? I know it might be a lot to take in, but we’re here to
help you every step of the way.

-- 641 of 1282 --

640
Management
• Offer an urgent face-to-face appointment to examine patient, check her vitals,
weight, height, BMI, blood pressure, pulse, and perform an ECG to assess
her heart health. Assess for signs of malnutrition such as bradycardia, cold
extremities, hair thinning, or muscle weakness.
• Arrange urgent blood tests, including FBC, U&E, LFTs, TFTs to assess
nutritional status and thyroid function, as well as FSH, LH, oestradiol, and
prolactin to check for hormonal imbalances. Include iron studies, B12, and
folate to screen for nutrient deficiencies.
• Offer a referral to an eating disorder clinic for further assessment and
specialized support. A team of specialists, including doctors, psychologists,
dietitians, and mental health professionals, will conduct a detailed assessment
to understand her condition. A dietitian will create a structured meal plan to
help Ava restore a healthy weight in a safe and gradual manner. A
psychologist will offer cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT-E) for eating
disorders: A structured therapy designed to help Ava understand and change
unhelpful thoughts and behaviors related to food, weight, and body image.
• Explain to Ava that if test results or examination findings show concerning
abnormalities, there may be a need for admission to ensure she remains safe
and healthy. Reassure her that this would only be to provide the right medical
support to help her body recover.
• Acknowledge that this conversation may feel overwhelming and reassure her
that she is not alone. Let her know that there are people who specialize in
helping with these concerns and that the goal is to help her feel stronger and
healthier.
• Encourage family involvement with Ava’s consent, as they can provide
additional support during her recovery. Provide resources on eating disorders
and support groups to help her understand that recovery is possible with the
right help.
• Safety net and advise that if she ever experiences fainting, dizziness,
palpitations, or feels very weak, she should seek urgent medical attention
immediately.
• Arrange weekly or biweekly follow-ups to track progress, adjust care as
needed, and ensure she is receiving the right support from all necessary
services.

-- 645 of 1282 --

644
Explain to the patient
Peter, I want to acknowledge that you’ve been through a really tough time. Spending
several weeks in the hospital, especially in ICU, is a major event for anyone, and it’s
completely understandable that you’re still feeling the effects, both physically and
emotionally.
What you’re describing, having trouble sleeping, hearing hospital beeping sounds at
night, smelling antiseptics, and feeling low in mood, are things that some people
experience after being in intensive care. This is known as Post-ICU Syndrome, and it
happens because the body and mind go through a huge amount of stress during a
critical illness. Some people also develop symptoms similar to Post-Traumatic Stress
Disorder (PTSD) because being in ICU can be a distressing experience, even if you
don’t fully remember everything that happened.
Your body has been healing, but your mind is still processing what you went through.
The good news is that these feelings are common and treatable. You are not alone
in this, and we have ways to help you recover fully, just like we’ve been helping your
lungs and body get stronger again.
The next step is to support both your physical and mental recovery. There are things
we can do, such as talking therapy (like CBT or trauma-focused therapy), relaxation
techniques, and in some cases, short-term treatments to help with sleep if needed.
Management
• Acknowledge his symptoms and explain that post-ICU experiences like this
are common due to the stress of a long hospital stay. Reassure him that he is
not alone, and these symptoms can improve with the right support.
• Discuss that he likely has post-ICU syndrome, which can cause low mood,
sleep disturbances, and intrusive memories of the hospital. Explain that some
patients develop PTSD-like symptoms after critical illness.
• Offer sleep hygiene advice—reduce screen time before bed, avoid caffeine,
establish a bedtime routine, and consider relaxation techniques
• Offer a short course (3–7 days) of zopiclone, a non-benzodiazepine sleep aid,
to help improve sleep. Explain that while this medication can be helpful in the
short term, it carries a risk of dependence, so it should be used cautiously.
Advise alternate-day dosing rather than daily use to reduce this risk.
• Emphasize that for long-term improvement, it is important to incorporate good
sleep hygiene practices and consider CBT for insomnia, which has been
shown to be highly effective in improving sleep quality.

-- 646 of 1282 --

645
• Offer referral to mental health team to confirm the diagnosis and initiate
management with trauma focused CBT, Eye movement desensitization and
reprocessing (EMDR) etc.
• If he prefers, you can also offer SSRI- Sertraline and explain that symptoms
may temporarily worsen in the first week before improving, and that the full
benefits of the medication typically become noticeable after around four
weeks.
• Signpost to important charities that support patient suffering from post-ICU
syndrome-for example, ICUsteps- the intensive care patient support
charity.
• Provide safety netting advice—if he feels suicidal, severely distressed, or
unable to cope, he should seek urgent medical help.
Learning point from this station
Some patients with PTSD may worry that sleeping tablets could worsen nightmares,
vivid dreams or disrupt sleep further. Benzodiazepines are known to exacerbate
nightmares and contribute to sleep disorders, and while zopiclone can also have
this effect, it is listed as an uncommon side effect in the BNF.
If offering zopiclone, reassure the patient that this side effect is rare and does not
affect most people. To minimize the risk, advise intermittent dosing rather than daily
use. If the patient still experiences issues or remains concerned, consider CBT and
sleep hygiene measures alone
Mirtazapine can help with both sleep and mood but also carries a risk of worsening
nightmares
Prazosin is another medication which is prescribed for PTSD associated
nightmares/sleep issues but this is off-label and discussions should be made with a
psychiatrist before initiating this medication.
For chronic insomnia (lasting more than 3 months):
• For adults over 55, consider melatonin for a maximum of 13 weeks.
• For other adults with chronic insomnia that significantly impacts daytime
functioning, consider daridorexant, but only if CBT-I (CBT for Insomnia) has
been tried without success, is unavailable, or is unsuitable. The duration of
treatment should be as short as possible, with a review within 3 months to
assess effectiveness and the need for continuation.
Promethazine is not usually used for insomnia. It is commonly used for
insomnia associated with pruritus and urticaria alone

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Acknowledge that you can see his notes and blood test results, but ask him to
explain in his own words what led to him having the blood tests.
• Ask about the heartburn or abdominal pain including onset, location, duration,
severity, whether it radiates anywhere, whether it worsens with food or lying
down, and whether it improves with medications such as antacids.
• Ask about tiredness including duration, progression, impact on daily activities,
and whether it is worse at any particular time of the day.
• Ask about red flag symptoms including weight loss, night sweats, early
satiety, loss of appetite, dark or tarry stools, fresh blood in stools, vomiting,
vomiting blood, and difficulty swallowing.
• Ask if he has noticed any bleeding from anywhere.
• As he is recovering from a recent myocardial infarction, ask about chest pain,
shortness of breath, palpitations, dizziness, fainting, reduced exercise
tolerance, leg swelling, unusual sweating, nausea, or worsening fatigue, as
these may indicate cardiac complications or anaemia.
• Ask about his diet, particularly whether it includes iron rich foods.
• Ask about medication adherence, including aspirin, clopidogrel, and
lansoprazole.
• Ask about previous gastrointestinal issues such as ulcers, gastritis, reflux, or
gastrointestinal bleeding.
• Ask about social history including smoking, alcohol intake, occupation, stress,
and caffeine use.
• Ask how his symptoms are affecting his daily life.
• Ask about his ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a diagnosis of microcytic anaemia, possibly secondary to chronic
gastrointestinal blood loss in the context of dual antiplatelet therapy with
aspirin and clopidogrel.
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to examine the abdomen and assess
observations.
• Arrange further investigations including ferritin to confirm iron deficiency and a
coeliac screen.
• Offer a FIT test and perform urinalysis to check for haematuria.
• Explain that if ferritin comes back low, iron replacement therapy will be
started, and he will be referred to gastroenterology for upper gastrointestinal
endoscopy, and possibly colonoscopy if the FIT test is positive, under the two
week wait pathway.

-- 651 of 1282 --

650
• Increase lansoprazole to 30 mg twice daily for gastroprotection.
• Inform the patient that you will send an urgent advice and guidance request to
cardiology to seek specialist input regarding dual antiplatelet therapy and
determine if it is safe to stop one agent. Aspirin is often stopped and the
patient continues on clopidogrel alone, but this decision will be made by the
cardiology team.
• Provide safety net advice to seek urgent medical attention if he develops
black stools, vomiting blood, worsening fatigue, dizziness, chest pain, difficulty
swallowing, or early satiety.
• Arrange follow up to review blood results and next steps.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Patrick, thank you for taking the time to speak with me today. I can see that you have
been feeling quite tired, and your blood tests show that you have anaemia. This
means your body does not have enough healthy red blood cells to carry oxygen
around effectively, which is likely contributing to your tiredness.
Based on the results, the red blood cells are smaller than usual, which suggests this
may be due to iron deficiency. To confirm this, we will do another blood test called
ferritin to check your iron levels. Does that make sense so far?
If this confirms iron deficiency, we will start you on iron tablets to help build your
levels back up. One common cause can be low iron in the diet, but you have told me
your diet is good. In your case, it is possible that the aspirin and clopidogrel you are
taking may be irritating your stomach lining and causing small amounts of bleeding
over time.
In men of your age, when we see this type of blood picture, we also need to rule out
more serious causes, like cancer. I want to reassure you that I am not saying this is
cancer, but it is important that we check properly to be safe. Are you following me so
far?
The next step would be to arrange a face to face appointment so I can examine your
tummy in more detail. We will also do further tests, including checking your iron
levels like I mentioned earlier, screening for coeliac disease, which is a condition
where the body reacts to gluten and can cause this type of anaemia, and a stool test
to look for hidden blood that you would not be able to see. We will also check your
urine for any blood.
If iron deficiency is confirmed, we will refer you to a specialist for further tests, such
as a camera test to look at your stomach and bowel, to check for any source of
bleeding, ulcers, or in rare cases, cancer.
I understand you are concerned about your blood thinning medications. These are
very important because they help prevent blood clots and reduce your risk of another

-- 652 of 1282 --

651
heart attack. However, we also need to balance this with the risk of bleeding. I will
discuss your case with the cardiology team to see if it is safe to stop one of the
medications. For now, it is important that you continue taking both until we have
specialist advice.
In the meantime, we will increase your stomach protection by increasing your
lansoprazole dose, which will help reduce irritation and the risk of bleeding.
I know this is a lot of information, but we will take this step by step. Our aim is to find
the cause of the anaemia, treat it appropriately, and make sure your medications are
safe for you.
I do not expect this to happen, but if you notice black stools, vomiting blood,
worsening tiredness, dizziness, chest pain, difficulty swallowing, or early fullness
when eating, please seek urgent medical attention by calling the GP practice or NHS
111 if out of hours.
We will follow up once your results are back and keep you fully informed. Does that
make sense? Is there anything you would like me to go over again?
Learning Point from This Station
This station highlights the importance of recognising iron deficiency anaemia in an
older patient and identifying underlying causes, particularly gastrointestinal blood
loss. In patients on dual antiplatelet therapy, chronic low grade bleeding from the
gastrointestinal tract is a key consideration, but this should never be assumed
without proper investigation.
A critical principle is that iron deficiency anaemia in men and postmenopausal
women must always be treated as a potential malignancy until proven otherwise
This requires appropriate investigation, including ferritin, coeliac screening, urinalysis
to check for haematuria, and FIT testing. Once iron deficiency is confirmed, the
patient should be referred urgently for endoscopy under the two week wait pathway.
Most patients who develop complications while on dual antiplatelet therapy may
need to be stepped down to a single antiplatelet agent. In many cases, this involves
stopping aspirin and continuing clopidogrel long term. However, this decision should
involve the cardiologist for specialist advice.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/mi-secondary-prevention/prescribing-
information/antiplatelet-treatments/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/anaemia-iron-deficiency/

At work, you mostly wear gloves as advised, but occasionally, you have used your

If Asked Further:

Marking Scheme – History Taking
• Ask about when the skin changes started and how they have progressed over
time.
• Ask about specific symptoms—any pain, itching, irritation, scaling, or
blistering.
• Ask about whether the affected areas have changed in size, shape, or
number over time.

-- 655 of 1282 --

654
• Ask about any other areas of the body affected—face, lips, arms, legs, or
genital region.
• Ask about any history of trauma, burns, or chemical exposure directly to the
affected areas.
• Ask about work-related exposure—what chemicals are handled, whether
gloves are always used, and any known irritants in the workplace.
• Ask about family history of skin conditions (especially vitiligo, eczema,
psoriasis) or autoimmune diseases.
• Ask about personal medical history—any previous skin conditions, thyroid
problems, diabetes, or other autoimmune conditions.
• Ask about any recent infections, illnesses, or vaccinations (as these can
sometimes trigger vitiligo in predisposed individuals).
• Ask about medications or creams used on the affected areas.
• Ask about lifestyle factors—smoking, alcohol, stress levels, and diet.
• Ask about psychological impact—how the skin changes are affecting his
confidence, social interactions, or mental well-being.
• Ask about concerns—what worries him the most about these changes.
• Ask about his expectations
• Give diagnosis of possible Vitiligo
Explain to patient
Kofi, I completely understand why this is worrying you, especially since people at
work have started noticing. From what you’ve described, this looks like a condition
called vitiligo. This happens when the body’s immune system, which normally
protects us from infections, mistakenly attacks the cells in your skin that produce
pigment. As a result, some areas of your skin lose their colour over time.
I know you’re wondering if the chemicals at work might have caused this. While
some chemicals can lighten the skin, that usually happens only in the exact spots
where they touched the skin. But in your case, the patches are slowly getting bigger
and are not just in specific places where you had direct contact with chemicals. That
makes it less likely that your job caused this.
The good news is that vitiligo is not harmful to your health. It doesn’t cause pain or
make you sick, but I understand that the way it looks can be upsetting, especially if
people are commenting on it. The positive thing is that there are treatments that can
help slow it down and, in some cases, even bring some colour back to the skin.

-- 656 of 1282 --

655
If patient asks, can treatment completely reverse my skin back to its normal
condition
Kofi, that’s a very good question. Treatment can help slow down or stop the spread
of vitiligo, and in some cases, it can even help bring some pigment back to the skin.
However, I want to be honest with you—there is no guarantee that the skin will return
completely to its original colour, and results vary from person to person."
The earlier we start treatment, the better the chances of seeing some improvement.
The treatment I’m recommending, a potent steroid cream, has been shown to help
restore pigment in some people, especially when used in the early stages. If it
doesn’t work as well as we hope, there are other options we can explore, including
light therapy (phototherapy) and specialist treatments.
Management
• Offer further blood test such as Thyroid function tests and thyroid
autoantibodies. Offer autoantibodies for other autoimmune conditions only if
the person's history, family history, and/or other test results are suggestive.
• Advise and offer 4-star or 5-star UVA rating and sun protection factor 50 to
people with vitiligo. This should be applied to affected patches and
surrounding skin before going outdoors into the sun.
• Offer vitamin D test in people with vitiligo who are avoiding all sun exposure.
• Offer a referral to a camouflage team for expert guidance on covering the
affected areas. These teams are usually based in hospital outpatient
departments, but if not, you can contact your local dermatology department or
the organization Changing Faces for support. Alternatively, patients may
choose to use their own fake tan or cosmetic products to help blend the skin
tone.
• Offer a trial of potent steroid cream for 2 months but review after the first 4
weeks to see if there are any improvement
• Advise on potential side effects of topical steroids include skin thinning
(atrophy), stretch marks (striae), increased hair growth (hypertrichosis), skin
discoloration, acne or folliculitis, delayed wound healing, and, with prolonged
use, potential suppression of the skin's natural steroid production
(tachyphylaxis or adrenal suppression in high-potency steroids applied over
large areas).

-- 657 of 1282 --

656
• Inform patient that - Vitiligo can be unpredictable—some people have only
small patches, while for others, it spreads. We will monitor it closely and
adjust treatment as needed.
• If symptoms are worse or not better after treatment with topical steroids, then
refer to dermatology.
• If mental health is affected, you can refer patient for CBT
• Follow up in 4 weeks
• Safety net and advise - If you notice rapid spreading, new patches elsewhere
on your body, or any changes in your general health, let us know immediately.
Learning point from this station
Summary of Vitiligo
1.Determine if the vitiligo is segmental or non-segmental.
• Segmental vitiligo is usually unilateral (affecting one side) and tends to
progress rapidly before stabilizing.
• Non-segmental vitiligo is typically bilateral, progresses gradually, and is often
linked to autoimmune conditions.
2.Management in Primary Care: Offer a potent topical corticosteroid once daily
for up to 2 months (off-label use) to adults requesting treatment who meet ALL
of the following criteria:
• They have non-segmental vitiligo that is localised or limited (affecting less
than 10% of the body surface area).
• The treatment will not be applied to the face.
• The patient is not pregnant.
• There are no contraindications to corticosteroid use.
• They understand the potential risks and side effects of treatment.
THEY MUST MEET ALL THE CRITERIA TO OFFER TOPICAL STERIODS IN
PRIMARY CARE
3. Referral to Dermatology:
• Routinely refer all other cases of vitiligo who do not meet the above criteria to
dermatology

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Begin by acknowledging that you are aware he was seen by your colleague
yesterday regarding his cough, to demonstrate that you have reviewed his
notes and are familiar with the background. Then ask whether his symptoms
have improved, worsened, or remained the same since that consultation.
• Ask about the nature of the cough – duration, frequency, whether it is dry or
productive, and colour of sputum
• Ask about the presence of haemoptysis (coughing up blood)
• Ask if he experiences breathlessness, if so, ask if it is constant or exertional
and whether it is getting worse. Ask about orthopnoea (whether he
experiences shortness of breath when lying flat) and paroxysmal nocturnal
dyspnoea (waking up suddenly at night feeling breathless), to help rule out
heart failure.
• Ask about any associated symptoms – fever (to rule out pneumonia), wheeze,
palpitations, chest pain, pain on inspiration (pleuritic pain), ankle
swelling/pedal oedema (to rule out heart failure)
• Ask about the patient’s cancer history – confirm remission status, and date of
last oncology review

-- 661 of 1282 --

660
• Ask about whether there have been any recent symptoms suggestive of
recurrence of lymphoma such as lumps, bumps, or swollen glands,
particularly around the neck, armpits, or groin.
• Ask about other red flag symptoms, including loss of appetite, unexplained
weight loss, night sweats or fatigue
• Ask about any history of recent travel or immobility, to help rule out pulmonary
embolism
• Ask about contact with persons with prolonged cough (to rule out possible
tuberculosis contact)
• Ask about his occupation and whether there has been any possible exposure
to asbestos, as this could increase the risk of mesothelioma or benign
asbestos-related pleural effusion.
• Ask about his social and lifestyle history, including whether he smokes or has
ever smoked and his alcohol intake.
• Ask how these symptoms have affected him in terms of his daily life, work,
and overall wellbeing.
• Explain the chest X-ray results showing pleural effusion and inform the patient
that this could potentially be linked to his lymphoma, possibly indicating a
return of the cancer.
Example of explanation to the patient
Peter, thank you for waiting. I’ve looked at your chest X-ray, and it shows that there
is some fluid collected around your left lung. This is something called a pleural
effusion, and it can explain why you’ve been having a cough.
There are different reasons why this can happen. Sometimes it’s due to an infection,
heart problems, or inflammation. However, given your previous history of lymphoma,
we do have to consider whether this could be linked to that, by that, I mean a
possible return of your cancer. Right now, we can’t say for sure what has caused it,
so we need to do some more tests to find out.
I am going to speak with the respiratory team today, and I will arrange for you to be
seen at the specialist chest clinic within the next 24 to 48 hours. They will arrange a
CT scan, which is a special scan that gives a clearer and more detailed picture of
your chest.
At the clinic, they may also do a jelly scan of your chest (this is the same type of
scan used in pregnancy), to help them see the fluid. While they do that, they may
use a small needle to draw out some of the fluid. This can help relieve the pressure

-- 662 of 1282 --

661
in your chest and will also be sent to the laboratory to help us find out what has
caused the fluid to build up.
Depending on how things look, they may also recommend putting in a small tube
(chest drain) to help drain more fluid, but that decision will be made by the chest
specialists based on your assessment.
If the tests show that the cause is cancer, your case will be discussed by a team of
specialists, called an MDT (multidisciplinary team), where experts come together to
look at your results and agree on the best treatment plan for you.
I will also give you a sick note for work, as I completely understand that you’re not
feeling well enough to work right now.
While waiting for your appointment, if your breathlessness gets worse, especially if
you’re breathless while resting, or if you notice any new symptoms, you must seek
urgent medical help straight away or go to hospital. Please also let your wife or
anyone close to you know, so they can support you if your symptoms suddenly get
worse.
Do you have any questions about what we’ve discussed today
Management
• Offer to refer the patient to the respiratory ambulatory pleural service to be
seen within 24 to 48 hours.
• Inform the patient that he will be offered a CT scan to look further into his
chest to understand the cause of the fluid. Explain that the respiratory team
may also perform an ultrasound-guided diagnostic aspiration, which may
also be therapeutic, to relieve symptoms and collect a sample of the fluid
for the laboratory to help determine the cause.
• Explain that there is also a possibility that the team may recommend a chest
drain, but this will depend on the assessment and decision of the respiratory
specialists.
• Inform the patient that if the tests confirm cancer, the case will be discussed
at a multidisciplinary team (MDT) meeting, and the team will come up with
a treatment plan that is suitable for him.
• Meanwhile, offer baseline blood test including FBC, U+E’s , LFTs, and CRP.

-- 663 of 1282 --

662
• Offer a sick note to support time off from work, given that his current
symptoms are affecting his ability to carry out his job.
• Safety net the patient by advising that if his breathlessness worsens,
especially if he becomes breathless at rest, or if he develops any new
concerning symptoms, he should seek urgent medical attention or go directly
to the hospital.
• Advise that it is important to inform those around him, such as his wife or
close contacts, so they are aware of his condition and can assist him in
getting help if his symptoms suddenly worsen.
Learning Point from This Station
The most common causes of pleural effusion are congestive heart failure, cancer,
pneumonia, and pulmonary embolism. In patients with a history of cancer, a new
pleural effusion must always raise the suspicion of malignancy or cancer recurrence,
especially when there are no other symptoms suggesting an alternative cause. It is
crucial not to miss cancer-related symptoms in this context.
Management of Pleural Effusion:
If the patient has a small to medium (moderate) pleural effusion and is clinically
stable (not breathless, normal oxygen saturations, stable respiratory rate, and vitals),
they can be managed via the ambulatory respiratory pleural service, ideally seen
within 24 to 48 hours, and may not require admission.
If the effusion is large, the patient is breathless, especially at rest, or has
deteriorating observations or signs of clinical instability, then hospital admission may
be required for urgent assessment and possible intervention.
Regardless of stability, all cases of new pleural effusion must be discussed with
the respiratory team for further guidance and planning.
In cases of small pleural effusion, where the cause is known and expected to resolve
(for example, post-infection), no immediate treatment may be necessary. The
respiratory team would typically arrange a repeat chest X-ray in 4 to 6 weeks to
confirm that the fluid has resolved.
Further Reading
https://patient.info/doctor/pleural-effusion-pro

• Normal bone density is defined as a T-score of -1.0 or above.
• Osteopenia is defined as a T-score between -1.0 and -2.5.
• Osteoporosis is defined as a T-score of -2.5 or lower.

Marking Scheme – History Taking
• Ask about her understanding of the DEXA scan and what she expects from
this discussion.
• Ask about her recent vertebral fracture—how did it happen? Any trauma or
sudden movement?
• Ask about red flags from vertebral fracture- weakness in legs, inability to
control bowel and bladder, worsening back pain
• Ask about other possible risk factors for fractures, including: Family history of
osteoporosis or fractures, Smoking or alcohol intake, Long-term steroid use or
other medications affecting bone health (e.g. PPI)
• Ask about any other unexplained fractures in the past
• Ask about diet and lifestyle—calcium intake, vitamin D levels, physical activity,
weight-bearing exercises.
• Ask about any recent falls, balance issues, or concerns about mobility.
• Ask about home situation
• Explain to patient about normal Bone density scan but inform that the
presence of a vertebral fracture signifies the presence of osteoporosis, even if
the bone mineral density (BMD) is not within the diagnostic range.
Example of explanation to the patient
Penelope, I have your DEXA scan results, and they show that your bone density is
technically in the normal range, but it is very close to the level where we start to
worry about bone weakness.
Even though your bone scan does not show osteoporosis, the fact that you have
already had a vertebral fracture tells us that your bones are likely more fragile than

-- 667 of 1282 --

666
they appear on the scan. This means that you are at higher risk for future fractures,
and we should consider starting treatment to protect your bones and prevent any
further breaks.
I strongly recommend that we start a bone-strengthening medication, such as
bisphosphonates, which will help keep your bones strong and reduce the risk of
future fractures. We will also make sure your calcium and vitamin D levels are where
they should be, and I’ll give you advice on exercise and fall prevention to keep you
as strong as possible.
Management
• Offer blood tests to check TSH levels, vitamin D, calcium, FBC, and U+Es to
assess any underlying factors affecting bone health
• Offer to calculate her calcium intake using a calcium calculator by sending her
a questionnaire to complete, where she can record the amount of calcium-rich
foods she consumes, such as milk, cheese, and other dairy products. This will
help determine if she is meeting her daily calcium requirements or if
supplementation is needed.
• Offer a referral to physiotherapy or occupational therapy to assess her home
environment for fall prevention measures and consider enrolling her in a falls
prevention program, which includes both OT and physiotherapy support
• As she lives alone, offer a falls alarm to ensure safety in case of future
incidents
• Since osteoporosis treatment is indicated, discuss bisphosphonate therapy,
explaining that it helps strengthen bones and reduce the risk of future
fractures. Inform her about potential side effects, including gastrointestinal
discomfort and, in rare cases, osteonecrosis of the jaw, and advise her to
schedule a dental check-up before starting treatment to ensure good oral
hygiene
• Explain that treatment with Bisphosphonates may continue for 10 years
• Inform her that she may also be placed on Vitamin D and calcium tablets but
the blood results and calcium calculator will guide this
• Encourage regular exercise like walking
• Offer smoking cessation
• Review in 3 months

-- 668 of 1282 --

667
• Safety net- if she gets any new or worsening back pain, weakness in her legs,
inability to control her bladder or bowel or any other concerns/falls, to seek
urgent medical advice.
Learning point here
The decision to consider initiating osteoporosis treatment in individuals with a
vertebral fracture without requiring a DEXA scan is based on expert consensus from
the SIGN guideline development group. They recognise that the presence of a
vertebral fracture itself is indicative of osteoporosis, regardless of whether bone
mineral density (BMD) falls within the diagnostic threshold. Additionally, evidence
supports that individuals with vertebral fractures have an increased risk of future
fractures, including both vertebral and non-vertebral fractures.

670
• Ask about recent medication changes, particularly the impact of oxybutynin,

Example of Explanation to The Carer
Thank you, Susan, for calling and for looking out for Edith. I completely understand
your concern, UTIs are a common cause of increased urination, and they can
sometimes lead to confusion, especially in older adults. You’re absolutely right to be
thinking about this. However, Edith does not have other typical UTI symptoms, such
as a burning sensation when passing urine, pain, blood in the urine, fever, or back
pain.
One important thing to consider is her medication. She started taking oxybutynin two
months ago, and this can sometimes cause urinary retention, making someone feel
like they need to pass urine more often. It can also lead to confusion, which might
explain her symptoms. Other things, like constipation, can also contribute, although
you’ve mentioned that her bowels are fine. Sometimes, infections other than a UTI
can also cause these symptoms, but we’re not sure of that at the moment.
I understand why you’d like us to start antibiotics, but because Edith doesn’t have
clear UTI symptoms, we need to check her properly first. Overusing antibiotics can
lead to resistance and unnecessary side effects, so we want to be sure it’s the right
treatment. That’s why we need to carefully assess before starting antibiotics.
The next step would be to review her in person to assess her condition more
thoroughly. During this assessment, we will examine her abdomen and carry out a
general assessment to look for any signs that may indicate an infection or any other
underlying cause of her symptoms. If necessary, we may stop the oxybutynin and
consider an alternative. Meanwhile, I will advise that she should drink plenty of
fluids. If she becomes unwell, develops a fever, or her confusion worsens suddenly,
please call back or seek urgent medical attention.
I really appreciate you calling to raise this concern, it’s important, and your input is
invaluable in making sure Edith gets the right care.

-- 672 of 1282 --

671
Management
• Offer a home visit to assess the patient in person. Explain to the carer that
you would like to examine her abdomen and perform a general assessment to
check for any signs that may indicate an infection.
• If there is any suspicion of infection following the assessment, she may need
hospital admission for urgent blood tests, monitoring, and further
management
• However, based on everything the carer has reported about the patient, an
infection does not appear to be the cause of her symptoms. It is more likely
that her current medication, oxybutynin, is contributing to these changes.
Therefore, advise that oxybutynin should be discontinued and a more suitable
alternative, such as mirabegron, should be considered.
• Safety net- Inform carer that meanwhile, she should ensure patient is drinking
plenty of fluids, if her situation becomes worse or she is unwell, then please
seek urgent medical advice –particularly call ambulance to arrange admission
into hospital.
Learning Point from this Station
It is important to recognise that while urinary tract infections (UTIs) are common in
older women, anticholinergic medications such as oxybutynin can also cause
confusion and delirium. According to NICE guidance, immediate-release oxybutynin
should not be offered to older women who may be at higher risk of sudden
deterioration in their physical or mental health. This is because it can lead to
significant impairment in daily functioning, chronic confusion, and episodes of acute
delirium.
NICE also advises that urine dipstick testing should not be used to diagnose a UTI in
women aged over 65 years, as false positives are common in this age group.
Instead, a midstream urine (MSU) sample, ideally collected in the morning, should
be sent for culture to confirm infection.
A diagnosis of UTI in women aged over 65 years should be considered if there is:
Isolated new-onset dysuria, ortwo or more of the following urinary or non-specific
symptoms:
• Fever
• New frequency or urgency
• New urinary incontinence

-- 673 of 1282 --

672
• New suprapubic pain
• New visible blood in the urine
• New or worsening delirium (while considering other potential causes)
• New or worsening general malaise, lethargy, or reduced daily functioning
where no other cause is identified

one dose of Amitriptyline, it is unlikely to cause serious harm, but older adults can be
more sensitive to its effects and amitriptyline can have delayed action, so we should

One thing to be aware of is that Amitriptyline can lower blood pressure, and Arthur is
already on Amlodipine, which also lowers blood pressure. While we don’t expect an

confirm if it’s okay to go ahead with his usual medications? Also, if you have already

It’s really important that you picked this up early and escalated it, which allows us to
manage the situation safely. Can I ask if this incident has been reported within the
care home? I only ask because having these incidents formally reviewed isn’t about
blaming anyone but about ensuring the right measures are in place to prevent this
from happening again. I know care homes work hard to keep residents safe, so it’s
always good practice to see if any adjustments can be made to help avoid errors in
the future."

drowsiness, dizziness, or confusion. If anything concerns you, don’t hesitate to call

-- 677 of 1282 --

676
Management Plan
• Advise to monitor the patient closely, as Amitriptyline has a delayed onset of
action, meaning symptoms could develop later.
• Reassure that since this was a single low dose, it is unlikely to cause harm,
but caution is still necessary.
• Explain that you will check the BNF and TOXBASE to ensure there are no
significant interactions before confirming whether it is safe to continue with his
regular medications.
• Recommend that this incident should be formally reported and investigated
within the care home, not to place blame, but to help prevent similar errors in
the future and to ensure patient safety.
• Advise checking the patient’s blood pressure as Amitriptyline can affect blood
pressure.
• Provide safety netting advice, instructing the carers to seek urgent medical
attention if the patient develops racing heartbeat, dizziness, drowsiness,
confusion, or low blood pressure.
Takeaway from This Station
NICE defines poisoning as a state resulting from the administration of excessive
amounts of any pharmaceutical agent, including drugs, household products,
industrial chemicals, or plant/animal derivatives.
In this case, the patient accidentally ingested a single safe dose of Amitriptyline (25
mg), rather than multiple doses or an excessive amount.
NICE also advises that people who have taken poisons with delayed action should
be referred urgently to hospital, even if asymptomatic. This includes tricyclic
antidepressants (e.g., Amitriptyline), aspirin, iron, paracetamol, co-phenotrope, and
all modified-release preparations.
However, in this case, the definition of poisoning does not fully apply, as the patient
did not take an excessive dose—only a single therapeutic dose, which is unlikely to
cause harm. Additionally, he is in a care home where he can be effectively
monitored.
If he were living alone at home, hospital admission could be considered for closer
monitoring. However, in a supervised setting like a care home, he can be managed
safely with regular observation, blood pressure checks, and symptom monitoring

Example of explanation to the patient
Youssef, the most likely cause of your symptoms, your increased thirst, frequent
urination, and abdominal pain, is the fact that your calcium level is too high. One of
the possible reasons for this is a condition called hyperparathyroidism. The
parathyroid glands are four small glands located in the front of your neck, and their
job is to regulate calcium levels in your body. Sometimes, they produce too much
parathyroid hormone (PTH), which causes calcium levels to rise excessively, leading
to the symptoms you’re experiencing."
High calcium can be serious, especially when it causes symptoms, and the safest
option would be for you to be admitted to the Acute Medical Unit (AMU) in hospital
for treatment and monitoring to help lower your calcium levels and relieve your
symptoms."
However, as you are not keen on going into the hospital right now, we can take an
urgent approach in primary care. I’d like to organise some urgent blood tests to
check your current calcium levels, parathyroid hormone, vitamin D, and kidney
function to understand what’s causing this. While we wait for the results, it is really
important that you drink plenty of fluids to help your body manage the high calcium.
I also want to reassure you that your intake of milk or yogurt is very unlikely to
be the cause of this issue, so you don’t need to worry about that.
If at any point you start feeling worse, such as experiencing worsening of your
symptoms, confusion, racing of your heart, severe weakness, or if the blood in your
urine comes back, you must go straight to A&E for urgent treatment.
I will arrange for your blood tests as soon as possible, and we will follow up very
soon once we have the results. If you change your mind about being admitted in the
meantime, please let me know.

-- 681 of 1282 --

Management
• Offer a referral to the Acute Medical Unit (AMU) as he is symptomatic and
requires urgent treatment.
• If he declines admission, assess his capacity to make this decision. If he has
capacity and understands the risks, proceed with urgent investigations in
primary care, including calcium, parathyroid hormone (PTH), vitamin D,
U&Es, LFTs, CRP, ESR and TFTs to determine the underlying cause.
• Advise him to drink plenty of fluids to help lower his calcium levels.
• Provide clear safety netting advice—if he develops confusion,
palpitations, chest pain, worsening symptoms, or if the blood in his
urine returns, he must go to A&E immediately.
• Encourage him to inform someone around him about his condition so they
can help him seek urgent care if needed.

-- 684 of 1282 --

683
Example of explanation to the patient
Andrew, I understand that you’re a nurse practitioner, but I will explain things in
simple terms as if you weren’t a healthcare professional, so apologies if I’m
describing things too simply. I just want to make sure we cover everything clearly.
From what you've described, your symptoms are most likely due to reflux, which
happens when acid from your stomach travels up into your food pipe (oesophagus),
causing heartburn and discomfort. This can be made worse by certain triggers like
stress, diet, weight changes, and excessive coffee intake, which can relax the valve
at the bottom of the oesophagus, allowing acid to come up more easily."
Prescribing Omeprazole would be a good option, as it helps reduce the amount of
acid your stomach produces, which should relieve your symptoms. However, before
starting, would it be okay to check for a common bacteria called Helicobacter pylori
(H. pylori) with a simple stool test? This bug can sometimes cause similar symptoms,
and if it’s present, the treatment would be slightly different.
It’s really reassuring that you don’t have any red flag symptoms like difficulty
swallowing, weight loss, or vomiting blood, which would be more concerning.
However, since stress, diet, and weight changes could be contributing, it may also
help to make some lifestyle adjustments, such as reducing fatty or spicy foods,
avoiding eating late at night, and trying to manage stress levels where possible.
How do you feel about this plan?
Management
• Offer face to face to assess abdomen
• Offer H. Pylori stool testing
• Offer Omeprazole 20mg OD
• Discuss lifestyle modifications to help reduce symptoms: Reduce intake of
takeaway and fatty/spicy foods, avoid eating late at night and allow 2–3 hours
before lying down after meals, limit caffeine and alcohol, which can worsen
reflux.
• Offer patient to try stress-reducing activities, such as exercise or relaxation
techniques. You can offer headspace app which is free for NHS workers and
this can help with stress coping mechanisms
• Discuss exercise and consider weight management strategies
• Safety net- If symptoms do not improve after 8 weeks of treatment, or if they
worsen despite medication, we may need to investigate further.

Marking scheme

• Establish Fraser competence by assessing if the patient understands the
nature, risks, and implications of emergency contraception and sexual activity.

• Ask about her partner’s age to assess if there are any legal implications or
concerns about exploitation.
• Ask about previous sexual activity—whether this is her first time or if she has
been sexually active before.
• Ask about her last menstrual period—and most importantly, if her periods are
regular
• Ask if she has had any other episodes of unprotected sex since her last
menstrual period, apart from the one she mentioned, to ensure accurate
assessment and appropriate management
• Ask about previous use of contraception—whether she has ever used any
form of birth control, including condoms.
• Ask about any previous pregnancies or use of emergency contraception.
• Ask about risk factors for STIs—whether her partner used a condom, if she
has had multiple partners, and if she has had STI testing before.
• Ask about medical history, including any contraindications to emergency
contraception, such as liver disease or hormone-sensitive conditions.
• Establish Fraser competence by assessing if the patient understands the
nature, risks, and implications of emergency contraception and sexual activity.
• Ask about any regular medications she is taking, as some medications can
interact with emergency contraception.
• Ask about her social situation, including support at home, relationships with
parents, and whether she has a trusted adult she can talk to.

687
Example of explanation to the patient
Aminah, thank you for coming in and for trusting me with this. I can see you’re
worried about getting pregnant after having unprotected sex, and I want to reassure
you that we have options to help reduce that risk.
There are two types of emergency contraception. One is in tablet form, and the other
is a small device called a coil, which is inserted into the womb through the vagina."
There are two tablets, and they work by delaying your ovary from releasing an egg,
which helps prevent pregnancy. However, they may not be as effective if you have
already ovulated. The coil, on the other hand, works by stopping a fertilized egg from
implanting in the womb and can also be used as a long-term contraceptive method
to prevent pregnancy in the future."
Are you following me so far, Aminah?
Now, in your case, you mentioned that your last period was three weeks ago, and
your cycle is usually 28 days long. This means that your ovulation (when your
ovaries release an egg) would have likely happened around day 14, so the
emergency contraception tablets are less likely to be effective at this stage. The coil
would be a much better option in preventing pregnancy.
What are your thoughts on this? If you decide to go for the coil, we will arrange for
this to be inserted at the sexual health clinic today. However, if it cannot be done
today, we can still provide you with the emergency contraceptive tablet as a bridging
option while you wait to get the coil. If you do not want the coil at all, we can still offer
you the tablet, although it may not work as well in your situation."
How do you feel about these options?
Beyond emergency contraception, I strongly encourage you to consider a form of
ongoing contraception to prevent future unplanned pregnancies. There are various
options, including the contraceptive pill, implant, injection, coil, or patch. I’ll provide
you with some leaflets so you can read more about them, and we can discuss what
might work best for you.
Another important thing to consider is the risk of sexually transmitted infections
(STIs), as unprotected sex increases the risk of infections such as chlamydia or
gonorrhoea. I can arrange a sexual health screening for you if you’d like.
I would like to see you again in 2–3 days to talk about ongoing contraception and
answer any questions you may have. In the meantime, if your period is delayed by
more than seven days or if it is lighter than usual, please take a pregnancy test to
check whether the emergency contraception has worked.
Do you have any questions about what we’ve discussed so far?

-- 689 of 1282 --

688
Management
Reassure the patient and thank her for coming in.
• Explain the available emergency contraception options: tablets and the coil
(IUD). The tablets come in two types, both of which work by preventing
ovulation, while the coil prevents implantation and can also serve as a long-
term contraceptive method.
• If she chooses the tablet, inform her that it is less likely to be effective in her
case, but it can still be offered. Advise her to take a pregnancy test if her
period is delayed by more than seven days or if it is lighter than usual.
• Levonorgestrel 1.5mg is recommended for individuals weighing less than
70kg or with a BMI below 26.
• For those weighing more than 70kg or with a BMI over 26, a double dose
(3mg) is required for optimal effectiveness.
• Emphasize the importance of ongoing contraception to prevent future
unplanned pregnancies. Briefly outline the different contraceptive options
available and provide leaflets for further information, encouraging her to
consider and communicate her preferred choice.
• Discuss the risk of sexually transmitted infections (STIs) following unprotected
sex and offer a sexual health screening.
• Encourage her to involve her parents in the discussion, but if she is not
comfortable doing so, respect her decision.
• Arrange a follow-up appointment in 2–3 days to discuss ongoing
contraception and address any further concerns.
• Remind her to take a pregnancy test if her period is delayed by more than
seven days or if it is lighter than normal.
• Offer safeguarding referral- Please see below

-- 690 of 1282 --

689
Learning point from this station
Sexual activity with a child aged 13, 14, or 15
It is a criminal offence for anyone aged 16 or older to engage in any sexual contact
with someone under 16, even if the younger person consents.
Sexual activity with a child under 13
This is classified as statutory rape and must be reported to the police immediately.
Position of trust offences
It is illegal for anyone in a position of trust (e.g., teachers, carers, youth workers) to
have any sexual contact with a person under 18 in their care or authority.
So, a 16-year-old having sex with a 14-year-old, is technically an offence under UK
law. However, the law primarily aims to protect young people from exploitation rather
than criminalising consensual relationships between peers.
The patient above should be referred to safeguarding team or discussed with the
safeguarding lead.
They will assess the situation rather than automatically escalate to the police.
If no coercion, exploitation, or risk is identified, they may provide supportive
intervention (e.g., advice on healthy relationships, contraception, emotional well-
being) rather than punitive action.
If there are concerns about pressure, power imbalance, or potential risk, further
steps may be taken.
So how do you tell Aminah that you will inform safeguarding
Aminah, I appreciate you being open and honest about this. I want you to know that
my priority is your safety and well-being. Because you’re under 16, any kind of
sexual activity must be considered from a safeguarding perspective. That doesn’t
mean you’re in trouble, and it’s not about punishing the person involved—our role is
simply to make sure you are safe and that nothing has happened that could put you
at risk.
As part of my responsibility, I do need to share this with the safeguarding team. They
are there to offer support, not judgment, and they will assess whether any further
action is needed.
If there are no concerns about pressure, coercion, or harm, then the focus will
simply be on making sure you have the right guidance and support.
I know this might feel a bit worrying, but you’re not alone in this. If you have any
concerns or if there’s anything you want to ask, I’m here to listen and support you.

-- 691 of 1282 --

690
NOTE: If you have safeguarding concerns but are unsure whether to escalate
to safeguarding team, you can first discuss them with the safeguarding lead
for guidance.

693
• ASK ICE—ask dad what he thinks is causing the issue and what he is most
worried about.
• Make a diagnosis of constipation with overflow soiling / diarrhoea (faecal
impaction).
Example of explanation to the patient
Mr Anderson It sounds like Sophie is struggling with constipation, and the brown
stains you’ve noticed in her underwear are likely due to something called ‘overflow
soiling.’ This happens when hard stool gets stuck in the bowel, and softer stool leaks
around it, which can look like diarrhoea. This is also known as faecal impaction. It’s
quite common in children and can be treated. We need to adjust her Movicol to help
clear the blockage and make it easier for her to pass stools regularly. Along with
that, improving her diet by adding more fibre and fluids will really help in the long run.
I know it can be tough with a busy schedule, but small changes can make a big
difference.
Management
• Offer face-to-face to examine abdomen
• Increase Movicol gradually to clear the impaction- four sachets daily for 2
days, then six sachets daily for 2 days, then eight sachets daily. Treat until
impaction resolves.
• Encourage a high-fibre diet (fruit, vegetables, whole grains).
• Advise to Increase fluid intake (more water, less juice).
• Advise to try to reduce fast food and include home-cooked meals where
possible.
• Encourage regular toilet time—after meals is best
• Offer resources on childhood constipation and diet advice-signpost to ERIC
WEBSITE
• Review in 1-2 weeks to check response to Movicol adjustment.
• Safety Netting- advise to seek urgent review if she develops severe pain,
vomiting, persistent bloating, or blood in stools.

Marking Scheme
History taking
• Ask about duration and progression of the cough
• Ask about cough characteristics -dry, productive, worsening at night
• Ask about associated symptoms- recent flu symptoms, wheeze, fever,
postnasal drip (Feeling of mucus draining from the back of the nose down the
throat), throat clearing, nasal congestion, taste changes
• Ask about reflux symptoms – heart burn, cough that is worse during or after
eating,
• Ask about red flag symptoms -weight loss, haemoptysis, night sweats
• Ask about history of allergies or asthma
• Ask about triggers exposure to irritants like cigarette smoke, and allergens like
pollen, dust mites, and mold.
• Occupational exposure- for example - occupational dust in builders
• Ask about medication use (any recent new medications like ACE inhibitors)
• Ask about smoking history, home situation etc.
• Elicit ICE
• Give diagnosis of potential Post-nasal drip
Example of explanation to the patient
Emma, from what you’re describing, particularly the sensation of something draining
into your throat, a sour taste, and frequent throat clearing, your symptoms are likely
due to postnasal drip. This happens when your nose produces too much mucus, and
instead of coming out through your nostrils, it drips down the back of your throat.
That’s likely why you’re feeling that constant tickle, the need to clear your throat, and
why your cough is worse at night. It can be caused by things like a cold, sinus
irritation, or even allergies. The good news is that it’s treatable, and we can try some
simple medications to help dry up the extra mucus and stop the irritation.

-- 697 of 1282 --

696
Management
• Offer face to face to listen to chest, examine nostrils etc.
• Offer a non-sedating antihistamine (e.g., loratadine or cetirizine) and
decongestants (pseudoephedrine). This can be gotten over the counter.
Decongestants should be used for short duration (7 days) to avoid rhinitis
medicamentosa
• If she reports sinus symptoms- for example nasal congestion, runny nose,
facial pain etc. You can consider intra-nasal steroids.
• Avoid throat irritants (e.g., strong perfumes, smoking environments)
• Review in 2 weeks – symptoms start to improve after 2 weeks.
• Heavily safety net- Advise patient, if symptoms are worsening, blood in
sputum, unwell, SOB, weight loss, then seek urgent medical advise
• Signpost to patient resources: NHS website for postnasal drip.

Example of explanation to the patient
Olivia, from what you’re describing, particularly the combination of excess facial hair,
irregular periods, and difficulty losing weight, it sounds like you may have a condition
called Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS).
PCOS is a common hormonal condition that affects how the ovaries work. In PCOS,
there is hormonal imbalance which leads to unwanted hair growth on the face and
body, irregular or missed periods, and challenges with weight management. It’s

-- 700 of 1282 --

699
important to check your hormone levels to help confirm this. There are ways to
manage your symptoms and help with the hair growth.
Management

amounts of weight loss can improve symptoms. A healthy diet and regular
exercise can help balance hormones and regulate period.

has used this method already, then offer topical eflornithine for facial
hirsutism. Eflornithine is not licensed to children under 19 and pregnant
women
• Inform her that if she is open to it, a combined contraceptive pill can help
regulate periods and reduce hair growth over time.
• Laser hair removal is an option which an provide long term effect but not
usually covered by NHS
• Follow-Up: Arrange a review after blood tests.
