Patient booked an appointment to discuss concerns
Patient’s story
You are Lily Harrington, a 21-year-old female. You booked this telephone
consultation because you suddenly felt dizzy and lost consciousness while
volunteering at a local hospice.
You were helping at the reception desk and supporting fundraising. Suddenly, you
felt lightheaded and dizzy before blacking out
Lost consciousness for about 2-3 minutes. Others told you that you had some slight
shaking while unconscious
You did not wet yourself. You did not bite your tongue.
If asked about how you felt after waking up: Felt tired but recovered after a while. No
confusion, memory loss, or difficulty speaking
Paramedics checked you over. They advised you to go to A&E, but you didn’t go
because you didn’t want to waste time
Idea: You don’t know why this happened. You’re worried it could be a seizure
because your cousin has epilepsy.
Concerns: You want to know if you’re safe to drive because you’re considering a job
as an estate agent that requires driving
Social history: Non-smoker, does not drink alcohol. Lives with parents and siblings.
You are not sexually active.
SAY NO TO ANY OTHER QUESTIONS ASKED
Marking scheme
History
• Ask about the syncopal episode
• Ask about what patient was doing prior to the episode – for example- stress,
standing for long periods
• Ask if there were any symptoms before the syncopal episode- for example,
did she experience any warning signs such as light-headedness, nausea,
sweating, weakness, or visual disturbances? OR did syncopal episode occur
suddenly without warning?
• Ask how many minutes the syncopal episode lasted for

-- 703 of 1282 --

702
• Ask if there was loss of consciousness
• Ask if there were any witness and if she was told what happened during the
episode- for example shaking, jerky movements etc.
• Ask what happened post syncopal episode- was there any tiredness/fatigue,
incontinence, confusion, tongue biting/bleeding, head injury or any other
injuries.
• Ask if there are previous syncopal episodes
• Ask if patient was unwell in anyway prior to incident – for example-any recent
chest, ear or urine infections, flu symptoms etc.
• Ask if there were any chest pain, palpitations, SOB
• Ask about social history- smoking, alcohol, illicit drug use, occupation and
driving
• Ask if there are any family history of syncope, epilepsy or sudden death
• As she is female- ask LMP and ensure there is no risk of pregnancy
• Make a diagnosis of likely vasovagal syncope
Example of explanation to the patient
Lily, from what you’ve described, particularly the dizziness before passing out, the
short duration of unconsciousness, and feeling tired afterward without confusion, it
sounds like you may have experienced a simple faint (vasovagal syncope) rather
than a seizure. Fainting can happen when the body overreacts to certain triggers like
standing for too long, stress, or dehydration, causing a temporary drop in blood
pressure and blood flow to the brain.
The fact that you had some mild shaking while unconscious can happen with
fainting, but seizures usually last longer, involve confusion afterward, and often
include tongue biting or loss of bladder control, which you didn’t experience.
Management
• Offer face to face to assess – check heart sounds, pulse, blood pressure,
neurological examination
• Offer investigations- ECG to rule out arrhythmia, FBC, U+E’s, LFTs, TFTs,
• Advise on potential triggers that may have caused the episode and suggest
strategies to avoid them. If the triggers are unclear or unknown, recommend
keeping a record of symptoms, noting when they occur and what they were
doing at the time.

-- 704 of 1282 --

703
• Advise on staying well hydrated, not standing for long, if feeling faint, try to lie
down and raise legs to improve blood flow
• As the patient is a Group 1 driver, driving is generally not restricted, and there
is no requirement to inform the DVLA, provided the episode occurred while
standing. However, she should refrain from driving until a full
assessment is completed to confirm that this was a simple faint and not
due to another underlying cause.
• Safety net- advise patient to seek urgent medical attention if she experiences
chest pain, palpitations, breathlessness, or if it happens again unexpectedly

• Ask if there is any chance, she could be pregnant and if she’s done any

Example of explanation to the patient
Thank you for sharing all of that, Samantha. From what you have described, there
are two main possibilities for why your periods have stopped. One is that it could be
related to the Desogestrel pill, which you have been taking regularly for the past six
months. It is quite common for this type of pill to cause a reduction in bleeding or
even complete absence of periods in some women, especially after the first few
months of use.
The second possible reason is that this might be the start of the menopause. You
are 47, and you mentioned your sister went through the menopause at 45, so it
would not be unusual if you were starting to notice some changes now. Does that
make sense?
You also mentioned feeling more irritable, getting some joint pains, and occasional
night sweats. These can all be early signs of the perimenopause – that is the phase
leading up to the menopause, when hormone levels start to change. Are you
following me so far?
Now, you asked whether we should do a blood test, like an FSH test, to check if this
is menopause. That is a very good question. In women over 45, we do not normally
do hormone tests to diagnose menopause. This is because hormone levels can go
up and down from day to day, so the results are not always helpful. Instead, we
usually go by your age, your symptoms, and your period pattern.
The one thing we do need to rule out is pregnancy. I know you have been taking
your pill regularly, but no method is 100% effective. So just to be safe, I would
recommend doing a urine pregnancy test. Would you be comfortable with that?

-- 708 of 1282 --

707
Management
• Do not offer a blood test to check FSH, as FSH levels fluctuate at this age
(46-50) and are not a reliable indicator of menopause
• Advise a urine pregnancy test to rule out pregnancy, as she is having
unprotected sex and no contraceptive method is 100% effective
• Inform her that contraception should be continued until two years after her
last natural period if under 50, or one year if over 50.
• Advise staying well hydrated and maintaining a balanced diet to support
energy levels
• Explain that menopause symptoms can be managed with both hormonal and
non-hormonal options, and offer to discuss further if she is interested
• Advise that if she develops new symptoms or has any concerns in the
meantime, she should seek medical advice promptly.
Learning Point from This Station
This station highlights the importance of recognising the common causes of
secondary amenorrhoea in women in their late 40s, particularly the impact of
progestogen-only contraception and the natural onset of perimenopause.
Desogestrel, a progestogen-only pill, frequently leads to reduced or absent periods
and may mask the menstrual changes typically associated with menopause.
Clinicians should be aware that routine FSH testing is not recommended for
diagnosing menopause in women aged 46-50, as hormone levels fluctuate
significantly during the perimenopausal transition, making the results unreliable.
However, serum FSH testing may be appropriate in the following circumstances:
• Women aged 40–45 years with menopausal symptoms and a change in their
menstrual cycle
• Women under 40 years with suspected premature ovarian insufficiency (POI)
• Women over 50 years who are using progestogen-only contraception,
including depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA)
Do not routinely perform FSH or other hormone tests to diagnose menopause in:
• Individuals aged 45 years or over who are otherwise well and experiencing
typical menopausal symptoms

-- 709 of 1282 --

708
• Those using combined oestrogen and progestogen contraception, high-
dose progestogens, or hormone replacement therapy (HRT)
Effective communication around expectations, reassurance about the pill’s effects,
and safety-netting regarding symptom progression are key components of good
clinical care in this scenario.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/menopause/diagnosis/diagnosis-of-menopause-
perimenopause/

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Michael, from what you’ve described, along with the findings from your ECG, it looks
like you have a condition called Atrial Fibrillation (AF). This is an irregular heart
rhythm where the heart beats in an uncoordinated way. This condition can develop
due to certain risk factors, and in your case, high blood pressure may have
contributed to it. Hypertension puts extra strain on the heart over time, making AF
more likely to occur. The concern with AF is that it increases the risk of stroke
because it can lead to blood clots forming in the heart. Since you've already had a
stroke, it's especially important to manage this properly.
Michael, we used a medical calculator to assess your risk of having a stroke due to
Atrial Fibrillation, and the results show that your risk is raised. Because of this, we
strongly recommend starting a blood thinner called Apixaban to help reduce the
chance of a clot forming and causing another stroke. While Apixaban is important for
reducing your stroke risk, it is a blood thinner, which means it can increase the risk of
bleeding. Most people tolerate it well, but there is a risk of bleeding-for example easy
bruising or nosebleeds. However, if you experience unusual bleeding, such as
passing blood in your urine or stools, coughing up blood, or persistent heavy
bleeding, even nose bleeds or bruise in your skin, you should seek medical attention.

-- 713 of 1282 --

712
It’s also very important to be cautious about falls or injuries, especially head injuries.
If you ever hit your head, even if you feel fine, you will need to go to the hospital for a
CT scan to check for any internal bleeding. If you have any concerns, please don’t
hesitate to reach out.
In addition, we will also be offering you a medication called Bisoprolol, which helps
control your heart rate and makes it easier for your heart to function more efficiently.
These medications are important for managing your condition and reducing future
risks. We will monitor you closely and ensure you’re on the right treatment plan.
Michael, we will discuss stopping your Clopidogrel with the stroke team, as
continuing it alongside Apixaban may increase your risk of bleeding. In the
meantime, we will offer you Lansoprazole, a medication that helps protect your
stomach from irritation and reduces the risk of bleeding complications. We will keep
you updated once we have guidance from the stroke team.
Management
• Offer face to face to listen to heart sounds- if murmurs are present, then he
will need echocardiography
• Offer further blood test including thyroid function test, U+E’s, LFTs, FBC
• Offer beta-blockers for rate control-for example- Atenolol 50mg OD or
Bisoprolol 1.25mg OD
• Offer Apixaban 5mg twice daily. Advise the patient about the risk of bleeding
as a possible side effect. Explain that if he sustains any head injury, he must
attend the Emergency Department for a CT head scan, even if he feels well
and has no symptoms.
• Advise that we will consult the stroke team about discontinuing his
clopidogrel, as it may increase his risk of bleeding. In the meantime, offer
Lansoprazole.
• Safety net patient- to seek urgent medical help if he develops severe chest
pain, sudden breathlessness, or weakness on one side of the body.
• Follow up in 1-2 weeks to assess response to treatment.

Management
• Do not restart antipsychotics (Aripiprazole) in primary care.

effects, and starting from scratch may delay symptom control; this is why input
from a mental health specialist is needed to ensure he is managed safely and
effectively.
• Educate the patient not to stop psychiatric medication without discussing with
a healthcare professional first

-- 717 of 1282 --

716
• Ask if he is able to delay his trip temporarily to allow for urgent review and

paranoia, hallucinations, confusion, or any concerns about his safety or
behaviour
Learning Point from this station
Restarting antipsychotics is not always straightforward. The decision depends on
several factors, including the length of discontinuation, the severity of current
symptoms, risk of side effects, and the pharmacokinetic properties of the drug.
In patients who have missed more than a few days of treatment, restarting at the
previous full dose may increase the risk of adverse effects, such as
extrapyramidal symptoms, sedation, or cardiovascular complications. On the other
hand, restarting at a low dose may delay therapeutic benefit, which can be
particularly concerning in the context of active psychosis.
Therefore, when a patient presents with recurrence of psychotic symptoms and
wishes to resume antipsychotic treatment, the GP should urgently contact psychiatry
or the local crisis team to confirm the appropriate plan. Some antipsychotics require
dose re-titration if missed for more than 1–2 weeks, and psychiatric input is essential
to ensure safe and effective management.

-- 718 of 1282 --

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset and progression of her tiredness, including whether it has
improved, worsened, or remained the same
• Ask about any triggers or relieving factors
• Ask about symptoms suggestive of vitamin B12 or folate deficiency, including
numbness or tingling in the hands or feet, balance problems, memory
changes, difficulty concentrating, shortness of breath, palpitations, dizziness,
or reduced exercise tolerance
• Ask about gastrointestinal symptoms suggestive of coeliac disease or
malabsorption, including diarrhoea, reflux, bloating, constipation, abdominal
pain, steatorrhoea (stools that are difficult to flush), or unintentional weight
loss
• Ask about her dietary intake, particularly sources of vitamin B12 and folate
• Ask how long she has been avoiding pasta and wheat, and whether she
avoids gluten-containing foods (this is important because before testing for
coeliac disease, a patient needs to be consuming gluten-containing foods for
a minimum of 6 weeks).
• Ask about mood, sleep, appetite, weight changes, and menstrual history
• Ask about social history, including alcohol intake, smoking, illicit drug use, and
occupation
• Ask about family history of gastrointestinal conditions, particularly coeliac
disease
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a diagnosis of macrocytosis, likely secondary to vitamin B12 deficiency
given her vegetarian diet, with coeliac disease as a possible differential,
considering her symptoms triggered by pasta and wheat.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you, Amira, for speaking with me today. I know you have been feeling tired for
quite some time, and you were wondering whether this might be related to your
thyroid. The reassuring news is that your thyroid function is normal, so your current
dose of levothyroxine appears to be appropriate. Are you following so far?
One thing that did show up on your blood test is that your red blood cells are slightly
larger than usual. We call this macrocytosis. This can sometimes be linked to vitamin
deficiencies, particularly vitamin B12 or folate, which can be more common in people
who follow a vegetarian diet. Does that make sense?
You also mentioned that whenever you eat pasta or wheat, you develop diarrhoea.
That raises the possibility of a condition called coeliac disease, where the body

-- 726 of 1282 --

725
reacts to gluten-containing foods. Coeliac disease can sometimes lead to poor
absorption of nutrients, including vitamin B12, which could explain your blood test
findings. Are you following me so far?
What we need to do next is arrange further blood tests to check your vitamin B12
and folate levels. If these are low, we can replace them with supplements, and your
tiredness should improve.
In addition, we should test for coeliac disease. It is important to know that for the
coeliac blood test to be accurate, you need to be eating gluten regularly for at least
six weeks beforehand. As you have been avoiding wheat and pasta for the last six
months, the test may not be reliable at the moment. Would you be willing to
temporarily reintroduce some gluten into your diet so that we can test properly? We
can discuss how to do this safely and in a manageable way.
If coeliac disease is suggested on the blood test, we would refer you to a specialist
for further assessment and confirmation.
In the meantime, to help with your tiredness, it may be helpful to pace yourself,
prioritise important tasks, and maintain a regular sleep routine. Gentle physical
activity can also improve energy levels. As you are vegetarian, it would be sensible
to ensure your diet includes good sources of vitamin B12, such as dairy products,
eggs, and fortified cereals.
I do not expect this to happen, however, if your symptoms worsen, or if you develop
tingling in your hands or feet, balance problems, or memory difficulties, please seek
medical advice promptly.
We will arrange the tests and review everything once the results are back. Does that
all sound clear?
Management
• Arrange further blood tests, including vitamin B12, folate levels, and coeliac
serology
• Explain that for coeliac testing to be accurate, she needs to be consuming
gluten-containing foods for at least six weeks prior to testing. Ask whether she
would be willing to temporarily reintroduce gluten so that the test results are
reliable
• Inform her that if vitamin B12 or folate deficiency is identified, this can be
corrected with appropriate supplementation
• Inform her that if coeliac disease is suggested on the blood tests, she will be
referred to a gastroenterologist for further assessment and confirmation of the
diagnosis.

-- 727 of 1282 --

726
• Provide advice on managing her tiredness, including energy conservation
techniques, pacing activities, and prioritising important tasks
• Encourage a regular sleep routine and good sleep hygiene
• Advise on ensuring adequate intake of vitamin B12-rich vegetarian sources,
such as dairy products, eggs, and fortified cereals
• Provide safety-net advice to seek urgent medical attention if her symptoms
worsen or if she develops tingling in her hands or feet, balance problems, or
memory difficulties.
• Arrange follow-up once blood test results are available to review findings and
adjust management accordingly
Learning Point from this Station
Isolated macrocytosis (raised MCV without anaemia), is a common finding in general
practice. The most frequent causes include alcohol use, vitamin B12 or folate
deficiency, liver disease, hypothyroidism, reticulocytosis. certain medications such as
azathioprine, methotrexate, hydroxycarbamide, and some antiretroviral drugs.
In this case, vitamin B12 deficiency is a likely possibility given the patient’s
vegetarian diet. However, coeliac disease must also be considered, particularly as
she reports diarrhoea after consuming gluten-containing foods. Malabsorption
secondary to coeliac disease can lead to vitamin deficiencies and macrocytosis.
Additionally, her history of autoimmune thyroid disease increases the risk of other
autoimmune conditions, including pernicious anaemia and coeliac disease. This
highlights the importance of considering associated autoimmune disorders in
patients with existing autoimmune diagnoses.
A crucial clinical point is that coeliac serology is only reliable if the patient has been
consuming gluten regularly for at least six weeks prior to testing. As this patient has
been avoiding gluten for some time, it is essential to discuss the need for temporary
gluten reintroduction before testing in order to obtain accurate results.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/coeliac-disease/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/anaemia-b12-folate-deficiency/

Marking scheme
History Taking
• Ask about when the itching started and how it has progressed over time.
• Ask about whether the itching is localised or widespread and if it has changed
in intensity or distribution over time.
• Ask about specific associated symptoms, including rash, redness, dryness,
swelling, pain, or oozing.
• Ask about associated sensory symptoms , crawling sensation (Psychogenic
cause), burning sensation (Neuropathic cause or lymphoma), prickling itch
after a hot bath or shower (May indicate polycythemia)
• Ask about contact with persons with similar itch (think scabies)
• Ask about any associated triggers for example, hot weather, cold weather,
sweating, stress, certain fabrics, or specific activities.
• Ask about any history of skin conditions such as eczema, psoriasis, or
urticaria.
• Ask about systemic symptoms or red flags that may suggest malignancy,
including weight loss, night sweats, lumps or bumps, fever, and fatigue.
• Ask about any recent infections or illnesses
• Ask about any new medications, including over the counter drugs and herbal
supplements. Medications such as opioids, statins, and ACE inhibitors can be
potential causes of itching.

-- 730 of 1282 --

729
• Ask about environmental and occupational factors, including recent travel, use
of new soaps, detergents, skincare products, or exposure to chemicals or
irritants at home or work. Certain occupations, such as working in hot
environments like kitchens, can predispose to dry skin and itching.
• Ask about personal and family history of allergies, atopy, or autoimmune
conditions.
• Ask about social history including smoking, alcohol consumption, stress
levels, and diet.
• Ask about sleep disturbance and whether the itching worsens at night.
Nocturnal itching may suggest underlying causes such as kidney disease,
liver disease, or psychological factors.
• Ask about the psychological impact, including how the itching is affecting
confidence, daily activities, and mental well being.
• Ask about the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a diagnosis of generalised pruritus, with differentials including dry skin
and lymphoma.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
David, thank you for explaining everything so clearly. From what you have told me,
what you are experiencing is something we call pruritus, which simply means itching.
In many cases, this can be caused by relatively simple things such as dry skin,
especially with changes in weather, frequent hot showers, or the use of certain
soaps or products. This is a very common cause and is often manageable.
There are also other causes of itching, including skin conditions or infections, but
based on what you have told me, these seem less likely at the moment.
However, because you have also mentioned some unintentional weight loss
alongside the itching, we do need to think about other possible causes. One of these
can be more serious conditions such as a certain type of blood cancer called
lymphoma. I want to reassure you that I am not saying this is what you have, but it is
important that we consider and rule out these possibilities. Does that make sense so
far?
The next step would be to examine your skin and check for any lumps or swellings,
particularly in areas such as your neck, armpits, and groin. I would also recommend
some blood tests to look for any underlying causes, including a full blood count, liver
and kidney function, thyroid function, and iron levels.
In the meantime, to help with the itching, I would advise using a regular moisturiser
to keep your skin well hydrated. We can also consider an antihistamine to help

-- 731 of 1282 --

730
relieve the itching, especially at night. I should mention that antihistamines can
sometimes make you feel drowsy, so if that happens, you should avoid driving or
operating machinery.
Once we have your results, we will review them together and decide on the next
steps if needed.
If you notice any new symptoms such as lumps, worsening weight loss, night
sweats, or if the itching becomes significantly worse, please let us know urgently.
Does that all make sense?
Management
• Offer a face to face appointment to examine the skin, check for any lumps or
swellings suggestive of lymphadenopathy, and perform an abdominal
examination to assess for splenomegaly.
• Advise the patient to keep a symptom diary to help identify any potential
triggers or patterns associated with the itching.
• Offer blood tests including full blood count, HbA1c, urea and electrolytes, liver
function tests, CRP, ESR, thyroid function tests, and iron studies including
ferritin.
• Offer moisturisers. Note that for dry skin, ointments are generally more
effective than creams
• Offer antihistamines. Start with a non-sedating antihistamine such as
cetirizine as first line, and this can be increased gradually up to four times the
standard dose if required.
• If symptoms remain poorly controlled, particularly if sleep is affected, consider
a sedating antihistamine such as chlorphenamine at night. In some cases, a
non-sedating antihistamine can be used during the day and a sedating one at
night, although this approach is more commonly used in conditions such as
urticaria.
• Provide safety net advice. Although he will be reviewed face to face, advise
that if symptoms worsen or if he develops new symptoms such as night
sweats, lumps or swellings, or new skin changes, he should seek urgent
medical attention.

-- 732 of 1282 --

731
Learning Point from This Station
This station highlights the importance of approaching generalised pruritus
systematically, particularly when there is no visible rash. While dry skin is a common
and benign cause, the presence of red flag symptoms such as unexplained weight
loss should prompt consideration of underlying systemic causes, including
malignancy such as lymphoma.
A key principle is that itch without rash is not always dermatological and may reflect
underlying conditions such as liver disease, kidney disease, thyroid disorders,
diabetes, or haematological malignancies.
Initial management should focus on thorough history taking, identifying red flags, and
performing appropriate baseline investigations including blood tests. Symptomatic
treatment with emollients and antihistamines can be offered, but this should not
delay investigation if concerning features are present
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/itch-widespread/
https://www.pcds.org.uk/clinical-guidance/pruritus-without-a-rash

-- 736 of 1282 --

735
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Rebecca, based on your symptoms and the investigations we’ve done, it seems that
your recurrent abdominal pain, bloating, vomiting, and difficulty passing wind are
likely caused by a sub-acute bowel obstruction. This is probably due to adhesions
from your previous abdominal surgeries, such as the appendix operation, excision of
endometriosis and your bowel operation.
Adhesions are bands of scar tissue that can form after surgery, and they can cause
the bowel to become partially blocked, leading to symptoms like pain, bloating, and
difficulty with passing wind or having a bowel movement. This can explain the waves
of symptoms you’ve been experiencing.
I understand you’ve been thinking this might be IBS, but IBS usually doesn't cause
such severe symptoms like distension, vomiting, or not passing wind. The intensity of
your pain, to the point of needing to visit A&E, is also unlikely to be typical of IBS.
So, while IBS can cause discomfort, it doesn't typically lead to episodes like you've
described.
What we need to do now is refer you to the surgeons through their rapid access
clinic, as your symptoms suggest that adhesions are causing the obstruction. I will
inform them of your symptoms, and they should be able to see you within a week,
but typically it’s sooner than that.
Once seen by the surgeons, they may recommend a CT scan of your abdomen to
get a clearer picture of the situation. Based on the findings, they might offer a
procedure such as a laparoscopy, which is a camera test to look inside your bowel.
During this procedure, they may also be able to remove the adhesions causing the
obstruction, which should help improve your symptoms.
In the meantime, if you experience any of the symptoms again, such as severe pain,
bloating, or difficulty passing wind, you must go back to the emergency department
straight away for further evaluation. We want to make sure we catch any issues
early, especially if the obstruction happens again.
Management
• Offer referral to surgical assessment unit or surgical rapid access clinic to be
seen urgently, usually within a week (But can be seen earlier)
• No need for a face to face as she is currently not symptomatic and Moreso,
she was seen 2 days ago by a health care professional
• Surgeons may consider CT Abdomen and laparoscopy to directly visualize
and treat the adhesions (adhesiolysis)

-- 737 of 1282 --

736
• Offer her a sick note to help explain her situation to her employer and provide
some support at work
• Strongly safety-net regarding symptoms of bowel obstruction—advise her to
return to A&E immediately if she experiences any recurrence of her
symptoms, as this could indicate a developing or full-blown bowel obstruction.

Marking scheme
History
• Ask patient about symptoms that led to him having a swab
• Ask if symptoms are getting better, worse or still the same
• Ask whether the discharge is coming from around the foreskin or directly from
the urethra. Urethral discharge is more commonly seen in STIs, whereas in
cases of balanitis, the discharge typically collects under the foreskin
• Ask about bleeding and skin splitting of foreskin, phimosis (difficulty retracting
foreskin).
• Ask if there’s any tightening of the foreskin or difficulty pulling it back, which
could suggest phimosis. Also check if there’s been any issue with the
foreskin getting stuck and not returning to its normal position after being
retracted, which may indicate paraphimosis
• Ask about urinary symptoms –frequency, burning sensation, haematuria

-- 740 of 1282 --

739
• As symptoms are recurrent – ask for diabetes questions- urinary frequency,
thirst, eating too much, weight loss/weight gain, family history of diabetes
• Ask about sexual history – any new sexual partners, any symptoms in sexual
partner, any recent STI screen or previous history of STI, has this affected his
sexual life.
• Ask patient’s ICE
• Give diagnosis of Balanitis and explain results of swabs
Explain to the patient
James, your swab results show that you have Candida, which is causing a condition
called balanitis. This is an inflammation of the head of the penis caused by a fungal
infection. It’s quite common and treatable, but because this has happened more than
twice, it's important for us to look at a few other things to make sure we’re
addressing everything.
One thing we need to consider is diabetes, a condition where the body has trouble
managing sugar levels. This can sometimes make infections like Candida more
likely. To be thorough, we’ll arrange a blood test to check for diabetes. It’s just a
precaution, and it will help us understand what might be going on
Another consideration is that, even though your partner doesn't have any symptoms,
it’s still possible that she could be carrying the infection and passing it back to you.
It’s a good idea for her to get tested as well, and she may need treatment even if she
doesn't have symptoms. We would recommend discussing this with her in a way that
doesn’t cause any unnecessary stress.
Until the infection clears up, I’d recommend avoiding sex for now, just to give your
body the best chance to heal and to avoid spreading it.
I’d also recommend that you to self-refer to the sexual health clinic, just to make sure
everything is in order and to catch anything early if needed.
Is Candida an STI?
James, Candida itself is not classified as a typical sexually transmitted infection
(STI), but it can be passed through sexual contact. Candida is a type of fungus that
naturally exists in small amounts in the body, including in the genital area.
Sometimes, for various reasons like a weakened immune system or hormonal
changes, it can overgrow and cause an infection, which is what you're experiencing.
Although it can be passed during sexual activity, it’s not always linked to sexual
transmission. In some cases, both partners can have it, even if one person isn’t
showing symptoms. That’s why it’s a good idea for your partner to get tested too, just
to make sure we’re not missing anything.

-- 741 of 1282 --

740
Management
• Offer HBA1C test for diabetes as symptoms are recurrent
• Offer further Clotrimazole 1% cream to use for another 7 days to make it 14
completed days for treatment or can use it till his symptoms settle.
• Offer topical hydrocortisone 1% cream once daily for 14 days or until
symptoms settle
• Alternatively, you can offer combination cream – Canesten HC (Combination
of clotrimazole 1% and hydrocortisone 1%) BD for 7 days
• Advise to abstain from sexual activity until treatment completes or consider
condom use. Please note that Clotrimazole can cause damage to latex
condoms
• Advise sexual health screening via sexual health clinic
• Advise that the partner should also be screened, as there is a possibility of
candidal infection being passed between sexual partners.
• Advise on good hygiene measures: wash the penile head area daily using
lukewarm water and avoid using harsh soaps. Where possible, gently retract
the foreskin and ensure the glans penis is completely dry before replacing the
foreskin. Recommend using an emollient as a soap substitute to reduce
irritation.
• Safety netting and worsening advice given: advise the patient to seek urgent
medical attention if he develops severe pain, blood in the discharge,
ulceration, significant swelling, or any problems with the foreskin—such as
difficulty retracting it (phimosis) or inability to return it to its normal position
after retraction (paraphimosis).

mother may be having difficulty managing some of the tasks that she used to handle
independently, and this could indicate that she may benefit from additional support.
There are several possible factors that could be contributing to these changes. In
people who present with symptoms like your mother's, one of the possible factors
could be a decline in memory. However, other factors, such as physical health
issues, infections, or even constipation, could also be at play.
What we would normally do for someone in a similar situation is arrange a routine
health visit as soon as possible. During this visit, we would conduct an examination,
assess her overall health, might take some blood test if needed and also take a look
at her home situation to see if there are any immediate concerns. If we suspect an
infection, we would treat it accordingly. If constipation seems to be an issue, we
would also treat that. If it appears she may be struggling to cope at home, we would
inform the safeguarding team /social services who will assess whether it’s
appropriate for her to continue living independently, and if not, we would discuss
options such as a nursing home or additional care services to support her.

Learning point from this station
The key lesson in this station is the importance of maintaining confidentiality and
speaking in general terms when discussing a patient’s health, especially when the
patient has not given permission for their information to be shared.
In this scenario, Margaret, the patient, has not given consent for her son, Alex, to
discuss her health with the doctor. Despite Alex being her next of kin, patient
confidentiality must still be respected, and specific details regarding Margaret’s
health, such as her diagnosis, medications, or treatment plan, should not be
disclosed without her consent.
You can take a full history from Alex, but do not divulge any information about the
patient to him without her explicit consent.

challenging at times because you still eat junk food occasionally.
You’ve recently started doing daily walks about 2 weeks ago, but are unsure about

You are looking for medications to help you lose weight, as you find it difficult to
maintain a healthy weight despite your efforts.

obstructive sleep apnoea and feel that it has helped with your sleep quality.

-- 748 of 1282 --

747
Ideas: You feel that medication would help you lose weight more effectively.
Concerns: You’re worried about the impact of your weight on your health,
particularly with obstructive sleep apnoea.
Expectations: You would like the GP to provide options for weight loss medications

Marking Scheme
History Taking
• Ask how long patient has been struggling to lose weight
• Ask about previous weight loss efforts, including any specific diets or
strategies tried in the past
• Ask about the current diet, including common food choices (junk food, snacks,
etc.).
• Ask about overall level of activity
• Ask about the impact of weight on his health, particularly in relation to his
sleep apnoea and any other comorbid conditions.

pain, lower back pain, breathlessness, or any impact on daily activities or

• Ask about social history: Occupation (To find out if sedentary), smoking,

Example of explanation to the patient
Andrew, your BMI of 34 places you in the obese category, and I’m really glad that
you’re seeking help to reduce your weight. I understand that dieting can be
challenging, but I want to congratulate you on taking the important step of starting
daily walks—that's a great start!
For long-lasting weight loss results, it’s important to focus on a balanced diet. I know
it can be hard, but I’m happy to refer you to a dietitian who can tailor a diet plan that
fits your lifestyle and helps you reach your goals. They’ll be able to work with you on
creating a plan that’s both manageable and sustainable.

-- 749 of 1282 --

748
Additionally, I recommend a referral to weight loss services, where they can offer
counselling and provide an exercise routine that will support your weight loss
journey.
To prevent obesity and maintain long-term results, many people need to aim for 45–
60 minutes of moderate-intensity physical activity a day, especially if they don’t
reduce their energy intake. For example, walking or jogging would be excellent. A
good starting goal would be to aim for 150 minutes of exercise per week, which can
be broken down into 30 minutes for 5 days.
You’re already on the right track, and with a bit more support, I’m confident that you
can make steady progress toward your goals
In terms of weight management medications, we do have some options. One
medication is Orlistat, which works by preventing fats from being absorbed in the
body. It can be quite effective, but it does come with some side effects, such as oily
stools, faecal incontinence, and abdominal discomfort. Other common side effects
can include flatulence, increased bowel movements, and stomach cramps. So, while
it can help with weight loss, some people find the side effects uncomfortable.
Another option is Mounjaro (Tirzepatide), which is an injection that can help with
weight loss by reducing appetite and increasing satiety. It’s quite effective, but it also
comes with some side effects, such as nausea, vomiting, and diarrhoea. These side
effects can be managed, but they may be difficult for some people to tolerate.
However, since you’ve only started with lifestyle changes just 2 weeks ago, I would
suggest that we continue focusing on diet and exercise for at least 3 months. Some
people are able to achieve significant weight loss with just these changes, and it’s
important to give it some time. If, after 3 months, you’re still struggling to lose weight,
we can then revisit the idea of starting medications like Orlistat or Mounjaro,
depending on your progress and your preferences.
What are your thoughts on this approach? Does it sound like something you’d be
willing to continue for the next few months
Management
• Offer blood tests, including thyroid function, to rule out any underlying medical
causes of weight gain.
• Advise the patient to continue with current diet and lifestyle changes, as he is
only two weeks into implementing exercise routines.
• Explain that if there is no significant improvement after two to three months,
medication may be considered.

-- 750 of 1282 --

749
• Discuss medication options such as Orlistat and GLP-1 agonists (e.g.,
Mounjaro).
• Offer a referral to a dietitian for additional nutritional support.
• If interested, the patient can also be referred for counselling and exercise
routine through local weight management services.
• He can also be offered Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT) for weight
management or Mindful Eating programs can help address emotional eating
and support long-term behaviour change.
• Arrange follow-up in 3-4 months to assess progress with weight loss and
evaluate any changes in sleep quality or sleep apnoea symptoms.
Learning point from this station
The lesson from this station is that for a patient with stage 1 obesity and
comorbidities, drug treatment should be considered after ensuring that lifestyle
changes (such as diet and exercise) have been started
In John's case, since he has only recently started walking/exercising for 2 weeks, it is
too early to consider medication. According to the guidelines, if lifestyle interventions
have been tried without significant improvement, or if the patient is not willing to
continue lifestyle changes, then weight loss medications can be considered as he
meets the criteria (BMI of 34, comorbidities).
The key takeaway is that lifestyle changes should be prioritized and given adequate
time to show results before moving to pharmacological treatments.

-- 757 of 1282 --

756
Example of explanation to the patient
Steven, thank you for being honest. I can see this has been weighing heavily on you.
Based on what you’ve shared, it sounds like you're going through a tough time, and
gambling might be at the centre of it. You’re not alone, many people in similar
situations feel stuck, and I want you to know help is available.
I understand you're asking for sleeping tablets, but I believe the sleep difficulties may
be more of a symptom of what you’re going through emotionally and mentally,
especially around gambling and the stress it brings.
I’m less inclined to suggest sleeping tablets straight away. These medications don’t
address the root cause of your sleep difficulties and can actually lead to dependency
or other complications if used long-term. Instead, I’d really like to help support your
sleep and mental wellbeing in a healthier, more sustainable way.
We have something we call sleep hygiene measures-
• Like, avoiding screens an hour before bed: ‘Blue light’ from phones, tablets,
and TVs can suppress melatonin, the hormone that helps you sleep. Try to
avoid using any of these devices at least an hour before going to bed.
• waking up at the same time every morning, even after a poor night’s sleep or
on weekends. This helps regulate your body clock.
• Avoiding naps during the day, as even short naps can interfere with night-time
sleep and make it harder to fall asleep later.
• Creating a wind-down routine: Activities like reading a book, taking a warm
bath, or listening to calming music before bed can help your body transition
into sleep mode.
Now, alongside these sleep-focused changes, I think it’s important we look at the
bigger picture, especially the role gambling might be playing in your sleep issues and
stress levels. There are excellent, confidential services that can support you
There are specialist services that offer confidential help for gambling. You don’t need
to face this alone. We can connect you with services like the National Gambling
Helpline and refer you to a local addiction support team, where they have experience
helping people with exactly what you're going through.
CBT (Cognitive Behavioural Therapy) also known as talking therapy has been shown
to be particularly effective for managing gambling addiction and improving sleep and
emotional control.

-- 758 of 1282 --

757
And of course, everything you’ve said today remains confidential unless we feel
someone is at risk of serious harm, and I will always talk to you before sharing
anything further
Management
Offer brief advice on sleep hygiene (reduce screen time, bedtime routine, stress
reduction, avoiding naps during the day etc.)
Offer to provide leaflets with other sleep hygiene methods
Advise against the use of sleeping tablets. However, if the patient has already
implemented good sleep hygiene measures without success, and their insomnia is
causing significant daytime impairment and distress, a short course (3–7 days) of a
non-benzodiazepine hypnotic (such as Zopiclone) may be considered
Offer referral to any of the below
• National Gambling Helpline (run by GamCare) –
• GambleAware
• Primary Care Gambling Service
• GamLearn
• Gamblers Anonymous
Offer referral to CBT
Safety netting about suicide risk, low mood/worsening mental health
Arrange follow-up in 4 weeks to assess progress and emotional wellbeing
Lesson from This Station
The key takeaway is to identify underlying causes when patients present with non-
specific symptoms like insomnia or irritability. Gambling disorder is a behavioural
addiction with real mental, emotional, and physical consequences.
Patients may initially request symptom relief (e.g., sleeping tablets) but be open to
help if gently and empathetically explored.
Management should focus on behavioural and psychological support, not just
medication. Recognizing the need for confidentiality and building trust is vital in
helping the patient engage with services.

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset, location, character, and frequency of the abdominal pain

• Ask about red flag symptoms, including rectal bleeding, unintentional weight
loss, fever, night sweats, or persistent fatigue
• Ask about any recent dietary changes
• Ask about family history, including inflammatory bowel disease, coeliac
disease, or colorectal cancer
• Ask about social history, including smoking, alcohol intake, diet, and
occupation
• Ask about the impact of symptoms on daily life and work
• Ask about the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a diagnosis of suspected irritable bowel syndrome

-- 761 of 1282 --

760
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you, Frank, for explaining everything so clearly. From what you have
described, this sounds most consistent with irritable bowel syndrome, often called
IBS. Is that something you have heard of before?
IBS is a very common condition where the bowel becomes more sensitive than
usual. This can cause symptoms such as crampy abdominal pain, bloating, and
changes in bowel habit, including constipation or diarrhoea. Does that make sense
so far?
Importantly, IBS does not mean there is anything structurally wrong with your bowel,
and it is not dangerous. It simply means the bowel reacts more strongly to things like
stress, certain foods, caffeine, or alcohol, which can trigger symptoms.
That said, I would like to be thorough and arrange some basic tests, including blood
tests and a stool test, to rule out other conditions such as inflammation or coeliac
disease. Would you be happy for me to arrange those?
I would also like to see you face to face to examine your tummy and, if appropriate,
perform a back passage examination, with a chaperone present, to make sure we
are not missing anything else. Would that be okay with you?
In terms of managing the symptoms, keeping a symptom diary can really help
identify triggers so we can work out what to avoid. Simple lifestyle measures such as
regular meals and drinking enough fluids can make a big difference.
Because stress seems to play a role in your symptoms, techniques such as
relaxation exercises, mindfulness, breathing techniques, and sometimes talking
therapy can be very helpful. What are your thoughts about that? Is there anything
there that you feel you might be open to trying? You do not have to decide today. I
can give you some information to look through in your own time and see what feels
manageable for you.
Increasing your physical activity can also make a big difference. Even something like
30 minutes of moderate exercise, such as brisk walking, jogging, or cycling, can help
improve your mood, reduce stress, and improve IBS symptoms as well as your
overall health. Do you think that is something you could realistically incorporate into
your routine?
For the crampy pain, I can prescribe a medication called mebeverine, which helps
relax the bowel muscles and can reduce discomfort. Would you like to try this?
As your constipation seems to be more prominent than the diarrhoea, I would
suggest gradually increasing foods rich in soluble fibre, such as oats or linseeds.
It is important to increase fibre slowly to reduce the risk of bloating and wind. Does
that sound reasonable?

-- 762 of 1282 --

761
If symptoms do not improve, we can consider referral to a dietitian to explore a
more structured approach, such as a low FODMAP diet. This involves temporarily
reducing certain hard-to-digest carbohydrates for a few weeks and then gradually
reintroducing them to identify your personal triggers. We would only consider this if
simpler measures do not help.
There are also medications that can help if needed. For example, loperamide can be
used for diarrhoea and certain laxatives can help with constipation. I would advise
avoiding lactulose, as it can sometimes worsen bloating. Medication would usually
be considered if lifestyle and dietary changes are not enough. I just want to reassure
you that we have further options if needed.
If at any point you develop new symptoms, such as blood in your stool, unexplained
weight loss, or fever, I would want you to contact us straight away.
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to perform an abdominal examination and
digital rectal examination
• Offer baseline investigations, including full blood count, urea and electrolytes,
C-reactive protein, coeliac serology, and faecal calprotectin, to exclude
alternative pathology
• Advise keeping a symptom diary to help identify triggers and guide avoidance
strategies
• Discuss stress management, encouraging relaxation techniques such as
mindfulness, yoga, or talking therapy where appropriate
• Advise the patient to aim for at least 30 minutes of moderate-intensity physical
activity on five days of the week, such as brisk walking, jogging or cycling, as
this can help improve IBS symptoms, reduce stress, and support overall
health.
• Provide lifestyle and dietary advice, including regular meals, adequate
hydration, and gradually increasing dietary soluble fibre (e.g. oats and
linseed). Note: Increasing soluble fibre intake should be gradual to avoid
flatulence and bloating
• For abdominal cramping, offer an antispasmodic, such as mebeverine, to help
relax gut smooth muscle.
• Inform the patient that if symptoms do not improve, we can consider
referral to a dietitian for a low FODMAP diet. This involves temporarily
reducing specific hard-to-digest carbohydrates for two to six weeks, followed
by gradual reintroduction to identify personal triggers.
• If the low FODMAP approach is not effective, symptomatic medications
can be used. These are available over the counter, such as loperamide for
diarrhoea and appropriate laxatives for constipation. Note that lactulose

-- 763 of 1282 --

762
should be avoided in IBS as it increases bloating and flatulence
(wind/gas/fart)
• Provide clear safety-net advice to seek medical attention promptly if
symptoms worsen or if new features develop, such as rectal bleeding,
unintentional weight loss, or fever
• Arrange follow-up in four to six weeks to review symptoms and response to
treatment

-- 766 of 1282 --

765
History Taking
• Ask about onset, duration, and progression of leg symptoms
• Ask about nature of pain: burning, tingling, pins and needles
• Ask about relieving or aggravating factors or if the symptoms get worse at
certain times of the day—especially at night (Typical of diabetic neuropathy)
• Ask if pain radiates to anywhere
• Ask about any new back pain
• Any weakness or numbness in lower legs or balance issues
• Ask if patient has noticed any ulcerations or change in colour of feet
• Ask about any recent trauma
• Ask for other symptoms to suggest autonomic neuropathy – GI symptoms
(abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, faecal incontinence), Genitourinary
symptoms (Erectile dysfunction in men, urinary incontinence in both men and
women), Cardiac (Postural hypotension- dizziness on standing), gustatory
sweating (sweating while eating)
• Ask about Impact on mobility and sleep
• Ask about social history including occupation, alcohol, smoking, diet etc.
• Explore patient's ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a diagnosis of likely diabetic neuropathy
Explain to the patient as below
Margaret, thank you for sharing what you’re going through. From the way you’ve
described the burning and tingling pain in both feet, particularly worse at night and
now affecting your walking, this sounds like diabetic peripheral neuropathy, which is
a known complication of diabetes. It happens when high blood sugar levels over time
start to damage the small nerves, especially in the feet and lower legs.
The good news is that your recent HbA1c result shows improvement, which is
encouraging. Keeping your blood sugars well-controlled is one of the most important
ways to slow down or prevent further progression of this condition.
We do regular monofilament testing for this exact reason, to assess sensation in the
feet — and your recent result shows some reduced sensation already, so it’s
important we act early.

-- 767 of 1282 --

766
While diabetic neuropathy is likely, we still need to take a thorough approach. Would
it be okay if I bring you into the surgery today, I'll need to examine the nerves in your
legs and feet today.
We’ll also arrange blood tests to rule out other possible causes of nerve pain, such
as vitamin B12 deficiency, thyroid problems, or other metabolic issues.
If needed, we can also consider a nerve conduction study, which helps us assess
how severely the nerves are affected.
In the meantime, we do have some medications that can help with this type of nerve
pain — one of them is called amitriptyline, which I can offer you today. Just to check
with you — your notes say you have no allergies, is that correct?
This medication can sometimes cause drowsiness, especially when you first start it,
but most people tolerate it well. It’s often taken at night, which can also help with
your sleep, since you mentioned the pain is worse at night. Many patients find it very
helpful in controlling these kinds of symptoms.
Management
• Offer face to face for neurological examination
• Offer further blood test including B12, Thyroid function test.
• Offer referral for nerve conduction studies and electromyography to diagnose
and assess the extent of nerve damage
• Offer a choice between amitriptyline, duloxetine, gabapentin, or pregabalin.
• Advise that once we rule out other potential causes like B12 deficiency,
thyroid issues etc, then we will refer to local Foot Protection Team.
• Advise on regular foot self-checks and to report any ulcerations, change in
colour, pain or worsening sensation in legs/feet
• Safety net and advise the patient to seek urgent medical attention if they
notice any weakness in the lower limbs, worsening pain, changes in leg
colour, or development of ulcers.
Learning point in this station
This station reinforces the importance of recognising diabetic peripheral neuropathy
in patients with type 2 diabetes who present with burning or tingling pain in both feet,
especially when it begins to affect mobility.
It also highlights the need to consider and rule out other causes of neuropathy, such
as vitamin B12 deficiency, which can occur as a side effect of long-term metformin
use, as well as thyroid dysfunction or other metabolic conditions.

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset of hair loss, whether it was sudden or gradual, and which
areas of the scalp are affected
• Ask whether the hair loss is due to shedding or thinning. Note: shedding is
more suggestive of telogen effluvium or iron deficiency anaemia. Thinning is
more suggestive of conditions such as female androgenetic alopecia,
polycystic ovary syndrome or hypothyroidism
• Ask about patches of hair loss, scarring, redness, rashes, or scaling of the
scalp
• Ask about the hair care routine, including any recent changes in hairstyle,
traction, heat treatments, or use of new hair products
• Ask about family history of hair loss
• Ask whether the patient has started any new prescribed medications or is
using over-the-counter treatments or supplements

delivery, and whether the birth experience was traumatic
• Ask about dietary changes or significant weight loss or gain

-- 771 of 1282 --

770
• Ask about the menstrual cycle, whether periods have returned, and if they are
heavy, as this may contribute to iron deficiency anaemia
• Ask about social history, including smoking, alcohol intake, home
circumstances, mood, and psychological wellbeing
• Ask about the impact of hair loss on the patient’s confidence and daily life
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a diagnosis of likely postpartum telogen effluvium
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you, Sarah, for explaining everything so clearly. From what you have
described, this sounds like something we call postpartum telogen effluvium. Have
you heard of this before?
It is a very common type of hair shedding that can happen a few months after
pregnancy, particularly after a difficult or stressful delivery, like the one you
experienced. Our hair normally sheds every day, but this is usually not noticeable.
During periods of stress or hormonal changes, more hair than usual can shed at the
same time, which is why it becomes more noticeable. Are you following so far?
I know you were concerned that ramipril might be the cause. Hair loss is listed as a
possible side effect of ramipril, but based on the timing and pattern of your hair loss,
this fits much more with telogen effluvium.
The good news is that this condition is self-limiting and usually improves on its own
over time without any treatment. The safest approach at the moment would be to
watch and wait and see how things progress. If the hair loss does not improve or
gets worse, we can then review the ramipril and consider switching to an alternative
medication. What are your thoughts about that? Does that seem reasonable to you?
To be thorough, I would like to arrange some blood tests, including checks for iron
levels and thyroid function, as low iron or thyroid problems can sometimes contribute
to hair loss. I would also like to see you face to face to examine your scalp and hair
and, if needed, perform a simple hair pull test to help confirm the diagnosis. Would
that be alright with you?
In the meantime, some people find it helpful to use styling techniques, hair sprays or
hairpieces to improve the cosmetic appearance while the hair regrows.
I suggest we review this again in two to three months to see how things are
improving. If the hair loss becomes significantly worse, you notice patchy loss, scalp
changes, or you have any new concerns before then, please contact us sooner.

-- 772 of 1282 --

771
Management
• Reassure the patient that the presentation is most consistent with postpartum
telogen effluvium, which is common and self-limiting
• Offer a face-to-face review to assess the hair and scalp and to perform a hair
pull test
• Acknowledge that ramipril can be associated with hair loss; however, explain
that in this case the hair loss is more likely due to telogen effluvium
• Advise a watch-and-wait approach, as symptoms often improve
spontaneously over time
• Explain that if hair loss worsens or fails to improve, ramipril can be stopped
and an alternative antihypertensive considered
• Offer blood tests to exclude other contributory causes, including full blood
count, thyroid function tests, ferritin, vitamin B12, and vitamin D
• Discuss cosmetic and supportive measures to improve appearance and
confidence, such as hairstyling techniques, colouring, hairsprays to enhance
the look of the hair, hairpieces, or wigs
• Provide safety-net advice to recontact the practice if hair loss worsens, new
symptoms develop, or she has ongoing concerns
• Arrange follow-up in two to three months to review progress
Learning Point from this Station
Postpartum telogen effluvium is a common and self-limiting condition that typically
presents two to four months after delivery and causes diffuse hair shedding. It is
particularly associated with physiological stress, hormonal changes, and complicated
or traumatic pregnancies.
It is also important to consider medication-related causes of hair loss, especially
when the timing of symptom onset coincides with the initiation of a new drug.
According to the BNF, ramipril and other ACE inhibitors can cause alopecia.
However, in this case, the clinical context is much more consistent with postpartum
telogen effluvium rather than medication-induced hair loss.
This station highlights the importance of negotiation and shared decision-making in
primary care. A watch-and-wait approach is often appropriate, as it avoids
prematurely stopping a medication that is effective and suitable for the patient’s
underlying condition. Clear explanation and reassurance can help address patient
concerns while maintaining safe management.
If the patient strongly prefers not to continue ramipril despite counselling, alternative
antihypertensive options can be considered, including angiotensin receptor blockers
such as losartan or candesartan, or calcium channel blockers such as amlodipine,
taking into account individual risk factors and breastfeeding status where relevant.

-- 773 of 1282 --

772

alopecia/diagnosis/differential-diagnosis/
https://dermnetnz.org/topics/telogen-effluvium

History Taking
• Ask about onset of the cough and if cough is productive of sputum
• Ask about pattern of cough — is it worse at night? And if so, does it affect

• Ask about associated symptoms (fever, vomiting, sore throat, nasal

-- 776 of 1282 --

775
• Ask if he suffers from frequent viral infection.
• Ask about environmental history (smoking- any smokers at home? pets,
damp/moulds)
• Ask for any personal of family history of atopy (eczema, hay fever, asthma)
• Ask about PBIND – pregnancy, birth, immunisation, nutrition, and
development. Immunisation history is already provided, so acknowledge it
instead of asking, as this shows you’ve made use of the information available.
For example: 'Mrs O’Sullivan, I can see that Ryan is up to date with his jabs –
that’s great
• Elicits Mother’s ICE
• Give a diagnosis of potential Asthma
Mrs O’Sullivan, from everything you’ve described — the dry cough, how it gets worse
at night and when Ryan is running around, and the fact that his dad has eczema, this
does sound like it could be asthma.
Have you heard about asthma before?
Asthma is a long-term condition where the small airways in the lungs can become
tight or narrowed from time to time, especially during colds, at night, or after running
around. That’s what causes the coughing or noisy breathing.
What I’d like to do first is see Ryan in person, so I can listen to his chest and
examine him — if that’s okay with you.
In children under the age of 5, we don’t have any reliable tests to confirm asthma
because most children that age are too young to do the breathing tests properly. So
instead, what we usually do is try out some asthma medicines and see if they help,
that helps us understand if this is likely to be asthma.
What we normally do is give two types of inhalers:
• One that helps calm the airways down (used every day for 8 weeks)
• And another that’s used only when symptoms flare up, like during coughing
fits or after exercise.
After 8 weeks, we’ll bring Ryan back in to review how he’s getting on. If things
improve with the inhalers, it gives us a good clue that asthma is likely the cause.
You also mentioned antibiotics and that’s a really good question. In Ryan’s case,
there’s no sign of an infection, so antibiotics wouldn’t help. His symptoms are more
likely due to sensitive airways rather than anything caused by bacteria bug.

-- 777 of 1282 --

776
Management
• Offer face to face to assess chest
• Offer 8-weeks trial of paediatric low-dose inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) as
maintenance therapy (e.g. Clenil modulite MDI 100mcg 2 puffs BD) alongside
a short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) for symptom relief
• Offer a spacer device for delivery of medications
• Advice to keep symptoms diary to help identify any potential triggers and
avoid them where possible.
• Review in 8 weeks' time
• Safety net and advise mum- If patient’s symptoms don’t improve after using
the inhaler as advised, or if he needs the reliever inhaler (blue one) more than
3 times a week, or if he’s still waking at night regularly with cough or breathing
difficulty, then please seek urgent medical advise
• Safety net re red flags as well- advise mum that if patient becomes breathless
even when resting, struggles to speak or eat, his breathing looks hard or fast
with his tummy sucking in, or his lips or face look pale or bluish — please
seek urgent medical attention immediately.
Learning point from this station
How do you differentiate between viral induced wheeze and asthma
You’d look for clues like:
• Are the symptoms only happening during colds? Suggests viral wheeze
• Does the child cough or wheeze after running, laughing, or at night when not
sick? Suggests asthma
• Any family history of asthma, eczema, or hay fever? Supports asthma
diagnosis
• Is the cough getting better between colds or is it always lingering? Persistent
cough outside of illness, asthma more likely
• Any evidence of atopy (eczema, allergies)? Supports asthma
Viral wheeze = occurs in children aged 6 months – 5 years. Wheeze only happens
with colds/chest infection, no symptoms between, no clear atopic features.
Asthma = wheeze is more frequent and has a history of occurrence in the absence
of infection/cold and can be triggered by various things (exercise, night-time), often
has atopic background.

-- 778 of 1282 --

777
Management of viral induced wheeze usually involves using SABA (e.g. salbutamol
via a spacer device) alone but can require nebulisation and hospital admission if
there are severely unwell
NOTE: In children under 5 who present with wheeze and a respiratory infection, it
can be very difficult to tell the difference between an infective asthma flare-up and
viral-induced wheeze. The two often look the same. However, if there is a history of
wheeze occurring without an infection, this makes asthma more likely.

want to use any creams anymore. You want a tablet
Say NO to any other questions asked.

-- 781 of 1282 --

780
Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about onset, duration, and progression of acne.
• Ask about other sites involved apart from the picture he has sent—such as
the other sides of the face affected, chest, or back.
• Ask about presence of scarring or pigmentation (for example- ask if patient
has noticed any scarring or changes in the colour of his skin, like dark spots
or patches, in the affected area)
• Ask about potential triggers – such as face creams, excessive washing of
face, contraceptives and periods (In females)
• Ask about previous treatments tried and compliance (remember acne
treatments are usually tried for at least 3 months to assess effectiveness).
• Ask about impact on mood, self-esteem, and social life.
• Ask about any mental health concerns – especially low mood, self-harm,
suicidal ideation.
• Ask about social history: smoking, alcohol, drug use, school/college,
family/home life
• Ask about family history of acne
• Rule out systemic involvement/ red flags- ask about fever and joint pains
• Ask about ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Give diagnosis of Moderate Acne
Example of explanation to the patient
Kwame, I really appreciate you being so open today, I can see how much this is
affecting your confidence and mood. Before we go further, can I just ask, how much
do you know about acne and what causes it?”
[Pause to check understanding , if limited, continue below]
Acne is a very common skin condition, especially in teenagers and young
adults. It happens when the small glands in your skin produce too much oil,
and this oil mixes with dead skin cells and blocks the pores. That blockage
can lead to spots, blackheads, whiteheads, and sometimes red, inflamed
bumps. It's not caused by dirt, and it's not your fault. It’s mainly down to
hormones, and some people are just more prone to it.
You’ve done the right thing by using the Duac cream for the last 4 months, but since
it hasn’t helped much, we can now try a combination of an oral antibiotic, something

-- 782 of 1282 --

781
like lymecycline, with a different cream called Epiduo (Adapalene plus benzoyl
peroxide) . We’d usually give that a 3-month trial to see how well it works
That said, I understand you're not too keen on using creams anymore and are
interested in trying Accutane, also known as isotretinoin. I also appreciate that your
acne is starting to impact your mental wellbeing and self-esteem. For all these
reasons, I’ll refer you to see a dermatologist. This is because Accutane (isotretinoin)
isn’t prescribed in primary care, as it requires specialist initiation and close
monitoring. The dermatologist will carry out a proper assessment and decide if it’s
the right next step for you
In the meantime, if you’re open to it, we can start the oral antibiotic and cream now
to try and give you some relief while you wait for that appointment. But if you’d rather
wait and speak to the dermatologist first, that’s completely okay too.
Does that all sound okay?
Now, because this has been affecting your mood, I also want to support your
emotional wellbeing. I’d recommend talking therapy, like cognitive behavioural
therapy (CBT). This isn’t about saying something is wrong, it’s about helping you
manage the emotional side of having acne, deal with comments from others, and
rebuild your confidence. It gives you tools to cope with how acne makes you feel.”
You can self-refer to NHS talking therapies, or I can send the referral myself if that’s
easier for you. And we’ll check in with you regularly to see how you’re getting on.
Kwame, you’re not alone in this and you’re doing all the right things. We’ll take this
step by step and support you all the way.
Management
• Inform patient of the next step in management- oral antibiotics for moderate
acne (e.g., lymecycline 408mg OD or doxycycline 100mg OD for 3–6 months)
in combination with a topical non-antibiotic treatment e.g., Epiduo (benzoyl
peroxide plus adapalene) or topical azelaic acid 15% or 20 %.
• Refer to dermatology for consideration of isotretinoin (Accutane) in line with
NICE guidance - NICE says refer people to dermatology if their acne of any
severity, or acne-related scarring, is causing or contributing to persistent
psychological distress or a mental health disorder
• While he is awaiting dermatology referral, if he likes, you can offer oral
antibiotics plus a topical agent (Epiduo cream of Azelaic acid)
• Offer referral to CBT as acne is affecting his mental health

-- 783 of 1282 --

782
• Advise against picking or squeezing spots.
• Advise to avoid excessive cleansing, which can lead to dryness and skin
irritation
• Safety net: return if acne worsens, mood deteriorates/ thoughts of suicide or
self-harm, or side effects to medication (If patient agrees to oral antibiotics
plus a topical cream).
• Follow-up in 8–12 weeks to review response (If patient agrees to oral
antibiotics plus a topical cream).
Lesson from this station
It’s important to tailor acne management to the individual patient. In this case, the
patient did not want to use any more creams, as his roommates at school had been
commenting on his acne, which added to his distress. His acne was clearly causing
psychological impact.
According to NICE guidelines, patients with acne of any severity should be referred
to dermatology if their acne is causing or contributing to persistent psychological
distress or a mental health condition.
As a GP, you should also understand and address the impact on the patient’s mental
health and offer appropriate support, such as talking therapy.
Finally, oral antibiotics shouldneverbe used alonefor acne. NICE advises against
oral antibiotic monotherapy because of the risk of antibiotic resistance — they must
always be combined with a suitable topical agent.

Marking scheme
History Taking
• Begin by exploring the mechanism of injury—clarify the details while
demonstrating that you’ve acknowledged the information already provided.
For instance, you might say: 'Rachel, I understand you broke your wrist after a
fall, is that correct? Could you tell me a bit more about what happened?This
demonstrates to the examiner that you’ve reviewed the notes and are
effectively utilizing the information already provided
• Recognize the inconsistencies in her story and approach them with genuine
curiosity and a desire to understand more. For example- Thank you Rachel
for sharing that with me. I did notice a slight difference between what you’ve
said — that you had a fall in the garden — and what was written in the A&E
notes, which mentioned it happened while walking your dog. I completely
understand that sometimes details can come out differently, especially after a
stressful event and it can also be a sign that someone might be going through
a difficult time. I just want you to know that you’re in a safe space here, and
we’re here to help in any way we can, so feel free to open up, is that okay?
• Ask about other forms of domestic abuse aside physical abuse which she has
already told you including

785
• Sexual abuse (ask if her partner forces her anything sexual that she didn’t
want to or didn’t feel comfortable with?)

• Financial abuse (Ask if her partner controls her money or stops her from
having access to her own finances)
• Controlling/coercive behaviour (Ask if her partner tries to control where she
goes, who she sees, or what she does)
• Ask if she feels safe at home
• Ask about her wrist — Ask for compartment syndrome red flags- swelling,
redness, numbness, worsening pain etc.

Explain to patient

difficult that was for you, and I want you to know that you’ve done absolutely the right
thing by telling someone.
What you’ve described is domestic abuse — and this is not your fault. You don’t
have to go through this alone, and there’s help available.
If you don’t feel safe at home, we can help you arrange a safe place to stay. There
are organisations like Women’s Aid that specialise in supporting women in your
situation — they can offer emergency accommodation, practical help, and emotional

to deal with domestic abuse and can help keep you safe.
We can also arrange for you to speak to a specialist domestic abuse worker, they
can help you make a safety plan, give you advice on housing, legal options, or
financial support, support you through any police or court processes, if you choose to

786
Everything is done confidentially and at your own pace — you’re always in control of

I’d also recommend considering talking therapy. It can give you a safe, confidential
space to process what’s happened, work through your emotions, and rebuild your
confidence.
You are not alone, and you don’t have to make any decisions today. But I’ll support
you every step of the way and together we can plan what feels safe and right for you
Rachel, you mentioned you're still struggling with pain in your wrist and that’s
understandable given the injury. You can try taking ibuprofen, which you can get
over the counter. It’s an anti-inflammatory and should help with both pain and
swelling. It tends to work well, but I’d recommend taking it with food, as it can
sometimes irritate the stomach.

• Numbness or tingling in your hand or fingers
• Changes in colour (like your hand going pale or blue)
• Worsening pain, even after taking medication
• Or increasing swelling in the hand or arm
…please seek urgent medical advice. These can be signs of something called
compartment syndrome — it’s a rare complication where pressure builds up in the
muscles and can stop blood flow. It’s serious, but catching it early makes a big
difference.
Also, since your right hand is affected and you use it for work as a cleaner, I’ll do a
sick note for you for the next two weeks to give you time to recover properly. We can
review things after that and see how you’re doing.
Management
• Offer referral to safeguarding team/specialist domestic abuse worker- if
patient wishes
• Offer referral to domestic abuse agencies like Women’s aid that can help with
emergency shelter, finances and support.
• Advise patient to get Ibuprofen over the counter for managing pain
• Offer mental health support or talking therapy if appropriate.

-- 788 of 1282 --

787
• Provide fit note for time off work for initial 2 weeks and then assess
• Safety net: ensure the patient knows she can return anytime and how to

• Safety net about compartment syndrome – advise that if she notices any

Learning Point from this Station:
Always be alert to inconsistencies in the history, especially around injuries — they
may be a red flag for domestic abuse. Create a safe, non-judgmental space to
explore this sensitively.
Provide holistic care: address the physical injury, offer appropriate pain relief, ensure
safety netting, issue sick notes where needed, and crucially, offer support for
emotional and safeguarding needs including referral to specialist domestic abuse
services and talking therapy
