SAY NO TO ANY OTHER QUESTIONS ASKED
Marking Scheme
History Taking
• Ask about nature of headache: onset, location, severity, pattern
• Ask about associated symptoms: visual changes, epigastric pain, nausea,
vomiting
• Ask about red flags- weakness in any part of the body, slurred speech,
abnormal jerky movement/seizures
• Ask about fetal movements
• Ask about bleeding and discharge down below
• Ask about if she has done any blood pressure readings at home
• Ask about family history of pre-eclampsia and previous history of issues with
blood pressure
• Ask about medication use during pregnancy
• Ask about social history-smoking, alcohol, home situation and if she has
support at home
• Ask about ICE-idea, concerns, expectations
• Make a diagnosis of pre-eclampsia
Example of explanation to the patient
Priya, thank you for sharing all of that. I know how uncomfortable and worrying these
symptoms must be, especially during pregnancy.
Although you've had migraines in the past, your current symptoms, the headache,
blurred vision, upper tummy pain, and the high blood pressure reading, raise
concern for a condition called pre-eclampsia. This is a condition that can happen in
the second half of pregnancy, and it affects the blood pressure and, in some cases,
the function of organs like the liver or kidneys.
Your reading of 150/90 mmHg, along with these symptoms, means we need to take
this seriously. Even though stress might be playing a part, it’s important that we rule
out anything more serious. It wouldn’t be safe for us to assume this is just a migraine
without checking further.
I’d like you to go to the maternity assessment unit today to be seen more urgently.

-- 791 of 1282 --

790
Just to be clear, I’d advise you not to drive yourself, is anyone at home with you right
now? If your husband is around, it would be ideal for him to go with you. If anything
gets worse on the way, for example, if the headache becomes severe, the vision
worsens, or you start to feel unwell, please stop and call 999 immediately.
Once you get to the maternity unit, the team will carry out a set of checks to assess
you and the baby. These usually include:
• A repeat blood pressure check
• A urine test to check for protein
• Some blood tests to check your liver and kidney function and other markers
• And they’ll also monitor your baby to make sure everything is okay
Depending on what they find, they may offer medication or other treatment to
prevent any complications.
If everything looks normal, that’s reassuring, and we can then safely look at better
ways to manage your headache. But we do need to get this checked out properly
today.
Does that all make sense? Are you happy to head over now with your husband’s
support?
Management Plan
• Urgent referral to the Maternity Assessment Unit for same-day assessment
• Advise not to drive – confirm if someone (e.g., husband) is available to
accompany her
• Safety net and advise- If symptoms worsen enroute (e.g., severe headache,
visual changes, vomiting, feeling faint), advise to stop and call 999
immediately
• Inform patient what will happen at the maternity unit, she will be offered:
repeat blood pressure checks, urine dipstick test to check for protein, blood
tests (FBC, U&Es, LFTs, coagulation profile), fetal monitoring (CTG or
Doppler depending on gestation and unit protocol)
• If pre-eclampsia is confirmed, team will initiate appropriate antihypertensive
and supportive treatment
• Avoid prescribing analgesics at this stage until pre-eclampsia is excluded
Learning point from this station

-- 792 of 1282 --

791
In pregnant women, headache should never be dismissed lightly. While pre-
eclampsia is a key consideration from 20 weeks' gestation, always keep in mind
other serious causes like cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, which can present
with persistent or severe headache, especially if it doesn’t respond to usual pain
relief or is associated with neurological symptoms.
The key lesson here is in pregnancy, a headache that is new, persistent, or unusual
must always prompt a careful and urgent assessment — assuming it’s “just a
migraine” can risk missing serious, potentially life-threatening conditions.

Patient’s Name: Adam Reynolds

Dear Doctor,
Mr. Adam Reynolds was recently admitted via the Acute Medical Unit with a 3-day
history of bloody diarrhoea and crampy abdominal pain. He was initially resuscitated
successfully, with supportive care and intravenous fluids.
A colonoscopy was performed during admission, which revealed left-sided ulcerative

inflammation, friability, and contact bleeding consistent with a moderate flare of his
known ulcerative colitis.
He was commenced on intravenous corticosteroids, to which he responded well.
Following clinical improvement, he was stepped down to oral prednisolone 40mg
once daily, with instructions to taper the dose by no more than 5mg every 5 to 7
days, depending on clinical response and under outpatient supervision.
He was also restarted on mesalazine (Salofalk®) 500mg three times daily for
maintenance therapy. Mr. Reynolds was provided with verbal and written information
on medication adherence and the importance of attending follow-up.
At discharge, he was given the contact details of the IBD Clinical Nurse Specialists
and advised to get in touch should he experience worsening symptoms or require
support between appointments.
He will be followed up in our gastroenterology outpatient clinic to monitor his
progress and response to treatment.
Please do not hesitate to contact us if further information is required.
Yours sincerely,
Dr. Rajesh Patel MBBS, MRCP (UK)

-- 797 of 1282 --

796
Learning point from this station
When a patient presents with a suspected flare of inflammatory bowel disease
(IBD) , such as ulcerative colitis or Crohn’s disease, the first step is to assess the
need for urgent admission.
Features suggesting possible hospital admission include:
• High-frequency bloody diarrhoea (typically more than 6–8 episodes/day)
• Nocturnal diarrhoea
• Fever, signs of dehydration, tachycardia, or hypotension
• Severe abdominal pain or suspected intestinal obstruction
• Palpitations, dizziness, or significant fatigue
• Raised inflammatory markers, anaemia, or evidence of malnutrition (BMI
<18.5, or sudden weight loss)
In remote consultations, a detailed symptom history becomes crucial. Asking about
the number of stools per day, presence of blood, associated systemic symptoms,
and ability to stay hydrated helps guide whether the patient is safe to manage in the
community or needs urgent hospital care.
If the patient is stable and does not require admission, we can manage them in
primary care if they are under a shared-care agreement with gastroenterology. If no
agreement is in place, clinical judgement is needed. Ideally, we seek same-day
advice from their specialist team. However, if this is not possible and the patient is at
risk of deterioration, it may be appropriate to initiate a short course of oral steroids
while arranging urgent follow-up.
This reflects NICE guidance, which says: “Consider arranging an urgent specialist
gastroenterology review appointment or seeking specialist advice. Consider
prescribing drug treatment for disease flare-ups according to the person’s shared-
care agreement, such as starting a time-limited course of oral corticosteroids, if
appropriate, whilst awaiting specialist review.”

-- 798 of 1282 --

Patient’s Story
You are Daniel Atkinson, a 42-year-old man. You called your GP this morning
because you noticed that one side of your face (left) is not working properly. When
you looked in the mirror, your mouth looked droopy, and when you tried to smile,
only one side moved. You’ve also realised you can’t close your left eye fully.
If asked: No slurred speech, No limb weakness or numbness, No visual disturbance,
dizziness, or loss of balance, No facial pain, rash, or earache, No fever, headache,
or neck stiffness
You can raise your eyebrows on the right side but not the left
You have never experienced this before
Say NO to any other symptoms asked
Social history: You do not smoke, you drink alcohol occasionally; you live alone at
home, and you work as a lorry driver
Ideas: You’re scared this might be a stroke
Concerns: You’re worried it could be something serious and permanent, and you do
not know if you can go to work with this or if this affects your ability to drive
Expectations: You want to know what’s happening and whether it can be treated

-- 799 of 1282 --

798
Marking scheme
History
• Ask about onset and progression of facial weakness
• Ask about side affected and specific muscles involved (forehead, eye, mouth)
• Ask about eye symptoms (dryness, inability to blink or close eye)
• Ask specifically if they’re unable to raise the eyebrow/ wrinkle their forehead
on the affected side, this helps differentiate Bell’s Palsy from a stroke. Bell’s
Palsy is a lower motor neurone weakness that affects the whole face,
including the forehead, so the person can’t raise their eyebrow or wrinkle
their forehead. In stroke (upper motor neurone), the forehead is usually
spared, the person can still raise their eyebrow. This is why we say, “upper
spares upper”
• Ask about difficulty chewing, dry mouth or drooling of saliva
• Ask about any hearing issues such as everyday sounds seeming
uncomfortably/unusually loud or painful, even though they don’t seem to
bother other people (Hyperacusis)
• Ask about red flags that may point to a stroke: limb weakness, slurred speech,
dizziness, headache
• Ask about fever, rash, ear pain and hearing loss that may indicate herpes
zoster oticus infection (Ramsay Hunt syndrome)
• Ask for any lumps or bumps around the face, weight loss, night sweats that
may indicate parotid tumour
• Ask about social history-smoking, alcohol, occupation
• Elicit ICE
• Make a diagnosis of Bell's Palsy
Explain to patient as below
Daniel, thank you for explaining everything so clearly. From what you’ve described;
the drooping on one side of your face, difficulty smiling, and not being able to close
your eye, especially with the forehead also affected, this sounds like a condition
called Bell’s Palsy.
This happens when one of the nerves that controls the muscles on one side of your
face becomes temporarily irritated or inflamed. It’s quite different from a stroke, and
the fact that you’re not having any problems with your arms, legs, speech, or balance
is really reassuring. In strokes, people usually can still move their forehead and raise
their eyebrow, which doesn’t seem to be the case for you.

-- 800 of 1282 --

799
The next step is for us to see you in person, so that we can examine the muscles
and nerves in your face and the rest of your body, just to be thorough and make sure
we’re not missing anything else.
If everything fits with Bell’s Palsy, we’ll start you on steroid tablets, prednisolone
50mg once a day for 10 days — as early treatment can help speed up recovery.
It’s also really important to take care of the eye on the affected side, especially if you
can’t close it fully. I’ll prescribe some lubricating eye drops to use frequently during
the day, and an eye ointment for night-time to keep the eye moist. These are
available on prescription or can also be bought over the counter.
If you can’t close the eye properly when you sleep, you should gently tape it shut at
bedtime using microporous tape. And when you’re outdoors, wearing sunglasses
can help protect the eye from dust and dryness.
You also mentioned driving, you should still be able to drive as long as your eyesight
isn’t affected, and you feel safe. But if you’re unsure, it’s a good idea to let the DVLA
know about your condition. They’ll be able to give you clear advice based on your
situation.
We’ll arrange a follow-up in 2 to 3 weeks to see how you’re getting on. Most people
make a full recovery within a few weeks to 3 months. If you’re not improving after 3
weeks, we can arrange for a specialist to review things further.
Does that all sound okay? Would you like me to go over any part of that again?
Management
• Arrange a face-to-face review to examine the facial muscles and check for
any other concerning signs.
• Start prednisolone 50mg once daily for 10 days, as patient presented within
72 hours of symptom onset.
• Offer lubricating eye drops for frequent daytime use and eye ointment for
night-time protection.
• Advise the patient to tape the eye closed at night using microporous tape if it
doesn’t fully close.
• Recommend wearing sunglasses outdoors to protect the eye.
• Reassure that they can continue to drive if vision is not affected but suggest
informing the DVLA for formal guidance.
• Book a follow-up appointment in 2–3 weeks to assess recovery.
• If no improvement after 3 weeks, arrange referral for further specialist
assessment.

-- 801 of 1282 --

800
• Provide clear safety-netting – Advise that If symptoms suddenly get worse, or
if he develops weakness in his arms or legs, slurred speech, or balance
problems, seek urgent medical help – call 111 or 999.
• Also advise that if his eye becomes red, painful, or his vision changes, get
urgent review
Learning point from this station
The key lesson from this station is that Bell’s Palsy is a lower motor neurone facial
weakness affecting the entire side of the face, including the forehead, and can
usually be safely distinguished from stroke through careful remote history. Early
treatment with steroids within 72 hours improves the chance of full recovery. It's
important to assess for red flags, arrange timely follow-up, and give clear advice on
eye care, especially when the eye does not close fully. A face-to-face review is
important to confirm the diagnosis and exclude other causes. If symptoms do not
improve within 3 weeks or do not fully resolve by 3 months, referral to neurology or
ENT should be considered. Any ocular symptoms at any stage require urgent referral
to ophthalmology.

Example of explanation to the son
Thanks for sharing everything so openly today, Kevin. I can hear how much you care
for your mum, and how hard you are working to support her at home. It is clear that
you are doing your very best in a really challenging situation.
From what you have described, it does sound like your mum’s dementia has
progressed. The fact that she is mostly bedbound now, not taking her medication
properly, and no longer recognises you, even though you have been her main carer,
are signs that we may be looking at the more advanced stage of Dementia. That is
understandably very difficult to come to terms with, and I want you to know you are
not alone in this. Are you with me so far?
I also wanted to mention something about her medications. I completely understand
that you started crushing her tablets because you were trying to help her, especially
as she has been struggling to swallow. But just so you are aware, some medications
are not safe to crush, as doing so can reduce how well they work or even cause side
effects. So, it is really important that any changes to how medicines are given, like
crushing or mixing with food, are only done after speaking to a GP or pharmacist.
Does that make sense?
In terms of her medications, based on what you have told me, we would be looking
at stopping some of the non-essential ones. For example, alendronic acid, which is
for bone health, may no longer be helpful, it needs to be taken while sitting upright,
and because your mum is now mostly bedbound, continuing it could actually cause
tummy irritation or even harm.
Other medications we might consider stopping include her cholesterol tablet, aspirin,
calcium supplement, and her stomach protection tablet. Are you with me so far?
For the more essential medications, like her diabetes treatment, we can look at
alternatives, such as liquid formulations or even simplifying the medication list so that
it is safer and easier to manage at home.
I will speak to the pharmacy team and arrange a proper medication review to make
sure we are doing what is safest and most appropriate for her current stage of
health.
Also, based on everything you have shared today, I think it would be helpful to
involve the adult safeguarding team, not because anyone has done anything wrong,
but to make sure that both you and your mum are getting the right level of support at
home.
Looking after someone with advanced dementia can be incredibly demanding, and it
is completely understandable to feel overwhelmed at times. The safeguarding team
can assess what additional help might be available, look at whether more care is
needed, and hopefully help ease some of the strain on you as a carer.

• Inform the son that we will consider stopping non-essential medications,

• Gently advise the son, in a non-judgemental way, not to crush or hide

• Refer to adult safeguarding due to multiple concerns: the son is the sole carer

-- 807 of 1282 --

806
Learning point from This Station
This station highlights the importance of recognising that difficulty with swallowing,
eating, and managing daily activities can be part of the end-stage features of
dementia. It also reinforces the need to review and reduce medication burden, as
many medications may no longer be appropriate or beneficial in advanced dementia.
An important part of the assessment is considering common differentials for new or
worsening swallowing difficulties. These include frailty, progression of dementia,
and medication-related causes such as improper administration of alendronic
acid, which can cause oesophageal irritation or erosion leading to swallowing
problems.
In end-stage dementia, it is crucial to understand that swallowing difficulties are
mainly due to neurological dysfunction, affecting the brain’s coordination of
swallowing muscles. Patients may struggle to initiate a swallow, even if they are not
complaining of pain or obstruction. Whereas in cancer-related dysphagia, patients
often report food sticking, pain on swallowing (odynophagia), and mechanical
blockage.
Another key learning point is the importance of deprescribing, stopping non-essential
medications to focus on comfort and quality of life.
Finally, an essential takeaway is the need to educate carers in a supportive and non-
judgemental way. Crushing medications without medical advice can reduce their
effectiveness or increase the risk of harm. Involving the wider MDT, including
pharmacists, SALT, and district nurses, ensures a safer, more person-centred
approach to care.

-- 808 of 1282 --

-- 810 of 1282 --

809
• Give the patient an address phrase to remember (5 components) --
Example: John, Smith, 42, High Street, Bedford. (Scoring= Correct: 0
points. Incorrect: 3 points)
• About what time is it (within one hour)? (Scoring =Correct: 0 points.
Incorrect: 3 points)
• Count backwards from 20 to 1. (Scoring= Correct: 0 points 1 error: 2 points
More than 1 error: 4 points)
• Say the months of the year in reverse order- for example December,
November, October and so on (Scoring= Correct: 0 points, 1 error: 2 points,
more than 1 error: 4 points)
• Repeat the address phrase from earlier (Scoring= Correct- all 5 correct= 0
points, 1 error: 2 points, 2 errors: 4 points, 3 errors: 6 points, 4 errors: 8
points, All wrong: 10 points)
The higher the score, the greater the level of cognitive impairment
• 0–7: Normal cognitive function
• 8–9: Mild cognitive impairment
• 10 or more: Greater likelihood of significant cognitive impairment or dementia
— further assessment recommended
Please note:In an exam setting, we believe that you are not expected to recall the
exact scoring breakdown of the 6CIT. What’s important is to understand that multiple
errors (2 or more), particularly in memory recall (Remembering the address) or
reverse tasks (Counting from 20 backwards or saying the months of the year in
reverse), should prompt suspicion of cognitive decline/impairment.
Importantly, as highlighted by NICE guidance, you should not rule out dementia
solely because someone scores normally on a cognitive assessment tool. This
means that if the clinical history is suggestive, for example, if the patient or a family
member reports progressive memory loss, difficulties in daily function, or personality
changes, you can and should still refer for further assessment, even if their cognitive
test score appears within the normal range.
As GPs, we do not make formal diagnosis of dementia in primary care; this is the
role of secondary care. Our role is to screen for impairment, document findings, and
refer appropriately to a Memory Clinic or the Older Adults Mental Health Team.
Make a diagnosis of cognitive impairment and possible dementia
Example of explanation to the patient

810
David, thank you for sharing this. I can hear how much this is affecting your life,
especially with work and your future plans. It’s completely understandable that you’re
feeling worried.
From what you've told me; this could be related to a cognitive problem, that means
changes in how your memory and thinking are working. There are many possible
causes for this, and one of them is dementia, which I know you’re worried about.
But before we make any assumptions, we need to do some further tests to
understand what’s going on. This will include some blood tests to check for things
like vitamin levels, thyroid function, and other general health markers. I’ll also check
your blood pressure, as that can affect memory too.
I would like to refer you to a memory clinic, they’re specialists who will do a full
memory assessment, which may include an MRI or CT scan of your brain, and they’ll
look more closely at how your memory and thinking are functioning. This will help us
work out what’s causing these symptoms and what we can do to help.
While we wait for those tests, there are some things that can help your brain health:
gentle exercise, socialising, puzzles or memory games, and writing things down in a
diary or using your phone calendar to help keep track of daily events.
David, you mentioned that your memory problems have started to affect your work,
and I’m really sorry to hear about the recent incident at Tesco. I can imagine how
stressful that must have been.
While we’re still working out what might be causing these changes, it’s a good idea
to speak to your HR department to see if they can offer any temporary adjustments
to your duties, perhaps something with less responsibility or that relies less on
memory, just to take some pressure off while we investigate further.
If you would like, I’m happy to write a GP letter or fit note to support that
conversation, sometimes having something in writing can make it easier to explain
the situation.
You’ve done exactly the right thing by coming in today, and we’ll continue to support
you step by step as we go forward.
David, based on what you've told me, it doesn’t sound like your memory is affecting
your driving at the moment, so you may still be able to drive. However, it’s important
to let the DVLA know about your symptoms, they’ll review the information and give
you proper guidance on whether it’s safe for you to continue driving. They’ll make the
decision based on your safety and that of others, and we’ll support you through that
process if needed.
I’ll make sure to write down everything we’ve discussed today in a paper —
including the next steps and who we’re referring you to. That way, you can
look back at it later or share it with your wife if needed.

Lesson from This Station
This station highlights the importance of recognising early cognitive decline,
especially when it begins to affect a patient’s daily life or work. A key takeaway is the
need to address the impact on employment. In this case, offering support through a
GP letter, fit note, or occupational health referral can help the patient access
workplace adjustments and reduce stress while investigations are ongoing.

In an exam setting, you're not expected to recall the exact scoring of the 6CIT. What
matters is recognising that multiple errors, particularly in memory recall or reverse
tasks, should raise suspicion of cognitive impairment.
Importantly, as NICE guidance states, a normal score on a cognitive test does not
rule out dementia. If the history suggests progressive memory loss or functional
decline, you should still refer for further assessment.
GPs do not diagnose dementia , our role is to screen, document, and refer
appropriately to a Memory Clinic or Older Adults Mental Health Team for formal
assessment and management.

Marking scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about onset, duration, location and character of the pain
• Ask for radiation (leg pain, sciatica)?
• Ask for red flag symptoms that may indicate cauda equina like saddle
anaesthesia, bladder/bowel control, leg weakness.
• Ask for red flag symptoms that may indicate malignancy/spinal infection like
weight loss, fever, night sweats
• Ask about history of trauma
• Ask about impact on work, daily life, sleep, mood
• Ask about previous investigations/treatments for this back pain
• Clarify dose/frequency of codeine use (If the patient is on high doses or been
on medication for a long time, you may need to plan a gradual reduction
rather than stopping suddenly, to avoid withdrawal symptoms)
• Ask what happens if he misses a dose of his codeine?
• Ask about social history: smoking, alcohol, sensitively about past or current
recreational drug use, occupation, living condition
• Ask about mental health (low mood, stress)
• Chronic mechanical low back pain likely with codeine dependence
Example of explanation to the patient
Stanislaw, thank you for explaining everything so clearly. From what you’ve told me;
it sounds like you’ve been living with long-term back pain for over a year now. That
can be really frustrating, and it’s something we often see in people after road
accidents.
When back pain lasts for a long time, it’s often because the muscles, joints, and
nerves in the back become sensitive. It doesn't always mean something serious is
wrong, but the pain can still have a big impact on your life and how you feel day to
day.
I also appreciate that codeine has helped you manage the pain in the past. I can
prescribe you a small supply of codeine today, but I’d like us to make a plan to
gradually reduce and stop it. That’s because, when codeine is used regularly over
time, especially in higher doses, the body can get used to it, and it may stop working
as well. Some people then find they need to keep increasing the dose, which can be
dangerous.

-- 816 of 1282 --

815
Codeine can affect the liver, bowel, and even cause changes in mood, sleep, and
memory. It can also become something the body relies on, and coming off it
suddenly can cause withdrawal symptoms. That’s why it’s safest to wean off it
slowly, with support.
Does that sound okay to you?
Alongside this, there are other things we can try to help manage the pain. One of
them is physiotherapy. I know you’ve tried physio in Poland, and it didn’t help, but
sometimes a different approach with a therapist here, including exercises tailored to
your body and lifestyle, can make a difference, if you're open to giving it another go.
We can also consider other painkillers, like naproxen or ibuprofen, which can help if
the pain becomes unbearable. I’d need to check that you’ve never had any stomach
problems or asthma before prescribing them and may give you something to protect
your stomach if needed.
Another option that helps some people is CBT (cognitive behavioural therapy) —
talking therapy. It’s not because the pain is in your head, far from it. But it can help
you learn ways to cope with long-term pain, reduce stress around it, and feel more in
control. It’s something we can offer if you’re open to trying it.
We can also refer you to our social prescriber who can help with anything you might
need as you settle in here in the UK and in our local community, including local
services, community support, and help understanding the NHS.
I’ll arrange a review in 3–4 weeks so we can see how you’re doing and adjust things
if we need to. How does that sound?
Management
• Offer face-to-face review to assess back (including reflexes, sensation,
power)
• You can also use the STarT Back questionnaire to assess risk and tailor
treatment
• Prescribe a small supply of codeine with a clear plan to gradually reduce use
over time
• Discuss the risks of long-term codeine use, including dependence, tolerance,
and harm to liver, bowel, mood, and mental health
• Offer referral to physiotherapy, acknowledging previous experience but
encouraging a different approach tailored to his needs
• Offer CBT (talking therapy) to help manage the emotional and mental impact
of living with long-term pain

-- 817 of 1282 --

816
• During weaning phase- You can consider prescribing naproxen or ibuprofen
to use PRN as a safer alternative; first check for history of stomach problems
or asthma, and add a PPI if needed
• Provide information on self-help resources (e.g. Versus Arthritis website for
exercises and pain management advice)
• Refer to social prescriber for support with understanding the NHS, and access
to community resources as patient is new to the NHS and the UK
• Arrange a follow-up appointment in 3–4 weeks to assess progress and adjust
the plan if needed
• Safety-net: advise to seek urgent help if he develops leg weakness,
numbness, difficulty passing urine, or if the pain worsens suddenly
Lesson from This Station
This station highlights the importance of recognising the risks of long-term opioid
use, particularly in patients with chronic (long-term) non-cancer pain like low back
pain. A key learning point is to identify when someone may be at risk of dependence,
even if they don't display withdrawal symptoms, and to approach the conversation
sensitively and non-judgementally.
Rather than abruptly stopping the medication, the aim is to develop a clear and
supported plan to gradually wean the patient off opioids, while introducing safer,
evidence-based alternatives such as:
• Non-opioid pain relief (e.g. NSAIDs like naproxen) PRN
• Physiotherapy tailored to the patient’s needs
• CBT and psychological support, which are proven to help people manage
long-term pain
• Social prescribing, especially important for newly arrived or socially vulnerable
patients
It also reinforces the GP’s role in offering education, promoting self-management,
using resources like Versus Arthritis, and ensuring regular review and follow-up.
This station is not just about refusing a request, it’s about recognising pain, risk, and
vulnerability, and working collaboratively with the patient to build a safe, respectful,
and sustainable treatment plan.

Example of explanation to the patient
Leah, thank you for being honest about what’s happened. I can hear how committed
you are to staying on track, and I know this situation is really distressing for you.
Because methadone is a tightly controlled medication, and you're under the care of
the drug and rehabilitation team, I’m not able to prescribe it safely from the GP
practice without speaking to them first. This is to make sure your treatment stays
coordinated and safe.
The best course of action now is to contact your recovery team directly as soon as
possible, they are best placed to advise and provide you with the support you need.
If you’ve been unable to reach them, I can help by trying to contact them on your
behalf today.
If you do start to feel unwell or begin having withdrawal symptoms — like sweating,
shaking, stomach cramps, or feeling restless — it’s important not to try and manage
this yourself. Please do not use any street drugs to cope, as the risk of overdose is
very high, especially if your body is no longer used to heroin or other opioids. Even a
small amount could be dangerous or life-threatening.
If withdrawal symptoms become severe, and you're struggling to manage, the safest
thing to do is to go to your local A&E/ seek urgent medical care where they can
assess you and offer appropriate support.
Have you been given any information before about harm reduction — things like
safer use, avoiding injecting, and what to do in an overdose situation? If not, we can
go through that together or connect you with someone who can.
And because you’ve got a young child at home and there’s been an accidental spill
of methadone, I will need to make a referral to our safeguarding team. This isn’t
about judgement or blame; it’s a standard step we take to make sure both children

-- 821 of 1282 --

820
and parents are supported and safe. It also helps ensure you have the right help
around you going forward.
Leah, while we’re going through everything today, you mentioned that you smoke. I
just wanted to gently bring it up because stopping smoking, even after many years,
can really benefit your health, especially your lungs, your heart, and even your
mental wellbeing.
It can also make a big difference to your recovery journey. People often find their
energy improves, they sleep better, and it saves quite a bit of money too.
There’s absolutely no pressure — I know now might not feel like the right time. But
when you're ready, we have free stop smoking support available locally. They can
offer advice, support, and even treatments that help with cravings. If you’d like, I can
give you some information, and you can decide in your own time.
We’ll do all we can to support you. You're doing the right thing by asking for help,
let’s take it one step at a time together.
Management
• Do not prescribe methadone from the GP practice. Avoid being pressured into
issuing opioid prescriptions in an unplanned or emergency manner to manage
withdrawal symptoms
• Explain to the patient that: Methadone must be prescribed by their specialist
or drug and alcohol recovery team.
• Advise patient to contact her recovery team directly as soon as possible if
reachable
• You can also attempt to contact her named keyworker or local drug and
rehabilitation team to help with prescription
• Offer smoking cessation advise
• If withdrawal symptoms occur, advise do not attempt to self-manage with
street drugs or opioids
• Warn about risk of overdose if street opioids are used — especially if injected
due to loss of tolerance
• Advise to go to the nearest A&E or seek urgent medical advice if symptoms
become severe (e.g., vomiting, agitation, tremors, or risk of relapse)
• Check if patient has received harm reduction education, including Dangers of
injecting, overdose prevention, naloxone access etc. If not, offer referral to a
keyworker or recovery support worker for this

-- 822 of 1282 --

821
• Make a child safeguarding referral due to methadone spill in a household with
a child present — explain this is standard and supportive, not punitive. Note
that its standard practice to refer to children safeguarding whenever a child is
living in a home where drug use or treatment is involved. The aim is to ensure
the child’s safety and well-being, and to assess for any risk of neglect.
• Safety net: advise to attend A&E or call 111 if feeling unwell, in withdrawal, or
at risk of relapse
Lesson from This Station
This station highlights the importance of balancing empathy with safety when
managing patients on opioid substitution therapy. You must empathise with the
patient's distress, acknowledge that withdrawal is unpleasant, and offer support, but
also understand that prescribing methadone in general practice for acute withdrawal
is not appropriate.
Instead, refer to or liaise with the local drug and alcohol service, and if withdrawal
symptoms become severe, advise the patient to attend A&E for safe management.
Use this opportunity to offer harm reduction advice, and do not feel pressured to
prescribe — your role is to support safely, not to enable risk.
It's essential to recognise safeguarding concerns here, especially when a child lives
in a household where a parent misuses or is prescribed controlled drugs. Even
accidental spills may raise concerns around supervision, access, and potential
neglect.

like both at home and at school, and whether they’re having a significant impact on

-- 825 of 1282 --

824
You mentioned that his older brother isn’t like this, and I can see why that might
make you worry. But it’s worth remembering that no two children are the same, even
within the same family. Children develop and express themselves differently. Some
are naturally more active or excitable, while others are more calm or quiet. That
doesn’t always mean something is wrong, just that every child has their own
temperament and pace.

which is specifically designed for parents who are managing children with high
energy or possible ADHD traits. Many parents find it helpful, not just for practical tips,

Management

school begins to notice learning difficulties, attention issues, or challenging

-- 826 of 1282 --

825
Learning Point from This Station
This station highlights the importance of taking parental concerns seriously while
recognising that not all high-energy behaviour is pathological.
A diagnosis like ADHD requires that symptoms are persistent, occur in multiple
settings (e.g. home and school), and significantly impact day-to-day functioning —
which is not the case here.
It also reminds us to acknowledge comparisons with siblings and gently reassure
that every child develops differently.
The GP’s role is to offer reassurance, support, and structured follow-up through
watchful waiting, while encouraging parenting support strategies and maintaining
close communication with school. This approach helps avoid premature labelling
while still keeping the child under careful review.

827

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask when the patient first noticed the sensation of a lump in the throat.
• Ask whether the sensation is constant or if it comes and goes (intermittent
symptoms are more typical of globus sensation, while constant symptoms
may suggest structural or more serious causes like malignancy).
• Ask if there is any associated throat pain.
• Ask if she has noticed any lumps or swellings in the neck.
• Ask if she has any difficulty swallowing (dysphagia) or pain when swallowing
(odynophagia).
• Ask if she experiences heartburn, or a sour taste in the mouth, which may
suggest acid reflux.
• Ask about symptoms of postnasal drip, such as a runny nose or mucus
dripping down the back of the throat.
• Ask about any nausea or vomiting, if present, ask whether the vomiting has
included blood.
• Ask about red flag symptoms: unintentional weight loss, night sweats,
persistent hoarseness, or changes in the voice.
• Ask if she has noticed any particular triggers for the throat sensation (e.g.,
stress, certain foods, caffeine, time of day).
• Ask if she frequently clears her throat or finds herself "dry swallowing"
(swallowing without any food or liquid, often just swallowing saliva)
• Ask about any recent emotional stress, including anxiety or difficult life events.
• Ask about smoking history, alcohol use, and occupational exposures (e.g.,
voice strain, dust, fumes).
• Give a likely diagnosis of globus sensation.

-- 829 of 1282 --

828
Example of explanation to patient
Hailey, thank you for sharing everything with me today, I can hear how much this has
been worrying you, especially with everything going on in your personal life. I
understand that you're quite concerned this lump sensation might be something
serious, like cancer, and you were hoping for a scan to rule that out.
From what you've told me, and based on the fact that you’ve had no weight loss, no
difficulty swallowing, no voice changes or other red flag symptoms, this doesn’t
sound concerning for cancer at this stage. What you're describing actually sounds
quite like a condition we call globus pharyngeus, have you come across that term
before?
It’s where you feel a lump in the throat, but when we examine the area, there’s no
actual lump there. That doesn’t mean what you’re feeling isn’t real, far from it, but it
usually isn’t due to anything harmful. It’s a very common symptom, and it can often
be brought on or made worse by stress and emotional strain. Given what you’ve
been dealing with recently, especially around your partner’s health and the stress at
work, it’s very possible that this is playing a role. The good news is that once we get
on top of the stress and support your wellbeing, this sensation usually improves.
However, I would still like to invite you in for a face-to-face appointment so we can
take a proper look at your neck and throat, just to make sure there’s nothing we’re
missing.
I know you’ve had a look yourself and haven’t noticed anything unusual, which is
reassuring, but an examination allows us to check for any lumps, swellings, or subtle
changes that might not be visible. Would that be okay with you?
You mentioned that you would like to have a scan, and I completely understand your
concern. At this stage, based on the information you have provided and the absence
of any red flag symptoms, a scan or referral is not necessary. However, if anything
changes, such as a worsening of your symptoms or if we find anything concerning
during the examination, we will certainly take the appropriate next steps, which may
include arranging further investigations or referring you to a specialist.
In the meantime, there are a few strategies that may help ease your symptoms.
Would you like me to talk you through them?
One option is to consider some stress management approaches, such as
mindfulness, talking therapies like Cognitive Behavioural Therapy, or gentle physical
activity such as yoga. Some patients also find relaxation apps like Headspace
helpful. Do any of these sound like something you might be open to trying?
If work has been particularly overwhelming, I can also provide you with a short-term
fit note. A bit of time away might give you the space to reset and focus on your
wellbeing. What do you think?

-- 830 of 1282 --

829
In addition, many people with this kind of throat sensation find themselves frequently
clearing their throat or swallowing even when there is no need to. This can actually
make the feeling more noticeable. One simple tip is to sip chilled, fizzy water when
the urge to clear your throat arises, this can help settle the reflex and reduce
discomfort.
Would you like to explore any of these options further?
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment for further assessment, including
examination of the neck and throat.
• Acknowledge that stress and anxiety may be contributing factors and
recommend support strategies such as Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT),
mindfulness, yoga, regular exercise, or relaxation apps like Headspace.
• Provide vocal hygiene advice, including reducing caffeine intake, avoiding
habitual throat clearing or dry swallowing, and suggesting sipping chilled
carbonated water when the urge arises.
• Offer a short-term fit note as work-related stress is impacting her health and
performance.
• Reassure the patient that, given the absence of red flag symptoms, a scan or
referral is not indicated at this stage.
• Advise that if symptoms persist, worsen, or if anything concerning is found on
examination, further investigation, including a referral to a specialist may be
necessary.
• Safety-netting Advice: Advise the patient to seek urgent medical attention if
the throat sensation becomes constant or noticeably worsens or she develops
any new symptoms such as difficulty swallowing, voice changes, unexplained
weight loss, pain, or any visible lumps or swellings.
• Offer follow-up appointment in 3 to 4 weeks to assess progress and review
response to the current management plan.
Learning Point from This Station
Globus sensation is a subjective feeling of a lump or foreign body in the throat,
often described as "something stuck," even though no actual obstruction is found. It
is sometimes referred to as globus pharyngeus.

-- 831 of 1282 --

830
Common causes include, gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD),
psychological factors such as anxiety and stress, increased upper oesophageal
sphincter pressure, pharyngeal irritation or inflammation which may lead to increased
sensitivity of the throat, contributing to the globus sensation. (e.g. pharyngitis,
tonsillitis, postnasal drip from chronic sinusitis), and oesophageal motility disorders
(e.g. cricopharyngeal spasm).
Most cases can be effectively managed in primary care through a careful history,
exclusion of red flag symptoms, and appropriate examination. Over-referring can
lead to unnecessary and invasive investigations, increase patient anxiety, and
potentially worsen the symptom.
However, referral to ENT for urgent flexible nasoendoscopy should be considered if
the sensation is persistent and constant (not intermittent), or if it is associated with
any of the following:
• Pain on swallowing (odynophagia)
• Difficulty swallowing (dysphagia)
• Unexplained weight loss
• Night sweats
• Persistent hoarseness
• History of smoking or significant alcohol use
These features may suggest a more serious underlying pathology such as
malignancy and warrant urgent (2-week wait) assessment.
Further reading
https://bjgp.org/content/65/639/554
https://patient.info/doctor/globus-sensation-pro

-- 834 of 1282 --

833
Marking scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Clarify why the patient requested the cholesterol test (explore background and
motivation)
• Ask about symptoms of cardiovascular disease: including chest pain,
shortness of breath, palpitations, dizziness, headaches, and any weakness or
numbness affecting one side of the body, which may indicate stroke or TIA.
• Ask about lifestyle factors including diet, exercise, and occupation (sedentary
work increases CVD risk)
• Ask about smoking, alcohol, and substance use
• Ask about any personal or family history of cardiovascular disease (heart
attacks, strokes, high blood pressure, diabetes)
• Explore understanding of cholesterol and its risks (identify misconceptions)
• Explore Ideas, Concerns, and Expectations, especially fear of heart attack
and desire for statins.
• Give a diagnosis of raised cholesterol with low QRISK3 of 4%
Example of explanation to the patient
Daniel, thanks for coming in today and for being proactive about your health.
I’ve reviewed your blood results, and they show that your total cholesterol is raised at
6.0, and your LDL cholesterol, which is the 'bad cholesterol', is also raised. These
are important findings because raised cholesterol can increase the risk of heart and
blood vessel problems over time.
To help us understand your overall risk, we use a tool called QRISK3. This takes into
account your age, blood pressure, smoking status, cholesterol, and other factors to
estimate your chance of having a heart attack or stroke over the next 10 years. Your
score comes out at 4%, which we consider to be low risk. To put it in perspective, in
a group of 100 people with the same risk factors as you, about 4 are likely to have a
heart attack or stroke over the next 10 years.
That said, even with a low risk, there’s still benefit in reducing it further, and that’s
where lifestyle changes come in. You mentioned earlier that your diet isn’t the
healthiest, are there things you feel you could change?
Some practical steps include:
• Eating a healthy, balanced diet, including at least 5 portions of fruit and
vegetables each day

-- 835 of 1282 --

834
• Cutting down on foods high in saturated fat like fried food, processed meats,
and certain baked goods
• Checking food labels, aiming for foods with unsaturated fats like olive oil, nuts,
and oily fish
You also mentioned that you don’t currently exercise much. Have you had any
thoughts about how you could increase your activity levels?
The general recommendation is to aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate activity
per week, that could be something like brisk walking, cycling, or swimming. Even
walking briskly each day so that your heart rate increases a bit is a great place to
start.
You also told me that you smoke, have you considered cutting down or stopping?
The reason I ask is, smoking not only raises your cholesterol but also significantly
increases your risk of heart attacks, strokes, and certain cancers. We have excellent
stop-smoking support services, and I’d be happy to refer you if you’re open to it.
All of these changes, diet, exercise, stopping smoking, not only help improve your
cholesterol but also support your general health. They may also help with weight
management, and I noticed from your records that your weight is a bit on the higher
side.
If patient still wishes to start cholesterol tablets:
Since you're keen to start cholesterol tablets, we can certainly talk through that. The
type of medication we’d offer is called a statin, and it works by lowering the level of
cholesterol your body makes. It’s effective at reducing the risk of heart problems in
the long term.
Like all medications, statins can have side effects. Some people experience muscle
aches, tummy upset, or effects on the liver, but not everyone gets these, and for
many people the benefits outweigh the risks.
If you’re happy to go ahead, we can prescribe the statin today. We’ll then repeat your
blood tests in about 3 months to see how well it’s working and to monitor for any side
effects. If at any point you have concerns, you can let us know and we can review
things together.

-- 836 of 1282 --

835
Management
• Reassure that although cholesterol is raised, QRISK3 score is low (4%), so no
current indication for statin per NICE guidelines
• Explain that the patient’s QRISK3 score is 4%, meaning their estimated risk of
having a heart attack or stroke over the next 10 years is 4%. In simple terms,
this means that out of 100 people with similar risk factors, 4 may go on to
develop cardiovascular disease within the next decade. While this is
considered a low risk, it’s still important to explore ways to lower it further
through lifestyle changes such as improving diet, increasing physical activity,
and stopping smoking.
• Offer lifestyle advice: smoking cessation referral/support (key modifiable risk),
heart-healthy diet advice (leaflet or referral to dietitian if available), encourage
regular aerobic exercise (30 mins, 5x/week)
• If the patient still wishes to proceed with statin treatment, take time to explain
what statins are, their potential benefits in lowering cholesterol and reducing
cardiovascular risk, as well as the possible side effects, such as muscle
aches, tummy upset, or effects on the liver.
• If, after a balanced discussion, the patient is well-informed and happy to go
ahead, then it would be reasonable to offer statin therapy.
• According to NICE guidelines, even when the QRISK3 score is below 10%,
statin treatment can still be considered if lifestyle change alone is
ineffective or inappropriate (e.g. due to comorbidities or clinical judgement),
or if the patient expresses an informed preference to start treatment, or
where there is concern that the calculated risk may underestimate their
true cardiovascular risk.
• Arrange a repeat lipid profile in 3 months, regardless of whether the patient
starts statin therapy or opts for lifestyle changes alone. If statins are not
started, the repeat test will help assess the effectiveness of lifestyle
interventions. If statins are prescribed, the test will monitor response to
treatment and check for any changes that might guide ongoing management.
• Offer referral to Health and Wellbeing Coach or lifestyle support services if
patient wishes
• Provide written materials or links to trusted lifestyle and CVD risk resources
(For example NHS website or British Heart Foundation)
• Safety-net: advise to seek urgent medical help if he develops symptoms (e.g.
chest pain, breathlessness, palpitations, weakness or numbness affecting one
side of the body)

-- 837 of 1282 --

836
• Offer follow-up appointment in 3 months to review progress.
Learning Point from This Station
This station explores the management of mildly raised cholesterol in a low-risk
patient and highlights the importance of shared decision-making in primary
prevention of cardiovascular disease (CVD).
NICE recommends offering statin therapy for primary prevention to the following
groups:
• People with a 10-year QRISK3 score of 10% or more, including those with
type 2 diabetes
• People with type 1 diabetes, chronic kidney disease (CKD), or familial
hypercholesterolaemia
• People aged 85 years and older, where benefits may still be seen in reducing
non-fatal myocardial infarction
However, NICE also makes it clear that if the QRISK3 score is below 10%, clinicians
should not automatically rule out statin therapy. Statins may still be considered if:
• The patient has an informed preference for treatment after a discussion of
risks and benefits,
• Or there is concern that the QRISK3 score underestimates true risk (e.g., due
to comorbidities or unrecorded risk factors such as strong family history in
adopted individuals or smoking in younger patients).
This case also reinforces the importance of supporting lifestyle changes, including
diet, physical activity, smoking cessation, and weight management, as the first-line
approach in low-risk individuals.
Regardless of whether a statin is prescribed, it is essential to arrange a repeat lipid
profile after 3 months. This helps evaluate the effectiveness of lifestyle interventions
or response to medication and informs decisions about ongoing care.
Further reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/lipid-modification-cvd-prevention/management/primary-
prevention-of-cvd/#assessing-response-to-treatment

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Clarify the reason for today's call, confirm worsening joint symptoms and
impact on function
• Ask about symptoms of RA flare: morning stiffness, joint swelling, pain,
fatigue
• Ask which joints are currently affected and whether any other joints are
involved.
• Ask if the patient is having difficulty moving the affected joint or is unable to
move it at all. Ask whether the joint is hot or swollen, and enquire about any
systemic symptoms such as fever or feeling unwell. This helps to rule out
septic arthritis, which should be suspected if a single joint is hot, swollen,
immobile, and accompanied by systemic features.
• Ask about extra-articular features of rheumatoid arthritis, including: Eyes (any
redness or changes in vision, which may suggest episcleritis or scleritis),
Lungs (any shortness of breath, persistent cough, or chest pain, which could
indicate interstitial lung disease), Heart (any chest pain, palpitations, or
breathlessness, which may suggest pericarditis) , Skin (any unusual rashes,
easy bruising, or painful red spots, which may be signs of vasculitis)
• Ask about the functional impact of symptoms on activities of daily living,
including the ability to work, write or type, and carry out domestic tasks such
as cooking, cleaning, and self-care.
• Confirm last dose of methotrexate, any side effects experienced
• Ask if still taking folic acid
• Ask about other medications used in the meantime (e.g., NSAIDs)
• Explore any symptoms of infection (cough, fever, UTI, sore throat)- This is
particularly important as restarting methotrexate in the presence of an active

-- 841 of 1282 --

840
infection can delay recovery and increase the risk of serious complications,
including sepsis.
• Clarify why bloods were missed – explore understanding of monitoring
requirements
• Ask about social history including occupation, smoking and alcohol history.
• Explore Ideas, Concerns, Expectations
• Give a diagnosis of likely Rheumatoid Arthritis flare due to discontinuation of
methotrexate
Example of explanation to patient
Thomas, thank you for getting in touch today and for being so open about what’s
been going on. I’m really sorry to hear how much your symptoms have flared, I can
appreciate how difficult that must be, especially given the demands of your work.
I agree with you, it does sound like you're experiencing a flare of your rheumatoid
arthritis, and the stopping of methotrexate has likely contributed to that. You had
good control previously, so it's understandable you're keen to restart it.
The challenge is that methotrexate can affect your liver, kidneys, and reduce your
blood cells, which can make you more vulnerable to infections. Because of this,
national safety guidelines and our shared care agreement with Rheumatology
require regular blood tests before we can safely prescribe it. That’s why it was
stopped when the routine monitoring was missed.
I completely understand that work got hectic and that this wasn’t intentional. The
important thing now is to get a new set of blood tests done. We’ll check your full
blood count, kidney and liver function. Once we have the results, and everything
looks safe, we can then get you restarted on methotrexate without further delay.
In the meantime, you mentioned you only just started taking ibuprofen yesterday.
Taking it regularly, three times a day with food can help control the inflammation.
Alternatively, we can prescribe naproxen, which is a stronger anti-inflammatory,
along with omeprazole to protect your stomach.
If those don’t give enough relief, we also have the option of short-term steroid tablets
to settle the flare, but I’d prefer to hold off unless really necessary.
I’ll also send a message to the rheumatology team today to inform them of your
symptoms and ask if they have any additional advice or would like to see you
sooner.
Would it help if we booked your blood test today and arranged a follow-up call once
the results are back, so we can keep things moving quickly?

-- 842 of 1282 --

841
Exploring future support and safety-netting:
Going forward, how do you think we can support you in making sure these routine
blood tests aren't missed again?
One idea might be to set a recurring reminder on your phone or choose a set day
well in advance to make it part of your schedule. We can also send you a reminder a
month before your next blood test is due, to give you plenty of time to get it booked.
What do you think would work best for you?
Finally, if your symptoms get significantly worse, for example, if your hands become
very swollen, extremely painful, hot to touch, or you develop a fever or feel unwell —
please contact us immediately. That could indicate an infection in the joint, which
would need urgent attention.
Management
• Acknowledge patient is likely experiencing a flare of rheumatoid arthritis due
to methotrexate being withheld
• Explain methotrexate cannot be restarted until up-to-date blood tests are done
to ensure safety
• Arrange urgent blood tests: full blood count, liver function tests, kidney
function (U&Es), and CRP.
• Reinforce importance of regular monitoring for methotrexate due to risk of
liver, kidney, and blood-related complications.
• Prescribe anti-inflammatory treatment for symptom relief: advise taking
ibuprofen regularly with food and prescribe omeprazole for stomach
protection. Alternatively, offer naproxen with omeprazole if stronger anti-
inflammatory relief is needed or preferred
• Consider short course of oral steroids if symptoms are severe or not
controlled with NSAIDs
• Advice that you will send an update to rheumatology team informing them of
current symptoms and your intentions on restarting methotrexate once all
blood tests are back and within reference range
• Discuss practical strategies to support future blood test compliance to avoid
missed bloods (e.g. setting reminders, booking well in advance)
• Offer pneumococcal and annual influenza vaccination to reduce infection risk
due to immunosuppressive therapy

-- 843 of 1282 --

842
• Safety-net: advise patient to seek urgent review if symptoms worsen,
especially if joint becomes hot, swollen, immobile, or if they develop a fever or

• Book a follow-up appointment or telephone review once blood results are

Learning Point from This Station
This case highlights the critical importance of safety monitoring in patients prescribed
immunosuppressive medications such as methotrexate. As per NICE guidelines and
shared care protocols, patients on disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drugs
(DMARDs) require regular blood tests, typically every 2 to 3 months, to monitor for
potentially serious adverse effects.
These adverse effects may include:
• Myelosuppression (reduced blood cell production)
• Hepatotoxicity
• Renal impairment
• Pulmonary toxicity
• And an increased risk of infection
If a patient misses their monitoring, it is the GP’s duty to withhold methotrexate until
updated blood results confirm it is safe to resume. Restarting treatment without
appropriate monitoring can result in severe complications, including bone marrow
suppression and organ toxicity.
In patients presenting with a flare of rheumatoid arthritis, it is important to liaise with
rheumatology or seek specialist advice where possible. However, while awaiting
a specialist review, GPs may consider interim management to alleviate symptoms
and maintain function.
Management options during a flare include:
• NSAIDs (e.g. ibuprofen or naproxen with gastroprotection)
• Oral corticosteroids
• Intra-articular steroid injections (if a single joint is significantly affected)
For oral steroid use, a reducing course of glucocorticoids over 2–4 weeks can be
started in appropriate cases while awaiting specialist input. An example 2-week

-- 844 of 1282 --

843
course could be: Prednisolone 10 mg daily for 7 days, then 5 mg daily for 7 days,
then stop.
Importantly, this scenario also illustrates the value of health promotion and practical
planning. Helping patients develop strategies to avoid missing future blood tests,
such as setting reminders or scheduling tests well in advance, can: support long-
term medication adherence, prevent disease recurrence and reduce the risk of
complications from delayed treatment.
This station integrates key principles of safe prescribing, flare management, and
patient engagement in long-term condition care.
Further reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/dmards/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/rheumatoid-arthritis/management/confirmed-
ra/#management-of-an-ra-flare

Since the assault, you have been unable to have penetrative sex. When you try to be
intimate with your husband, the muscles around your vagina tighten up
automatically, and penetration becomes painful or impossible. You become anxious
and tense, even though you love your husband and want to be close to him.
You did not have this problem before the assault. Your sexual relationship with your
husband was normal before this, but since the incident you feel your body “shuts
down” whenever intercourse is attempted. You feel frustrated and embarrassed
because you want things to go back to how they were.
You love your husband very much but you have not been able to tell him about the
rape, as you feel guilty and keep thinking that if you had stayed home that night,
none of this would have happened.

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Acknowledge that the patient has brought a very personal and difficult issue
and thank her for trusting you. For example, thank you for sharing something
so personal with me today, Priya. I can only imagine how difficult this must be
to talk about. Please be assured that we will do our best to support you. Are
you comfortable talking about what happened 8 months ago?
• Show respect and sensitivity by informing her that you will be asking
numerous personal questions regarding her sexual history. This shows good
interpersonal skills and helps prepare the patient for the discussion.
• Ask how long she has been experiencing difficulties with intimacy
• Ask what happens during attempted intimacy, for example, whether she
experiences fear, anxiety, physical pain, or involuntary tightness. This is to
help differentiate an emotional trauma response from secondary vaginismus.
• Ask if sexual intercourse was comfortable prior to the traumatic event.

-- 847 of 1282 --

846
• Ask if she has had any medical review, STI screening, or forensic examination

• Screen for symptoms of PTSD by asking about nightmares, flashbacks,

• Ask about her mood, sleep, appetite, concentration, and general functioning.
• Ask about suicidal thoughts or self-harm ideation.

and what support network she has around her.
• Ask about her ideas, concerns, and expectations regarding what might be

• Give a diagnosis of secondary vaginismus, likely triggered by the sexual

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Priya, thank you for trusting me with something so personal. I can hear how difficult
the last few months have been for you, and I want you to know that everything you
are describing is an understandable response to what you went through. No one
should ever have to experience that kind of trauma, and none of this is your fault.
From what you have told me, it sounds like your body is responding to the trauma by
tightening the muscles around the vagina automatically whenever intimacy is
attempted. This is something we call secondary vaginismus, and it is very common
in women who have experienced sexual trauma. It is not something you are
choosing or doing deliberately, and the fact that it is happening does not mean you
will never be able to be intimate again. With the right support, most women make
very good progress.
There are a few things we can offer to help you through this, and you do not have to
go through it alone.
One very effective option is pelvic floor physiotherapy. These are specialist
physiotherapists who understand vaginismus very well. They will work with you
gently, at your own pace, to help you relearn how to relax and control the pelvic floor
muscles.

-- 848 of 1282 --

847
Another treatment that many women find helpful is vaginal dilators, or vaginal
trainers. These are smooth, gradually increasing sizes of devices that help
desensitise the area and reduce the automatic muscle tightening. You would use
them in a very controlled and gentle way, only when you feel ready, and you can buy
them over the counter. The physiotherapist can also guide you on how to use them.
I would also recommend psychosexual therapy, ideally with someone experienced in
supporting survivors of sexual assault. They can help you work through the
emotional and psychological impact of what happened, rebuild confidence, and
gradually reduce the fear and anxiety associated with penetration or intimacy.
You mentioned that you have not been able to tell your husband yet. I completely
understand how hard that must feel. There is no pressure to disclose anything before
you are ready. However, having his understanding and support often helps couples
move forward together. If you feel unable to tell him on your own, a psychosexual
therapist can support you with that conversation when you feel ready.
There are also specialist sexual violence support services that offer counselling and
emotional support. These services are confidential and tailored to survivors of rape
or assault.
Before we finish, I want to reassure you that what you are experiencing is treatable,
and you are not alone. We will take this step by step, at a pace that feels safe for
you.
How does that sound so far? Would you like me to go over anything again ?
Management
• Acknowledge the trauma and validate the patient’s feelings; reassure her that
her reactions are understandable and that help is available.
• Explain that her symptoms are consistent with secondary vaginismus, which
can occur after traumatic sexual experiences such as rape.
• Offer referral to a pelvic floor physiotherapist experienced in vaginismus.
Explain that they will guide her through gentle pelvic floor exercises that help
her regain control of the vaginal muscles and reduce involuntary tightening.
• Inform her that another helpful treatment option is the use of vaginal dilators
(also called vaginal trainers). These are smooth devices that come in
gradually increasing sizes and can be used at her own pace to help
desensitise the area and reduce pain or muscle spasm during penetration.
They can be purchased over the counter.

-- 849 of 1282 --

848
• Offer referral to psychosexual therapy, with a focus on sexual trauma. Explain
that this helps address the fear, anxiety, and emotional impact associated with
penetration, and works alongside physical therapy to improve symptoms.
• Acknowledge that you understand how difficult it may feel to tell her husband
about the rape, but gently explain that sharing this with him might help him
understand what she is going through and allow him to support her. If she
does not feel able to have that conversation herself, reassure her that the
psychosexual therapy team can help guide and support this discussion when
she feels ready.
• Offer referral to sexual violence victim support charities (e.g., Birchall Trust, if
local), where she can also access counselling services and support tailored to
survivors of sexual assault.
• Safety net: Advise her to seek urgent help through the GP urgent line or 111 if
she develops thoughts of suicide, self-harm, or feels unable to cope.
Learning Point from This Station
This case highlights the importance of recognising the psychological and physical
consequences of sexual trauma, particularly how it can impact intimacy, emotional
wellbeing, and relationships.
Secondary vaginismus and post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) can occur
either independently or together following sexual assault. Understanding and
distinguishing between psychological trauma (such as PTSD) and physical
responses (such as vaginismus) is essential to ensure the patient receives
appropriate, trauma-informed care.
PTSD is primarily psychological. The individual may experience fear, anxiety,
flashbacks, and avoidance behaviours related to sexual activity or touch. The patient
says things like "I feel afraid", "I avoid intimacy", "I get flashbacks", "I cannot trust
anyone"
Management focuses on:
• Trauma-focused CBT or EMDR
• Support from sexual assault or rape crisis services
• Psychosexual counselling
• Possibly SSRIs for mood or anxiety if indicated

-- 850 of 1282 --

849
Secondary vaginismus involves involuntary physical contraction of pelvic floor
muscles, making penetration painful or impossible. It is often related to psychological
triggers but manifests physically. The patient says things like "It hurts", "It will not go
in", "My body tightens up"
Management includes:
• Psychosexual counselling and pelvic floor physiotherapy
• Desensitisation techniques and graded exposure
• Medication is not always needed unless co-morbid anxiety or depression is
present
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/post-traumatic-stress-disorder/

-- 853 of 1282 --

852
• Ask if he has tried or considered non-drug approaches such as CBT,
breathing techniques, or exposure therapy.
• Ask about his ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Give a working diagnosis of flight anxiety.
Example of explanation to the patient
Thank you for sharing this with me, James. I can hear how difficult flying has been
for you and how much you rely on diazepam to manage those feelings of anxiety. I
understand you have used it before and felt it helped, so I appreciate this request is
important to you.
However, current medical guidance no longer supports prescribing diazepam for
flight anxiety. The reasons are mainly to do with safety. Diazepam can cause
sedation, confusion, and breathing problems, especially at high altitudes. It also
increases the risk of blood clots in the legs which can travel to the lungs on long
flights and can reduce your ability to respond in an emergency. In addition, it is illegal
in some countries, which can pose serious legal risks while travelling. Are you
following me so far?
Are you happy for me to talk you through some other alternatives that can really
help?
Some people find that breathing exercises or using distraction techniques, like
listening to music or podcasts, watching a film, reading, or even doing puzzles, can
really help keep them calm during a flight. There are also calming apps, such as
Headspace, which guide you through relaxation exercises step-by-step and can be
very effective. What are your thoughts on trying some of these?
In the longer term, we can work together on finding a more lasting solution. Once
you are back in the UK, I would be very happy to arrange a follow-up appointment.
We can explore options like talking therapy, and structured fear-of-flying courses
offered by airlines like British Airways and EasyJet, these have helped many people
feel more confident and in control when flying.
How does that sound to you?

-- 854 of 1282 --

853
Management
• Explain that diazepam is no longer prescribed for flight anxiety, as it carries
risks including sedation, confusion, increased risk of deep vein thrombosis
(DVT), respiratory depression, and reduced ability to respond in emergencies.
• In addition, diazepam is illegal in some countries, creating additional legal and
safety risks when flying internationally
• Offer safer and evidence-based alternatives for managing flight anxiety.
These include breathing exercises, distraction techniques such as listening to
music or podcasts, watching a film or TV show, reading, or doing puzzles, and
using calming and mindfulness apps like Headspace.
• If the patient still insists and explains that he has already tried breathing and
distraction techniques with no improvement, you may offer a very short course
of diazepam, for example two tablets of diazepam 2 mg only. Make it clear
that this is a one-off prescription and will not be repeated in the future.
• Explain that a long-term approach is also important. Options such as talking
therapy and structured fear-of-flying courses, like those offered by British
Airways or EasyJet are helpful long-term strategies to overcome flight anxiety.
• Offer a follow-up appointment after his return to the UK to review his
symptoms and create a personalised care plan for ongoing anxiety
management.
Learning Point from This Station
Flight anxiety, also known as aerophobia, is common. It can develop after a
traumatic incident, such as severe turbulence or a previous flight-related emergency,
or it may stem from a fear of losing control. On a train or in a car, a person can
choose to get off, if needed. On a plane, you have no control over when you can
leave. This loss of control can feel overwhelming.
Symptoms of flight anxiety may include: panic attacks even before the flight, or
symptoms during the flight such as shaking, feeling dizzy or nauseous, sweating,
a fast heartbeat (palpitations), shortness of breath, or a full panic attack while
onboard.
Diazepam is no longer recommended for managing flight anxiety due to its safety
risks, including sedation, impaired reaction in emergencies, increased risk of deep
vein thrombosis (DVT), paradoxical agitation, and legal issues in certain countries.
Instead, safer and evidence-based approaches should be encouraged. These
include structured breathing exercises, distraction techniques (such as music, films,

-- 855 of 1282 --

854
or reading), mindfulness apps like Headspace, and referral to airline-run fear-of-
flying courses (e.g. British Airways or easyJet programmes).
Further Reading
https://patient.info/news-and-features/how-to-manage-flight-anxiety

appetite, weight loss or gain, non-healing wounds, tingling in the hands or

-- 858 of 1282 --

857
• Ask about any chest pain or shortness of breath (Cardiovascular
complications of diabetes)
• Ask about social and lifestyle factors, including smoking, alcohol intake, diet,
weight, physical activity level, and occupation.
• Ask about family history of diabetes or other medical conditions.
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations (ICE).
• Explain that the blood test shows pre-diabetes and raised cholesterol, but with
