-- 920 of 1282 --

919
Expectations: You would like the GP to prescribe something to help with your
symptoms
Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about onset of symptoms
• Ask which joints are affected. Please note: Reactive arthritis usually presents
as an asymmetrical oligoarthritis, most often involving the lower limb joints
such as the knees, ankles, and sometimes the sacroiliac joints. Oligoarthritis
means 2–4 joints are involved. Asymmetrical means different joints are
affected on each side (for example, the left knee may be affected while the
right knee is not). This is important to distinguish from rheumatoid arthritis,
which typically causes a symmetrical arthritis, affecting the same joints on
both sides, such as both hands.
• Ask about redness, swelling, or stiffness of joints
• Ask about swelling of fingers or toes (dactylitis)
• Ask if he is able to move the affected joints and whether he can comfortably
bear weight on them.
• Ask about the diarrhoea episodes from 4 weeks ago and whether symptoms
are still present.
• Ask if anything makes the symptoms better or worse
• Ask whether the symptoms are getting worse, improving, or staying the same
• Ask about constitutional symptoms such as fatigue, fever, and malaise
• Ask about other features of Reiter’s triad to rule out sexually acquired reactive
arthritis: conjunctivitis (red or itchy eyes) and urethritis (burning sensation
when passing urine or discharge)
• Ask about painful, red eyes to rule out uveitis. If present, this requires same-
day ophthalmology referral and review.
• Ask about skin rash or nail changes (to rule out psoriatic arthritis). Also note
that some skin changes such as erythema nodosum and keratoderma
blennorrhagica may occur with reactive arthritis
• Ask about mouth ulcers
• Ask what the patient has already tried for pain relief or control of symptoms

-- 921 of 1282 --

920
• Ask about social history including occupation, smoking, and alcohol
• Ask how the symptoms are affecting his daily activities and work
• Explore ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a diagnosis of reactive arthritis
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Oscar, thank you for explaining everything to me. From what you have told me, and
looking back at your records, it seems that your joint pain and stiffness started a few
weeks after you had that tummy upset when you came back from Thailand. This fits
with something we call reactive arthritis. Have you ever heard of that before?
Reactive arthritis is a condition that causes pain, redness and swelling in different
parts of the joints after an infection. In your case, this happened after your stomach
bug. Normally your body’s defence system fights the infection and then settles down,
but sometimes it overreacts and also affects healthy tissue. That is what has caused
the stiffness and pain in your joints now. Does that make sense?
I understand you are worried because you are self-employed, and missing work has
financial consequences. Our aim is to help you feel better and get you back to work
as soon as possible.
The first step is to manage the inflammation and pain. I can prescribe you a
medicine called naproxen, which reduces swelling and stiffness. Because this can
sometimes irritate the stomach, I would also give you another tablet called
omeprazole to protect your stomach. Does that sound reasonable to you?
I would also like to bring you in today to examine your joints, just to make sure we
are not missing anything else. Alongside that, I will arrange some blood tests to
confirm what is happening and to check if there is anything else contributing. Are you
happy to have these done?
The good news is that this condition usually settles on its own within three to six
months. However, if your symptoms are not improving, we may need to seek
specialist advice.
To support you in getting back to work, physiotherapy can be very helpful. They can
teach you exercises to strengthen your joints and improve movement, which should
make things easier day to day.
I can also refer you to a social prescriber, who can talk through financial or practical
support that may help you while you are off work. For example, they can advise
about Employment and Support Allowance, which provides some income if you are
unable to work due to your health, and the Access to Work scheme, which offers
grants to help pay for practical support, equipment or adjustments that make it easier

-- 922 of 1282 --

921
to continue your job. This can be really helpful for someone who is self-employed,
like yourself. Is this something you would want to explore?
Just to be safe, if your symptoms get much worse, if you find you cannot move your
joints at all, or if you develop a fever, please seek urgent medical attention.
Management
• Offer a face-to-face consultation to assess and examine the affected joints
• Arrange blood tests including FBC, ESR, CRP, rheumatoid factor, antinuclear
antibodies, and HLA-B27
• Offer non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) together with a proton
pump inhibitor (PPI). Explain that NSAIDs help reduce both pain and
inflammation, but they can cause side effects such as indigestion, stomach
irritation, and bleeding, which is why a PPI is prescribed alongside to protect
the stomach.
• If the patient has already taken over-the-counter non-steroidal anti-
inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), such as ibuprofen, and this has not helped,
offer naproxen. If NSAIDs are not tolerated, offer paracetamol with or without
codeine, but explain that this will only control pain and not inflammation.
Steroids, such as prednisolone, may also be used, but these should only be
prescribed under the guidance of rheumatology.
• Offer referral to physiotherapy. Explain that this can be helpful as
physiotherapists can teach exercises to improve symptoms and aid early
return to work.
• Offer referral to a social prescriber who can provide information about support
and benefits, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Access to
Work (a grant to help pay for practical support at work).
• Reassure the patient that reactive arthritis is usually self-limiting, with
symptoms resolving in 3–6 months.
• If symptoms have not resolved after three months, consider referral to
Rheumatology, as this may indicate progression to chronic reactive arthritis.
• Arrange follow-up in 4 weeks to review progress and discuss the outcome of
the rheumatology appointment
• Safety net: Advise the patient to seek urgent medical attention if symptoms
worsen, if he is unable to move the joint, or if he develops a fever.

-- 923 of 1282 --

922
Learning Point from this Station
Reactive arthritis is a type of seronegative spondyloarthritis that typically develops 1
to 6 weeks after a gastrointestinal or urogenital infection.
Reactive arthritis most commonly presents with asymmetrical oligoarthritis.
Oligoarthritis means that 2 to 4 joints are involved. Asymmetrical means that different
joints are affected on each side, for example the left knee may be affected while the
right knee is not. Less commonly, the condition may present as polyarthritis,
involving 5 or more joints, or as monoarthritis, affecting a single joint.
Other characteristic features include:
• Inflammatory back pain
• Dactylitis, which is swelling of entire digits such as fingers or toes
• Enthesitis, which is inflammation at the sites where tendons insert into bone.
Common examples include the Achilles tendon and the plantar fascia at the
heel, often presenting as heel pain.
Reactive arthritis is usually triggered by a preceding infection.
Post-enteric: The most commonly associated gut pathogens are Campylobacter,
Salmonella and Shigella.
Post-venereal: It may follow Chlamydia trachomatis infection, and it has also been
associated with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV).
The condition is more common in white patients, partly due to the higher prevalence
of the HLA-B27 gene, which is associated with susceptibility.
Investigations may include full blood count (FBC), erythrocyte sedimentation rate
(ESR), C-reactive protein (CRP), antinuclear antibodies, rheumatoid factor, and
HLA-B27. Stool culture may be helpful if there are ongoing gastrointestinal
symptoms, while urine testing or genital swabs for nucleic acid amplification testing
should be performed if a sexually transmitted infection such as chlamydia or
gonorrhoea is suspected.
Management involves the use of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
as first-line treatment. If NSAIDs are not suitable or are contraindicated, paracetamol
with or without codeine can be used, although this will control pain rather than the
underlying inflammation. Corticosteroids may also be used but should be
prescribed under the guidance of a rheumatologist.
Antibiotics may also be appropriate if there is a confirmed ongoing infection. For
example, in this patient, if diarrhoea had persisted, treatment with azithromycin, as
recommended by microbiology following the stool culture result, would have been
suitable.

-- 924 of 1282 --

923
Reactive arthritis is usually self-limiting, and symptoms typically settle within three to
six months. However, some people can experience persistent or recurring
symptoms, which may lead to longer-term disease. If symptoms last for more than
three months, the patient should be referred to rheumatology for further
assessment and to consider treatments such as disease-modifying antirheumatic
drugs (DMARDs).
Further Reading
https://patient.info/doctor/orthopaedics/reactive-arthritis-pro
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/spondyloarthritis-psoriatic-arthropathy/
https://gpnotebook.com/en-GB/pages/rheumatology/reactive-arthritis

• No Known Drug Allergy
Recent Notes/Consultation
Seen 6 weeks ago by Alan Green (Physiotherapist)
Presenting Complaint: Patient self-referred to physiotherapy reporting low back
pain persisting for 4 weeks, exacerbated at night, disrupting sleep. She describes
occasional tingling to the right posterior thigh. No history of trauma. Patient is
employed as a health care assistant in a nursing home, requiring prolonged

function.
Examination:
• Posture: Mild flattening of lumbar curve, slight stiffness noted.
• Palpation: Tenderness over lower lumbar spine and paraspinals.
• Lumbar ROM: Flexion and extension moderately reduced due to pain.
• Neuro: Power, reflexes, and sensation grossly intact.
• Straight Leg Raise: Negative.
• No acute neurological deficit identified.
Impression: Features are consistent with non-specific mechanical low back pain.
No clinical evidence of nerve root compression or serious spinal pathology.
Plan:
• Analgesia with ibuprofen and paracetamol. Advised to contact GP if stronger
medication is required.
• Reassurance given and encouraged to remain active.
• Core strengthening and lumbar mobility exercises prescribed.

-- 926 of 1282 --

925
• Posture correction and ergonomic advice for work.
• Follow-up: Review in 4–6 weeks. Escalation if neurological deficits or red flags
develop.
Telephone Consultation (3 weeks ago – Dr David Burnham, Clinical
Practitioner Role)
Presenting Complaint: Recurrent low back pain with tingling in the right posterior

me, especially at night.
If Asked to Explain Further: Your back pain has been ongoing for about 10 weeks
and has gradually been getting worse. You have already seen a physiotherapist,
who gave you some exercises, but these have not made much difference. You also
spoke to a GP last week, who prescribed naproxen. This has taken the edge off the
pain slightly, but the pain is still worse at night.

or bowel symptoms.
ONLY SAY BELOW IF ASKED

feelings of being hot and cold, although you are not certain whether you have had a

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about onset of back pain
• Ask about the nature of the pain, aggravating and relieving factors, and
whether the pain radiates
• Ask whether the patient has noticed any weakness in one or both legs.
• Ask about early morning back stiffness, and if present, clarify how long it lasts.
Note: Inflammatory back pain (e.g. axial spondyloarthritis, ankylosing
spondylitis) typically causes early morning stiffness lasting more than 30
minutes, worse with rest, and improving with exercise. Mechanical back pain
may cause stiffness, but this usually lasts less than 30 minutes.
• Ask about any history of trauma or falls.
• Ask about cauda equina red flags: urinary incontinence, urinary retention,
faecal incontinence, perianal or perineal sensory loss (saddle paraesthesia).
• Ask about systemic symptoms such as weight loss, night sweats, and fever,
to rule out malignancy and serious conditions such as spinal tuberculosis,
discitis, or epidural abscess.
• Ask about lumps or bumps around the neck (lymphadenopathy, which may be
seen in TB or lymphoma)
• Ask if she has experienced any cough or shortness of breath.

-- 928 of 1282 --

927
• Ask about recent travel or contact with anyone with a prolonged cough
• Ask about BCG vaccination against TB, for example by checking whether she
has a scar on her upper arm. (note: BCG status does not rule TB in or out but
provides useful background information).
• Ask about family history of cancers (e.g. multiple myeloma, sarcoma,
lymphoma)
• Ask about symptoms of hypercalcaemia, which may be associated with
multiple myeloma: bone pain, abdominal pain, confusion, muscle weakness,
constipation, thirst, and polyuria.
• Ask about the impact of symptoms on daily activities and mood.
• Ask about social history including occupation, smoking, and alcohol
• Ask about the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a diagnosis of suspected Pott’s disease (tuberculosis of the spine),
while also considering malignancy as an important differential to be ruled out.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Nusrat, thank you for explaining everything to me today. From what you have told
me, and looking at your background, I am concerned that there may be a more
serious reason for your back pain. The fact that it is worse at night, and that you
have noticed weight loss and night sweats, makes me think we need to investigate
this further. Are you following me so far?
One possible cause of your symptoms is an infection in the spine called tuberculosis
of the spine, sometimes called Pott’s disease. Have you heard of tuberculosis
before, sometimes shortened to TB?
TB is an infection caused by bacteria bug that most often affects the chest (lungs),
but it can sometimes affect other parts of the body, including the bones in the back.
That can lead to the kind of symptoms you are experiencing.
TB is seen all over the world, but it is a little more common in people who were born
outside the UK. I want to reassure you that if this does turn out to be TB, it can be
treated effectively, although it does need specialist care. Does that make sense so
far?
What I would like to do today is arrange some urgent tests, including blood tests, an
X-ray of your back, and a chest X-ray. The chest X-ray is important because
sometimes TB can also affect the lungs, and if that is the case it can be passed on to
others. While we are waiting for these investigations, I will refer you urgently to the
infectious diseases team. They are the specialists who look after people with TB.

-- 929 of 1282 --

928
They may also arrange an MRI scan and, if needed, involve spinal specialists. Are
you happy for me to arrange all of these for you?
Because you work in a nursing home with vulnerable people, I would strongly advise
you to temporarily stay away from work until we have confirmed that it is safe for you
to return. This is important because if TB is affecting your lungs, there is a risk it
could be passed on to the residents or your colleagues, and many of them may be at
higher risk of becoming unwell. This is only a precaution while we complete the tests
and get the specialist team involved. I can provide a fit note for your employer to
explain this so that you are fully supported. How do you feel about that?
For now, please continue taking your pain relief. If you notice any worsening
symptoms such as weakness in your legs, problems with bladder or bowel control,
numbness around your bottom, or if you feel suddenly more unwell, please seek
urgent medical help straight away. I will also arrange to review you in a week to
check you have been seen by the specialists.
Management
• Arrange a same-day face-to-face review to examine the back and perform a
full neurological assessment.
• Offer urgent investigations: chest X-ray (to assess for possible pulmonary
TB), lumbosacral spine X-ray, full blood count, urea and electrolytes, C-
reactive protein (CRP), and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR).
• Refer urgently to an infectious diseases specialist without waiting for results.
She should be seen within 1 week.
• Explain to the patient that the specialist may arrange further investigations
such as MRI of the spine and initiate treatment with antibiotics. They may also
involve spinal specialists if required.
• Advise that, because she works in a nursing home, she should temporarily
stay away from work. This is to ensure she does not pose a risk of
transmission to vulnerable patients until she has been fully assessed, and
pulmonary TB has been excluded by the specialist team.
• Provide a fit note for work.
• Continue analgesia for pain relief.
• Safety net: Advise the patient to seek urgent medical attention if she develops
worsening pain, weakness in the legs, loss of bladder or bowel control, or
numbness in the perineal region (possible cauda equina syndrome). Also

-- 930 of 1282 --

929
advise her to contact the GP if she develops a cough, feels more unwell, or
notices any new concerning symptoms.
• Arrange follow-up in 1 week to confirm that she has been seen by the
specialist and that treatment has been initiated.
Learning Point from this Station
This case highlights the importance of taking a careful history and using the
information available to guide your working diagnosis in general practice. Although
there are numerous possible differentials for back pain with systemic features,
tuberculosis of the spine (Pott’s disease) is the most likely diagnosis in this context.
Other differentials to consider include:
• Multiple myeloma – this is an important cause of back pain and systemic
symptoms but is primarily seen in older adults, most commonly over the age
of 60. It is therefore less likely in a 34-year-old patient.
• Lymphoma – this is another important close differential, as it can present in
younger adults with systemic features such as weight loss, night sweats, and
lymphadenopathy. However, in this case the patient reports no palpable or
visible lymph node swellings, and her background of being born in a TB-
endemic country makes spinal tuberculosis a more likely explanation.
• Sarcoma – primary bone sarcomas can present with back pain, but they are
very rare, particularly in adults without a significant family history or risk
factors.
• Discitis – this is an infection of the intervertebral disc space often associated
with vertebral osteomyelitis. It typically presents with insidious back pain,
localised tenderness, restricted mobility, and sometimes fever or weight loss.
Staphylococcus aureus is the most frequent pathogen. Risk factors include
immunosuppression, diabetes, and intravenous drug use. Discitis has a
bimodal age distribution, most commonly affecting children under 10 years old
and older adults, particularly men over 50. In this case, discitis is a possible
differential but is less likely than spinal TB. The patient is not in the common
affected age group and has no clear risk factors such as immunosuppression
or IV drug use. Her epidemiological background instead makes spinal TB the
more probable diagnosis.
Health promotion is very important in this case. The risk of transmission from
extrapulmonary TB, such as Pott’s disease, is generally low. Transmission is higher
in pulmonary or laryngeal TB. However, because a chest X-ray has not yet been

-- 931 of 1282 --

930
performed to rule out pulmonary TB, and the patient works in a nursing home with
vulnerable people, she must be advised to remain away from work until formally
assessed and cleared by the infectious disease specialist. They may also liaise with
occupational health physicians regarding safe return to work.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/tuberculosis/
https://patient.info/doctor/infectious-disease/spinal-tuberculosis

-- 932 of 1282 --

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Check with the mother whether she understands why the swab was taken
• Ask about the baby’s current symptoms, including discharge, swelling, or
redness in the eyes
• Ask about the colour of the discharge
• Ask whether one or both eyes are affected
• Ask about red flag features of serious infection, including fever, persistent
irritability with high-pitched or inconsolable crying, excessive sleepiness or
floppiness, reduced responsiveness, poor feeding or refusal of feeds, neck
stiffness and reduced wet nappies.
• Ask about PBIND history: pregnancy, birth, immunisation, nutrition (feeding
method), and development
• Ask about social history, including home situation, who lives at home, father of
the child, financial support, and whether she is schooling or working.
• Ask about her mood and how she is coping
• Break the bad news sensitively, explaining that the swab confirmed chlamydia
infection in the baby’s eyes
• Explain the likely mode of transmission: from mother to baby during vaginal
delivery
• Signpost before asking personal questions, then ask about the mother’s
health, including sexual partners, vaginal discharge, abdominal or pelvic pain,
fever, abnormal vaginal bleeding, and dysuria.
Example of Explanation to the Mother
Charlotte, thank you for answering my questions and for coming in today to talk
about your baby’s swab results. The test we did has shown that Jack has an
infection in his eyes called chlamydia. Have you heard of this before?
Chlamydia is a type of infection that usually affects adults. It is what we call a
sexually transmitted infection, which means it can be passed on through sex without
a condom. In babies, it can sometimes be passed from the mother to the baby during
birth. This is the most likely way Jack picked it up. Are you following me so far?
It is possible that you may have this infection yourself, even if you do not have any
symptoms, because chlamydia can sometimes stay in the body without causing

-- 935 of 1282 --

934
signs of illness. To help look after both your health and Jack’s, I would like to ask you
some personal questions about your sexual health, if that is okay with you.
Are you sexually active at the moment? Have you had any partners in the last 6
months? Have you noticed any discharge down below, pain in your tummy, or
discomfort when passing urine?
Thank you for answering those questions.
The good news is that this can be treated with antibiotics. For Jack, I would suggest
you take him straight to the paediatric assessment unit today. There he will be seen
by a children’s eye doctor. They will assess him and may start treatment with oral
antibiotics. It is important he is seen today because in young babies this infection
can sometimes spread to the lungs and cause a type of chest infection called
pneumonia, which can be serious. Are you following me so far?
Because this infection can also affect mothers, it is important that you are tested and
treated as well. The best place for this is a sexual health clinic. They can give you
the right treatment, test for other infections, and also help with something called
contact tracing, which means making sure your partner is treated too. Are you happy
to attend a sexual health clinic?
If you are not comfortable going to the sexual health clinic, I can arrange treatment
and testing for you here. For treatment, we would offer you an antibiotic called
erythromycin. This can sometimes cause a bit of tummy upset, but most people do
well with it. Can I just confirm that you have no allergies? Perfect.
For the testing, you can either give us a urine sample or take a vaginal swab
yourself, which we will send to the laboratory. This will check for chlamydia and any
other possible infections. Would you be happy to go ahead with that?
Because Jack is so young, things can change quickly. You are going straight to the
hospital today so he will be closely checked. However, if before you get there, he
develops a high fever, becomes floppy, feeds poorly, or you notice anything else that
worries you, please stop and call 999 for urgent help.
Also, if at any point you develop symptoms such as tummy pain, abnormal
discharge, pain when passing urine, or fever, please contact us straight away or
seek urgent medical help.

-- 936 of 1282 --

935
Management
• Arrange urgent same-day referral of the baby to paediatrics/ophthalmology for
systemic antibiotic treatment and monitoring
• Offer sexual health screening and treatment to the mother
• Explain that the most appropriate place for her to receive sexual health
screening and treatment is a genitourinary medicine (GUM) clinic. They will
also arrange contact tracing to ensure her partner is treated. If she does not
feel able to inform her partner directly, reassure her that the GUM clinic can
carry out partner notification anonymously, so she does not have to do this
herself if she prefers.
• If she declines to attend a sexual health clinic, offer testing and treatment in
primary care.
• If screening is done in primary care, offer a self-taken vulvovaginal swab or a
first-catch urine sample for diagnosis
• Do not wait for test results before offering treatment
• Antibiotics of choice: If not breastfeeding, prescribe doxycycline 100 mg twice
daily for 7 days. If breastfeeding, prescribe erythromycin 500 mg twice daily
for 14 days. Note that azithromycin is another option, but as it is present in
breast milk, the BNF advises it should only be used if no suitable alternatives
are available.
• Advise the mother that, as she is taking her baby straight to hospital today,
the doctors will monitor him closely. However, if before arrival the baby
develops high fever, becomes floppy, feeds poorly, or shows any worrying
changes, she should stop and call 999 or seek urgent medical help
immediately.
• Provide safety net advice for the mother: advise her to seek urgent medical
attention if she develops abdominal pain, fever, abnormal vaginal discharge,
or any new concerning symptoms.
• Arrange follow-up in 1- 2 weeks to review how both the mother and the baby
are doing.

-- 937 of 1282 --

936
Learning Point from this Station
Ophthalmia neonatorum (ON) refers to any conjunctivitis occurring within the first
28 days of life. It is most commonly infective in origin. Chlamydia trachomatis is the
leading single cause of infective neonatal conjunctivitis, with Neisseria gonorrhoeae
also recognised as an important cause. Other bacterial pathogens include
Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Pseudomonas and
Haemophilus species, as well as other Gram-negative organisms. Viral infections are
less common but may result from herpes simplex virus, adenovirus, or enteroviruses.
The main risk factor for ophthalmia neonatorum due to chlamydial or gonococcal
infection is the presence of a sexually transmitted infection in the mother. The rate of
vertical transmission from an infected mother to her infant is estimated to be
between 30–50%.
In primary care, any neonate (a child within the first 28 days of life) presenting
with red and sticky eyes, profuse purulent discharge, or swollen eyelids and
surrounding cellulitis should be referred same day for specialist assessment.
Swabs should not delay referral or treatment, as prompt initiation of treatment
reduces the risk of permanent visual impairment or overwhelming systemic infection.
Management also includes assessment and treatment of the mother and her
partner(s). It is essential to confirm whether the mother is breastfeeding before
prescribing antibiotics, as certain agents such as doxycycline are
contraindicated in breastfeeding mothers due to risks including permanent
discoloration of the infant’s teeth and, occasionally, dental hypoplasia.
Further Reading
https://patient.info/doctor/paediatrics/ophthalmia-neonatorum
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/conjunctivitis-infective/

-- 938 of 1282 --

If the doctor asks if you need help cutting down your alcohol: Accept the offer of

Ideas: You are not sure what the blood test result means.

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Start by acknowledging that you can see from his records that he attended the
blood donor clinic and was referred to the GP to discuss his results.
• Ask if he is currently experiencing any symptoms.
• Ask specifically about neurological symptoms that may occur with vitamin B12
deficiency, such as blurred vision, tingling or numbness in the hands or feet,
difficulty with balance or coordination (ataxia), muscle weakness, memory
problems, or confusion.
• Ask about general symptoms of B12 deficiency, including persistent tiredness,
headaches, mouth ulcers, loss of appetite, diarrhoea and unintentional weight
loss.
• Ask about diet, particularly whether he is vegan or vegetarian, as B12 and
folate deficiency is more common in these groups.
• Ask about alcohol intake and establish a clear history.
• Ask CAGE questions to screen for alcohol misuse: ask if he has ever felt he
should Cut down on his drinking, if people have ever Annoyed him by
criticising his drinking, if he has ever felt Guilty about his drinking, or if he has
ever needed a drink first thing in the morning (Eye-opener) to steady his
nerves or get rid of a hangover. Note: A score of two or more on the CAGE
questions suggests a significant likelihood of an alcohol problem that warrants
further assessment. However, even a single “yes” response should prompt

-- 941 of 1282 --

940
further evaluation using a more detailed tool, such as the AUDIT
questionnaire.
• Ask if he has ever tried to reduce his alcohol intake and what happened,
including whether he experienced any withdrawal symptoms (this is important
to assess the severity of alcohol dependence and the potential need for
medical support when cutting down)
• Ask whether he drives, as individuals with alcohol dependence or significant
alcohol problems should not drive and must inform the DVLA.
• Ask about other aspects of social history, including smoking, illicit drug use
(noting that nitrous oxide can cause vitamin B12 deficiency), and occupation.
• Make a diagnosis of macrocytosis without anaemia, most likely related to
excess alcohol intake.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
James, thank you for booking this appointment so we can go through your results
together. When you went to donate blood, the clinic did a routine test. What they
found is that the size of your red blood cells is bigger than normal. We call this
macrocytosis. Have you ever heard of this before?
There are a few possible reasons why this can happen. Sometimes it is linked to low
levels of vitamins in the body, such as vitamin B12 or folate. Sometimes it can be
related to the liver, or occasionally to a thyroid problem. The thyroid is a gland in the
neck that produces hormones which control your body’s energy, and when the levels
are low it can cause this kind of result. Are you with me so far?
Another possible cause is alcohol. Drinking more than the recommended amount
can affect the blood in this way, even if you feel well otherwise. From what you have
told me about your drinking over the past three months; this may be playing a role
here. Are you following me so far?
What I would like to do is arrange some blood tests to check your vitamin levels,
thyroid function, and liver health, so we can get a clear picture. Would you be happy
to have these tests done?
It would also be helpful for us to look a little more closely at your alcohol intake, as
drinking at the level you described can sometimes affect the blood and the liver.
Cutting down can make a real difference to your health, and I know this is not always
easy to do on your own. To support you, I can send you a short questionnaire which
will help us see what level of support may be useful. I can also link you with local
services who work with people to reduce gradually and safely. How does that sound
to you?

-- 942 of 1282 --

941
In the meantime, I would not recommend cutting back suddenly on your own, as this
can sometimes cause withdrawal symptoms. These may include shakiness,
restlessness, a racing heartbeat, or confusion. If you notice any of these or anything
else that worries you, please seek medical help straight away.
James, there is one important point I need to raise. Because of the amount of
alcohol you are drinking at the moment, the DVLA requires that they are informed.
This also means that, for your safety and for the safety of others, you should stop
driving for now. I understand this may be difficult, but it is a legal requirement and
also the safest option while we work on getting things back under control. This is
about keeping you and others safe. How do you feel about that?
You asked about when you might be able to donate blood again. First, we need to
do some further blood tests, check for any vitamin issues, and help you bring your
alcohol intake down to a safer level. Once your health is back on track, the blood
donation service can look at your eligibility again. Does that make sense?
Management
• Offer blood tests to investigate possible causes of macrocytosis, including
repeat full blood count, vitamin B12, folate, thyroid function, and liver function
tests.
• Offer the patient the AUDIT questionnaire, explaining that it helps assess the
extent of his alcohol intake and guides what level of intervention may be
necessary.
• Advise reducing alcohol intake, explaining that excessive alcohol can cause
the current blood picture and may also damage the liver and other organs in
the long term.
• Inform him that support services are available if he would like help to reduce
his alcohol intake.
• Refer the patient to local community alcohol withdrawal services, explaining
that cutting down suddenly on his own may cause withdrawal effects, and the
community team can support him to reduce safely.
• Advise on maintaining a healthy, balanced diet.

-- 943 of 1282 --

942
• Explain that if vitamin B12 or folate deficiency is identified, replacement
therapy will be offered.
• Advise not to drive and to inform the DVLA, as driving is not permitted in the
context of harmful or excessive alcohol use.
• Inform the patient that he will only be able to donate blood again once any
underlying cause of his blood results has been identified and treated, and
once his alcohol intake is reduced and stable. Blood donation services do not
accept donations from people with ongoing harmful alcohol use.
• Safety net by advising the patient to seek medical help if he develops
symptoms of acute alcohol withdrawal such as tremors, agitation, palpitations,
confusion, or restlessness.
• Arrange follow-up in 2–3 days to review and discuss the blood test results.
Learning Point from this Station
Macrocytosis without anaemia is a common finding in primary care. The main
causes include alcohol misuse, hypothyroidism, liver disease, certain drugs (such as
antimetabolites like methotrexate or hydroxycarbamide), and more rarely
myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS).
Myelodysplastic syndrome usually presents with macrocytic anaemia and other
cytopenias, such as thrombocytopenia or neutropenia, but it can occasionally
present with isolated macrocytosis.
Investigations should include a full blood count, blood film, reticulocyte count, liver
function tests, thyroid function tests, vitamin B12, and folate. Immunoglobulins
should also be checked if the total protein is raised, or where there is
hypercalcaemia or unexplained renal impairment. If the common causes of
macrocytosis have been excluded, particularly in elderly patients, early
myelodysplastic syndrome should be considered and referral to haematology is
recommended.
This station also highlights the importance of screening for alcohol dependence in
patients with significant alcohol intake. While NICE recommends the use of the
AUDIT questionnaire for a full assessment, the shorter CAGE tool can be used
quickly during a consultation to assess for alcohol problem.
Patients with harmful or dependent alcohol use must be advised to stop driving and
inform the DVLA, as this is both a safety and a legal requirement.

-- 944 of 1282 --

943
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/alcohol-problem-drinking/
https://patient.info/doctor/mental-health/alcoholism-and-alcohol-misuse-management

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about onset and duration of the sleep disturbance
• Ask whether her sleep was previously normal before this episode
• Ask about any recent changes in her environment, regular carers, or daily
routine
• Ask whether she experiences daytime sleepiness and what activities she
engages in during the day (being active during the day can promote a natural
sense of tiredness that makes sleep easier at bedtime and may also reduce

-- 947 of 1282 --

946
anxiety and restlessness. Avoiding long daytime naps helps maintain the
person’s normal sleep–wake cycle)
• Ask whether she has been unwell recently, for example cough, fever, sore
throat, or any reported pain or discomfort
• Ask whether she is eating and drinking adequately
• Ask whether she appears more confused than usual
• Ask whether she has been aggressive or attempted to harm herself, other
residents, or staff members
• Ask about bowel habits, including constipation or diarrhoea (constipation is a
common and reversible cause of sleep disturbance, agitation, and confusion
in older adults. If constipation is present, ask about symptoms suggestive of
faecal impaction such as hard lumps, abdominal pain, bloating, or overflow
incontinence)
• Ask about urinary symptoms such as frequency, smelly urine, abdominal pain,
or haematuria
• Ask whether she has recently started any new medication
• Ask about her mood and whether she appears more withdrawn than usual
• Make a working diagnosis of insomnia, likely secondary to constipation
Example of Explanation to the Carer
Thank you, Harriet, for explaining what has been happening with Beatrice. From
what you have described, she has been experiencing disturbed sleep for the past
few nights, with restlessness and wandering at night, and increased drowsiness
during the day.
It does sound like her recent constipation may be contributing to these symptoms, as
both seem to have started around the same time. You mentioned that you were
wondering about sleeping tablets. I completely understand why you might consider
this option, but it is important to know that sleeping tablets can increase the risk of
falls, cause daytime sleepiness, and may worsen sleep patterns in the long term. Are
you following me so far?
I think the best first step would be to address her constipation, as this may improve
both her comfort and sleep. I can prescribe some medication to help relieve the
constipation, but please ensure that she maintains adequate fluid intake and has
enough fibre in her diet. Shall we try that approach first and see how she gets on?

-- 948 of 1282 --

947
To help improve her sleep naturally, there are several things that can make a big
difference. These include exposure to natural daylight during the day, encouraging
daytime activity and personalised engagement, keeping the bedroom quiet, dark,
and at a comfortable temperature, and avoiding caffeine or stimulating drinks in the
evening. Being active during the day helps promote tiredness at bedtime and
reduces anxiety and restlessness. Do you think these adjustments could be
organised for her in the care home?
You mentioned you were worried about her falling. Thank you for pointing this out, as
it is important that we do everything we can to prevent this. Is it possible for one-to-
one carer supervision to be arranged temporarily at night to help reduce the risk of
falls and ensure her safety when she becomes restless? Would this be something
that can be organised in the care home?
Other helpful measures include anti-fall stockings, properly fitting footwear, and
personal alarms so that she can call for help if she does fall. It is also important that
her environment remains dementia-friendly, free from hazards, and lights left on in
key areas at night to improve visibility and reduce the risk of falls.
I will ring again in three to four days to check on her progress. If there is no
improvement, I will arrange to visit and examine her in person so that we can discuss
further options. Meanwhile, if her symptoms worsen, or if she becomes aggressive or
poses a risk to herself or others, please seek urgent medical attention.
Management
• Explain to the carer that constipation is likely contributing to the sleep
disturbance and that resolution may restore normal sleep.
• Offer laxatives to help relieve constipation. Begin with a first-line bulk-forming
agent such as Ispaghula husk (Fybogel). If Fybogel is offered, emphasise the
importance of adequate fluid intake to avoid worsening constipation or risk of
intestinal obstruction.
• If Fybogel has been tried with no improvement, consider macrogol, lactulose,
or senna(especially if stools are soft but difficult to pass).
• If faecal impaction is suspected, offer a suppository such as bisacodyl for soft
stools, glycerol alone or glycerol with bisacodyl for hard stools, or a mini
enema such as docusate. Confirm whether a district nurse or carer is
available and able to administer the enema. If this is not possible, offer a high-
dose oral macrogol.

-- 949 of 1282 --

948
• Encourage non-pharmacological measures to improve sleep, including
exposure to natural daylight, increased daytime activity, engagement in
personalised activities, keeping the bedroom quiet, dark, and at a comfortable
temperature, and minimising caffeine or stimulating drinks in the evening.
• Explain that being active during the day promotes tiredness at bedtime and
reduces anxiety and restlessness.
• Ask whether one-to-one carer supervision can be arranged temporarily at
night to reduce the risk of falls and ensure safety during periods of
restlessness.
• Recommend anti-fall measures such as anti-fall stockings, properly fitting
footwear, and personal alarms to allow her to call for help if she falls. Ensure
the environment is dementia-friendly, free from hazards, and has good lighting
to improve visibility.
• Offer to contact the care home in 3–4 days to review progress, and if there is
no improvement, a home visit can be arranged for further assessment.
• Provide safety-netting advice that if symptoms do not improve, or if the patient
becomes aggressive or poses a risk of harm to herself or others in the care
home, urgent medical attention should be sought immediately.
Learning Point from this Station
Sleep disturbance and nocturnal agitation are common in patients with Alzheimer’s
dementia. These symptoms often result from disruption of the circadian rhythm,
environmental factors, pain, or intercurrent illness. Identifying and addressing
reversible causes such as constipation, pain, or infection is essential before
considering pharmacological treatment.
If sleep disturbance is significantly affecting the patient’s wellbeing or creating risk to
themselves or others, a short course of zopiclone may be considered. However, this
should only be initiated after non-pharmacological measures have been optimised.
Potential adverse effects include daytime sedation, poor coordination, cognitive
impairment, and increased risk of falls. Long-term use can lead to tolerance,
dependence, and increased mortality.
According to NICE guidance, melatonin should not be offered to manage insomnia in
people living with Alzheimer’s disease, as evidence does not support its
effectiveness in this population.
It is also important to assess whether the patient poses a risk to themselves or to
others. In cases where there is significant aggression that has led to, or may lead to,
harm, a short course of lorazepam may be appropriate while awaiting specialist

-- 950 of 1282 --

949
review. Discussion with an old age psychiatrist should be arranged to consider the
need for further behavioural management or antipsychotic therapy, where
appropriate.
Another important differential diagnosis is sundowning syndrome. Sundowning, or
sundown syndrome, describes increased agitation, anxiety, and confusion in people
with dementia during the late afternoon and evening. Management involves
maintaining a symptom diary to identify possible triggers and patterns of behaviour.
Carers should be encouraged to keep a brief record of what happens beforehand to
help establish contributing factors. Non-pharmacological strategies include
distraction techniques such as playing calming music or watching a familiar film,
encouraging regular daytime physical activity and exercise to promote better
night-time sleep, and ensuring rooms are well lit during the day and adequately
illuminated at night using both natural and artificial light. If all measures fail, referral
to an old age psychiatrist may be required for consideration of antipsychotic
therapy.
Further Reading
https://patient.info/doctor/mental-health/dementia-pro
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/dementia/

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Confirm consent to discuss with the daughter and verify her Lasting Power of
Attorney (LPA) status.
• Ask about the onset of the aggressive and concerning behaviours.
• Establish whether there are any immediate safety concerns for the patient or
her husband, including risk of harm or any history of physical violence towards
herself or her husband.
• Ask exactly what behaviours or symptoms the patient is exhibiting.
• Ask if there are any specific times of day when the behaviour worsens, and
whether there are any specific triggers such as changes in routine, hunger,
thirst, or conflict situations.
• Ask whether the symptoms are improving, worsening, or remaining the same.
• Ask whether the behavioural symptoms started soon after initiation of
donepezil by the psychiatrist. (People with frontotemporal dementia should
not be offered memantine or acetylcholinesterase inhibitors such as
donepezil, as these can worsen behavioural and cognitive symptoms by
affecting cholinergic systems that are not the primary issue in frontotemporal
dementia)
• Ask about her mood and whether she appears more withdrawn than usual.
• Ask about symptoms to rule out delirium or other reversible causes such as
fever, cough, breathlessness, urinary symptoms, constipation, diarrhoea,
abdominal pain, or any recent fall or head injury.
• Ask about her diet and fluid intake to assess for possible dehydration or
nutritional triggers.
• Ask about her social situation, including who lives at home, who her main
carer is, and what level of support is currently in place.
• Ask about smoking and alcohol intake.
• Ask about driving status, and if she is still driving, advise that she must stop
and inform the DVLA of her diagnosis.
• Ask about the daughter’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a working diagnosis of behavioural and psychological symptoms of
Pick’s disease (frontotemporal dementia), with donepezil likely contributing to
the worsening of these symptoms.

-- 954 of 1282 --

953
Example of Explanation to the Patient’s Daughter
Thank you, Evelyn, for explaining everything to me. From what you have told me, it
sounds as though your mother’s behaviour has become more difficult over the past
few months, and that has understandably been very hard for both you and your dad.
What you are describing can happen in people with her type of dementia, called
frontotemporal dementia, also known as Pick’s disease. Can I ask what you already
know about this condition?
This condition affects the parts of the brain responsible for behaviour, personality,
and emotional control, so people can sometimes become more irritable,
argumentative, or behave in ways that are quite out of character. Does that make
sense so far?
It is also possible that the medication your mother is currently taking, donepezil,
might be contributing to these changes. Donepezil is useful for some types of
dementia, but in frontotemporal dementia it can sometimes make behaviour worse
rather than better. Are you following me so far?
I think the best next step would be for me to discuss her case with the specialist (Old
Age Psychiatry) team to review her medication and decide whether it might be best
to stop donepezil. They may also consider other medicines, such as antipsychotics
or antidepressants (SSRIs), to help reduce agitation and stabilise her mood. Would
you be happy for me to do that?
In the meantime, it would be important to make the home as safe as possible.
Please remove or keep out of reach any items that could cause harm, such as
knives, sharp objects, cleaning products, or anything else dangerous. Are you okay
with that so far?
When your mum becomes agitated, try using calm and reassuring communication.
Speak gently, avoid arguments, and use a calm tone. Non-verbal techniques can
also help, for example, active listening, gentle reassurance, and the use of pictures
or symbols if she struggles to understand. These approaches can really help reduce
her distress.
I will also refer to the safeguarding team for a home situation and care needs
assessment, as it sounds like your dad is finding it difficult to cope. They will look at
what extra help or carers can be arranged to support both of them at home.
Another option we can consider, if you are worried about your father’s safety or if
things become too difficult to manage at home, is respite care. This would mean your
mum could stay temporarily in a care home while the specialist psychiatry team
reviews her medication and starts treatment to help manage and control her

-- 955 of 1282 --

954
symptoms. It can provide a period of stability for everyone. Would that be something
you, your dad, or your mum might consider?
Management
• Explain that her mother’s symptoms are consistent with what can occur in
people with frontotemporal dementia (Pick’s disease), as this condition affects
the parts of the brain responsible for behaviour, personality, and emotional
control.
• Inform her that it is also possible that the medication donepezil may be
contributing to these behavioural changes.
• Offer to discuss the case with the Old Age Psychiatry team to review her
medication and determine whether donepezil should be discontinued.
• Explain that the psychiatry team may consider other medications, such as
antipsychotics or selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), to help
manage agitation or behavioural symptoms if required.
• Advise her to remove potential hazards from the home or keep them out of
her mother’s reach, such as knives, sharp objects, cleaning products, or any
other dangerous items, to maintain safety.
• Advise the use of verbal and non-verbal de-escalation techniques, such as
active listening, calm and reassuring verbal responses, and the use of
pictures or symbols to aid communication and reduce agitation.
• Offer to make a referral to the safeguarding team for a home situation and
care needs assessment, as her father may be struggling to cope with the
current situation, and they can assess whether carers or additional support
will be needed to help manage safely at home.
• Offer respite care (temporary accommodation in a care home) as an
alternative option while arrangements are being made with the psychiatry
team to manage her symptoms. This can provide a period of stability and
support for her mother, especially if the daughter is worried about her father’s
safety or if her father is finding it extremely difficult to manage at home.
• Provide safety-net advice to seek urgent medical attention if her mother
becomes violent, poses a danger to herself or others, or if her father is unable
to manage safely at home.

-- 956 of 1282 --

955
Learning Point from this Station
Behavioural and psychological symptoms are common non-cognitive features of
dementia. These symptoms, often referred to as behaviour that challenges, may
include agitation, aggression, distress, and psychosis.
In frontotemporal dementia (Including Pick’s disease), it is important to recognise
that memantine and acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitors such as donepezil,
should not be used, as they can actually worsen behavioural and cognitive
symptoms. It is possible that the specialist may have prescribed donepezil in error;
however, it is essential to discuss with the psychiatry team first before discontinuing
the medication, as there may be a specific clinical reason for its use.
Management of behaviour that challenges in dementia involves both non-
pharmacological and pharmacological approaches.
The initial focus should be on exploring possible reasons for distress and addressing
reversible factors. This includes checking for clinical or environmental causes, such
as pain, delirium, constipation, infection, inappropriate care routines, or adverse
effects of medication.
Advise moving the person to a safe, low-stimulation environment, such as a quiet
room away from others, to reduce agitation. Encourage the use of verbal and non-
verbal de-escalation techniques, such as active listening, calm verbal reassurance,
and the use of pictures or symbols to improve communication and reduce anxiety.
If the person is at risk of harm to themselves or others, or is experiencing agitation,
hallucinations, or delusions causing significant distress, a short course of oral
lorazepam can be considered. This should be followed by an urgent discussion or
referral to the Old Age Psychiatry team for consideration of long-term
antipsychotic treatment to help control the symptoms.
Further Reading
https://patient.info/doctor/mental-health/frontotemporal-dementia
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/dementia/management/follow-up-of-confirmed-
dementia-in-primary-care/#managing-non-cognitive-symptoms

Patient’s Story (Role Player’s Brief)

If Asked to Explain Further: You have been on HRT for two years, which you
started when you reached menopause. It helped relieve your hot flushes and
improved your mood.
However, your sister was diagnosed with breast cancer four weeks ago. She is
awaiting surgery and further treatment. She is 55 years old and was told that her
HRT use might have increased her risk of developing breast cancer. Her cancer
affected only her right breast.
There is no other family history of breast, ovarian, prostate, or any other type of
cancer, and you do not have any Jewish ancestry.
You currently feel well and have not noticed any lumps or changes in your breasts.
Your mood is stable, and all menopausal symptoms are well controlled. You have no
abnormal vaginal bleeding. You perform regular breast self-examinations. Your last
NHS breast screening was two years ago and reported as normal. You believe you
are due for your next screening next year.

-- 958 of 1282 --

957
Social History: You do not smoke or drink alcohol. You work as a real estate agent.

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask the reason she wants to stop HRT.
• Acknowledge and sympathise with her about her sister’s breast cancer
diagnosis, and ask how her sister is coping at the moment.
• Ask how old her sister is and whether the diagnosis involved one breast or
both (one first-degree relative with bilateral breast cancer diagnosed
before the age of 50 is considered high risk and warrants referral to
secondary care breast services for genetic testing)
• Ask how long she has been on HRT.
• Ask about the original indication for starting HRT and whether her
menopausal symptoms have now resolved (This will guide management by
helping determine suitable alternatives if she decides to stop HRT, for
example, non-hormonal vaginal moisturisers or lubricants for vaginal dryness,
and cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) or SSRIs for hot flushes and mood
symptoms.)
• Ask whether she performs regular breast self-examinations and if she has
noticed any changes such as lumps, nipple discharge, skin dimpling, or pain.
• Ask about any vaginal bleeding, spotting, or discharge, as postmenopausal
bleeding always requires urgent investigation.
• Ask if she has noticed any other systemic symptoms, such as weight loss,
fatigue, or loss of appetite.

-- 959 of 1282 --

958
• Ask about her mood and how she is coping emotionally with her sister’s
diagnosis.
• Ask if there is any other family history of breast cancer (including male breast
cancer), ovarian, prostate, pancreatic, sarcoma, or any other type of cancer,
apart from her sister.
• Ask if her sister’s cancer is known to be hormone receptor positive, and
whether any genetic testing (such as BRCA testing) has been discussed or
offered to the family.
• Ask if she has any Jewish ancestry (explain that this is because certain ethnic
backgrounds, particularly Jewish heritage, are associated with a higher risk of
BRCA gene mutations)
• Ask about her social history and lifestyle, including smoking, alcohol intake,
occupation, home situation, diet, and level of physical activity.
• Explore her ideas, concerns, and expectations.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you, Katie, for sharing your concerns with me, and I am really sorry to hear
about your sister’s recent breast cancer diagnosis. I can completely understand why
this has made you feel anxious, especially since you are currently on HRT
You are right that HRT does slightly increase the risk of breast cancer, and the risk
tends to rise the longer it is used. However, this risk usually goes down after
stopping HRT, although it can last for up to about 10 years after stopping it. Are you
following me so far?
From what you have told me and what we know so far, your overall risk remains low.
The benefits of using HRT for up to about five years, such as controlling hot flushes,
improving mood, and enhancing quality of life, often outweigh the possible risks for
most women with similar risk factors to you. Does that make sense so far?
However, it will be important to keep in touch with your sister’s doctors or follow her
progress to find out if she has had genetic testing, for example, for the BRCA gene.
If her test turns out to be positive, it might mean that your own risk is a bit higher,
and we can then refer you to a genetic clinic for more advice. But for now, based on
your family history, there is no indication for urgent genetic testing.
Aside from HRT, there are a few other things that can also affect your risk of breast
cancer. Research shows that being overweight and not getting enough physical
activity can slightly increase that risk. I completely understand that making lifestyle
changes can be challenging, but small gradual steps, such as eating a healthy
balanced diet, being more active, and working towards a healthy weight, can make a

-- 960 of 1282 --

959
real difference in lowering your overall risk and improving your general wellbeing. Do
you think this is something you might be able to start working towards?
If you feel you would rather stop HRT, that is absolutely fine. We have several non-
hormonal options that can help improve your symptoms. What are your thoughts
about that?
If you decide to continue HRT, that is also perfectly reasonable. We will encourage
you to keep attending your regular breast screening and to continue breast self-
examinations at least once a month. If you ever notice any lumps, nipple changes,
pain, discharge, or any swelling under the arm, please let us know straight away.
I can also send you some leaflets about breast cancer risk and HRT, so you can
read through them in your own time.
How does that all sound so far? Would you like us to review things again in about 2–
3 weeks once you have more information about your sister’s results?
Management
• Acknowledge her concern and empathise with her about her sister’s recent
breast cancer diagnosis, recognising how worrying this must be.
• Explain to the patient that she is right that HRT does increase the risk of
breast cancer, and that this risk rises with the duration of use. The risk
gradually declines after stopping HRT but can persist for up to 10 years after
discontinuation.
• Reassure her that, based on her history and current information, her personal
risk is low, and that the benefits of HRT for up to five years of use are likely to
outweigh any potential harm.
• Emphasise the importance of following up her sister’s case, particularly to find
out whether any genetic testing (such as BRCA testing) has been performed,
as a positive result could alter her own risk assessment.
• Inform her that, aside from HRT, there are other factors that can influence
breast cancer risk. For example, research has shown that being overweight
and lack of physical activity can increase the risk of breast cancer. Sensitively
explain that adopting a healthier lifestyle by increasing physical activity, eating
a balanced diet, and working towards gradual weight reduction, can help
lower her risk further and improve her overall wellbeing.
• Advise her that if she chooses to stop HRT, non-hormonal options are
available to help manage symptoms: For vaginal dryness, recommend non-
hormonal vaginal moisturisers or lubricants. For hot flushes or mood changes,

-- 961 of 1282 --

960
suggest cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) as first-line, or SSRIs/SNRIs
such as venlafaxine as alternatives.
• Advise her to keep up to date with her sister’s progress, especially to find out
if her sister has had any genetic testing. Explain that this information will help
decide whether she also needs genetic counselling or a referral to a genetic
clinic. Reassure her that, for now, based on her family history, there is no
immediate need for a genetic referral.
• Offer to send her patient information leaflets to read more about HRT and
breast cancer risk for her understanding and reassurance.
• Encourage her to continue regular breast self-examinations and to report any
new lumps, pain, nipple changes, discharge, or swelling around the armpit
urgently.
• Offer a follow-up appointment in 2–3 weeks to check whether her sister has
had any genetic testing, as the results may help decide if she should stop
HRT and whether a referral to a genetic clinic is needed.
Learning Point from this Station
People who are at increased risk of breast cancer and require referral to
secondary care breast services for further assessment and genetic testing include:
• One first-degree female relative diagnosed with breast cancer under the
age of 40 years.
• One first-degree male relative diagnosed with breast cancer at any age.
• One first-degree relative with bilateral breast cancer, where the first primary
cancer was diagnosed under the age of 50 years.
Seek specialist advice from secondary care if any of the following are present in
the family history, in addition to breast cancers in relatives not fulfilling the standard
referral criteria:
• Bilateral breast cancer.
• Male breast cancer.
• Ovarian cancer.
• Jewish ancestry, particularly Ashkenazi Jewish heritage.
• Sarcoma in a relative younger than 45 years of age.
• Glioma or childhood adrenal cortical carcinoma in a relative.

-- 962 of 1282 --

961
• Complicated patterns of multiple cancers occurring at a young age.
• Two or more relatives with breast cancer on the father’s side of the family.
• Uncertainty about whether referral is appropriate.
• The person is not sufficiently reassured by the information provided.
Breast cancer risk associated with HRT:
• Increases with combined oestrogen–progestogen therapy compared to
oestrogen-only HRT.
• Rises with the duration of use.
• Is higher in people currently taking HRT than in those who have taken it in the
past.
• Declines after stopping HRT but persists for at least 10 years after stopping
use.
• There is very little or no increase in breast cancer risk with oestrogen-only
HRT.
If a woman taking HRT has any of the above risk factors, her HRT should be
stopped, alternative non-hormonal treatment provided, and specialist advice sought
immediately.
For women who are at low or population-level risk of breast cancer (that is, they do
not meet referral criteria), it is likely that the benefits of HRT for up to five years of
use outweigh any potential harm.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/breast-cancer-managing-fh/

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset and duration of double vision.
• Ask if the double vision occurs when looking in specific directions (for
example, when looking to the side or up and down).
• Ask if the double vision happens only when both eyes are open and goes
away when one eye is closed. If the double vision goes away when one eye is
closed, it means the problem is how the two eyes work together. This is called
binocular double vision and can be caused by cranial nerve palsy affecting
eye muscles due to issues like a stroke, tumour, or inflammation. If the
double vision stays even when one eye is closed, it means the problem is
coming from that eye itself. This is called monocular double vision, and it

-- 966 of 1282 --

965
can be caused by problems such as a cataract or an issue with the lens or
cornea.
• Ask if the images are side-by-side or one above the other, as this can indicate
which eye muscles may be involved.
• Ask if the symptoms are constant or intermittent
• Ask if they are any specific triggers such as when tired or after prolonged
screen time.
• Ask if there are any associated symptoms such as headache, nausea, or
vomiting (which may suggest raised intracranial pressure or a brain tumour),
drooping eyelids or difficulty swallowing (suggestive of myasthenia gravis or
cranial nerve involvement), or limb weakness or facial numbness (which can
occur with stroke, multiple sclerosis, or a brain tumour).
• Ask about other visual symptoms, such as blurred vision, red eyes, or painful
eyes (painful eyes may suggest optic neuritis, which can occur in multiple
sclerosis).
• Ask about any recent eye trauma
• Ask if he has ever worn glasses or had a squint in childhood, as
decompensating strabismus is a common benign cause of diplopia in young
adults.
• Ask about any muscle weakness, particularly in the upper and lower limbs,
that worsens with activity and improves with rest, as this pattern may suggest
myasthenia gravis.
• Ask about the impact on daily life or occupation, including whether double
vision interferes with reading, driving, or work activities. It is important to note
that people who have diplopia must not drive and must notify the DVLA, as
driving with double vision can be unsafe and is a legal requirement to report.
• Ask about social history, including smoking, alcohol, and illicit drug use.
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a diagnosis of diplopia likely due to convergence insufficiency
contributed by prolonged screen use.

-- 967 of 1282 --

966
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Wayne, thank you for explaining everything so clearly. From what you have told me,
it sounds like you have a condition called diplopia, which simply means double
vision. There are different possible causes for this, but the fact that both your eye
examination and your assessment by the private GP were normal is very reassuring.
What I think may be causing your double vision is something called convergence
insufficiency. Is this something you have heard of before? It means that the muscles
that help your eyes work together are struggling a little, especially when you focus on
things close up, like your computer screen.
Because you spend a lot of time on the computer, your eye muscles are probably
being overworked, and that can make the double vision worse. Taking regular
breaks will help a lot. I usually recommend something called the 20-20-20 rule. Every
20 minutes, look away from your screen and focus on something about 20 feet away
for around 20 seconds. This gives your eye muscles a chance to relax and recover.
Does that make sense?
If possible, try to reduce the total amount of time spent looking at screens where you
can, and make sure the room is well lit and your posture is comfortable.
There is another, much less common condition called myasthenia gravis that can
sometimes cause symptoms like double vision, especially after the muscles of the
eyes have been used for a long time, such as during prolonged screen use. It is a
rare condition that mainly affects the muscles controlling the eyes and eyelids, but it
can also affect the face, speech, chewing, swallowing, and other parts of the body.
It happens because the body’s immune system mistakenly interferes with the way
nerves communicate with muscles, causing weakness. From what you have told me,
this is very unlikely in your case, but to be thorough, I would like to arrange a blood
test to check for this condition so that we can rule it out completely.
Would you be happy for me to organise this blood test?
Because your symptoms have been present for quite some time, I will refer you to
the eye doctors at the hospital, known as the ophthalmology team, for a more
detailed check. They will be able to confirm the diagnosis and may show you some
simple eye exercises to help strengthen your eye muscles and reduce the double
vision. Are you happy for me to make this referral?
If your symptoms suddenly get worse or you develop any new problems such as
headaches, drooping eyelids, eye pain, weakness, or numbness, please seek
medical attention urgently.
We will plan to review you again in about four to six weeks to see how things are
going. Does that sound reasonable to you

-- 968 of 1282 --

967
Management
• Offer a routine referral to the Ophthalmology team for a comprehensive
assessment, given the two-year history of double vision. This will help confirm
the diagnosis, rule out any other underlying causes, and allow the orthoptist
(a specialist in eye movement and coordination) to provide guided eye
exercises if needed.
• Advise on lifestyle and screen use. Explain that staring at digital screens for
long periods can strain the eye muscles and contribute to double vision.
Encourage him to take regular breaks using the 20-20-20 rule, which means
every 20 minutes, look away from the screen and focus on an object about 20
feet away for 20 seconds to help reduce eye fatigue.
• Encourage him to limit overall screen time where possible and ensure good
lighting and correct posture when using computers or gaming devices.
• Offer a blood test to rule out myasthenia gravis, specifically a serum anti-
acetylcholine receptor (AChR) antibody test, as ocular myasthenia can
present in this manner.
• Safety net: Advise him to seek urgent medical attention if his symptoms
suddenly get worse, or if he develops a headache, eye pain, drooping of one
eyelid, weakness in any part of the body, or numbness, as these could
indicate a neurological problem.
• Offer follow-up in 4–6 weeks to review progress and discuss any findings from
ophthalmology.

-- 969 of 1282 --

968
Learning Point from this Station
Diplopia refers to the perception of two images of a single object, either all the time
or intermittently. The two images may be separated horizontally, vertically, or both,
depending on which eye muscles or nerves are affected
Diplopia is classified as either monocular or binocular, and distinguishing between
the two is the first essential step in diagnosis:
Monocular diplopia (persists when one eye is closed) usually indicates an ocular
problem, such as a cataract, corneal irregularity, or lens defect.
Binocular diplopia (resolves when one eye is closed) occurs when the eyes are
misaligned, typically due to a muscle or cranial nerve problem affecting eye
movement.
Common Causes of Binocular Diplopia
• Neurological Conditions: Conditions like stroke, brain or head injury,
tumours, and raised intracranial pressure can damage the nerves controlling
eye movement.
• Myasthenia gravis: Causes fluctuating diplopia and ptosis due to fatigable
muscle weakness. Symptoms may vary throughout the day and often worsen
with tiredness or repeated eye movement.
• Convergence insufficiency: A benign and common cause, often worse with
fatigue or prolonged near work, such as reading or extended screen use.
• Strabismus (squint): Misalignment of the eyes, present from birth or
acquired later, is a frequent cause
• Multiple sclerosis (MS): May present with optic neuritis (pain on eye
movement and blurred or altered vision) or diplopia secondary to internuclear
ophthalmoplegia.
• Systemic Diseases: Diabetes and Graves' disease can affect the eye
muscles and nerves, leading to misalignment
• Drug or alcohol intoxication: Can cause temporary diplopia, usually
accompanied by other systemic or behavioural signs.
• Psychosomatic causes: A diagnosis of exclusion, suspected when the
history is inconsistent or symptoms are atypical and do not match objective
findings.
Monocular diplopia is usually caused by refractive errors, corneal or lens problems
such as astigmatism or cataract, or may develop as a complication after eye surgery.

-- 970 of 1282 --

969
It is important to note that myasthenia gravis is an important differential
diagnosis to consider because the diplopia in this condition is fatigable, meaning it
worsens with sustained use of the eye muscles and improves with rest.
Sometimes, ocular myasthenia can be the only early sign of myasthenia gravis,
occasionally lasting for years before more general symptoms appear. However, it is
usually associated with ptosis (drooping of the upper eyelids), and the double vision
is not limited to screen use but can occur during any task that strains the eye
muscles, such as reading. The diplopia often worsens as the day goes on due to
muscle fatigue.
About 50–60% of people who first present with ocular symptoms will later develop
generalised myasthenia gravis, usually within one to two years. However, this
remains an uncommon condition, seen most often in younger women (under 40) and
older men (over 60).
Further Reading
https://patient.info/doctor/history-examination/diplopia-and-iii-iv-and-vi-cranial-nerve-
lesions
https://patient.info/doctor/neurology/myasthenia-gravis-pro

Example of Explanation to the Patient

blood test results, and I can see that your thyroid levels are now much lower than
they should be, which means your thyroid has become underactive. This explains
why you have been feeling tired, cold, and constipated recently.
From looking at your records, it appears that there was an error when your blood test
result was reviewed a few weeks ago. You were advised to increase your
carbimazole dose when, in fact, the blood test showed that your thyroid was already
becoming less active. I am really sorry about that, Ann. I completely understand how
upsetting this must be, especially as it has affected how you feel and your work.
We take errors like this very seriously, Ann. I am not entirely sure why or how this
happened, but I want to reassure you that I will be reporting this formally through our
practice incident reporting system. This means we will investigate exactly what went
wrong and put measures in place to make sure it does not happen again. Are you
following me so far?

support you through that process as well.
Please stop your carbimazole from today, Ann. Would you be happy to come in for
an assessment so that I can check your heart rate, skin, and examine you properly
to make sure you are otherwise well? I will also speak to the specialist
(endocrinology team) urgently today to get their advice about whether you need to
start thyroid hormone replacement, which is a simple tablet called levothyroxine. Are
you following me so far?

975
Learning Point from this Station
This case highlights the professional duty of candour, a key ethical and
professional obligation for all healthcare professionals. When something goes wrong
in a patient’s care that causes, or has the potential to cause, harm or distress,
clinicians must be open, honest, and transparent with the patient.
According to the General Medical Council (GMC), this duty includes:
• Telling the patient (or, where appropriate, their advocate or family) when
something has gone wrong.
• Offering a sincere apology.
• Providing appropriate support or a remedy to put things right where possible.
• Explaining clearly what has happened, what is known and not yet known, and
what the short- and long-term effects may be.
An apology is more than simply saying “sorry.” It involves addressing what the
patient truly needs to hear. Patients expect to be told three things as part of a
genuine apology:
• What happened.
• What can be done to deal with any harm caused.
• What will be done to prevent someone else being harmed in the same way.
Importantly, this conversation should happen as soon as possible once the error is
recognised. Clinicians should not wait until the outcome of a formal investigation
before informing the patient, and a support person (such as a relative or colleague)
should be available for the patient during the discussion.
From a clinical perspective, this case also illustrates the need to recognise and
manage carbimazole-induced hypothyroidism, a potential complication of
antithyroid therapy. When this occurs, the correct management is to:
• Stop carbimazole immediately.
• Seek specialist advice from endocrinology.
The specialist may choose between two main treatment approaches, depending on
the patient’s clinical situation:
Titration regimen: After stopping carbimazole, thyroid function tests (TFTs) are
repeated. If thyroid levels return to normal, a lower dose of carbimazole may be
restarted. The dose is then adjusted regularly based on free thyroxine (FT4) levels,
with the goal of maintaining euthyroidism on the lowest effective dose.

-- 977 of 1282 --

976
Block-and-replace regimen: In this approach, a steady dose of carbimazole is
continued to block thyroid hormone production, while levothyroxine is added to
replace thyroid hormone and maintain normal thyroid function.
Further Reading
https://www.gmc-uk.org/professional-standards/the-professional-standards/candour--
-openness-and-honesty-when-things-go-wrong/the-professional-duty-of-candour
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/hyperthyroidism/management/management/

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Confirm with him that, according to his notes, he and his wife have been trying
