been trying to conceive for about two years, and you were advised to have a
semen analysis done. Is that correct?
• Ask whether he followed the laboratory instructions correctly before the
semen test, like maintaining sexual abstinence for at least two days and no
longer than seven days before giving the sample, and ensuring the specimen
was transported to the lab promptly under the correct conditions.
• Ask if he has any children or has ever fathered a pregnancy from his current
or any previous relationship.
• Signpost sensitively: “I am going to ask you some personal questions now, is
that alright?”
• Ask if he is sexually active, and if so, the frequency of sexual intercourse.
• Ask whether he has multiple sexual partners.
• Ask if he has ever been diagnosed or treated for a sexually transmitted
infection (STI).

-- 981 of 1282 --

980
• Ask if he has any problems with erection or ejaculation.
• Ask whether he has noticed any bending or curvature of the penis.
• Ask if he has noticed any lumps, swelling, or bumps in the testicles.
• Ask if he has noticed any enlargement of breast tissue (gynaecomastia).
• Ask if he had mumps during childhood or adolescence.
• Ask if he has ever had any operations or injuries involving the genital area.
• Ask about his occupation, and specifically whether he drives long distances,
uses heated seats, or wears tight underwear, as these can affect sperm
production and quality.
• Ask about current stress levels and whether this has affected his relationship
or sexual performance.
• Ask whether there has been any recent illness, as this can temporarily affect
semen quality
• Ask about social and lifestyle factors, including exercise, diet, weight,
smoking, alcohol consumption, and recreational drug use.
• Ask if he has any other medical conditions or is taking any prescribed, over-
the-counter, or herbal medications that the practice may not be aware of.
• Inform the patient that the results show low sperm concentration and reduced
motility, findings consistent with oligoasthenozoospermia.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
David, thank you for explaining everything. I have had a look at your results, and
what they show is that both the number of sperm and the way they move are a little
below the normal range.
Let me explain what that means. When we check a semen sample, we look at a few
key things like, how many sperm are present, how well they move, and how healthy
they look. In your case, there are fewer sperm than average, and the ones that are
there are not moving as actively as we would normally expect.
This can sometimes make it take longer for a sperm to reach and fertilise an egg,
which can make conceiving a bit more difficult, but it does not mean you cannot have
children. Does that make sense so far?
There are quite a few things that can affect sperm count and movement, and often it
is not just one single cause.

-- 982 of 1282 --

981
Sometimes lifestyle factors play a big role. For example, being overweight, not
exercising enough, smoking, or drinking alcohol can lower sperm quality. Spending
long hours sitting down, especially when driving or using heated seats, can also
raise the temperature around the testicles, which can reduce sperm production.
Stress, lack of sleep, or even a recent illness can temporarily affect sperm as well. In
some men, it can be due to hormone changes or problems with the testicles, but that
is less common. Are you following me so far?
What we can do next is repeat the semen analysis in about three months. This is
because it takes roughly three months for your body to produce a full new cycle of
sperm. Doing the test again will help us see if the first result was just a one-off
change or if it shows a consistent pattern, because as I mentioned earlier, semen
quality can sometimes be affected by temporary factors such as stress or illness.
Does that make sense?
Meanwhile, there are a few lifestyle changes we can talk about that can help improve
both your overall health and your sperm quality. You mentioned that you drive long
distances and use heated seats. I would advise taking regular breaks during your
journeys and avoiding heated seats, as prolonged heat exposure can affect sperm
production. Do you think that’s something you could try?
You mentioned earlier that your weight is a little higher than you would like. I
completely understand that making lifestyle changes can be difficult, especially with
your busy job. However, research shows that small, steady steps towards a healthier
diet and a bit more movement can help improve sperm quality and overall wellbeing.
What small changes do you think might be realistic for you to start with?
We can catch up again in about three months to review how you are getting on and
discuss your repeat test results. Does that sound okay?
Management
• Offer a repeat semen analysis in 3 months and explain that this is because it
takes about three months for a full new cycle of sperm production
(spermatogenesis) to occur. This repeat test helps confirm whether the initial
findings are consistent and rules out temporary factors that can affect semen
quality, such as stress or illness.
• Offer general lifestyle advice, including maintaining a healthy weight,
engaging in regular exercise, eating a balanced diet rich in fruits and
vegetables, and managing stress effectively.

-- 983 of 1282 --

982
• As he drives long distances, advise regular breaks during journeys and avoid
the use of heated seats, as prolonged heat exposure can reduce sperm
quality.
• Offer stress management strategies, including talking therapy, calming
breathing and relaxation exercises, yoga, and the use of the Headspace app,
which provides science-based guided meditations and mindfulness
techniques to help reduce stress.
• If the situation is affecting his relationship, offer relationship counselling or
referral to a couples’ support service.
• Arrange follow-up in 3 months to review his progress and discuss the results
of the repeat semen analysis.
Learning Point from this Station
In the United Kingdom, male factors contribute to infertility in approximately
30% of couples, and semen analysis is the key first-line investigation when
assessing male fertility. For general practitioners, understanding how to interpret and
validate semen analysis results is essential for guiding patients appropriately.
It is important to note that specific collection instructions must be followed to
ensure accurate results. The specimen should be collected after a minimum of 2
days and a maximum of 7 days of sexual abstinence. Ideally, the sample should
be produced in a private room near the laboratory to minimise temperature
fluctuations and to control the time between collection and analysis.
If the sample must be collected elsewhere, the container should be kept close to
the body under clothing (for example, in the armpit) to maintain temperature,
and delivered to the laboratory within 30 minutes, but no later than 50 minutes
after collection. The specimen should be obtained by masturbation into a clean,
wide-mouthed, non-toxic plastic container and protected from temperature extremes
(below 20°C or above 37°C).
It is therefore crucial for clinicians to clarify whether these instructions were properly
followed, as failure to do so can affect semen quality and result interpretation.
If abnormalities are detected, the semen analysis should be repeated after three
months, as it takes about one complete spermatogenic cycle (approximately 74
days) to produce new sperm. This allows the clinician to distinguish between a
persistent issue and a temporary variation caused by factors such as stress, illness,
or lifestyle influences.
Referral to a specialist fertility clinic is indicated if any of the following are present:

-- 984 of 1282 --

983
• Two abnormal semen analysis results
• Previous genital pathology
• History of urogenital surgery
• Previous sexually transmitted infection (STI)
• Varicocele
• Significant systemic illness

https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/infertility/management/management/#initial-
investigations-in-men

Patient’s Story (Role Player’s Brief)
You are Trevor Brown, a 29-year-old male.

Over time, these symptoms have become worse, and now your nose feels blocked

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset and duration of symptoms.
• Ask how long he has been using the nasal decongestant spray (Otrivine) and
how frequently he is currently using it.
• Ask whether the nasal blockage and discharge affect one nostril or both.
(Unilateral symptoms are more likely to suggest another cause such as nasal
polyps, foreign body, carcinoma, or cerebrospinal fluid leak.)
• Ask about the colour of the nasal discharge. (Discoloured or purulent
discharge may indicate infection, such as acute bacterial sinusitis.)
• Ask if there are any particular triggers, irritants, or situations that worsen the
symptoms, such as exposure to dust, pollen, perfumes, or animal dander.
(Allergic rhinitis is often triggered by exposure to causative allergens such as
grass, trees, pets, or household dust.)

-- 987 of 1282 --

986
• Ask about associated symptoms such as nasal itching, sneezing, sore throat,
cough, fever, ear pain, facial pain, postnasal drip (sensation of mucus at the
back of the throat), or loss of smell.
• Ask about associated eye symptoms such as itching, redness, or watering, as
these may indicate allergic rhinitis.
• Ask if he has had any recent cold or flu-like illness.
• Ask about red flag symptoms that may suggest mailgnancy such as
nosebleeds, crusting, blood-stained discharge, facial swelling, or visual
symptoms.
• Ask about any personal or family history of atopy, such as eczema or asthma,
which may raise suspicion of allergic rhinitis.
• Ask about the impact of symptoms on sleep, daily activities, and confidence
with public speaking.
• Ask about social history including smoking, alcohol use, occupation, and
home environment.
• Explore his ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Give a diagnosis of rhinitis medicamentosa
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Trevor, thank you for explaining everything so clearly. From what you have told me;
it sounds like you have a condition called rhinitis medicamentosa. Have you heard of
that before?
Let me explain what that means. This condition happens when the nose becomes
dependent on nasal decongestant sprays like Otrivine. These sprays work well for
short-term relief, but if they are used for more than a few days, they can actually
make the congestion worse rather than better. What happens is that the lining inside
your nose becomes swollen again as soon as the effect of the spray wears off, so
you feel blocked and end up using it more and more. Does that make sense so far?
The good news is that this can be reversed. The main treatment is to stop using the
decongestant spray so that your nasal passages can recover. I know that may feel
difficult at first because your nose may feel more blocked for a few days, but things
will gradually start to improve.
You mentioned steroid tablets, and you are right that they can sometimes help in
treating nasal inflammation. However, we usually do not start with oral steroids
unless the nasal steroid sprays have been tried properly and have not worked. This
is because oral steroids can have more side effects, such as weight gain, mood

-- 988 of 1282 --

987
changes, and effects on blood sugar and bones, while the nasal spray acts only in
the nose and has far fewer side effects. Does that make sense so far?
So, I would like you to restart your steroid nasal spray (mometasone) and use it
every day for at least 4 to 6 weeks. It takes time to work, but it will gradually help
reduce the swelling and inflammation inside your nose so that you can breathe more
easily. Would you be happy to give this another try?
You can also use saline nasal sprays or drops, which are completely safe and can
help keep your nose clear if you feel congested.
You asked about how to use the nasal spray correctly, let me explain that. Take off
the cap, hold the spray in the opposite hand to the nostril you are going to use, tilt
your head slightly forward, place the nozzle just inside your nostril, and aim it slightly
outwards, away from the centre of your nose before spraying. That helps the
medicine reach the right part of the nasal passage.
I would like to see you again in about 4 to 6 weeks to check how you are getting on.
If your symptoms suddenly get worse, or if you notice pain, swelling, or blood-stained
discharge, please get in touch sooner.
Management
• Explain the diagnosis of rhinitis medicamentosa, clarifying that this is a
rebound nasal congestion caused by prolonged use of nasal decongestant
sprays such as Otrivine (xylometazoline).
• Offer a face-to-face review to examine his nasal passages and check for any
nasal polyps, masses, or other concerning findings.
• Advise that the main treatment is to stop using the nasal decongestant spray,
as continuing it will make symptoms persist or worsen.
• Advise the patient to restart the steroid nasal spray (mometasone) daily and
to use it consistently for at least 4–6 weeks to help reduce inflammation and
congestion. Explain that symptom relief may not be immediate and can take
several weeks to become noticeable.
• Offer saline nasal sprays or drops as a safe, non-medicated option to help
relieve congestion and dryness while symptoms settle.
• Demonstrate or explain the correct nasal spray technique: advise the patient
to remove the cap, hold the spray in the opposite hand to the nostril being
used, tilt the head slightly forward, place the nozzle just inside the nostril, and
aim it slightly outwards (away from the centre of the nose) before spraying.

-- 989 of 1282 --

988
• Offer a follow-up appointment in 4–6 weeks to review progress and symptom
improvement.
• Provide safety-netting advice to seek review if symptoms persist despite
treatment, worsen, or if he develops new symptoms such as facial pain,
swelling, or blood-stained discharge.
Learning Point from this Station
Rhinitis medicamentosa, also known as rebound nasal congestion, is inflammation
and swelling of the nasal lining caused by the prolonged use of topical nasal
decongestant sprays (such as Otrivine or xylometazoline). These sprays are
effective for short-term relief, but when used for more than a few days (7- 10 days),
they lead to a cycle of dependency where congestion worsens once the spray wears
off.
The main treatment is to stop using the decongestant spray completely. Patients
should be counselled that their nasal congestion may temporarily worsen after
stopping, and this should be explained clearly so that they do not interpret it as a
treatment failure.
Intranasal corticosteroid sprays (such as mometasone or fluticasone) can be used to
reduce inflammation and ease the rebound symptoms. Saline nasal sprays or drops
are also helpful to soothe and clear the nasal passages while the mucosa recovers.
In more severe or resistant cases, a short course of oral corticosteroids (for example,
prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg for 5 days) may be considered.
The key to successful management lies in patient education, ensuring they
understand that consistent use of steroid sprays and avoidance of topical
decongestants will allow the nasal mucosa to heal and restore normal breathing.
Further Reading
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538318/

not sure if you have had a chance to look at it yet. It is really painful, and I would like
some treatment for it, please.
If Asked to Explain Further: You first noticed a burning pain about seven days ago.
Then, around two days later (five days ago), a rash appeared in the same area on
your back. The rash is painful and feels like it is burning and throbbing. The pain has
been getting worse and is now disturbing your sleep. You have tried ibuprofen and
paracetamol, but they have not helped much.
You have not been in contact with anyone who has a similar rash. However, you
returned from Ghana about a week ago, where you were involved in some charity
work and visited a few unwell people. You are now worried that you may have picked
up an infection from them.
Social History: You live alone and work as a businessman, running an African food

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset of symptoms, including when the pain first started and
when the rash appeared.
• Ask if the pain came before the rash, as shingles pain typically starts a few
days before the rash appears.
• Ask if the rash is present anywhere else on the body. (Note: shingles affecting
the head, neck, or eye area requires same-day hospital assessment.)
• Ask about systemic symptoms such as fever, headache, malaise, or fatigue.
• Ask if new blisters (vesicles) are still appearing. This is important because
even if the patient has missed the initial 72-hour window for antiviral
treatment, antivirals can still be offered if they present within 7 days of rash
onset and new blisters are continuing to form.
• Ask if there is any discharge or oozing from the rash (which could suggest
secondary infection).
• Ask about contact with anyone with a similar rash.
• Ask how this has affected his daily life, including sleep, ability to work, and
general comfort.
• Ask about social history, including smoking and alcohol use.
• Ask if he has any other medical problems or is taking any regular or over-the-
counter medications not recorded in his notes.
• Ask about his ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a diagnosis of Shingles
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you, Koffi, for explaining everything so clearly. From what you have
described, and from the photo you sent, this looks very much like shingles, also
known as herpes zoster. Are you familiar with that term at all?
Shingles happens when a virus called the varicella zoster virus, the same one that
causes chickenpox, becomes active again after many years. It can stay quietly in the
body after someone has had chickenpox before, and for reasons we do not always

-- 993 of 1282 --

992
understand, it can wake up later in life and cause a painful rash like this. Does that
make sense so far?
You have not caught this from anyone, so please do not worry about that. Shingles
comes from a reactivation of your own previous chickenpox infection, not from
another person. However, while the rash is still open and weeping, it is contagious to
people who have never had chickenpox, they could catch chickenpox from you. So,
it is best to avoid close contact with people who are pregnant, newborn babies, or
anyone with a weak immune system until the rash has fully crusted over.
You mentioned that your daughter is pregnant and asked whether it is safe to visit
her. Can I just check if you know whether she has ever had chickenpox before?
Since your daughter had chickenpox as a child, she will already have immunity and
is protected, which is reassuring. However, I would still advise taking extra caution —
if possible, try to avoid direct skin contact with her until your rash has completely
dried and crusted over, just to be on the safe side. Another reason to be cautious is
that there may be other people at the event, such as pregnant women, young
children, or people with weakened immune systems, who could become unwell if
exposed. For that reason, it would be best to take extra precautions to prevent any
possible spread until your rash has fully healed.
As you have had this rash for about five days now, antiviral tablets such as aciclovir
may not be as effective, since they work best when started within the first three days
of the rash appearing. However, we can still manage your pain and help make you
more comfortable. I can prescribe a medication called amitriptyline, which is often
used to treat the nerve pain that can happen with shingles.
This medicine can sometimes cause drowsiness or dizziness, so it is important not to
drive or operate machinery if you feel this way. Most people tolerate it very well, but
if any side effects become troublesome, please let us know straight away so we can
review it for you.
Would you be happy to give this medication a try?
Keep the rash clean and dry, wear loose-fitting clothes to avoid irritation, and cover
any areas not under clothing until they have dried out. This helps prevent infection
and protects others.
If the pain gets worse, the rash spreads or starts oozing yellow fluid, or if you
develop any rash around your eyes, on your forehead, or changes in vision, please
contact the surgery or NHS 111 urgently.
Once the rash settles, you may notice the skin remains sensitive or sore for a while.
If this happens, we can review and adjust your treatment. And yes, when you turn
65, you will still be eligible for the shingles vaccine, which helps lower the risk of it
coming back.

-- 994 of 1282 --

993
Management
• No need for a face-to-face assessment as the rash and history are typical of
shingles.
• Offer a choice of amitriptyline, duloxetine, gabapentin, or pregabalin for pain
control, depending on suitability and patient preference.
• Advise the patient that antiviral medication may not be useful at this stage, as
he has presented after the 72-hour window where antivirals are most
effective.
• Advise on skin care: wear loose-fitting clothing to reduce irritation, keep the
rash clean and dry to lower the risk of infection, and cover any exposed
lesions while the rash is still weeping.
• Explain that it is unlikely he caught shingles from anyone, as shingles cannot
be caught from another person. Instead, shingles is caused by reactivation of
the varicella-zoster virus, which remains dormant in the body after a previous
chickenpox infection. However, a person with shingles can transmit
chickenpox to someone who has never had chickenpox or the vaccine, if they
come into direct contact with the rash.
• Advise him to avoid skin contact with people at high risk of complications,
such as immunocompromised individuals, newborn babies, elderly people,
and those who have never had chickenpox (especially pregnant women) until
the rash has fully crusted over.
• Reassure him that since his daughter had chickenpox as a child, she is
protected and unlikely to catch it from him. However, as a precaution, he
should avoid direct skin contact with her until the lesions have crusted over.
Explain that another reason to be cautious is that there may be other people
at the event, such as pregnant women, young children, or individuals with
weakened immune systems, so it would be best to take extra precautions to
prevent any possible spread.
• Explain that he can still receive the shingles vaccine at age 65, even though
he has already had shingles, as this helps reduce the risk of recurrence.
• Advise him to contact the practice or NHS 111 if the pain becomes worse, if
he develops any new eye symptoms (such as a rash near the eyes, forehead,
or nose), or if the rash spreads or becomes infected. Signs of infection to look
out for include redness spreading beyond the rash, swelling, warmth, yellow
discharge or pus, or if the area becomes very tender to touch.

-- 995 of 1282 --

994
Learning Point from this Station
Shingles (herpes zoster) is caused by reactivation of the varicella-zoster virus,
which lies dormant in the sensory nerve roots after a previous chickenpox infection.
It presents as a painful, blistering rash that typically affects one side of the body in
a single dermatome. The pain often starts a few days before the rash appears.
Antiviral therapy (aciclovir, valaciclovir, or famciclovir) can reduce the severity and
duration of symptoms and lower the risk of complications such as post-herpetic
neuralgia when started early.
Antivirals are most effective when started within 72 hours of the shingles rash
appearing because they work by inhibiting the virus during its active replication
phase. After this 72-hour window, the virus has usually replicated significantly, and
treatment provides limited benefit for otherwise healthy individuals. However,
patients with weakened immune systems (for example, those with cancer, HIV/AIDS,
or on immunosuppressive medication) may continue to experience viral replication
for longer, so antiviral treatment remains crucial for them.
Antivirals should be offered within 72 hours of rash onset to:
• People aged 50 years and over.
• People who are immunocompromised (depending on the degree of
immunosuppression).
• People with moderate or severe pain or a moderate or severe rash.
• People with non-truncal involvement (except those with head or neck
involvement, who should be referred for urgent specialist assessment).
• People with predisposing skin conditions (such as eczema).
If it is not possible to start treatment within 72 hours, antiviral therapy may still be
considered up to 7 days after rash onset, especially in those at higher risk of
complications, for example:
• Continued appearance of new vesicles.
• Signs of disseminated or neurological involvement.
• Older age (≥65 years).
• Immunocompromised individuals.
• Severe uncontrolled pain.
Intravenous aciclovir should be given to those who are severely
immunocompromised or have widespread or disseminated disease.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/shingles/

-- 996 of 1282 --

995
Station 194: Video Consultation
Patient’s Name: Jonathan Fernsby

Patient’s Story (Role Player’s Brief)

ONLY SAY BELOW IF ASKED

Social History: You live with your wife and are a retired builder. You do not smoke,
drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs.
Family History: There is no family history of heart problems or sudden cardiac

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Show that you have reviewed the patient’s notes before starting the
consultation. For example: “Jonathan, I can see from the hospital letter that
during your pre-operative assessment, they found your heart rate to be slower
than normal, is that right?”
• Ask if he has ever been told before that his heart rate is slow.

-- 998 of 1282 --

997
• Ask if he has felt unwell recently, to identify any underlying or acute illnesses
such as viral infections with diarrhoea or vomiting that might cause electrolyte
disturbances and contribute to bradycardia.
• Ask about any dizziness or light-headedness.
• Ask about fatigue or tiredness.
• Ask if there is any history of fainting or collapse.
• Ask about any chest pain, which could indicate underlying cardiac pathology.
• Ask about shortness of breath, especially during exertion. It's important to
assess exercise tolerance, as athletes with physiological bradycardia typically
maintain good exercise capacity. If a patient can perform moderate physical
activity without distress, their bradycardia is less likely to be pathological.
• Ask about palpitations or awareness of the heart beating fast or irregularly, as
this can indicate intermittent tachyarrhythmias such as sick sinus syndrome or
tachy-brady syndrome, where slow and fast rhythms alternate.
• Ask about any family history of heart disease or sudden cardiac death.
• Ask about smoking, alcohol, and recreational drug use (e.g., cocaine, which
can cause arrhythmias).
• Ask about over-the-counter medications or supplements (some may affect
heart rate).
• Ask about occupation, physical activity, and whether he has been a
competitive athlete, to help distinguish physiological from pathological
bradycardia.
• Make a working diagnosis of bradycardia.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Jonathan, thank you for coming in today. I can see you are understandably
concerned, especially with your knee operation coming up soon. Let me explain what
this finding means and what we can do next.
At your hospital appointment, they found that your heart rate was around 45 beats
per minute. For most people, a normal heart rate is usually between 60 and 100.
Having a slower rate like yours is called bradycardia. Is that something you have
heard of before?
Sometimes this can be completely normal, especially in people who are quite fit. In
other cases, it can happen because the electrical system of the heart is working a bit
slower than usual, or because of an underlying medical condition or medication
effect. Are you following me so far?
The good news is that you are feeling well and have not had any dizziness, chest
pain, or fainting, which is very reassuring. However, because you are due to have an
operation, we need to be sure that it is safe for you to proceed.

-- 999 of 1282 --

998
What I would like to do is repeat your heart tracing here in the practice to double-
check what the hospital found. I would also like to examine you and listen to your
heart sounds. If there are any extra heartbeats or murmurs, I will arrange a jelly scan
of your heart called an echocardiogram.
In addition, would it be okay if I arrange some blood tests, including one to check
your thyroid function? The thyroid is a small gland in the front of your neck that helps
control your body’s energy levels, and if it is not working properly, it can sometimes
cause the heart to beat more slowly. Are you following me so far? Are you happy for
me to arrange an appointment for these tests?
If your heart rate remains slow, I will arrange for you to wear a small heart monitor
for 24 hours. It is a simple device that records your heartbeat throughout the day
while you go about your normal activities. This helps us see if your heartbeat ever
pauses or skips, or if it changes in an unusual way.
Because you are due to have an operation, we will also discuss your results with a
heart specialist to get their advice. They can tell us whether you need any further
tests or if they would like to see you in their clinic before your surgery. Does that
sound reasonable to you?
If you develop any symptoms such as dizziness, fainting, chest pain, or shortness of
breath, please seek urgent medical help. Otherwise, we will arrange the ECG and
blood tests today, and I will be in touch once we have the results. Does that make
sense?
Management
• Offer the patient a repeat 12-lead ECG to confirm sinus bradycardia and to
check for any conduction abnormalities such as heart block.
• Arrange blood tests, including thyroid function tests, as hypothyroidism can
lead to bradycardia.
• Explain that if the repeat ECG still shows bradycardia, a 24-hour (or longer if
indicated) ambulatory ECG monitor will be arranged to assess for other
rhythm abnormalities associated with bradycardia, such as intermittent heart
block.
• Explain that because he is due to have an operation, you will seek advice
from the cardiology team if any further investigations are needed or if they
would like to review him in their clinic.
• Safety-net advice: Advise him to seek urgent medical attention if he develops
fainting or blackouts, severe dizziness, chest pain, shortness of breath,
confusion, or new or irregular palpitations.
• Inform the patient that you will arrange a follow-up appointment once the test
results are available and after receiving advice from the cardiology team.

-- 1000 of 1282 --

999
Learning Point from this Station
Bradycardia is defined in adults as a pulse rate below 60 beats per minute.
However, most individuals remain asymptomatic unless the heart rate falls below 50
beats per minute. While some cases are benign and physiological, others may be
pathological and require further investigation.
Sinus bradycardia may be physiological (a normal variation) and is often
associated with a high resting vagal tone, such as in endurance athletes and as part
of the diving reflex. However, it can also be pathological, resulting from underlying
cardiac, metabolic, drug-related, or systemic causes.
Common causes of pathological sinus bradycardia include:
• Drugs (negative chronotropes): Beta-blockers, calcium-channel blockers,
digoxin, amiodarone, clonidine, and verapamil.
• Metabolic and endocrine causes: Hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency,
severe obstructive jaundice.
• Cardiac causes: Acute myocardial infarction, sick sinus syndrome, pericardial
tamponade.
• Physiological or reflex causes: Hypoxia, hypothermia, pain, or Cushing’s
reflex (a systemic response to raised intracranial pressure causing
bradycardia, hypertension, and irregular breathing).
• Infective causes (rare): For example, relative bradycardia in typhoid fever.
All symptomatic bradycardia, significant arrhythmias, or heart rates below 50 beats
per minute should be discussed with a cardiologist.
Most patients with symptomatic bradycardia require same-day hospital admission for
monitoring, ECG assessment, and possible pacing if haemodynamic compromise is
suspected.
Further Reading
https://patient.info/doctor/history-examination/bradycardia

• Ask how the diarrhoea has affected his daily activities and quality of life

Example of Explanation to the Patient
William, thank you for speaking with me today. I completely understand how
uncomfortable this diarrhoea has been for you, especially if it is stopping you from
getting out and socialising with friends. Let me explain what could be going on and
what we are going to do to help.
You mentioned that you ate an expired chicken wrap on the day you got back from
hospital. That certainly could be relevant. Food poisoning from spoiled food can
sometimes cause short-term diarrhoea and cramping. However, given that you were
also recently admitted to hospital and treated with strong antibiotics, we have to
consider another possibility too.
Antibiotics can disturb the normal balance of bacteria in the gut, which sometimes
allows a particular bug, called Clostridioides difficile, or C. difficile, to grow. This can
cause diarrhoea, particularly in people who have recently been in hospital or taken
antibiotics. Are you following me so far? Do you have any questions?
To get to the bottom of this, I would like to send off a stool sample to check for this
infection. Would you be able to come to the surgery to collect a sample pot from
reception, or is there someone who could do that for you?
I can also see from your notes that your Metformin and Ramipril were stopped in
hospital because there was some strain on your kidneys at the time. You mentioned
that you restarted them at home, which is completely understandable if you were not
told why they had been stopped.
However, during an illness like this, especially with the risk of dehydration, those
medications can put further pressure on your kidneys. So, I would advise you to stop
both Metformin and Ramipril for now until you have fully recovered from this illness
and are eating and drinking normally again. Does all of that make sense so far?

-- 1005 of 1282 --

1004
You also asked about Amlodipine. That medication can usually be continued, as it
does not affect the kidneys in the same way as Metformin or Ramipril. Can I just
check if you have a blood pressure monitor at home, or whether the nurse who took
your blood today measured your blood pressure? The reason I ask is that when
people have diarrhoea and lose fluids, their blood pressure can sometimes drop.
Since you have said you are not feeling dizzy or light-headed and are still able to
drink well, it is reasonable to continue your Amlodipine for now. However, please
monitor how you are feeling. If you do start to feel dizzy, light-headed, or struggle to
drink enough, we may need to stop it as well
In terms of relief for your symptoms, I would advise not taking loperamide at this
point. Because we are considering C. difficile. Loperamide could make things worse
by slowing your gut down and trapping harmful toxins inside.
The most important thing is to keep drinking fluids regularly. If you are able to, using
oral rehydration salts can help replace lost fluids and salts and reduce the risk of
dehydration. Would you feel comfortable trying that?
It is also important to know that diarrhoea can be passed from one person to
another, so we should discuss some steps to help reduce the risk of spreading it.
You should wash your hands thoroughly with warm water and soap, especially after
using the toilet and before eating. It is also important to regularly clean toilet seats,
flush handles, taps, surfaces, and door handles. If possible, try to avoid preparing
food for others while you are unwell. You should also stay away from public places
or group settings until at least 48 hours after your diarrhoea has fully stopped. Is that
all right with you?
If at any point you are unable to keep fluids down, feel faint or dizzy, notice that you
are not passing urine as you normally would, see blood in your stools, or develop
severe tummy pain, please seek urgent medical attention straight away. You can do
this by calling 111 if it is out of hours or contacting our urgent line if the practice is
open.
Management
• Offer a stool culture, and ask the patient if he can collect a sample pot from
the GP surgery reception or if someone can collect it on his behalf
• Explain that the reason for sending a stool sample is to check for a specific
bacteria called Clostridioides difficile (C. difficile), which can sometimes cause
diarrhoea, particularly after recent antibiotic treatment.
• Advise the patient to stop Metformin and Ramipril temporarily, in line with sick
day rules, due to the risk of dehydration and further worsening of kidney
function.

-- 1006 of 1282 --

1005
• Advise that Amlodipine can usually be continued, as it does not affect kidney
function in the same way as Ramipril or Metformin. However, it should be
stopped if the patient is unable to maintain adequate fluid intake or develops
symptoms such as dizziness or low blood pressure.
• Advise against taking loperamide, as we are concerned about possible C.
difficile. Explain that loperamide is an anti-motility medicine that slows gut
movement, which can be dangerous in this context because it may trap
harmful toxins in the bowel
• Encourage regular fluid intake to prevent dehydration, and suggest using oral
rehydration salts (ORS) if available
• Advise on methods to prevent transmission of infection, including thorough
handwashing with liquid soap and warm running water, and drying hands
completely
• Advise that he should not attend social or public settings (including group
gatherings or community centres) until at least 48 hours after the last episode
of diarrhoea
• Reassure the patient that in most cases, diarrhoea should settle within one to
two weeks
• Provide safety-netting advice and instruct the patient to seek urgent medical
attention if he is unable to keep fluids down, feel dizzy or light-headed,
experience palpitations, notice reduced urine output, see blood in the stool, or
develop severe abdominal pain.
• Arrange a follow-up appointment once stool culture results are available and
after receiving microbiology advice to determine next steps in management.
Learning Point from this Station
This station highlights the importance of managing clinical uncertainty in primary
care. The patient presents with acute diarrhoea following the ingestion of an expired
chicken wrap, which raises the possibility of food poisoning. However, the clinical
context is more complex, as he was recently discharged from hospital following
treatment with broad-spectrum antibiotics, placing him at increased risk of
Clostridioides difficile (C. difficile) infection.
While food poisoning remains a possible cause, C. difficile infection is a significant
differential diagnosis that must not be overlooked, given the patient’s age, recent
hospital admission, and exposure to broad-spectrum antibiotics. C. difficile typically
presents with watery diarrhoea and abdominal cramping, but if not recognised and
managed early, it can lead to serious complications such as dehydration, colitis, or
even sepsis.

-- 1007 of 1282 --

1006
In most cases of uncomplicated acute diarrhoea of less than seven days' duration,
stool culture is not routinely indicated. However, a stool sample should be sent when
there are specific red flags, such as:
• Suspected C. difficile infection (recent antibiotic use, hospitalisation, or proton
pump inhibitor use)
• Presence of blood or mucus in the stool
• The patient is systemically unwell
• The patient is immunocompromised
• There is suspected food poisoning
• The patient has recently travelled abroad to any region outside Western
Europe, North America, Australia, or New Zealand, for example, parts of Asia,
Africa, South America, or Eastern Europe, where the risk of infectious
diarrhoea is higher.
Dehydration is a significant risk in patients taking certain medications, especially
during acute illness. This case also reinforces the importance of applying sick day
rules, using the SADMAN mnemonic to identify medicines that should be
temporarily withheld during periods of vomiting, diarrhoea, or poor oral intake.
• S – SGLT2 inhibitors (e.g. Dapagliflozin, Canagliflozin)
• A – ACE inhibitors (e.g. Ramipril, Captopril, Lisinopril)
• D – Diuretics (e.g. Furosemide, Bendroflumethiazide, Bumetanide)
• M – Metformin
• A – ARBs (e.g. Losartan, Candesartan)
• N – NSAIDs (e.g. Ibuprofen, Naproxen)
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/diarrhoea-antibiotic-associated/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/gastroenteritis/

progresses.
Example of Explanation to the Patient’s Wife
Laura, thank you for taking the time to speak with me today. I understand this is an
incredibly difficult period for you and your family, and I want to reassure you that we
are here to support you every step of the way.
From the hospital letter, I can see that Jack has now been identified as requiring
palliative care, which means the focus of his care is now on keeping him as
comfortable as possible. You mentioned that you have already been giving him
Oramorph through the PEG tube for his pain, and I am glad to hear it has been
helping him.
Let me go through the other medications with you, so you feel confident about what
they are for and when they might be used. These are what we often refer to as
'anticipatory' or 'just in case' medications. They are commonly prescribed for people
who are being cared for at home in the later stages of life to manage any symptoms
that may arise.
• Oramorph (liquid morphine) is for pain or breathlessness and, as you are
already doing, it can be given via his PEG tube.

-- 1012 of 1282 --

1011
The other medications are given as small injections under the skin.
• Hyoscine butylbromide is for noisy breathing caused by secretions.
• Levomepromazine helps with nausea or vomiting.
• Midazolam can be used if Jack becomes very anxious, agitated, or restless.
At the moment, I understand he is not experiencing these symptoms, which is very
reassuring, but it is good to be prepared so that if anything changes, we are not
caught off guard. Are you following me so far? Do you have any questions so far?
I will ask the district nurses to visit you at home to go through these medications with
you in more detail. They will be able to give the injections if and when they are
needed. If you feel comfortable, they can also show you how to give them yourself,
just in case there is ever a time when help is not immediately available. They will
also support you with managing the PEG feeding system and can assist with any
other nursing needs Jack may have. Is that all alright with you?
You also mentioned that Jack would prefer to die at home. That is a very personal
and important wish. I understand your concern about how this might affect your
children, and I really appreciate how much thought you are giving to both his wishes
and their wellbeing.
What we can do is arrange a best interest discussion involving you (as his LPA),
myself, the district nurses, and the community palliative care team. This will allow us
to make a clear plan together, aiming to honour Jack’s preferences while also
considering what is safe, practical, and emotionally manageable for the family. What
are your thoughts on that?
We can also refer you to the Macmillan team. They are very experienced in
supporting families through situations like this. They can offer counselling and
emotional support for you and also help you gently prepare your children in an age-
appropriate and sensitive way. Is this something you feel would be helpful for you
and your family?
I will complete an SR1 form, which helps support benefit claims under the special
rules for people in Jack’s situation, where his condition is advancing, and he needs
increasing support. This can help ensure you have financial support in place as
things progress. I will also arrange a referral to occupational therapy. They can visit
your home and recommend any adjustments or equipment that might help make
things easier and keep Jack as comfortable as possible. Does that all sound okay
with you?

-- 1013 of 1282 --

1012
Management
• Provide a clear and simple explanation of each anticipatory medication, often
referred to as 'just in case' medications. Explain that these are prescribed to
manage symptoms that may arise in the final stages of life and to ensure
comfort. For example, morphine is used to relieve pain and breathlessness
and can be given through his PEG tube. In the future, if needed, it can also be
delivered continuously via a syringe driver.
• Explain that the other medications are given by subcutaneous injection.
Hyoscine butylbromide helps with respiratory secretions which may cause
noisy or wet breathing. Levomepromazine is used to treat nausea and
vomiting. Midazolam is used for anxiety, restlessness, or agitation.
• Inform her that you will refer to the district nursing team, who will be able to
assist with administering the injectable medications and can also teach her
how to give them if she feels comfortable doing so. They will also support with
ongoing PEG tube care.
• Explain that in the future, a syringe driver may be used. This is a small
battery-operated pump that delivers continuous medication under the skin to
manage symptoms effectively when swallowing or multiple injections become
difficult.
• Acknowledge her husband’s expressed wish to die at home and offer to
arrange a best interests meeting involving yourself as the GP, the community
palliative care team, the district nurses, and herself as his Lasting Power of
Attorney. This meeting will allow for a shared care plan to be developed,
taking into account his wishes while also ensuring that the plan is safe,
practical, and manageable for the family.
• Offer to refer the family to the Macmillan service, who provide experienced
palliative care nurses and bereavement counsellors. They can also help
support the children by gently preparing them for what to expect and helping
them understand the situation. If the decision is made for him to remain at
home, Macmillan can assist in guiding the family through this, including
preparing them for the possibility of death at home.
• Inform her about the SR1 form, which is used to support benefit claims under
the Special Rules for End-of-Life Care for people living with a terminal illness.
Explain that completing this form can help ensure fast-tracked access to
financial support from the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP), which
may help with aspects of her husband’s ongoing care needs. Offer to
complete and submit the SR1 form on her behalf.
• Offer a referral to Occupational Therapy, who can visit the home to assess for
any practical modifications or equipment that may help keep her husband safe
and comfortable. This might include adaptations such as a hospital bed,
hoists, commodes, or pressure-relieving mattresses.

-- 1014 of 1282 --

1013
• Provide her with the dedicated contact numbers used for palliative care
patients, allowing her to have direct and timely access to the GP and district
nursing team during working hours for any concerns or support related to her
husband's care.
• Arrange a follow-up appointment or home visit within the next few days to
ensure the appropriate teams have been in touch and that she feels
supported going forward.
Learning Point from this Station
This station highlights the complexities of delivering holistic palliative care in the
community. It reinforces the importance of clear communication with families,
anticipatory care planning, and a multidisciplinary approach to end-of-life care.
In end-of-life and palliative prescribing, medications are aimed at managing
distressing physical symptoms such as pain, breathlessness, respiratory secretions,
agitation, nausea, and vomiting.
There are two main categories of prescribing:
• Just-in-case medications: Administered on an as-required (PRN) basis via
subcutaneous injection.
• Syringe drivers: Also referred to as continuous subcutaneous infusions
(CSCI), are used when the patient can no longer tolerate oral medication or
when symptoms are not adequately controlled with intermittent ‘just-in-case’
injections.
Commonly used medications include:
• For pain and breathlessness: Morphine or oxycodone.
• For anxiety, agitation, or restlessness: Midazolam.
• For nausea and vomiting: Haloperidol or levomepromazine.
• For respiratory secretions: Hyoscine butylbromide.
This station also highlights the sensitive and emotional difficulty of respecting a
patient’s wish to die at home while also considering what is safe and emotionally
manageable for their family. Organising a best interests meeting involving the GP,
palliative care team, and family can help develop a clear plan that honours the
patient’s preferences while ensuring that the emotional and practical needs of those
around them are supported. If a Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA) is in place, they
can formally represent the patient's voice in these discussions.

-- 1015 of 1282 --

1014
Emotional support for the main caregiver is especially important, particularly when
young children are involved. Preparing children for the death of a parent or close
family member is never easy, and many parents may not know how to start that
conversation. Trained counsellors and psychologists can help guide this process.
Macmillan nurses and local end-of-life counselling services can offer this support to
both the patient and their family.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/palliative-care-general-issues/
https://www.macmillan.org.uk/cancer-information-and-support/treatment/if-you-have-
an-advanced-cancer/end-of-life/preparing-a-child-for-loss

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Start by explaining that you have reviewed her recent notes and test results,
and check that they match her understanding. For example: “I can see you
had your routine diabetes blood tests, and a urine sample was also sent
because there were signs of a urine infection. Is that correct?”
• Ask about diabetic symptoms and possible complications, including increased
thirst, blurred vision, tingling in the hands or feet, and any non‑healing wounds
(particularly on the feet)
• Ask about the presence and onset of urinary symptoms, including frequency,
urgency, stinging/burning, nocturia, suprapubic discomfort, or visible blood in
urine
• Ask about systemic symptoms which may indicate a more severe UTI, such
as fever, rigors, or loin pain
• Ask whether she has had previous UTIs and, if so, how often (to identify
whether this may be a recurrent UTI)
• Ask about adherence to diabetic medications and whether she is experiencing
any side effects
• Ask about lifestyle factors including occupation, diet, exercise, smoking, and
alcohol intake
• Ask about the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a diagnosis of urinary tract infection in the context of poorly controlled
type 2 diabetes.

-- 1019 of 1282 --

1018
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you for attending today, Elizabeth, and for explaining everything so clearly.
I have reviewed your recent test results. As you know, we checked your blood sugar
levels, kidney function, and urine sample. The good news is that your kidney function
is completely normal, which is reassuring.
However, your HbA1c, which reflects your average blood sugar levels over the past
three months, has increased from 63 to 73 mmol/mol. This tells us that your diabetes
is currently not well controlled. In addition, the urine sample that was sent to the lab
has confirmed a urinary tract infection (UTI), caused by a common bug called E. coli.
Are you with me so far?
You asked why you might have developed a UTI, especially since it has never
happened before. UTIs are caused when bacteria, usually from the gut, enter the
urinary tract, sometimes through wiping after using the toilet or during sexual activity.
In your case, I suspect that your raised blood sugar levels may have increased your
risk. Poorly controlled diabetes can make it easier for bacteria to grow in the urine,
which raises the likelihood of getting infections like this. Does that make sense?
What we need to do now is treat the infection and take steps to improve your blood
sugar control.
For the UTI, I would like to start you on an antibiotic called nitrofurantoin, 100 mg
twice a day for 3 days. Most people tolerate this well, although some may experience
mild stomach upset. If you feel worse at any point or develop new symptoms, such
as tummy pain, blood in the urine, nausea, or vomiting, please contact the GP
surgery or ring 111 if it is out of hours. Are you happy to go ahead with this
treatment?
Now, regarding your diabetes, you are already on metformin and gliclazide. Because
your HbA1c has gone up, we should consider adding a third medication. One good
option is a tablet called dapagliflozin. This medication helps lower blood sugar levels
and provides added protection for the heart and kidneys.
As with any medicine, there are some possible side effects. You may be at slightly
higher risk of UTIs or vaginal thrush. Very rarely, there is a condition called
Fournier’s gangrene, which is a serious skin infection in the genital area. Symptoms
to watch out for include swelling, redness, or pain around the genitals, fever, or a
general sense of feeling very unwell. It is extremely uncommon, but if you ever
notice anything unusual, please let us know immediately or call 111.
That said, this medication is generally safe and very effective, and not everyone will
get these side effects. Are you comfortable with us trying this medication?
One more thing to be aware of is the ‘sick day rule’. If you are ever unwell with
vomiting, diarrhoea, or not eating or drinking properly, you should temporarily stop

-- 1020 of 1282 --

1019
taking dapagliflozin. This is to prevent dehydration. You can restart the medication
once you are eating and drinking normally again. I will give you written information
about this, is that okay?
Now let's talk a bit about lifestyle. You mentioned that because you work from home,
you are not as physically active during the day. Even something as simple as a 30-
minute walk every day can help lower your blood sugar and improve your overall
health. Do you think this is something you might be able to incorporate?
Also, well done on improving your diet, cooking more meals at home and eating
more vegetables is a great start and will definitely help with managing your diabetes.
Just to check, have you had your annual diabetic foot check and eye screening?
These are important as diabetes can affect the nerves in the feet and the small
vessels in the eyes. We recommend having these done yearly.
And lastly, because diabetes increases your risk of infections, we also recommend
staying up to date with your vaccinations, including the flu jab, pneumococcal
vaccine, and COVID booster. Have you had any of these recently?
Is everything I have said so far clear? Do you have any questions or concerns you
would like to go over together?
Management
• Offer an SGLT2 inhibitor (e.g. dapagliflozin or empagliflozin),
• Inform the patient of potential side effects, including increased risk of urinary
tract infections, genital thrush, and, very rarely, a serious infection called
Fournier’s gangrene. Reassure the patient that not everyone experiences
these side effects and that SGLT2 inhibitors are generally safe and effective
in helping to lower blood sugar. Advise her to contact the GP surgery or call
111 out of hours if she notices any concerning symptoms.
• Also explain the ‘sick day rules’ associated with SGLT2 inhibitors — advise
her to temporarily stop taking the medication if she becomes unwell with
vomiting, diarrhoea, reduced fluid intake, or any acute illness, to reduce the
risk of dehydration or diabetic ketoacidosis. She should only restart the
medication once she has fully recovered and is eating and drinking normally
again.
• Prescribe antibiotics for urinary tract infection based on culture sensitivity. For
example: nitrofurantoin 100 mg modified-release twice daily for 3 days or
Trimethoprim 200 mg twice daily for 3 days.
• Advise self-care measures such as maintaining good hydration.
• Explain that high blood glucose levels increase the risk of urinary tract
infections, and improving blood sugar control will help reduce the risk of
urinary tract infections.

-- 1021 of 1282 --

1020
• Advise on lifestyle measures, including a healthy balanced diet and regular
exercise, as these will help improve blood sugar control.
• Offer health promotion interventions, including routine vaccinations
(pneumococcal, influenza, and COVID), and ensure she is up to date with her
annual diabetic foot and eye checks.
• Arrange follow-up in 3 months for repeat HbA1c and full diabetic review.
• Safety‑net by advising that if urinary symptoms persist or worsen, or if she
develops fever, loin pain, haematuria, abdominal pain, nausea, or vomiting,
she should contact the GP urgently during working hours or call 111 out of
hours.
Learning Point from this Station
This station highlights the importance of holistic diabetes care and the relationship
between poor glycaemic control and increased susceptibility to infections, particularly
urinary tract infections (UTIs). People with type 2 diabetes are more prone to UTIs
for several reasons. High sugar levels in the urine (glycosuria) create an ideal
environment for bacteria to grow. Diabetes can also weaken the immune system
and, in some cases, affect bladder function, making it harder to fully empty the
bladder. All these factors increase the risk of infection.
This case also highlights the importance of recognising and managing poorly
controlled diabetes early, using current clinical guidelines and evidence-based
treatment.
According to NICE guidance, the HbA1c target is:
• 48 mmol/mol or less for patients managing type 2 diabetes with diet and
lifestyle alone, or with a single drug not associated with hypoglycaemia
(such as metformin).
• 53 mmol/molor less for patients on medications that can cause
hypoglycaemia, such as sulfonylureas (e.g., gliclazide).
If a patient’s HbA1c is not adequately controlled on a single medication and
rises to 58 mmol/mol (7.5%) or higher, add a second agent and intensify lifestyle
measures (diet and exercise). At this stage, the HbA1c target becomes 53
mmol/mol.
Note: Some local guidelines recommend an HbA1c target of 53 mmol/mol when a
patient is on two or more oral anti-diabetic medications. This 53 mmol/mol target
is a practical, short-term bridge goal during the treatment transition. It is chosen
because it is safer and more achievable while adjusting therapy, helping to avoid
sudden drops in glucose levels which could cause hypoglycaemia.

-- 1022 of 1282 --

1021
Once the patient is stable on dual therapy, especially if the drugs used have a low
risk of hypoglycaemia (such as metformin combined with an SGLT2 inhibitor like
dapagliflozin), the HbA1c target can be re-personalised. The goal should shift back
to 48 mmol/mol, as long as there is no significant risk of hypoglycaemia.
However, if the patient is on any medication known to cause hypoglycaemia, such as
sulfonylureas or insulin, the safer target of 53 mmol/mol should be maintained.
Adding a New Antidiabetic Medication: Understanding the NICE Threshold
According to NICE guidance, an HbA1c level of 58 mmol/mol (7.5%) is the threshold
at which treatment must be intensified by adding another anti-diabetic medication.
However, in real clinical practice, you are not expected to wait until 58 if a patient is
already above their personalised target.
For example: A patient is on maximum-dose metformin. Their most recent HbA1c is
54 mmol/mol, and their individualised target is 48 mmol/mol. What should you do?
You should not automatically escalate therapy based on the number alone.
Instead:
• Reinforce lifestyle measures (diet, physical activity, weight management).
• Check medication adherence and discuss any barriers.
If, despite this, the patient’s HbA1c remains above their target and they are open to
treatment escalation, it is perfectly appropriate to add a second agent, even if they
have not yet reached 58.
NICE does not prohibit treatment escalation before HbA1c hits 58. It simply sets 58
as the minimum point at which treatment must be intensified. You can act earlier if
clinically justified.
In this case, the patient was already on maximum doses of metformin and gliclazide,
and her HbA1c increased from 63 to 73 mmol/mol, warranting the addition of a third-
line agent.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/diabetes-type-2/

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you for sharing all of that with me, Julie. From what you have described, I can
understand why you are worried about whooping cough, especially with the recent
information shared by the school.
At the moment, based on everything you have told me, David’s symptoms do not
sound typical of whooping cough. He does not have the prolonged bouts of intense
coughing, the whooping noise, or vomiting after coughing, which are some of the
classic signs we look for. He is also otherwise well in himself and still able to go to
school, which is reassuring. So, it is more likely that this is a simple viral cough,
which is very common in children and usually gets better on its own.
You also mentioned that the teachers said several children in his class have
developed a cough. Viral coughs are very common in school settings and tend to
spread easily. It is possible that David and the other children may simply have a viral
upper respiratory infection rather than whooping cough. Are you following me so far?
However, I would like to see David face to face today to listen to his chest and check
him over if that is okay with you. Since you mentioned that you cannot attend today,
we can see him tomorrow instead. Does that work for you?
Because this is likely a viral cough rather than a cough caused by a bacterial
infection, antibiotics are not necessary at this stage. They would not provide any
benefit and may give him unnecessary side effects such as tummy upset and
diarrhoea, which could make the situation more difficult for him. Does that make
sense so far?
You also mentioned that David has not been vaccinated. I fully respect that every
parent makes decisions that they feel are best for their child and I understand that
you made this choice based on information you read online. There are indeed stories
on the internet about vaccinations being linked with autism, and it is understandable
that this can be concerning for parents. I do want to reassure you that there is no
proven link between vaccinations and autism or mental health conditions. Childhood
immunisations are carefully tested for safety and are designed to protect children
from serious diseases. For example, with whooping cough, vaccinated children are
either fully protected or, if they do catch it, they tend to experience a much milder
illness and recover more quickly. Are you following me so far?
If you feel comfortable, we can arrange an appointment for you and David to come in
so that he can have his vaccinations. If you would prefer to read more before making
a decision, I can send you information leaflets from trusted sources such as the NHS
and the World Health Organization. This will allow you to explore the information in
your own time. We can revisit this discussion whenever you are ready.
For now, I recommend plenty of fluids, rest, and paracetamol or ibuprofen if he is
uncomfortable. As he is otherwise well, there is no need to keep him off school.

-- 1026 of 1282 --

1025
Please continue to encourage handwashing and good hygiene to help reduce the
spread of infection.
If you notice any worrying symptoms such as worsening cough, fever, difficulty
breathing, drowsiness, not passing urine as normal, or not drinking well, please do
not hesitate to seek medical advice by contacting us immediately or calling 111 if it is
out of hours.
Management
• Reassure the mother that, based on the history, this does not currently sound
like typical whooping cough, as there is no whooping sound, no prolonged
coughing fits, no vomiting after coughing, and no confirmed contact with a
case of whooping cough.
• Explain that the symptoms are more consistent with a mild viral upper
respiratory tract infection.
• Advise that antibiotics are not needed, as they are only effective for bacterial
infections and this presentation does not suggest one.
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to assess the child further if required or if
symptoms worsen.
• Provide self-care advice including rest, adequate fluid intake, and the use of
paracetamol or ibuprofen if the child is uncomfortable.
• Discuss the importance of routine childhood immunisations and explain that
there is no reliable scientific evidence linking vaccines to autism or mental
health conditions.
• Inform her that vaccines protect children against serious diseases such as
whooping cough, and that vaccinated children who do catch the illness tend to
have a milder form and recover more quickly.
• Offer to send leaflets from reputable sources such as the NHS and World
Health Organization (WHO) so she can read more about the safety and
benefits of vaccination.
• Advise that there is currently no need to keep the child off school if he is well
enough to attend.
• Encourage good hand hygiene to help reduce the spread of infections.
• Provide safety-netting advice: advise the parent to seek urgent medical
attention by contacting the GP promptly or calling 111(if it is out of hours) if
the cough becomes worse, the child develops a high fever, has difficulty
breathing, appears drowsy, is not drinking well, or is not passing urine as
usual.

-- 1027 of 1282 --

Learning Point from this Station
Whooping cough (pertussis) is a notifiable bacterial infection caused by Bordetella
pertussis. The disease typically progresses through three phases: the catarrhal
phase (mild upper respiratory symptoms), the paroxysmal phase (characterised by
intense bouts of coughing, inspiratory whoop, and post-tussive vomiting), and the
convalescent phase (gradual recovery). Immunity from vaccination or previous
infection does not provide lifelong protection, but symptoms tend to be milder in
those with some immunity.
Whooping cough should be suspected if;
A person presents with a cough lasting 14 days or more without an obvious cause,
along with one or more of the following features:
• Paroxysmal coughing episodes
• A high-pitched inspiratory "whoop"
• Vomiting after coughing (post-tussive vomiting)
• Unexplained apnoeic episodes, especially in young infants
Or if a person has symptoms suggestive of whooping cough and:
• Has had close contact with a confirmed case within the last 21 days
• Is part of a group or setting where an outbreak is being investigated, such as
a school where whooping cough is known to be circulating
Management depends on the clinical severity of the case. Hospital admission should
be arranged for individuals with significant breathing difficulties, such as apnoeic
episodes, severe coughing fits (paroxysms), or signs of cyanosis, as well as those
with serious complications like seizures or pneumonia.
If hospital admission is not required, treatment can be provided in primary care.
Macrolide antibiotics, such as azithromycin, clarithromycin, or erythromycin, should
be offered if the cough started within the past 14 days, as this may reduce
transmission and severity of the disease.
In some cases, antibiotics may still be considered up to 21 days after symptom
onset, particularly if the person has close household contacts who fall into a priority
group for public health action. These groups include:
• Unimmunised infants born before 32 weeks of gestation who are younger
than 2 months old, regardless of the mother’s vaccination status.
• Unimmunised infants born after 32 weeks of gestation who are younger than
2 months old and whose mothers did not receive the whooping cough vaccine
after 16 weeks of pregnancy and at least two weeks before delivery
• Infants aged 2 to 5 months, regardless of vaccine history
• Infants aged 5 months to under 1 year who have had fewer than three doses
of the whooping cough vaccine

-- 1028 of 1282 --

1027
Children with suspected or confirmed whooping cough should be kept off school,
nursery, or childcare until they have completed 48 hours of appropriate antibiotic
treatment, or 14 days from the start of symptoms if not treated.
In this station, the child had a short history of dry cough without the typical features
of whooping cough. He was otherwise well and attending school. In addition, there
was no confirmed close contact with anyone known to have whooping cough. The
most likely diagnosis was a viral upper respiratory tract infection, and antibiotics
were not indicated. The focus is on reassuring the parent, giving clear safety-netting
advice, and having a respectful conversation about immunisations. It is important to
explore vaccine concerns with empathy, share accurate information, and offer to
discuss it again in the future. This shows you are taking the parent's beliefs seriously
and providing education in a supportive and non-judgemental way.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/whooping-cough/

Mrs Henderson was admitted via A&E following a 2-hour history of sudden central

Reason for consultation: The patient has booked a routine video consultation to
discuss some concerns.
Patients story.
You are Hilda Henderson, an 82-year-old female.

to move around the house. I was also started on lots of new medications. I have

If Asked to Explain Further: About a week ago, you had a frightening episode of

and discharged two days ago with several new medications that you do not fully
understand.
You remember being told to take one tablet at night called temazepam, which is the
only one you know the purpose of. While you were in hospital, the constant beeping
noises from the machines made you anxious and unable to sleep, so this medicine

-- 1031 of 1282 --

1030
was given to help you rest. You now feel better as you no longer hear those noises
at home.
During the day, you often feel dizzy, which makes it difficult to move around the
house. The dizziness is worse in the mornings and early afternoons and can happen
whether you are sitting or standing. The dizziness feels like light-headedness and a
sense of tiredness. It feels like a hangover, as if you drank alcohol the previous day,
but you have not taken any alcohol.
ONLY SAY BELOW IF ASKED
Social History: You live alone. Your husband passed away six years ago from
prostate cancer. You do not smoke or drink alcohol. Your daughter, who lives about
four miles away, does your shopping. You usually cook and clean for yourself, but
since feeling dizzy and tired, you have been unable to manage and have been
relying on ready-made meals. You do not drive
Ideas: You are not sure what is causing the dizziness but think it might be due to
one or more of your new medications.
Concerns: You are worried that you might fall if the dizziness continues.
Expectations: You would like the doctor to explain what the medications are for and

Say NO to any other questions/symptoms asked outside of the details already
