Questions for the Doctor
Can I stop these medications? Are they really all necessary?
Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Start by acknowledging that you can see from her discharge letter that she
was recently admitted to hospital, and ask how she is coping. For example:
“Mrs Henderson, I can see from your discharge letter that you were admitted
to hospital after having chest pain, and it was found that you had a heart
attack which was treated. How are you feeling now?”
• Ask about the onset, frequency, duration, and severity of the dizziness
• Clarify what she means by dizziness, whether it feels like light-headedness or
a spinning sensation
• Ask if the dizziness occurs in any particular position such as standing or
sitting, or if there are any specific triggers

-- 1032 of 1282 --

1031
• Ask about associated symptoms such as syncope (collapse/fainting
episodes), chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, visual changes,
headache, nausea, or vomiting
• Ask if she has noticed any swelling in her ankles, shortness of breath when
lying flat (orthopnoea), or waking up at night gasping for breath (paroxysmal
nocturnal dyspnoea), to help rule out heart failure, which can occur as a
complication of myocardial infarction.
• Ask about any falls, near falls, or unsteadiness when walking
• Ask whether she has been taking all the newly prescribed medications as
directed
• Ask if she has been eating and drinking normally since discharge (to rule out
dehydration or poor oral intake)
• Ask about sleep quality, whether this has improved and whether she
continues to take temazepam every night
• Ask about her mood and whether she has been feeling anxious or low, as
depression and anxiety are common after hospital admission and can occur
following a heart attack.
• Ask whether she has checked her blood pressure at home and, if so, whether
she has noticed it being lower than usual.
• Ask about her social history, including smoking history, alcohol use, who lives
at home, and how she is managing daily activities such as cooking, cleaning
and shopping
• Ask if she currently drives (this is especially important due to her dizziness, as
she should not drive when feeling dizzy. Also, post-myocardial infarction,
Group 1 drivers should check with their insurers to ensure they are still
covered for driving; however, they do not need to inform the DVLA. Group 2
drivers need to stop driving for a certain period and must inform the DVLA)
• Explore her ideas, concerns, and expectations about the medications and
dizziness
• Make a diagnosis of medication-induced dizziness, possibly related to next-
day sedative effects of temazepam or low blood pressure from new cardiac
medications.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Mrs Henderson, thank you for sharing how you have been feeling since coming
home. I can imagine that the past few days have been quite overwhelming,
especially after what you have been through in hospital and being started on several

-- 1033 of 1282 --

1032
new medications. It is completely understandable that you are finding it difficult to
adjust and that you are feeling dizzy at times.
Looking at your discharge letter, you were treated for a heart attack, and the hospital
team started you on medications that work together to protect your heart and reduce
the risk of another one. Let me go through them briefly so you know what each one
is for.
Aspirin and clopidogrel thin the blood slightly and help stop clots from forming, which
prevents blockages in the blood vessels and reduces the risk of another heart attack.
Ramipril lowers your blood pressure and helps your heart pump more effectively.
Bisoprolol slows your heart rate and reduces the strain on your heart. Atorvastatin
lowers cholesterol and helps prevent further blockages in your blood vessels, which
also lowers the risk of another heart attack. The GTN spray should only be used if
you experience chest pain; it works quickly to open up the blood vessels and relieve
the pain. Temazepam is a short-term medication to help you sleep, especially after
the stress of being in hospital. Are you following me so far?
The dizziness you are experiencing is likely related to temazepam, which can make
you feel sleepy or light-headed the next day. Since your sleep has improved, we can
think about stopping temazepam. What are your thoughts on that?
It is also possible that the combination of your new heart medications, such as
ramipril and bisoprolol, may lower your blood pressure and contribute to your
symptoms. Would it be alright if I ask you to come in so I can check your blood
pressure both sitting and standing, and also listen to your heart and chest to make
sure nothing else is contributing to the dizziness? If we find that your blood pressure
is lower than expected, we can look at reducing the dose of one of these medicines.
Are you with me so far?
Since you have not been on regular medications before, it can be confusing to
manage several tablets at once. I can refer you to our pharmacist, who can organise
your tablets into a dosette box or blister pack to help you take the right medicines at
the right time and avoid missing or doubling doses. Is that something you would find
helpful?
In the meantime, try to stand up slowly, especially when getting out of bed or from a
chair, and make sure you drink enough fluids during the day. This will help keep your
blood pressure steady and reduce both the dizziness and the risk of falls. Does that
make sense?
I would also like to arrange a blood test in the next few days to make sure your
kidneys are working well with the new medication you have started called ramipril.
Are you happy for me to book this appointment with our in-house healthcare
assistant?

-- 1034 of 1282 --

1033
Can I also check if you have heard from the heart rehabilitation team yet? They are
very important, as they can help improve your recovery and reduce the risk of
another heart attack.
If you get chest pain, please stop what you are doing, sit down, and use your GTN
spray as you were shown. If the pain is still there after 5 minutes, you can use the
spray again. If after the second dose the pain has not improved within another 5
minutes, or if at any point the pain gets worse or you feel very unwell, you must call
999 immediately.
Also, if your dizziness gets worse, or if you collapse, develop shortness of breath, or
notice your heart racing, please contact our urgent line immediately, or call 111 if it is
out of hours. Is that all clear? Would you want me to go over that again?
Management
Explain each of her medications and their purpose. For example:
1. Aspirin and Clopidogrel help to thin the blood and prevent platelets from
sticking together, reducing the risk of further clots and another heart attack.
2. Ramipril lowers blood pressure, reduces strain on the heart, and improves
survival after a heart attack.
3. Bisoprolol slows the heart rate, reduces the workload on the heart, and helps
prevent future cardiac events.
4. Atorvastatin lowers cholesterol, preventing fatty deposits from building up in
the arteries and reducing the risk of another heart attack.
5. Glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) spray provides quick relief from chest pain by
widening the blood vessels and improving blood flow to the heart. This should
only be used when chest pain occurs and not every day.
6. Temazepam is used short-term to help with sleep and reduce anxiety but can
cause next-day drowsiness or dizziness.
• Advise that her dizziness may be caused by her new medications. Explain
that temazepam can cause next-day sedative effects and, as her sleep has
improved since leaving hospital, it would be reasonable to consider stopping
it.
• Explain that since she has not taken regular medication before and is now on
several new medicines, it can understandably feel overwhelming. Offer a
referral to the pharmacist to review her medications and consider providing a
dosette box or blister pack to help her take the right tablets at the right time
and reduce the risk of missed or double doses.

-- 1035 of 1282 --

1034
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to examine her heart and chest and to check
her blood pressure both lying and standing to rule out postural hypotension or
low blood pressure from her new cardiac medications. Advise her that if low
blood pressure is confirmed, the dose of her medication may need to be
reduced.
• Advise her to stand up slowly, stay well hydrated, and move carefully to
reduce the risk of falls.
• Offer to book a blood test appointment with the phlebotomist or healthcare
assistant in 3–5 days to recheck her kidney function following the start of the
ACE inhibitor.
• Offer vaccinations, including the annual influenza vaccine.
• Check whether she has been contacted by the cardiac rehabilitation team and
encourage her to attend, as this can improve recovery and reduce the risk of
another heart attack.
• Provide safety-net advice: if she develops chest pain, she should stop what
she is doing, sit down, and use her GTN spray as instructed. If the pain has
not improved 5 minutes after the second dose of GTN, or if the pain becomes
worse at any point, she must call 999 immediately. If her dizziness becomes
worse, or if she collapses, or develops palpitations, shortness of breath, or
any other concerning symptoms, she should contact the surgery urgently or
call 111 if it is out of hours.
Learning Point from this Station
Post-myocardial infarction patients are usually placed on several medications for
secondary prevention, including dual antiplatelets, ACE inhibitors, beta blockers, and
statins. In addition to medication, cardiac rehabilitation is an important part of
recovery. This programme provides exercise, health education, lifestyle advice, and
support with stress management, and has been shown to improve long-term
outcomes and reduce the risk of another cardiac event.
Temazepam can cause next-day sedative effects, often described as a “hangover”
feeling, leading to daytime drowsiness and dizziness. In this case, the patient’s
dizziness may be due to temazepam or from newly started cardiac medications that
can lower blood pressure. It is also important to remember that dizziness can be a
sign of post-MI complications such as arrhythmias or heart failure, although this
patient did not present with symptoms to suggest these.
This station highlights the importance of clear, patient-centred communication when
reviewing a post-discharge patient who feels overwhelmed by new medications.
Taking time to explain the purpose of each medicine, involving the patient in
decision-making, and offering practical solutions such as pharmacist support for a
dosette box can significantly improve adherence and confidence. Empowering the

-- 1036 of 1282 --

1035
patient to understand and manage their treatment ultimately improves safety and
clinical outcomes.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/mi-secondary-prevention/

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Acknowledge the recent consultation and confirm the reason for today’s call.
For example: “Sophie, I can see from your records that you saw my colleague
three days ago because you were feeling tired, and they arranged some blood
tests. Is that right?”
• Ask about the onset and progression of tiredness.
• Ask whether the tiredness is constant or varies throughout the day, and
whether it is getting worse, improving, or staying the same.
• Ask about the impact of the tiredness on her daily activities, work, and quality
of life
• Ask about her periods, including her last menstrual period, whether her cycles
are regular, whether she has heavy bleeding, and whether there is any
possibility of pregnancy.
• Ask if she has noticed bleeding from any site, including blood in stools, dark
stools, abnormal vaginal bleeding, nosebleeds, gum bleeding, blood in urine,
or vomiting blood.
• Ask if she has new abdominal pain or any lumps or masses.
• Ask about associated gastrointestinal symptoms such as nausea, vomiting,
fever, diarrhoea, constipation, bloating, early satiety, or heartburn.
• Ask about symptoms of anaemia such as dizziness, shortness of breath on
exertion, palpitations, or chest pain.
• Ask about symptoms of vitamin B12 deficiency, including blurred vision,
tingling or numbness around the body, and any problems with balance or
walking.
• Ask about unintentional weight loss and night sweats.
• Ask about any known family history of bowel cancer or other cancers.
• Ask about family history of bowel cancer or any other type of cancer.
• Ask about her diet, particularly intake of iron-rich foods, and any recent dietary
changes.
• Ask about mood, sleep, and appetite.
• Ask about social history including smoking, alcohol use, occupation, and
stress.
• Explore her ideas, concerns, and expectations about her symptoms and test
results
• Make a diagnosis of microcytic anaemia, most likely iron deficiency anaemia,
with possible gastrointestinal blood loss or bowel cancer as an underlying
cause.

-- 1040 of 1282 --

1039
Management
• Offer a face to face appointment to examine her abdomen and assess her
observations.
• Arrange a FIT test and further blood tests including a coeliac screen.
• Offer a urine dip to check for haematuria.
• Start iron replacement therapy with ferrous sulfate 200 mg once daily for three
months.
• Offer vitamin B12 replacement, either oral or intramuscular depending on
symptoms and clinical judgement.
• Explain that if her urine test does not show any blood, she will be referred to a
gastroenterologist for further investigation, including bidirectional endoscopy
such as gastroscopy with or without colonoscopy , as iron deficiency in this
context may be due to gastrointestinal blood loss, including serious causes
such as cancer.
• Offer a fit note for amended duties or time off work if her symptoms are
affecting her ability to work.
• Advise a diet rich in iron including green leafy vegetables, beans, lentils, nuts,
and fortified cereals.
• Advise on vitamin B12 sources including fortified breakfast cereals, eggs,
milk, and cheese.
• Provide safety net advice to seek medical attention if symptoms worsen or if
she develops dizziness, fainting, palpitations, tingling sensations, blurred
vision, imbalance, shortness of breath, or any new concerns. Advise
contacting the surgery or calling NHS 111 if out of hours.
• Arrange follow up to review investigation results, including FIT and blood
tests, and reassess her response to treatment.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Sophie for taking the time to speak with me today. I can see from your
blood results that you have anaemia, which means your body does not have enough
healthy red blood cells to carry oxygen around effectively, and this is likely
contributing to the tiredness you have been experiencing.
Your results also show that both your iron levels and your vitamin B12 levels are low,
and these can both contribute to how tired you are feeling. Are you following me so
far?
One possible explanation for this could be your diet, as you follow a vegetarian diet,
and sometimes it can be more difficult to get enough iron and vitamin B12 from food
alone. However, when we see iron deficiency anaemia, we also need to make sure
that there is no blood loss from elsewhere in the body, particularly from the stomach
or bowel. In some cases, this can be due to more serious conditions such as cancer.

-- 1041 of 1282 --

1040
I want to reassure you that I am not saying you have cancer, but it is important that
we do not miss anything serious.
The next step would be to arrange a face to face appointment today so I can
examine your abdomen, check your blood pressure and heart rate, and also test
your urine for any hidden blood. I would also like to arrange further blood tests,
including a coeliac screen, which looks for a condition where the body reacts to
gluten and can cause this type of problem. In addition, I will give you a stool sample
kit so we can check for any hidden blood in your stool. Are you happy for me to
arrange all of this?
If your urine test does not show any blood, I will refer you urgently to a
gastroenterologist. They may arrange camera tests to look at your stomach and
bowel to check for any source of bleeding.
In the meantime, we will start treatment. I will prescribe iron tablets to help build your
iron levels back up, and we will also replace your vitamin B12. For the vitamin B12,
we can either use tablets or injections, depending on what you would prefer.
As regards your diet, how do you feel about including more iron rich and vitamin B12
rich foods, such as green leafy vegetables, lentils, beans, nuts, eggs, milk, cheese,
and fortified cereals?
You mentioned that your symptoms are affecting your work. Would it help if I
provided a fit note for amended duties or time off work while we investigate this and
help get your symptoms under control?
If you notice any new symptoms such as black stools, worsening tiredness,
dizziness, palpitations, tingling in your hands or feet, problems with balance, or
shortness of breath, please contact us urgently or call NHS 111 if it is out of hours.
Learning Point from This Station
The most common vitamin deficiency in vegetarians is vitamin B12 deficiency. This
is because the vitamin is naturally found almost exclusively in animal products such
as meat, fish, dairy, and eggs, which are excluded or limited in plant based diets.
Vegetarians are also more likely to have lower iron stores than people who eat meat.
This is because iron from plant sources is not as easily absorbed by the body.
However, this lady has been a vegetarian for many years and maintains a healthy
diet. She is also not having periods, which makes iron deficiency from menstrual
blood loss less likely. NICE suggests that premenopausal women under the age of
50 who do not menstruate should be considered for referral, as this may indicate an
underlying malignancy.

-- 1042 of 1282 --

1041
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/anaemia-iron-deficiency/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/anaemia-b12-folate-deficiency/

Patient’s Story (Role Player’s Brief)
You are Charles West, a 32 year old male.
Opening statement: Hi doctor, I am struggling to open my bowels.

past three to four weeks. Your stools are hard, and you sometimes go as long as

discomfort, but no significant pain. You have had no fever and no weight loss.

Initially, you were taking eight tablets daily, but you now take about four tablets a

You try to maintain a healthy diet as a sportsman, including a reasonable amount of

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask the patient to clarify what he means by struggling to open his bowels,
whether this is going less often than usual or passing harder stools
• Ask about the onset and progression of constipation
• Ask about stool consistency, for example whether stools are hard, small and
pebble-like, or large and difficult to pass
• Ask about his usual bowel habits and whether this problem is new for him or
has happened in the past.
• Ask about associated symptoms such as pain when passing stool (may
suggest anal fissures), abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, bloating, urinary
symptoms, abdominal masses, or anal lumps.
• Ask about the lump, including whether it feels hard, firm, or soft (hard lumps
may suggest cancer, whereas haemorrhoids are usually soft), whether it is
painful (which may indicate a thrombosed haemorrhoid or an anal fissure),
and whether it can be pushed back in (if it can be pushed back, this may
suggest a rectal prolapse or a prolapsed internal haemorrhoid).
• Ask about the colour of the lump, as thrombosed external haemorrhoids often
appear purplish, while prolapsed internal haemorrhoids may look bluish.
• Ask about symptoms of faecal impaction such as overflow diarrhoea/ soiling
of underwear, a feeling of incomplete evacuation, or needing to use fingers to
remove stool manually.
• Ask about red flag symptoms including noticeable blood in stools,
unintentional weight loss, fever, loss of appetite, night sweats, or a family
history of bowel cancer.
• Ask how the constipation and lump are affecting his daily life and functioning.
• Ask about his knee injury recovery, and specifically about the dose,
frequency, and duration of co-codamol use
• Ask about social history including smoking, alcohol use, occupation, and
home situation
• Explore his ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a diagnosis of constipation most likely secondary to co-codamol use,
with the lump likely representing a haemorrhoid.

1045
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Charles, thank you for explaining everything so clearly. From what you have told me,
it sounds like you have been struggling to open your bowels for the last few weeks.
Your stools are hard, you are having to strain, and you are not going as often as you
used to. This is what we call constipation, as you may already know, and in your
case it is very likely caused by the co-codamol tablets you are taking. Constipation is
a common side effect of the codeine component in this medication. Are you following
me so far?
You also mentioned feeling a lump around your back passage. From what you have
described, the lump is firm, purplish in colour, painful, and cannot be pushed back
inside. This is most likely a thrombosed haemorrhoid, which is a swollen blood
vessel that can occur when there is repeated straining, often due to constipation.
Does that make sense so far?
What I would like to do first is examine your tummy and have a quick look at the
lump with your permission, and with a chaperone present. This will help us
understand exactly what the lump is and reassure you that there are no worrying
features. Would that be alright?
We also need to look at the co-codamol, as this is likely contributing to your
constipation. If possible, reducing or stopping it will make a big difference to your
bowel habits. Do you feel your knee pain is controlled enough now that we could
reduce the co-codamol and switch to paracetamol or ibuprofen instead?
To help with your constipation, I would like to start you on a laxative to soften the
stools and make them easier to pass. Normally, we use a combination of two
laxatives, but because you are now stopping the co-codamol, we can begin with just
one and see how you get on. If things do not improve, we can add a second laxative
later. How does that sound so far?
We can start with macrogol, taking two sachets once each morning. This medicine
works by drawing water into the bowel to soften the stool. It can occasionally cause
mild side effects like tummy discomfort, nausea, or loose stools, but most people
tolerate it well. Are you happy to try this?
If the lump is confirmed to be a haemorrhoid, we can treat it with creams or
suppositories that are available over the counter. If anything about it looks unusual
once I examine it, we may ask a bowel specialist to review you.
Please keep hydrated and continue a good amount of fibre in your diet, which you
already do as a sportsman. This will support the laxatives in getting things moving
again.
If at any point you develop severe tummy pain, bloating, vomiting, blood in the stool,
weight loss, or if your symptoms get worse rather than better, please contact us
urgently or call 111 if it is out of hours. Does that all make sense?

-- 1047 of 1282 --

1046
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to examine his abdomen and perform a
rectal examination with a chaperone present to fully assess the lump and
exclude red flags.
• Advise reducing or stopping co-codamol if possible, and using alternative pain
relief such as paracetamol or NSAIDs if there are no contraindications.
• Offer laxatives, including an osmotic laxative or a stimulant laxative. Examples
of osmotic laxatives include macrogol or lactulose. Examples of stimulant
laxatives include senna, bisacodyl, and docusate. Note: lactulose may cause
gas and bloating and is not suitable for opioid-induced constipation.
NICE recommends that for constipation caused by opioid medicines, patients should
normally be offered both an osmotic laxative and a stimulant laxative, for example
Macrogol and Senna together. However, if he agrees to stop the opioid medication
(co-codamol), then it is reasonable to start with just an osmotic laxative and see how
he gets on. If things do not improve, a stimulant laxative can then be added. If he
continues taking opioid medication, he should be offered both types of laxatives
from the start.
• Advise him that laxatives should not be stopped suddenly. They can be
gradually reduced and stopped once he is passing soft, formed stools without
straining at least three times per week.
• Recommend increasing dietary fibre and maintaining good hydration.
• Explain that if the lump is confirmed to be a haemorrhoid, this can be
managed with haemorrhoid creams available over the counter. If there are
any concerns about the nature of the lump, referral to a bowel specialist may
be needed.
• Provide safety-net advice: Inform him that although he will be assessed today,
if he develops severe abdominal pain, abdominal swelling, vomiting, blood in
stools, weight loss, or worsening symptoms, he should seek urgent medical
advice by calling the surgery’s urgent line or 111 out of hours.
• Offer follow-up in two weeks to review his symptoms.

-- 1048 of 1282 --

1047
Learning Point from This Station
Opioid-induced constipation is very common, and it is important for GPs to recognise
it quickly. Opioids slow gastrointestinal motility by reducing peristalsis, which results
in hard stools, straining, and reduced bowel frequency.
Recommended treatment includes the use of stimulant laxatives and osmotic
laxatives
Bulk-forming laxatives, such as ispaghula husk (Fybogel), should not be used for
opioid-induced constipation. These agents work by absorbing water, increasing
stool bulk, and stretching the bowel wall to stimulate peristalsis. However, because
opioids suppress the bowel’s propulsive response, the bowel cannot effectively move
this increased bulk forward. As a result, bulk-forming agents may worsen symptoms,
leading to abdominal discomfort and, in rare cases, bowel obstruction. Similarly,
laxatives such as lactulose and sorbitol can produce gas, contributing to bloating and
abdominal distension, which may be poorly tolerated in this group of patients.
Extremely painful, acutely thrombosed external haemorrhoids presenting within
72 hours may require same-day surgical assessment, as reduction or excision
might be needed. However, after 72 hours, the severe pain has usually peaked, and
symptoms generally begin to improve with conservative management as the body
reabsorbs the blood clot over a few days to weeks. Since this patient presented five
days after the lump appeared, conservative treatment is appropriate.
However, if on examination you suspect prolapsed internal haemorrhoids or have
any concerns about the diagnosis or complications, it would still be sensible to refer
the patient for same-day surgical assessment.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/constipation/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/haemorrhoids/

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Paige, thank you for sending in the picture of your eye. I have had a good look at it,
and from what you have described, the appearance is consistent with a condition
called pterygium. Have you heard of this before?
Pterygium is a harmless, non-cancerous growth on the surface of the eye. It is quite
common in people who spend a lot of time outdoors, especially in sunny, dusty, or
windy environments, which fits with your time working in Dubai. Are you following me
so far?
The growth has become more noticeable, it has affected your confidence, and it has
started to extend slightly onto the cornea. The cornea is the clear window at the front
of the eye that covers the coloured part and the black centre part. Because of this, I
think it would be helpful for you to see an eye specialist. They will examine it properly
and discuss whether surgical removal would be suitable. This will be a routine
referral because your vision is normal and there are no worrying signs. Are you
happy for me to make this referral?
In the meantime, there are things you can do to help. Using sunglasses outdoors will
protect your eyes from sunlight and help slow down the growth. For the dryness you
mentioned, I can prescribe artificial tear drops such as hylo forte to keep the eye
comfortable. Would you be happy to try those?
If you notice any sudden changes in your vision, increasing pain, redness, or if the
lump grows quickly, please seek urgent medical advice. These symptoms are
uncommon, but if they occur, we would want to review you sooner. Is that all clear?
Do you have any questions you would like me to go over again?
Management
• Reassure her that the appearance is consistent with a pterygium, which is a
benign and non-cancerous growth.
• Explain that pterygium often occurs in people who spend long hours outdoors
in sunny, dusty, or windy environments.
• Inform her that because the pterygium has increased in size, is affecting her
confidence, and has started to encroach onto the cornea, which is the
transparent surface covering the iris (the coloured part of the eye) and pupil
(the black centre), a routine referral to Ophthalmology is appropriate. They will
assess it in clinic and discuss whether surgical removal would be suitable.

1051
Explain that this is a non-urgent referral, as her vision is unaffected and there

• Safety net by advising her to seek urgent care if she develops sudden visual
changes, eye pain, significant redness, or rapid growth of the lesion.
Learning Point from This Station
Pterygium is a benign growth of the conjunctiva commonly associated with chronic
ultraviolet exposure, dry climates, and outdoor occupations. It usually progresses
slowly and may extend onto the cornea.
Management depends on symptoms and the degree of corneal involvement:
• Urgent referral to Ophthalmology is required if the pterygium is encroaching
onto the cornea and causing reduced vision, recurrent inflammation, or rapid
enlargement. This is not a two-week wait referral, but these patients are
prioritised and often assessed quickly for possible surgery.
• Routine referral is appropriate if the pterygium is extending onto the cornea,
but vision remains unaffected. If uncertain, an Advice and Guidance
request to Ophthalmology can be helpful.
• Offer a routine referral if the cosmetic appearance is distressing for the
patient.
• For mild or stable cases, provide reassurance that this is a harmless and non-
cancerous growth that seldom causes serious problems.
• For dry eye symptoms, offer ocular lubricants such as hylo-forte (sodium
hyaluronate) or hypromellose.
• Advise consistent use of sunglasses outdoors to reduce UV exposure and
help prevent progression
Further Reading
https://gpnotebook.com/en-GB/pages/ophthalmology/pterygium-ophthalmology

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Acknowledge the nurse’s findings and ask the patient to describe what

• Ask about symptoms suggestive of complications of high blood pressure,
including headaches, visual changes, chest pain, shortness of breath,
palpitations, weakness or tingling in any part of the body, or slurred speech to

urgency, cloudy urine, foul smell, or visible blood to rule out urine infection.

-- 1055 of 1282 --

1054
• Ask about systemic symptoms such as fever or flank pain (to consider renal
stones) and swelling of the legs or face (to consider nephrotic syndrome or
glomerulonephritis).
• Ask about the use of over-the-counter medications (particularly NSAIDs) or
herbal supplements that may affect renal function.

and whether there is any possibility of pregnancy (This is important, as
menstruation can contaminate a urine sample causing false positive
haematuria on dipstick and pregnancy is an important consideration in the
context of raised blood pressure).
• Explore her ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a diagnosis of suspected hypertension with asymptomatic microscopic
haematuria and proteinuria, requiring further investigation to confirm true
hypertension and to exclude renal target organ damage.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Divya, thank you for talking me through what happened earlier today. I understand
this has come as a surprise, especially as you were attending for your adoption
medical and you are otherwise feeling well.
From what the nurse found, your blood pressure was higher than we would normally
expect, and your urine test showed small traces of blood and protein that are not
visible to the naked eye. I want to reassure you straight away that a single blood
pressure reading and a single urine dip test do not mean you definitely have a
medical condition, but they are important findings that we need to check properly.
Blood pressure can sometimes be temporarily raised due to stress, anxiety, or even
rushing to an appointment. For that reason, we do not diagnose high blood pressure
based on one reading alone. The best way to confirm whether your blood pressure is
genuinely high is by arranging either home blood pressure monitoring or a 24-hour
monitor, which gives us a more accurate overall picture. Are you following me so far?
Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring involves a small blood pressure machine
being attached to you for 24 hours. It checks your blood pressure regularly during
the day and night, and we use the average reading to decide whether your blood
pressure is truly high. While wearing the monitor, you should avoid driving,
exercising, and bathing or showering.

-- 1056 of 1282 --

1055
The other option is home blood pressure monitoring. This involves using a blood
pressure machine at home to check your readings twice a day, usually in the
morning and evening, for at least four days, and recording the results. Each time you
check your blood pressure, you take two readings at least one minute apart while
seated, and record the results.
Both options are reliable. Which of these would suit you better?
Regarding the urine result, the blood and protein seen are very small amounts and
you have no symptoms, which is reassuring. Sometimes this can be temporary, but
because the kidneys and blood pressure are closely linked, we need to repeat the
test and check some blood tests to make sure your kidneys are working well. This is
a routine and precautionary step.
Would it be okay if I see you face to face to carry out a few further checks? I would
like to listen to your heart sounds, look at the back of your eyes as high blood
pressure can affect the eyes, carry out an ECG which is an electrical tracing of your
heart, and arrange some blood tests to check your kidney function, blood sugar
levels, and cholesterol.
I would also like you to provide an early morning urine sample. This allows us to
accurately measure the amount of protein in your urine and helps us work out
whether today’s urine result was a one-off finding or whether your kidneys may be
under strain from blood pressure or another cause. Does that sound okay to you?
I also want to address your concern about adoption. These findings do not
automatically affect child adoption eligibility. Even if we confirm high blood pressure,
many people successfully adopt with well-controlled medical conditions. Adoption
assessments focus on how well a condition is managed and whether it affects daily
functioning, not simply whether a diagnosis exists.
Before we finish, I just want to let you know what symptoms you should look out for.
If at any point you develop a severe or persistent headache, changes to your vision,
chest pain, shortness of breath, or you notice visible blood in your urine, please seek
urgent medical help. You can contact our urgent GP line straight away, or call 111 if
it is out of hours. These symptoms would need to be checked promptly.
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment for clinical assessment, including
cardiovascular examination and fundoscopy.
• Offer urgent further investigations to assess for target organ damage,
including ECG, urine ACR (albumin to creatinine ratio), HbA1c, lipid profile
and renal function.
• Offer either ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) or home blood
pressure monitoring (HBPM) to confirm a diagnosis of hypertension.

-- 1057 of 1282 --

1056
• If ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) is offered, advise the patient
that the monitor will be worn for 24 hours. During this time, she should avoid
driving, exercising, and bathing or showering while the equipment is attached.
• If home blood pressure monitoring (HBPM) is offered, advise the patient to
record two blood pressure readings at least one minute apart, every morning
(between 06:00 and 12:00) and every evening (between 18:00 and 00:00), for
at least four days and ideally for seven days.
• Offer to repeat urinalysis in 3 to 5 days to reassess the haematuria. Advise
the patient that if this persists, further investigations will be arranged, including
an ultrasound scan of the kidneys.
• Provide lifestyle advice, including reducing salt intake, maintaining regular
physical activity, weight management, and stress reduction.
• Explain that these isolated findings do not automatically affect adoption
eligibility. Reassure her that even if high blood pressure is confirmed, many
people adopt successfully with well-controlled medical conditions. What
matters most is how well the condition is managed, whether it affects day-to-
day functioning, and whether it could interfere with parenting responsibilities.
• Provide safety-net advice to seek urgent medical attention by contacting the
GP urgent line, or calling 111 if out of hours, if she develops severe
headache, visual changes, chest pain, shortness of breath, or visible blood in
her urine.
• Offer follow-up to review blood pressure readings and investigation results.
Learning Point from This Station
For patients with a clinic blood pressure between 140/90 mmHg and 180/120 mmHg,
hypertension should not be diagnosed based on a single reading. These patients
should be offered ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) or home blood
pressure monitoring (HBPM) to confirm the diagnosis.
While awaiting confirmation, it is important to assess for target organ damage,
including fundoscopy for hypertensive retinopathy, a 12-lead ECG to assess cardiac
involvement such as left ventricular hypertrophy, urine dipstick testing for haematuria
and protein, urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio for accurate protein assessment, and
renal function tests. Additional investigations such as lipid profile and HbA1c should
also be considered to assess overall cardiovascular risk.
In this case, the urine dipstick showed haematuria and proteinuria with no obvious
alternative explanation. However, urine dipsticks are screening tools and do not
confirm kidney disease, and transient factors such as dehydration or vigorous

-- 1058 of 1282 --

1057
exercise can cause temporary haematuria or proteinuria. It is therefore important to
arrange urgent urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (ACR) and renal function tests to
assess for target organ damage. If persistent abnormalities are confirmed,
antihypertensive treatment should be initiated promptly.
NICE recommends confirming hypertension with ambulatory or home blood pressure
monitoring before starting treatment, as up to 25–30 percent of people with a single
raised clinic reading have white-coat hypertension. Initiating treatment too early risks
overtreatment, potentially causing hypotension, dizziness, and unnecessary long-
term medication in someone who may not be truly hypertensive
However, if blood pressure is 180/120 mmHg or higher, same-day specialist
assessment is required if there are features of accelerated hypertension, such as
retinal haemorrhages or papilloedema on fundoscopy, or life-threatening symptoms
including new-onset confusion, chest pain, signs of heart failure, or acute kidney
injury.
If there are no features requiring same-day referral, investigations for target organ
damage should be carried out urgently. Where target organ damage is identified,
antihypertensive treatment should be started immediately without waiting for ABPM
or HBPM results. If no target organ damage is found, blood pressure should be
rechecked within seven days.
So, if this patient’s blood pressure were 180/120 mmHg or higher with haematuria
and proteinuria on dipstick, this would indicate possible acute target organ damage
involving the kidneys. In that situation, antihypertensive treatment should be started
immediately, without waiting for confirmation with ABPM or HBPM, while urgent
investigations and specialist assessment are arranged.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/hypertension/diagnosis/diagnosis/

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Gwendoline, thank you for explaining everything so clearly. I have reviewed the
results of your camera test. It shows inflammation and small erosions in your food
pipe, which explains the burning chest symptoms you have been experiencing. One
of the tablets you are taking, alendronic acid, is well known to irritate the food pipe
and can cause this type of inflammation. Are you following me so far?
The good news is that this is treatable. The first step is to start a medication called
omeprazole, which reduces the amount of acid in your stomach and allows the food
pipe to heal. I would recommend taking this once daily for the next four weeks.
Would you be happy to take this medication?
Alongside the medication, there are some lifestyle measures that can help your
oesophagitis symptoms improve. These include avoiding late meals and spicy foods,
reducing caffeine intake, and staying upright for at least one hour after eating, rather
than lying flat.
I also want to talk about the alendronic acid. Based on your previous assessments,
your risk of fracture is low, and you have never had a fracture. I completely
understand why preventing fractures is important to you, especially given what
happened to your friend.

-- 1063 of 1282 --

1062
In your situation, continuing alendronic acid is more likely to worsen the inflammation
in your food pipe and your symptoms, and you do not necessarily need it now
because your fracture risk is low. So, the risk of continuing alendronic acid now
outweighs the benefit, so I would advise stopping it.
The reassuring thing is that stopping this medication for now does not significantly
increase your fracture risk. Does that make sense to you?
You are already doing many of the right things to protect your bones, such as
walking regularly and having a calcium-rich diet.I would also like to assess your
dietary calcium intake using a calcium calculator. I can send you a simple
questionnaire to complete, where you note how much milk, cheese, yoghurt, and
other calcium-rich foods you have each day.
If it turns out that you are getting enough calcium from your diet, you may not need
to continue taking Adcal. In that case, we could keep you on a low-dose vitamin D
supplement alone. We can review the results together and decide on the best option
for you.
I will arrange to review you again in four weeks to see how your symptoms are
settling. However, If at any point you develop difficulty swallowing, vomit blood,
notice black stools, have severe chest pain, or lose weight unintentionally, please
seek urgent medical help straight away by contacting us or calling 111 if it is out of
hours.
Management
• Offer full-dose proton pump inhibitor (PPI) therapy for four (4) weeks. For
example, omeprazole 20mg once daily for 4 weeks.
• Explain that her current fracture risk is low, and reinforce that her good dietary
calcium intake, vitamin D supplementation, and regular walking are all positive
protective measures. Advise that, in her case, the risks of continuing
alendronic acid currently outweigh the benefits, and therefore it should be
stopped.
• Reassure her that her FRAX score remains low, and that stopping alendronic
acid does not significantly increase fracture risk at present.
• Provide lifestyle advice to help improve oesophagitis symptoms, including
avoiding late meals, spicy foods, and other known triggers, reducing caffeine
intake, and remaining upright for at least one hour after eating.

-- 1064 of 1282 --

1063
• Offer to assess dietary calcium intake using a calcium calculator. Explain that
if her intake is adequate, she may not need to continue Adcal, and instead
could remain on low-dose vitamin D alone.
• Encourage her to continue lifestyle measures, including regular weight-
bearing exercise such as walking, and a balanced diet, to help maintain bone
strength.
• Advise that her fracture risk will be reassessed every five years, or sooner if
her clinical situation changes.
• Provide safety-net advice to seek urgent medical attention by contacting the
GP urgent line, or calling 111 if out of hours, if she develops difficulty
swallowing, vomiting blood, black stools, severe chest/tummy pain, or
unintentional weight loss.
• Offer follow-up in four weeks to review symptom resolution and response to
treatment.
Learning Point from This Station
Oral bisphosphonates such as alendronic acid commonly cause upper
gastrointestinal side effects, including dyspepsia and reflux, particularly within the
first month of treatment. These symptoms often improve with continued use and are
less likely when the medication is taken correctly. However, if gastrointestinal
symptoms are persistent or worsening, further investigation is required to exclude
complications such as oesophagitis, and oral bisphosphonates should be
discontinued.
In patients who continue to require bone protection, referral to a rheumatologist is
important to consider alternative treatment options. These include injectable
therapies such as zoledronic acid, denosumab, or teriparatide, as well as non-
bisphosphonate oral agents such as raloxifene or strontium ranelate.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/osteoporosis-prevention-of-fragility-
fractures/management/management/#lifestyle-information-advice

contraceptive pill slightly increases the risk of cervical cancer, especially when
used for more than five years).

cervical cancer risk), alcohol use, occupation, and living circumstances.
• Ask about her mood and how she is settling into life in the UK following recent
relocation.

-- 1067 of 1282 --

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Tatyana, thank you for explaining your situation to me, and I completely understand
why you are asking for a cervical screening test, especially as you are used to
having this done every year in Ukraine.
In the UK, cervical screening works a bit differently. It is offered routinely from the
age of 25, and if the previous result is normal, it is now done every five years rather
than every year. This is because the test is very reliable and checks for the virus that
usually causes cervical cancer. If the virus is not found, the risk of developing
cervical cancer over the next few years is low, which means it is safe to wait five
years before the next test. Are you following me so far?
From what you have told me, you had a normal cervical screening test just over a
year ago, and you do not have any symptoms such as abnormal bleeding, pain, or
unusual discharge. That means you are currently up to date with screening and do
not need to have another test now. Does that make sense so far?
Your details will be added to our system, and when your next screening is due, you
will automatically receive an invitation through the NHS, either by letter, text
message, or the NHS App, with instructions on how to book.
I also appreciate that you have recently moved to the UK during a very difficult time.
If you would like, I can refer you to our practice social prescriber. They can help you
understand how NHS services work, support you with settling into the area, and link
you with local community groups, wellbeing services, or social activities if you wish.
This can help with feeling more settled and connected. Would you find that helpful?
If at any point you develop symptoms such as bleeding between periods, bleeding
after sex, unusual vaginal discharge, or ongoing pelvic pain, please contact us
urgently. Those symptoms would need checking, regardless of when your last
screening was.
Management
• Explain that in the UK, cervical screening is offered routinely from the age of
25, but the screening interval depends on previous results. For people aged
25 to 64, screening is done every 5 years if results are normal.
• Reassure her that, based on her history of a normal cervical screening just
over a year ago, she is currently up to date with cervical screening and does
not need another test at this time.
• Explain that her records will now be updated, and she will be automatically
invited for her next cervical screening when it is due. Inform her that
invitations are usually sent via the NHS App, text message, or letter, with
clear instructions on how to book the appointment.

-- 1068 of 1282 --

1067
• Offer written information or NHS leaflets about cervical screening in the UK for
reassurance.
• Offer a referral to the practice social prescriber, who can help her settle into
life in the UK and understand how NHS services work. They can also link her
with local community groups, or social groups to help build social connections
and a sense of belonging.
• Provide clear safety-net advice to seek urgent medical review by calling the
GP urgent line or booking an urgent appointment if she develops symptoms
such as abnormal vaginal bleeding, bleeding after sex, unusual vaginal
discharge, or pelvic pain, as these would need assessment irrespective of
screening dates.
Learning Point from This Station
Cervical screening practices vary internationally, so patients who have moved to the
UK may have different expectations about screening frequency.
In the UK, cervical screening is offered to women aged 25–64. In England, Scotland,
and Wales, screening is carried out every 5 years if results are normal. In Northern
Ireland, screening is offered every 3 years for women aged 25–49, and every 5
years from age 50–64. Cervical screening is a population-based programme, and
unscheduled screening outside the recommended intervals is not part of the national
programme. If a woman has already had a cervical screening test within the
appropriate timeframe, repeat screening should not be offered.
However, any woman presenting with symptoms suggestive of cervical cancer, such
as abnormal vaginal bleeding, post-coital bleeding, or unexplained discharge, should
be referred urgently via the two-week-wait pathway rather than managed through
routine cervical screening.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/cervical-screening/

• Make a diagnosis of suspected contact dermatitis to latex condoms
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Maria, thank you for trusting me with something that is clearly very personal and
difficult to talk about. I want to reassure you that you have done the right thing by
coming forward, and this is something we see quite commonly.
From what you have described, the itching seems to happen every time you use
condoms and settles on its own afterwards, with no discharge, pain, or other
symptoms, and your recent STI screens have been clear. This pattern strongly
suggests that you are reacting to something in the condom, most likely latex or one
of the chemicals or lubricants on it. This is called contact dermatitis, and it is a skin
reaction rather than an infection. Does that make sense so far?
I would strongly advise that you do not stop using condoms, as they are very
important for protecting you against infections. However, there are good alternatives.
Many people with this problem do very well by switching to non-latex condoms.
These are designed specifically for people who react to latex and are widely
available in most supermarkets and pharmacies.

-- 1072 of 1282 --

1071
If the itching does come back, we can use simple treatments such as an
antihistamine tablet and a mild steroid cream called hydrocortisone, applied
externally for a short time, to help settle the irritation.
Another option I wanted to discuss with you is HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis, often
called PrEP. This is something you can access through a sexual health clinic, and it
provides extra protection against HIV alongside condom use. Are you with me so
far?
If your symptoms worsen, do not improve after switching condoms, or if you develop
discharge, pain, ulcers, or bleeding, please contact us immediately.
Although it is uncommon, I do want to mention that if you ever developed more
serious symptoms such as difficulty breathing, swelling of your lips or throat, or
feeling faint, that would be an emergency and you would need to call 999 straight
away.
We can review how you are getting on in a few weeks.
Management
• Reassure her that, based on the history, this is most likely contact dermatitis,
commonly due to latex or chemicals in condoms or lubricants, rather than an
infection.
• Strongly advise not to stop condom use, as condoms remain essential for
protection against sexually transmitted infections, especially given her work.
• Advise switching to non-latex condoms such as polyurethane or polyisoprene
condoms, which are suitable for people with latex sensitivity. SKYN is a
popular brand that specialises in making latex-free condoms.
• Advise attending a sexual health clinic to discuss HIV pre-exposure
prophylaxis (PrEP), which can provide additional protection against HIV
infection alongside condom use.
• Offer non-sedating antihistamines and 1% hydrocortisone cream for short
term use if symptoms re-occur in future.
• Safety-net by advising her to seek review if symptoms worsen, do not improve
after switching condoms, or if she develops discharge, pain, ulcers, or
bleeding.
• Advise her that although this is unlikely, some people can develop a severe
allergic reaction, which may present with difficulty breathing, swelling of the
tongue or throat, or dizziness, and if this occurs she should call 999
immediately.
• Offer follow-up in 4 weeks to review symptoms and response to changes.

-- 1073 of 1282 --

1072
Learning Point from This Station
Latex condom allergy occurs in a small proportion of people, and it is important for
GPs to recognise this as a cause of genital symptoms. Latex allergy can present in
different ways, ranging from localised reactions to systemic symptoms affecting the
whole body. Most commonly, symptoms are mild and may include local itching,
redness, swelling, or raised itchy rashes, as well as nasal congestion, runny nose, or
itchy, watery eyes.
Importantly, an allergy to latex condoms does not mean that condoms must be
avoided altogether. Non-latex condoms are widely available and provide an effective
alternative without increasing the risk of sexually transmitted infections. This station
highlights the importance of patient education, maintaining safer sex practices, and
addressing sensitive sexual health issues in a non-judgemental, supportive manner.
Further Reading
https://onlinedoctor.lloydspharmacy.com/uk/sexual-health-advice/latex-condom-
allergies
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/dermatitis-contact/

-- 1076 of 1282 --

1075
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Wesley, thank you for going through everything with me. From what you have
described, this sounds most likely to be a muscle strain in the lower back after lifting
something heavy, which is very common and usually settles with the right treatment.
Are you with me so far?
Because you also mentioned new urinary symptoms, such as needing to rush to the
toilet and getting up more at night, I would like to examine you in person. This will
allow me to check your back properly, test the nerves in your legs, and examine your
prostate, as problems with the prostate can sometimes cause the kind of urinary
symptoms you are describing. Does that sound okay?
There is just one extra thing I want to look into. You mentioned some urinary
symptoms, such as needing to pass urine urgently and waking up at night to go.
These symptoms are often caused by very common conditions in men your age,
such as an enlarged prostate, which is harmless. However, very rarely, prostate
problems can be more serious such as prostate cancer, so it is safe that we check
properly. I want to reassure you that nothing you have said today makes me strongly
worried, but it is important not to miss anything. Does that make sense?
To do this properly, I would like to see you in person so I can examine your back,
check your nerves, and also examine your prostate. We will also send off a PSA
blood test, which stands for prostate-specific antigen, to check how the prostate is
functioning and I would also like to do a urine test to check for any sign of infection.
How do you feel about coming in for that?
In terms of managing the pain, we can offer stronger pain relief than paracetamol.
Ibuprofen with a stomach-protecting tablet, such as omeprazole, is one option,
provided you have no medical reason to avoid it. If that is not suitable, we could try a
short course of co-codamol instead. How do you feel about trying that?
We will also encourage gentle activity, as resting too much can make back pain last
longer.
If you notice anything like weakness in your legs, numbness around the groin or
bottom area, difficulty controlling your bladder or bowels, or severe worsening pain,
please seek urgent medical help straight away by calling the urgent GP line or NHS
111. These can be signs of a more serious condition that needs immediate attention.
Are you happy with that advice
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to assess his back, carry out a neurological
examination, and perform a prostate examination.

-- 1077 of 1282 --

1076
• Offer further pain relief, starting with an NSAID such as ibuprofen together
with a gastric-protective medication like omeprazole. If NSAIDs are not
suitable for him, consider a short course of co-codamol instead.
• Offer a urine dipstick testing and urine culture to assess for infection, and a
PSA blood test (Prostate-Specific Antigen) test to evaluate the prostate.
• Advise that if PSA is elevated or the prostate feels abnormal on examination,
he will need a referral to urology on the suspected cancer pathway.
• Reassure him that simple muscular sprains often improve gradually with time.
• Advise gentle movement and maintaining normal activity levels, avoiding
prolonged rest, as staying mobile supports recovery.
• Provide safety-netting advice: Instruct him to seek urgent medical help if he
develops new leg weakness, numbness in the saddle area, difficulty passing
urine or controlling his bladder or bowels, or if the pain becomes significantly
worse. Advise him to call the urgent GP line or NHS 111 if any of these
symptoms occur.
• Arrange follow-up once his test results are available to discuss the next steps.
Learning Point from This Station
Low back pain is very common and is usually caused by simple musculoskeletal
strain. However, the presence of urinary symptoms in a man over 50 should always
prompt consideration of prostate disease, including prostate cancer, because
prostate cancer can metastasise to the spine and cause back pain.
Cancer that spreads to the bones (bone metastases) weakens the normal bone
structure by disrupting the bone’s natural repair process, making it prone to fracture.
In such cases, even relatively minor exertion or strenuous lifting can lead to a spinal
fracture. Therefore, it is essential to assess for red flags, inquire about urinary
symptoms suggestive of prostate disease, and arrange appropriate investigations
such as PSA testing and prostate examination.
This case highlights the importance of not overlooking serious underlying causes
when assessing back pain, particularly in men presenting with new urinary
symptoms.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/back-pain-low-without-radiculopathy/

1079
Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset of symptoms
• Ask about what the patient means by feeling “tired” – whether this refers to
physical fatigue, mental exhaustion, or a general sense of being unwell
• Ask whether the tiredness is improving, worsening, or staying the same.
• Ask whether minimal activity worsens the tiredness, as this may suggest post-
exertional malaise (consider chronic fatigue syndrome).
• Ask about sleep quality, whether she feels refreshed after sleep, whether she
sleeps more than usual, and if she has ever woken up gasping for air or been
told she snores (to rule out obstructive sleep apnoea).
• Ask about difficulty concentrating or experiencing brain fog.
• Ask about associated generalised body aches or joint pains.
• Ask about recent flu-like symptoms. (to rule out post-viral fatigue)
• Ask about red-flag symptoms that may suggest leukaemia relapse, including
fever, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, easy bruising, bleeding,
recurrent infections, bone pain, or breathlessness.
• Ask about her last menstrual period.
• Ask if she has experienced any constipation related to codeine use.
• Ask whether she is bleeding from anywhere to screen for anaemia.
• Ask about onset of tingling sensation in both legs and feet.
• Ask about the onset and progression of tingling in the hands and feet.
• Ask whether the tingling is associated with weakness, gait instability, blurry
vision, or headaches to rule out vitamin B12 deficiency.
• Ask about appetite, dietary intake, or any restrictive diet that may predispose
to vitamin B12 deficiency.
• Ask about her mood, whether she feels low or anxious, or has lost interest in
usual activities.
• Ask about social history including smoking, alcohol intake, home situation,
occupation, who lives with her, and what support she has.
• Ask how she is coping financially while not working.
• Ask how these symptoms have affected her daily life and routine.
• Ask about her ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a working diagnosis of post-chemotherapy fatigue and chemotherapy-
induced peripheral neuropathy
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Priscillia, thank you for explaining everything so clearly. I can hear how frustrating
this tiredness has been for you, especially when you are hoping to get back to work.
What I want to do is go through what may be causing these symptoms and how we

-- 1081 of 1282 --

1080
can help you feel better. Please stop me at any point if anything is unclear. Are you
happy for me to go ahead?
From what you have described, your symptoms sound very much like something we
commonly see after chemotherapy. Even though your treatment has finished and
you are in remission, many people experience significant fatigue for months
afterwards. This is something called post-chemotherapy fatigue, and it can make
even simple activities feel exhausting. The tingling in your hands and feet also fits
with chemotherapy-related nerve irritation, which is known as peripheral
neuropathy. These symptoms are unpleasant, but they are usually not dangerous,
and they often improve gradually over time.
To make sure we are not missing anything else, I would like to bring you in for a
face-to-face assessment so I can check your nerves properly and do a general
examination. Alongside that, we will organise some blood tests today. These will
check your blood count, thyroid function, vitamins such as B12 and folate, your
diabetes markers, your kidney and liver function, vitamin D, and your inflammation
levels. This will help us rule out things like anaemia, vitamin deficiencies, thyroid
problems, or infection, which could also contribute to tiredness and tingling. Does
that sound okay so far?
While we wait for the results, there are some practical steps that can make a big
difference. One is energy management, or pacing. This means planning your day so
that you balance activity with rest, avoiding pushing yourself too hard on good days,
as this can make the fatigue worse. A daily symptom diary is also helpful, as it allows
us to see any patterns and helps you plan your day in a way your body can cope
with.
You mentioned wanting to return to work, and it is good that you feel ready to start
thinking about that. Many people benefit from a phased return, starting with perhaps
an hour or two a day and gradually building up as your energy improves. I can
support you with a fit note and a phased-return plan if you would find that helpful.
There are also some general wellbeing measures that support recovery, such as
keeping well hydrated, eating regular balanced meals, doing short gentle walks if you
can manage them, and maintaining a steady sleep routine.
If you notice night sweats, unexplained bruising, bleeding, breathlessness, or bone
pain, please contact us urgently or call NHS 111, as these can sometimes be signs
that your leukaemia has returned. I am not expecting this to happen, but it is
important for you to know what to look out for.
We will review you once your blood results are back.

-- 1082 of 1282 --

1081
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to carry out a full neurological examination
and general physical examination.
• Offer urgent blood tests, including full blood count, thyroid function tests,
vitamin B12 and folate, HbA1c, renal and liver profile, vitamin D level, and
inflammatory markers.
• Encourage her to keep a daily symptom diary to identify any patterns or
triggers for her fatigue, which can help guide pacing and activity planning.
• Advise on energy management and pacing techniques. Encourage her to
balance activity with rest, avoid overexertion, and gradually increase activity
levels in small, manageable steps.
• If she wishes to return to work, offer a Fit Note recommending amended
duties or a phased return. For example, if she normally works eight hours per
day, she could begin with one to two hours per day and increase gradually as
tolerated.
• Provide general wellbeing advice, including maintaining good hydration,
eating a balanced diet, taking short low-intensity walks as tolerated, and
keeping a regular sleep routine.
• Advise her to seek urgent medical attention if she develops fever, night
sweats, unexplained bruising or bleeding, breathlessness, or bone pain, as
these symptoms may suggest relapse of leukaemia. She should contact the
urgent GP line or NHS 111 if these occur.
Learning Point from This Station
Post-chemotherapy fatigue and peripheral neuropathy are common long-term effects
of cancer treatment. These symptoms can continue for months even when the
patient is in remission. It is important to check for reversible causes of fatigue such
as anaemia, thyroid problems, vitamin B12 deficiency, or mood disorders. At the
same time, clinicians must remain alert for red-flag symptoms that could suggest
relapse, including night sweats, unexplained bruising, bleeding, breathlessness, or
bone pain.

Data Gathering and Diagnosis

blood tests were arranged at that time, and confirm that she is attending today

-- 1085 of 1282 --

1084
• Ask her to explain in her own words what led to having the blood tests done.
• Ask about the tiredness: onset, duration, whether it is getting worse, better, or
staying the same.
• Ask if there are any triggers for the tiredness or anything that makes it better.
• Ask about cancer red-flag symptoms, including unexplained weight loss, night
sweats, loss of appetite, or any lumps or bumps.
• Ask about symptoms of hypercalcaemia, for example whether the patient or
anyone around them has noticed confusion, feeling muddled, drowsiness,
increased thirst (polydipsia), palpitations, chest pain, abdominal pain,
constipation, increased urination (polyuria), or blood in the urine (haematuria).
• Ask about cough, shortness of breath, or coughing up blood to help rule out

• Ask about use of over-the-counter supplements, particularly vitamin D, as
excessive use can cause hypercalcaemia.

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Erin, thank you for explaining all your symptoms. I have had a look at your blood test
results, and I would like to go through them with you in a clear and simple way.
Please stop me at any point if something does not make sense
You mentioned that you had these blood tests because you were worried about
diabetes. The good news is that your results are normal, so you do not have
diabetes. How does that make you feel?
One of the results we checked was your calcium level, and this has come back
higher than normal. Are you with me so far?
Now, high calcium in the blood can happen for a few different reasons, but the most
common cause in someone your age is something called primary
hyperparathyroidism. This is when one of the parathyroid glands in the neck
becomes overactive and releases too much hormone, which then causes the
calcium level to rise. We cannot confirm this yet, but your results make it a strong
possibility.
To investigate this properly, I would like to arrange some further tests. These will
include a repeat calcium level, your vitamin D level, and a blood test called
parathyroid hormone, or PTH for short. These tests together will help us understand
exactly what is going on. Is that okay with you?

-- 1086 of 1282 --

1085
You mentioned that you eat yoghurt every day, and you are absolutely right that
yoghurt contains calcium. However, dietary calcium does not cause this degree of
raised calcium in the blood, so there is no need to stop eating yoghurt. Please
continue your normal diet.
While we are waiting for the next set of results, it is important for you to stay well
hydrated by drinking regular fluids during the day. Keeping hydrated helps the
kidneys flush out extra calcium.
If the next set of tests show that the parathyroid hormone is raised, we would then
refer you to the hormone specialists, called endocrinologists. They will discuss
treatment options with you, which may include monitoring, medication, or sometimes
an operation if needed.
Very high calcium levels can sometimes make people quite unwell. If you notice
symptoms such as worsening tiredness, confusion, severe tummy pain, vomiting,
muscle weakness, or a feeling that your heart is beating irregularly, please seek
urgent medical advice by calling the GP urgent line, NHS 111 if out of hours, or 999
depending on how unwell you feel. I am not expecting this to happen, but it is
important that you know what to look out for.
Management
• Offer further blood tests, including parathyroid hormone level, vitamin D level,
and a repeat adjusted calcium level.
• Explain to the patient that if the parathyroid hormone level is raised, this would
suggest primary hyperparathyroidism, and she would then be referred to an
endocrinologist (hormone specialist) to discuss treatment options. These may
include monitoring, medication, or surgery depending on the results and her
symptoms.
• Advise her to continue good hydration and maintain adequate fluid intake, as
this can help lower calcium levels.
• Reassure her that she does not need to stop yoghurt or other dietary calcium,
as normal dietary intake does not cause this level of hypercalcaemia.
• Safety net: Advise her to seek urgent medical help through the GP urgent line
or NHS 111/999 if she develops symptoms such as increasing drowsiness,
confusion, severe abdominal pain, vomiting, muscle weakness, or
palpitations, as these can indicate worsening hypercalcaemia.
• Arrange follow up once the blood test results are available.

-- 1087 of 1282 --

1086
Learning Point from this Station
Hypercalcaemia has several potential causes, but the two most common are primary
hyperparathyroidism and malignancy. When a patient presents with raised calcium
but has no red-flag symptoms suggestive of cancer, primary hyperparathyroidism
becomes the most likely diagnosis. It is a relatively common endocrine condition,
especially in women aged 50–60.
In primary care, the key steps are to confirm the diagnosis with blood tests including
parathyroid hormone (PTH) and vitamin D, ensure the patient is well hydrated, and
refer to endocrinology for definitive assessment and management.
Further Reading
https://patient.info/doctor/endocrine-disorders/hyperparathyroidism-pro
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/hypercalcaemia/

The safeguarding team will start looking into this straightaway, and depending on

your partner, especially as you have both been working towards reconciling.
However, your daughter’s safety must remain the priority. The safeguarding process
is designed not only to protect children but also to support families. They will make
sure that any involvement of her father is handled sensitively and appropriately, and
they can offer support with how to approach conversations with him so that it does
not lead to misunderstanding or conflict.

they feel this would help her, depending on how this experience has affected her.

Learning Point from This Station
Concerns about possible child sexual abuse require immediate safeguarding action.
The GP must prioritise the child’s safety, make an urgent referral to safeguarding,
ensure clear documentation, and communicate sensitively with the parent while
managing expectations about the investigation process. Early support, safety
planning, and appropriate signposting are essential.
