Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Start by acknowledging that you can see from his records that he had blood
tests done because of an upcoming DVLA assessment and ask him to tell you
more about it.
• Ask about alcohol history in detail, including previous intake, duration of
abstinence, and any relapse
• Ask about symptoms suggestive of liver disease, including yellowing of the
eyes or skin (jaundice), itching, dark urine, pale stools, fever, unintentional
weight loss, right-sided abdominal pain, and fatigue
• Ask whether he has been unwell recently, including symptoms such as sore
throat, cough, fever, runny nose, or flu-like symptoms (a recent viral illness
can cause a transient rise in liver enzymes).
• Ask about the use of over-the-counter medications, herbal remedies, or
supplements.
• Ask about recent travel abroad (to assess the risk of hepatitis A infection,
which is more common in certain endemic regions and can be acquired
through contaminated food or water)
• Ask about family history of liver disease
• Explore social history, including smoking, illicit drug use, occupation, and who
lives at home
• Ask about sexual history, including any new or recent partners
• Ask about previous blood transfusions or tattoos
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.

-- 1096 of 1282 --

1095
• Make a working diagnosis of abnormal liver function test results, most likely
related to supplement use.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you, Dylan, for explaining everything so clearly. I can understand why you are
keen to discuss your blood test results, especially with your DVLA assessment
coming up.
Your blood tests show some mild changes in a few of your liver enzymes, but the
rest of the results are reassuring. Are you following me so far?
The important point to emphasise is that the pattern we are seeing is not what we
would expect with ongoing alcohol use, which is reassuring given your history.
One possible explanation for these mild changes is the gym supplement you
mentioned. Some supplements can affect the liver, particularly when the exact
ingredients are not clear. For that reason, I would strongly advise that you stop
taking the supplement for now. Is that clear so far?
After our call, it would be helpful if you could check the supplement packaging and
send us a photo of the ingredients, if possible. This allows us to document it properly
and helps guide whether anything further needs to be done.
The next step is to repeat the liver blood tests in about 2 to 4 weeks. This gives the
liver time to settle once the supplement has been stopped. In many cases, the
results return to normal.
If the blood tests are still abnormal, we would then arrange further tests, which may
include more detailed liver blood tests, and a jelly scan (ultrasound scan) of the liver,
just to be thorough.
In terms of your DVLA assessment, based on these results there is nothing to
suggest ongoing alcohol misuse, and you can proceed with your appointment. The
DVLA sometimes use a separate blood test called carbohydrate-deficient transferrin,
which is more specific for detecting alcohol use, and this is usually negative in
people who are not drinking alcohol.
If you notice any new symptoms such as yellowing of the eyes or skin, itching,
unusual tiredness, tummy pain, or a change in the colour of your urine or stools,
please seek urgent medical advice by contacting the GP urgent line during working
hours or NHS 111 out of hours. Is that all clear? Do you have any questions?
.

-- 1097 of 1282 --

1096
Management
• Advise the patient to stop taking the supplement immediately, as this may be
contributing to the abnormal liver function test results.
• Advise him to check the contents of the supplement after the call and to send
a photograph of the packaging or ingredient list, so this can be documented
and may help guide further management.
• Offer repeat liver function tests in 2–4 weeks to assess for improvement after
stopping the supplement.
• Explain that if liver function tests remain abnormal, further investigations will
be arranged, including a full liver panel, hepatitis screen, and an ultrasound
scan of the liver.
• Reassure him that he can proceed with his DVLA medical assessment, as the
current blood test pattern is not consistent with ongoing alcohol use.
• Explain that the DVLA may use additional tests, such as carbohydrate-
deficient transferrin (CDT), which is more specific for detecting alcohol
misuse, and that this test is usually negative in people who are not drinking
alcohol.
• Provide safety-netting advice to seek urgent medical attention by contacting
the GP urgent line during working hours or NHS 111 out of hours if he
develops yellowing of the eyes or skin, itching, fatigue, abdominal pain, or a
change in the colour of urine or stools.
• Arrange follow-up in 2–4 weeks to review repeat liver function test results and
decide on next steps.
Learning Point from this Station
This station highlights the importance of a structured and proportionate assessment
of abnormal liver function tests in primary care. Abnormalities in liver enzymes
should always be interpreted carefully and in the context of the clinical history rather
than in isolation
A thorough history is essential to identify potential causes, including alcohol intake,
recent viral illness, travel-related exposure to viral hepatitis, sexual history, illicit drug
use including needle sharing, and the use of over-the-counter medications or herbal
and gym supplements. It is important to note that some gym supplements are a
known cause of liver enzyme derangement and may result in drug-induced liver
injury.
In this case, the liver enzyme abnormalities are mild and the patient is asymptomatic,
so initial management should focus on removing the suspected trigger, such as
stopping supplements, and arranging repeat liver function tests after 2–4 weeks.
This allows time for resolution and helps to avoid unnecessary investigations.

-- 1098 of 1282 --

1097
However, if liver enzymes are more than three times the upper limit of normal at
initial presentation, or if the patient has symptoms suggestive of liver disease, it
is important to arrange immediate further investigations rather than waiting as there
may be serious ongoing liver injury. These would include an ultrasound scan of the
liver and a full liver screen, including autoimmune markers, AST, and viral hepatitis
screening.
This station also reinforces the interpretation of biochemical patterns associated with
alcohol use. Gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) and mean corpuscular volume
(MCV) are commonly raised in chronic alcohol misuse. Normal values make alcohol-
related liver disease less likely. In addition, the DVLA may use carbohydrate-
deficient transferrin (CDT), which is a more reliable marker of prolonged heavy
alcohol consumption.
Further Reading
https://easl.eu/wp-content/uploads/2019/04/EASL-CPG-Drug-induced-liver-injury-
2019-04.pdf
https://www.gov.uk/guidance/drug-or-alcohol-misuse-or-dependence-assessing-
fitness-to-drive

management, Mr Ramsay is now discharged from our Rheumatology Clinic. Should
any concerns arise or his symptoms deteriorate, please do not hesitate to re-refer
him for further review.

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset of pain and whether it developed gradually or suddenly. A
gradual onset is typical of a flare of ankylosing spondylitis, whereas a sudden
onset may suggest an alternative cause such as a fracture.
• Ask about the nature of the pain, including location, severity, and whether it
improves with movement or worsens with rest.
• Ask about associated symptoms, including early morning stiffness, which is
characteristic of ankylosing spondylitis.
• Ask about neurological symptoms, including tingling, numbness, or weakness
in the legs.
• Ask about red flag symptoms, including recent trauma (which may indicate a
fracture), saddle paraesthesia, loss of bowel or bladder control, and bilateral
radicular pain or lower limb weakness (which may suggest cauda equina
syndrome), as well as unexplained weight loss, fever, or night pain (which
may indicate malignancy or systemic disease).
• Ask about extra-articular features of ankylosing spondylitis, including anterior
uveitis (red, painful eyes with blurred vision or photophobia), enthesitis (pain
at tendon insertion sites such as the Achilles tendon or plantar fascia), skin
changes suggestive of psoriasis, and gastrointestinal symptoms such as
diarrhoea or abdominal pain, which may indicate underlying inflammatory
bowel disease.
• Ask about social history, including occupation, smoking status, and alcohol
use
• Assess the impact of symptoms on daily activities, work, and functional ability
• Ask what treatments he has tried for pain relief and whether he has engaged
with physiotherapy
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a working diagnosis of a flare of ankylosing spondylitis
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you, Jack, for explaining all that. From what you have told me, this sounds like
a flare of your ankylosing spondylitis. It is reassuring that you do not have any red
flag symptoms at the moment, such as numbness, weakness, or problems with your
bladder or bowels.
Can I just check, what do you understand about ankylosing spondylitis?

-- 1102 of 1282 --

1101
Ankylosing spondylitis is a long-term condition that affects the spine and can cause
stiffness and pain, especially in the lower back. It tends to flare up from time to time,
and these flares can last for days or weeks before settling again. Does that make
sense so far?
Right now, I think it would be helpful to see you in person, so I can examine your
back and check your nerves, just to make sure nothing else is going on.
For the pain, I would recommend trying a stronger anti-inflammatory called
naproxen, which you would take regularly for a few days. I would also give you a
medication to protect your stomach while using it. Are you happy to try that?
Physiotherapy is also really important in ankylosing spondylitis. It can help with your
pain, movement, and posture, and may make it easier to cope at work. I would like to
refer you back to physiotherapy, as it sounds like your symptoms are now getting in
the way of your daily life. Would that be okay with you?
I can also get in touch with the rheumatology team who saw you before, just to ask
their advice and check whether they feel anything more might be needed at this
stage.
I understand you are self-employed and this is affecting your ability to earn. If you
would like, I can refer you to a social prescriber, who can explore any financial or
practical support that may be available to you. Would you find that helpful, or would
you prefer to focus on getting back to work?
Just so you are aware, if you develop any new symptoms, such as weakness in your
legs, numbness around the back passage, or loss of control over your bladder or
bowels, or if the pain suddenly becomes severe, you should seek urgent medical
help, that would mean calling 999 or going straight to A&E. If you are not sure, call
111 for advice.
We will arrange to follow up with you in two to four weeks, or earlier if I hear anything
new from rheumatology or if things get worse. Does that all sound okay?
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to examine the back and perform a full
neurological examination.
• Offer an alternative NSAID like naproxen for pain relief, alongside a proton
pump inhibitor for gastroprotection.
• Offer referral to physiotherapy and explain that physiotherapy can help
improve pain, mobility, and overall function, which may help him function
better at work.

-- 1103 of 1282 --

1102
• Offer to seek advice and discuss his current flare with the rheumatology team
to determine whether any additional or alternative treatments may be
appropriate.
• Provide advice on workplace adjustments, including regular breaks from
prolonged standing and stretching during the working day.
• Offer referral to a social prescriber, who can discuss any support or financial
aid he may be entitled to, which may be particularly relevant as he is self-
employed.
• Provide safety-netting advice to seek urgent medical attention by calling 999
or attending A&E if red flag symptoms develop, including weakness in the
lower limbs, loss of bowel or bladder control, numbness around the back
passage, or sudden severe back pain. Advise contacting NHS 111 urgently if
unsure.
• Arrange follow-up in 2–4 weeks to review symptoms, or earlier if new advice
is received from rheumatology.
Learning Point from this Station
Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is a chronic inflammatory condition that primarily affects
the spine and sacroiliac joints. It typically presents with inflammatory back pain,
which develops gradually, is worse in the early morning with stiffness lasting more
than 30 minutes, and improves with movement rather than rest. Patients with AS
often experience intermittent flares, during which symptoms worsen and functional
ability is reduced.
People with ankylosing spondylitis are at increased risk of osteoporosis and
spinal fractures due to long-standing inflammation. The combination of spinal
rigidity and osteoporosis increases the chance of fractures, with up to 10% of people
with axial spondyloarthritis experiencing a spinal fracture. These fractures may
happen with minimal or no trauma. Therefore, when a patient with AS presents with
new or worsening back pain, it is important to ask if the pain started after any
injury, even a minor one, as this could suggest a spinal fracture.
Clinicians should also be alert to red flag symptoms, including:
• Inability to control bladder or bowels
• Numbness in the saddle area
• New lower limb weakness
These features may indicate cauda equina syndrome and require urgent
escalation.
In addition, unexplained weight loss, fever, or night sweats should raise concern for
malignancy or systemic disease.
Extra-articular features of AS are also clinically important and may include:

-- 1104 of 1282 --

1103
• Uveitis (painful red eye, blurred vision, photophobia)
• Enthesitis (e.g. Achilles tendon or plantar fascia pain)
• Psoriasis
• Gastrointestinal symptoms, such as abdominal pain or diarrhoea, which may
point towards inflammatory bowel disease (IBD)
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/axial-spondyloarthritis-including-ankylosing-spondylitis/

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you, Rebecca, for explaining all that. I can understand why this has been
worrying for you, especially as the lumps have now appeared in more than one area.
From what you have told me and from the pattern of these painful lumps in areas
such as the armpits and groin, this is most consistent with a condition called
hidradenitis suppurativa. Is this something you have heard of before?
Hidradenitis suppurativa is a long-term skin condition that tends to flare from time to
time and affects areas where skin rubs together, such as the armpits and groin,
causing repeated painful lumps/boils. Does that make sense so far?
You mentioned you were worried that the lumps are spreading. What is actually
happening is that the condition can affect more than one area, rather than spreading
from one lump to another. That can feel alarming, but it is a recognised feature of
this condition.
Firstly, would it be okay if I arranged a face-to-face appointment so I can examine
the areas properly and rule out any other possible causes?
In terms of treatment, because the lumps are currently painful and red, this suggests
inflammation and possible infection. I would like to offer a short course of antibiotics
to help settle this. We have a couple of options. One option is the same antibiotic
you had previously, which is taken twice a day, and you said helped clear things up.
Another option is a different antibiotic that is taken four times a day. Which would
you prefer?
Just to double check, you do not have any allergies to medications, as noted in your
records, is that correct?
For pain, you can continue using simple pain relief such as paracetamol or
ibuprofen. I would also recommend using an antiseptic wash, such as Octenisan or
chlorhexidine, which can help reduce future flares. These are available over the
counter, and I can send you the name in a text message if that helps. Would you be
happy to try that?

-- 1109 of 1282 --

1108
You were also wondering whether this could be linked to your PCOS. Hormonal
factors do play a role in hidradenitis suppurativa, and people with PCOS are at a
higher risk of developing this condition, so you are right to think about that. However,
I would like to reassure you that there are several lifestyle changes we can talk about
that can help control the condition and reduce flares.
Wearing loose-fitting clothing and underwear is helpful, as tight clothing increases
friction and irritation, which can trigger flares. You are already eating healthily, which
is great, so I would encourage you to keep that up. Another important factor is
increasing physical activity, as this can help with weight reduction and reduce flares.
Do you think there are ways you could increase your activity levels, for example
going for a daily walk of up to 30 minutes?
You also mentioned that you smoke. Stopping smoking is really important, not just
for your overall health, but because smoking is known to make this condition worse. I
appreciate that stopping is not easy, but we have a lot of support available and can
help you with this if you would like.
If you notice the lumps becoming much more painful, spreading redness, a lot of
pus, fever, or if you start to feel unwell, please seek urgent medical advice by
contacting the practice urgent GP line during working hours or NHS 111 out of hours.
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to examine the lesions in the armpits and
groin to assess severity and exclude any other abnormalities
• As there are features suggestive of acute infection, including redness and
pain, offer treatment with either oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily
for 5 to 7 days or clarithromycin 500 mg twice daily for 5 to 7 days,
depending on suitability and tolerance.
• Advise regular simple analgesia, such as paracetamol or ibuprofen, to help
manage pain.
• Offer the use of topical antiseptic washes, such as Octenisan antimicrobial
wash or a 4% chlorhexidine solution, to be used regularly, as this can help
reduce flares.
• Advise the patient to keep a symptom diary to help identify possible triggers,
such as periods of stress, certain foods, hormonal changes, or other factors
occurring around flare episodes, as this may help reduce future flares.
• Acknowledge the patient’s ideas and concerns and explain that hormonal
factors are linked to hidradenitis suppurativa, and that people with polycystic
ovary syndrome are at increased risk of developing this condition.

-- 1110 of 1282 --

1109
• Reassure the patient that hidradenitis suppurativa is not caused by poor
hygiene
• Acknowledge that she already eats healthily and encourage gradual increases
in physical activity where possible, as this can support weight reduction and
help reduce flares.
• Offer smoking cessation advice and explain that stopping smoking can
improve her overall health and help reduce the frequency of flares.
• Advise the patient to avoid tight clothing and tight underwear, as tight
clothing can increase friction and irritation and may trigger flares.
• Advise against squeezing or manipulating the lumps, as this can worsen
inflammation and increase the risk of scarring.
• Safety-net by advising her to seek urgent medical review by contacting the
practice urgent GP line during working hours or NHS 111 out of hours if the
lesions become increasingly painful, develop spreading redness, start
discharging significant pus, are associated with fever, or if she feels unwell.
• Arrange follow-up in five days to assess whether the symptoms have
improved and resolved.
Learning Point from this Station
Hidradenitis suppurativa is a chronic inflammatory skin condition affecting apocrine
gland bearing areas, most commonly the axillae, groin, perineum, buttocks, and
under the breast.
It is more common in women and in people of Afro Caribbean ethnicity. Risk
factors include obesity, cigarette smoking, a positive family history, hormonal
influences, polycystic ovary syndrome, and stress.
Patients typically present with recurrent, painful nodules or boils in flexural areas,
which may rupture and discharge pus. Repeated inflammation can lead to scarring
and sinus tract formation. Management depends on disease activity.
If there are features suggestive of acute infection, such as fever, significant pain,
marked redness, a rapidly enlarging lump, or discharge of pus, offer oral antibiotics.
Prescribe flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 5 to 7 days, or clarithromycin 500
mg twice daily for 5 to 7 days if the patient is penicillin allergic.
If there are no features suggestive of acute infection, or once an acute infection
has resolved and inflammation persists, offer topical clindamycin 2%, applied
twice daily for three months, for mild disease. If disease is more widespread,
consider an oral antibiotic for three months. Options include lymecycline 408 mg

-- 1111 of 1282 --

1110
once daily, tetracycline 500 mg twice daily, doxycycline 100 mg once daily, or
erythromycin 500 mg twice daily.
For infective flares with tense, fluctuant abscess formation, consider same-day
referral to hospital for surgical assessment and incision and drainage to help relieve
pain.
All patients should be advised to use topical antiseptic washes, such as Octenisan
antimicrobial wash or a 4% chlorhexidine solution. Lifestyle advice should also be
offered to help reduce flares, including smoking cessation and weight reduction.
Refer the patient to a dermatologist if there is severe psychological impact or
significant impairment of quality of life, if there is no improvement despite
adequate first-line treatment for mild to moderate disease, if the disease is severe
at presentation in which case referral should be made while initiating first-line
treatment, if there is diagnostic uncertainty, if the patient is pregnant, or if there is
significant scarring or sinus tract formation.
Further Reading
https://www.pcds.org.uk/clinical-guidance/hidradenitis-suppurativa
https://patient.info/doctor/dermatology/hidradenitis-suppurativa-pro

• Ask about any medical contraindications to contraception, including

Example of Explanation to the Mother
Thank you, Julie, for taking the time to explain your concerns and for being so
thoughtful about Charlotte’s wellbeing. It is clear that you are trying to do the right
thing for her.
I think it is important to say first that it is completely appropriate to raise
contraception at this stage, especially as Charlotte is in a relationship. We can
certainly talk through the options today at a general level. However, I do need to be
clear that we cannot start any contraception without seeing and speaking to
Charlotte directly, as she is the patient. Her understanding, views, and consent are
central to this decision. Does that make sense so far?
There are several different forms of contraception available. One option is the pill,
which needs to be taken every day. There is also the contraceptive patch, which is
applied once a week for three weeks, and the vaginal ring, which is changed every
three weeks.
We also have long-acting reversible options, such as the coil, which is placed inside
the womb and can provide contraception for between five and ten years. There are
two types of coil, one that contains copper and one that contains hormones. Another
option is the contraceptive injection, which is given every twelve weeks, and the
implant, which is placed under the skin of the arm and lasts for up to three years.
All of these methods have their own advantages and disadvantages, and some may
suit Charlotte better than others. For example, the pill does not involve any
procedure, but it does need to be taken every day. Whereas, methods such as the
injection, implant, or coil do not need to be taken every day. Are you following me so
far?

-- 1115 of 1282 --

1114
What I will do is send you an information leaflet that explains these options clearly,
and it would be helpful if you could go through this together with Charlotte so she
can start thinking about what might suit her.
It is also important to be aware that these methods do not protect against sexually
transmitted infections. If there is any concern about infections, condoms should be
used. We usually encourage anyone entering a new relationship to consider sexual
health screening as well. Does that make sense?
The next step would be to arrange another appointment with Charlotte present,
ideally face to face if possible. During that consultation, I would speak with her
directly, assess her understanding and capacity to make decisions about
contraception, and talk through the options again in simple, clear language. Young
people are usually encouraged to involve their parents in these discussions, but it is
important to be clear that the decision to involve you in the discussions ultimately
rests with Charlotte unless she is assessed as lacking capacity.
If Charlotte were found not to have capacity, then any decision would need to be
made in her best interests, with appropriate involvement from you and, if needed,
other professionals. I will make sure you are kept informed and supported throughout
this process.
For now, I suggest we arrange a follow-up appointment within the next one to two
weeks with Charlotte present, so we can complete the assessment properly and
move things forward. In the meantime, it would be helpful for you to remind Charlotte
that if sexual activity were to happen, using condoms is important to reduce the risk
of pregnancy and protect against infections.
Management
• Thank the mother for raising her concerns and for the support she provides in
looking after Charlotte
• Provide a brief overview of the different types of contraception, including their
main advantages and disadvantages, and offer an information leaflet to read
through together with Charlotte to support an informed decision
• Explain that only condoms protect against sexually transmitted infections and
that sexual health screening is recommended for anyone who is sexually
active.
• Explain clearly that contraception cannot be prescribed without seeing and
speaking to Charlotte directly, as she is the patient and her understanding,
views, and consent must be assessed.
• Offer to arrange a separate face-to-face or telephone consultation with
Charlotte present, ideally without a parent in the room initially, to allow a
confidential discussion.

-- 1116 of 1282 --

1115
• Explain that during this consultation, Charlotte’s capacity and competence to
make decisions about contraception will be assessed, including her
understanding of sex, pregnancy, and contraception.
• Reassure the mother that young people are usually encouraged to involve
their parents in these discussions, but that this decision ultimately rests with
Charlotte unless she is assessed as lacking capacity.
• Explain that if Charlotte is found not to have capacity, any decision would
need to be made in her best interests, with appropriate involvement of parents
and relevant professionals.
• Arrange a follow-up appointment within one to two weeks with Charlotte
present to complete the assessment and discuss contraception in detail. In
the meantime, if sexual activity were to occur, advise that condoms should be
used.
Learning Point from this Station
This station highlights the importance of capacity, consent, and confidentiality when
discussing contraception in young people. Having Down’s syndrome or being under
the age of 16 does not automatically mean that a young person lacks capacity.
Children under 16 can consent to their own treatment if they have sufficient
understanding, intelligence, and maturity to fully appreciate what the treatment
involves. This is referred to as Gillick competence.
Parental concerns should always be acknowledged and respected; however,
contraception cannot be prescribed without directly involving the young person, as
their views, understanding, and consent are central to the decision-making process.
Further Reading
https://www.rcgp.org.uk/getmedia/8c4f05fe-c442-42a8-94e1-

https://www.nhs.uk/tests-and-treatments/consent-to-treatment/children/

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Start by acknowledging that he has attended to discuss the results of his
recent blood and urine tests from his routine diabetes review.
• Ask about symptoms suggestive of diabetic complications, including blurred
vision (which may suggest diabetic retinopathy), excessive thirst and
frequent urination (osmotic symptoms of poorly controlled diabetes),

complications of diabetes), pins and needles or numbness in the hands or

-- 1119 of 1282 --

1118
feet (diabetic neuropathy), blood in the urine, reduced urine output, or leg

• Ask about adherence to his prescribed medications.
• Ask whether he experiences any side effects from his medications, for
example metformin, which can cause gastrointestinal side effects like
diarrhoea, abdominal pain, or vomiting.
• Ask about social history and lifestyle factors, including smoking status, alcohol

• Explore his ideas, concerns, and expectations regarding diabetes control and

• Make a working diagnosis of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes, uncontrolled
hypertension, and suspected chronic kidney disease.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you, Azad, for explaining all that.
Looking at your results, your blood sugar levels are higher than we would like, which
fits with what you mentioned about sometimes missing your afternoon dose because
of work. To make things easier, I would suggest starting you on a type of metformin
that you only need to take once a day to help with compliance. It is called modified
release metformin, 1 g once daily. How does that sound?
We will also add another medication called dapagliflozin once daily. This helps to
control your blood sugar and also protects your kidneys and heart. Does that feel
manageable for you?
We would not start both medications at the same time. We will begin with metformin
first and see how you tolerate it. If you are tolerating it well, we will then introduce
dapagliflozin.
As you may already know, metformin can sometimes cause side effects such as
diarrhoea, abdominal discomfort, nausea, or vomiting. Dapagliflozin can increase the
risk of urine infections and, in rare cases, a more serious infection around the genital
area.
However, I want to reassure you that most people tolerate these medications well. If
you notice any side effects or have any concerns, please let us know. Does that
sound okay?
Your blood pressure is also higher than it should be, despite you taking ramipril
regularly. For that reason, I would recommend adding another blood pressure tablet
called amlodipine, starting at 5 mg once daily. This can help bring your blood
pressure down.

-- 1120 of 1282 --

1119
Possible side effects include ankle swelling or dizziness, so I would like you to let us
know if you notice these. Are you happy to try that?
Your blood and urine tests suggest that your kidneys are not working quite as well as
we would expect, and there is a small amount of protein leaking into your urine. This
can happen in people with diabetes and high blood pressure. However, it can
sometimes be temporary, and things like dehydration, recent illness, or exercise can
affect the results.
For that reason, we will repeat these tests in three months to look at the overall
pattern. If your kidney results still show a similar picture, we can then discuss further
ways to manage this.
The most important thing right now is to improve control of your blood sugar and
blood pressure, as this helps protect your kidneys. Does that make sense?
Because diabetes, high blood pressure, and kidney problems increase the risk of
heart attack and stroke, I would also like to check your cholesterol levels and
calculate your heart risk score. Depending on the results, we would usually
recommend starting a cholesterol-lowering medication. We can discuss this properly
once those results are back.
Alongside medication, lifestyle changes are really important. You mentioned that
your diet is not ideal, which is understandable given how busy you are at work.
However, do you think there are small changes you could make towards a healthier,
more balanced diet, including more fruit and vegetables? I can send you some
information leaflets to read through, and if you feel you are struggling, we can also
refer you to a dietitian who can help tailor advice to your routine. Is that something
you would like to try?
We also encourage increasing physical activity where possible. Even simple
changes, such as aiming for around 30 minutes of walking each day, can help
improve blood sugar control. What do you think about that?
I would also recommend keeping up to date with vaccinations, including the flu,
pneumococcal, and COVID vaccines, and making sure you attend your annual
diabetic eye screening, as this helps detect problems early.
If at any point you become unwell, for example if you develop tummy pain, vomiting,
excessive thirst, or confusion, please seek urgent medical advice by contacting the
GP urgent line during working hours or NHS 111 out of hours, as these symptoms
can be serious.
We will arrange a follow-up appointment in three months to repeat your blood and
urine tests and review how you are getting on.

-- 1121 of 1282 --

1120
Management
• Offer modified release metformin 1 g daily and add Dapaglifozin 10mg once
daily
• Explain that modified release metformin will be started first, and after about
two weeks, once it is confirmed that he is tolerating it well, dapagliflozin will
then be introduced.
• Inform him of side effects. Metformin can cause gastrointestinal upset
including diarrhoea and nausea. Dapagliflozin can increase the risk of urinary
tract infections and, in rare cases, a serious infection called Fournier’s
gangrene, which affects the genital and perineal area.
• Explain sick day rules for both medications. If he develops vomiting or
diarrhoea, he should temporarily stop these medications and restart them 24
to 48 hours after he is able to eat and drink normally again.
• Advise adding another medication to improve blood pressure control and offer
amlodipine 5 mg daily. Inform him about possible side effects including, ankle
swelling and dizziness.
• Inform him that the blood tests show his kidney function is not within the
expected range and that the urine test has detected some protein leakage,
which suggests his kidneys may be under strain. Explain that these tests will
need to be repeated in three months to confirm whether this is a one-off
finding or a persistent issue, as factors such as recent exercise, acute illness
or dehydration can temporarily affect the results.
• Offer further blood tests to check cholesterol levels and calculate his QRISK
score.
• Provide lifestyle advice, including increasing physical activity, eating healthily,
and aiming for gradual weight loss.
• Offer vaccinations including influenza, pneumococcal, and COVID
vaccination.
• Advise him to attend his annual diabetic eye screening.
• Provide safety-netting advice, including seeking urgent medical attention by
contacting the GP urgent line during working hours or NHS 111 out of hours if
he becomes unwell or develops symptoms such as abdominal pain, vomiting,
excessive thirst, or confusion, as these may indicate diabetic ketoacidosis.
• Offer follow up in 3 months with repeat HbA1c, renal function tests, and urine
ACR.
Learning Point from this Station
This station highlights the importance of holistic management of type 2 diabetes in
primary care. Poor medication adherence, uncontrolled blood pressure, and lifestyle
factors can lead to worsening blood sugar control, early kidney disease, and
increased cardiovascular risk.

-- 1122 of 1282 --

1121
In this case, the patient had a raised clinic blood pressure, and an additional
antihypertensive medication was started to improve control. Once a diagnosis of
hypertension has been established, clinic blood pressure readings are used to
monitor the response to lifestyle changes or medication. Ambulatory or home
blood pressure monitoring is not routinely required after diagnosis has been
established, unless white coat hypertension is suspected or a fourth
antihypertensive medication is being considered and resistant hypertension is
suspected.
The station also reinforces the correct approach to diagnosing chronic kidney
disease. A single abnormal result is not sufficient. NICE recommends that eGFR or
urine ACR abnormalities must be confirmed on repeat testing at least three
months apart. In this case, there is only one raised urine ACR and one eGFR
reading below 60 mL/min, so repeat testing is required before confirming a diagnosis
of CKD.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/diabetes-type-2/

Patient’s Story (Role Player’s Brief)
You are Heather Brady, a 56-year-old woman.
Opening Statement: Hello doctor. I would like to stop the insulin as it is making me

A nurse in the clinic showed you how to use the insulin pen and also sent you a

Since starting insulin, you wake up most mornings feeling giddy. By this, you mean
feeling light-headed, with difficulty concentrating and headaches. When this
happens, you usually need to take something sugary to feel better. You read that
these symptoms could be a side effect of insulin, and this has made you want to stop
taking it.
ONLY SAY BELOW IF ASKED:

You check your blood pressure at home regularly. It was 120/75 mmHg this morning,
and it is usually around the 120s for the top number and the 70s for the bottom
number.

-- 1125 of 1282 --

1124
These symptoms are affecting your daily routine, as you often end up going to work
late in the mornings because you do not feel well.
You do not have chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, visual changes, or

You take your gliclazide in the morning after breakfast and you take your insulin at

If Asked About Driving: You do drive, but you have never driven when you feel dizzy.

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask why the patient wants to stop insulin.
• Ask if there are specific times the symptoms occur (for example just in the
mornings or also in the afternoons and evenings) and how frequent they are
(for example if they occur daily).
• Ask about other symptoms of hypoglycaemia including sweating, tremor,
palpitations, confusion, headache, or visual disturbance.
• Ask about red flag or severe features of hypoglycaemia including loss of
consciousness, seizures, falls, or injuries related to hypoglycaemia.
• Ask whether she checks her blood glucose levels during these episodes and
what the recorded readings are.
• Ask whether she is aware of early warning symptoms of hypoglycaemia and
what actions she takes to relieve symptoms. Also ask if she has ever needed
help from someone during hypoglycaemic episodes (this is important as if she
is not hypoglycaemia aware or has ever required assistance from another

-- 1126 of 1282 --

1125
person, for example due to loss of consciousness, she will need to stop
driving and inform the DVLA)
• Ask whether insulin is taken alongside gliclazide and confirm the timing of
gliclazide dosing, as taking them at night or close together can cause early-
morning hypoglycaemia.
• Ask about missed meals, reduced appetite, or reduced evening food intake
(this is important as missing or eating very little in the evening increases the
risk of overnight and early-morning hypoglycaemia).
• Ask if the patient was taught how to use her insulin and also check if she has
any injection site problems such as pain or swelling (lipohypertrophy). Note
that poor injection technique and injection site problems such as
lipohypertrophy can cause hypoglycaemia as they can alter absorption rates.
• Ask whether she monitors her blood pressure at home and when it was last
checked, to help rule out postural hypotension as a cause of dizziness.
• Ask about lifestyle and social history including occupation (this is important as
taxi drivers, Group 2 drivers, or machine operators may need work guidance),
alcohol use, smoking, drug use, and prolonged exercise. Note that
hypoglycaemia can occur several hours after prolonged exercise or after
alcohol ingestion.
• Ask how the symptoms have affected the patient.
• Ask about her home situation and whether she has any support.
• Ask about the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a working diagnosis of medication induced hypoglycaemia, most likely
due to insulin and sulfonylurea use.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Heather for explaining all that so clearly. From what you have told me, the
giddy feelings you are getting in the mornings are most likely due to low blood sugar
levels. This has happened because you are currently on both insulin and gliclazide,
and together they can push your blood sugar too low. This effect is stronger because
your kidney function is reduced, which means your body is not able to recover from
low blood sugars as easily as it normally would. Does that make sense so far?
The good news is that insulin itself is not a bad treatment, and it can be very helpful
in keeping blood sugar levels under control, especially in people whose kidneys are
not working as well as they used to. What we need to do is adjust the treatment so
that it works well for you without causing these low sugar episodes. I would
encourage you to continue with insulin, as we can reduce the risk of these giddy
feelings by making some changes.
How do you feel about that? Would you be happy to continue with insulin if I talk you
through the steps we can take to reduce your risk of these symptoms?

-- 1127 of 1282 --

1126
If you are happy to continue with insulin, I would strongly recommend that we stop
gliclazide, as this tablet is well known to cause low blood sugars in people with more
advanced kidney problems. Removing it should significantly reduce your risk of low
blood sugar episodes.
Once gliclazide has been stopped, we can add another tablet called Dapaglifozin.
This medication does not usually cause low blood sugars and can work alongside
insulin to help keep your blood sugar levels under control. Does that sound okay to
you?
I would also like to offer you a blood sugar monitoring system called Libre. This uses
a small, wearable sensor on the arm to measure your sugar levels and sends real-
time readings, to a reader or smartphone app. It allows us to see your sugar levels
throughout the day and night without regular finger-prick testing. This helps us spot
patterns and adjust your treatment more accurately. Are you happy for us to arrange
that?
I will also refer you to the diabetes specialist nurses, who will help monitor and safely
adjust your insulin dose. They can use the information from the Libre device to guide
your treatment. Does that sound okay to you?
To help prevent overnight low sugars, it is important that you have a regular evening
meal and a small bedtime snack.
Because you will be continuing insulin, you do need to inform the DVLA. This does
not automatically mean you have to stop driving, but the DVLA will decide whether it
is safe for you to continue. You should also avoid driving if you ever feel symptoms
of low blood sugar. Does that make sense?
Heather, it is important to always carry a fast-acting sugary drink, such as fruit juice
or a fizzy drink that is not sugar-free, and to take this immediately if you feel
symptoms starting. Avoid chocolate or biscuits as they do not work quickly enough.
Please also let your family know about this so they can help if needed.
If you have any further low sugar episodes, or if things do not improve, please let us
know straight away. And if you ever become very confused, collapse, have a
seizure, or symptoms do not improve after sugary drinks, you or your family should
call 999 immediately.
Management
• Explain to the patient that the combination of insulin and gliclazide is causing
recurrent hypoglycaemia. Explain that in advanced chronic kidney disease,
the body’s normal counter-regulatory response to low blood sugar is impaired,
making hypoglycaemia more frequent and prolonged.

-- 1128 of 1282 --

1127
• Offer a continuous glucose monitoring device such as FreeStyle Libre.
Explain that this is worn on the arm, reduces the need for finger-prick testing,
provides real-time glucose readings on a smart device, and helps identify
patterns of low blood sugar.
• Reassure her that insulin is an effective medication for controlling blood sugar
but requires careful titration, especially in people with reduced kidney function.
If she agrees to continue insulin:
• Stop gliclazide (sulfonylureas are known to cause hypoglycaemia in severe
renal impairment).
• Advise a consistent evening meal and a bedtime snack (this helps reduce
nocturnal hypoglycaemia and early-morning symptoms).
• Refer urgently to the diabetes specialist nurse for close monitoring and insulin
dose titration, to reduce hypoglycaemia risk.
• Offer SGLT2 inhibitors (gliflozins) such as dapagliflozin or DPP-4 inhibitors
(gliptins) such as linagliptin, in addition to insulin.
• Explain sick day rules for dapagliflozin. If she develops vomiting or diarrhoea,
she should temporarily stop the medication and restart it 24 to 48 hours after
she is able to eat and drink normally again.
If she refuses to continue insulin:
• Stop insulin in line with her wishes and also stop gliclazide
• Offer SGLT2 inhibitors (gliflozins), such as dapagliflozin and DPP-4 inhibitors
(gliptins), such as linagliptin
• Note that when eGFR is less than 45 mL/min/1.73 m², SGLT2 inhibitors lose
their effectiveness in controlling blood sugar, so they are not suitable as
monotherapy and an additional agent is often required.
Driving and safety:
• Advise that she must not drive if she develops impaired hypoglycaemia
awareness or experiences severe hypoglycaemia requiring help from another
person, and that in this situation she would need to inform the DVLA.
• If she decides to go ahead with insulin, advise that she needs to inform the
DVLA, and the DVLA will determine whether it is safe for her to continue to
drive (for Group 1 drivers this applies if insulin treatment lasts, or is expected
to last, more than 3 months).
Hypoglycaemia education:
• Advise keeping fast-acting carbohydrates readily available, such as 150 to
200 mL of fresh juice or fizzy drink (not sugar-free or reduced sugar) and to
take this if symptoms occur.
• Advise avoiding biscuits or chocolate due to high fat, but low sugar content.
• Encourage informing and educating close family members or household
contacts about her hypoglycaemia risk so help can be provided if needed.

-- 1129 of 1282 --

1128
Encourage her to receive recommended vaccinations, including pneumococcal and

Advise that if she experiences further episodes of hypoglycaemia, she should let us

Safety-net clearly and advise her to let her loved ones know about her condition, and

worsen after taking sugary drinks.
Arrange follow-up with the diabetes specialist nurses.
Learning Point from this Station
Hypoglycaemia is a recognised adverse effect of insulin therapy and sulfonylureas
such as gliclazide. Chronic kidney disease is an independent risk factor for
hypoglycaemia, and the risk is significantly increased when insulin is combined with
sulfonylureas. In patients with diabetes and chronic kidney disease, this combination
can markedly increase the likelihood of recurrent hypoglycaemia
According to the Association of British Clinical Diabetologists (ABCD) clinical
practice guidelines for the management of hyperglycaemia in adults with diabetic
kidney disease (2021 update), sulfonylureas should be avoided alongside
insulin in people with an eGFR below 45 mL/min/1.73 m², unless there is clear
evidence of the absence of hypoglycaemia.
Gliclazide is the preferred sulfonylurea in renal impairment because it is primarily
metabolised in the liver, is shorter acting, and carries a lower risk of hypoglycaemia
compared with other sulfonylureas. However, caution is still required because, even
though gliclazide is mainly metabolised in the liver, its metabolites are excreted by
the kidneys. In patients with reduced kidney function, these metabolites can
accumulate, increasing the risk of hypoglycaemia.
Type 2 Diabetes Management in Renal Failure
In type 2 diabetes, advanced chronic kidney disease is an independent risk factor for
hypoglycaemia. Where possible, glucose-lowering medications that do not
increase the risk of hypoglycaemia are preferred.
Metformin is contraindicated in patients with an eGFR below 30 mL/min/1.73 m²
and in those receiving dialysis.
SGLT2 inhibitors (e.g. dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) can be initiated in patients with
an eGFR between 15 and 29 mL/min/1.73 m² and may be continued at lower eGFR
levels if previously started and well tolerated. However, SGLT2 inhibitors have

-- 1130 of 1282 --

1129
minimal glucose-lowering effect when eGFR is below 45 mL/min/1.73 m² and are
used primarily for renal and cardiovascular protection in this setting.
GLP-1 receptor agonists (e.g. semaglutide, liraglutide) have been studied in
patients with an eGFR as low as 15 mL/min/1.73 m² and retain glucose-lowering
efficacy across the range of eGFR, including in dialysis patients. They are an
attractive option, especially in patients with advanced chronic kidney disease and
obesity exceeding BMI thresholds for kidney transplant listing.
Selected dipeptidyl peptidase-4 inhibitors (e.g. linagliptin) can be used in
patients with an eGFR below 30 mL/min/1.73 m² and in those on dialysis, and
provide a safe and effective treatment option for patients who are not treated with
GLP-1 receptor agonists.
Note: DPP-4 inhibitors should not be used alongside GLP-1 receptor agonists, as
they work on the same pathway and offer no additional blood glucose control, while
increasing cost, side effects, and pill burden. Guidelines strongly recommend
discontinuing DPP-4 inhibitors when initiating a GLP-1 receptor agonist.
Pioglitazone improves insulin sensitivity, which is often reduced in advanced
chronic kidney disease, and remains effective at lowering blood sugar in patients
with advanced kidney disease. However, it can cause fluid retention, so careful
monitoring is required in patients with renal failure.
Insulin and short-acting sulfonylureas (like gliclazide) are often required to
control blood glucose when medications with a lower risk of hypoglycaemia are
contraindicated, not tolerated, unavailable, or insufficient to achieve adequate
glycaemic control.

https://www.ukkidney.org/sites/renal.org/files/Managing%20hyperglycaemia%20in%

https://www.gov.uk/guidance/diabetes-mellitus-assessing-fitness-to-drive#insulin-
treated-diabetes
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/insulin-therapy-in-type-2-diabetes/management/insulin-

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Clarify with the patient why the blood tests were performed.
• Ask about the onset, duration, nature, location, and exact distribution of the
pain.
• Ask if there are any relieving or aggravating factors (for example whether
stress or anxiety triggers or worsens symptoms).
• Ask about morning stiffness. Note that in fibromyalgia, stiffness is usually brief
and lasts less than 30 minutes, and occasionally up to one hour, whereas in
ankylosing spondylitis and other inflammatory joint conditions, stiffness
typically lasts longer than 30 minutes.
• Ask if there is any swelling in any of the joints.
• Ask if there is associated fatigue or feeling tired all the time (this is common in
fibromyalgia).
• If tiredness is present, ask whether it is disproportionate to the level of activity
(this is common in chronic fatigue syndrome and fibromyalgia).
• Ask about other symptoms related to fibromyalgia including headaches,
paraesthesia, and impaired memory or concentration.
• Ask about red flag symptoms including unexplained weight loss, night sweats,
fevers, weakness in the lower limbs, tingling in both legs, perianal or perineal
paraesthesia, or bowel or bladder dysfunction.
• Ask about the impact of symptoms on daily functioning, employment, family
life, and physical activity.
• Ask about sleep, appetite, low mood, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress
symptoms (for example flashbacks, nightmares, feeling detached or
emotionally numb, or avoidance), particularly given his military background.
• Ask if he has tried any treatments for his symptoms and whether they were
helpful.
• Ask about his social history including smoking, alcohol use, occupation, and
home situation.
• Ask about his ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a working diagnosis of possible fibromyalgia.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Peter for explaining everything again today. I know this has been going
on for a long time and has had a big impact on your life, especially with work and
day-to-day activities.
I want to start by reassuring you that your blood test results are all normal. They do
not show any evidence of rheumatoid arthritis. That is reassuring news.

-- 1134 of 1282 --

1133
From everything you have told me, your examination, and the normal blood tests,
your symptoms fit best with a condition called fibromyalgia. Have you heard about
this before?
Fibromyalgia is a long-term condition that tends to flare from time to time, causing
widespread pain, fatigue, and tiredness. It does not mean there is damage to your
joints or muscles, and it is not something that will cause long-term harm, but it can
be very distressing and exhausting. Are you following me so far?
One of the key parts of managing fibromyalgia is understanding your symptoms
better. I would encourage you to keep a simple symptom diary. This can help you
notice patterns, such as whether stress, activity, or poor sleep makes the pain
worse, and can help you manage your symptoms more effectively. Is this something
you would be happy to do?
Another important approach is something called pacing. This means balancing
activity and rest, rather than pushing through pain or fatigue and then crashing
afterwards. For example, breaking tasks into smaller chunks, resting before you feel
completely exhausted, and gradually building up activity over time. It is one of the
most effective ways to improve symptoms in the long term. How does that sound?
I would also like to refer you to physiotherapy. They can work with you on a graded
exercise programme that is tailored to you and your current ability, helping you stay
active without making symptoms worse.
Because pain and fatigue can affect how the brain processes pain signals, talking
therapies such as cognitive behavioural therapy can be very helpful. This is not
because the pain is “in your head”, but because CBT helps change how pain is
processed, improves coping strategies, and can reduce the overall impact on your
life. Would you be open to that?
In terms of pain relief, I understand that paracetamol and ibuprofen have not helped.
Alongside the non-medication approaches, we can try a low dose of a medication
called amitriptyline. This is taken in the evening and can help with pain and sleep.
We would start at a very low dose. Common side effects can include feeling drowsy
or having a dry mouth, but most people tolerate it well. I should mention that this is
an off-label use. This means the medication was originally licensed for different
conditions, but doctors have found that it can also be used safely in fibromyalgia and
can be helpful for symptoms like pain and poor sleep. Are you happy to try that?
Given the impact this has had on your work and finances, I would also like to refer
you to our social prescribing team. They can help with advice about financial
support, benefits you may be entitled to, and help with applications such as
Universal Credit, as well as signposting you to local support services. I think that
could be helpful while we work on improving your symptoms.

-- 1135 of 1282 --

1134
It is important that you let us know if you develop any new or worrying symptoms,
such as worsening weakness, numbness, problems with your bladder or bowels,
unexplained weight loss, night sweats, or if you start having thoughts of harming
yourself. If that happens, please seek urgent medical advice.
I would like to arrange a follow-up in about four weeks to see how you are getting on,
whether the medication is helping, and whether we need to adjust anything. How
does this plan sound to you?
Management
• No need for a face-to-face review, as he has already been examined by a
colleague and there are no new red flag symptoms.
• Advise him to keep a symptom diary, as this can help identify potential
triggers for worsening pain and support better self-management.
• Provide advice on pacing. Explain that pacing is an energy management
technique designed to balance activity and rest to avoid symptom flares. This
involves identifying current limits and staying within them rather than pushing
through fatigue. For example, breaking large tasks into smaller, manageable
activities with rest in between, resting before feeling exhausted, and gradually
increasing activity levels over time.
• Offer referral to physiotherapy, as they can provide tailored graded exercise
programmes to help improve symptoms and physical function.
• Offer cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT). Explain that talking therapy can
help change how the brain processes pain, improve coping strategies, reduce
psychological distress, and lead to an overall improvement in quality of life.
• For pain management, explain that as paracetamol and ibuprofen have not
been effective, medication can be considered alongside non-pharmacological
approaches. Offer low-dose amitriptyline, starting at 10 mg taken 2 to 4 hours
before bedtime. Inform him about common side effects such as drowsiness
and dry mouth, and reassure him that most people tolerate this medication
well. Explain that this is an off-label use of the medication.
• Offer referral to the social prescriber to provide advice on financial support
and benefits eligibility, help with applications such as Universal Credit or other
employment-related benefits, and signposting to local support services.
• Offer written information and leaflets about fibromyalgia for further reading.
• Safety-net clearly and advise him to seek urgent medical advice if he
develops red flag symptoms such as thoughts of suicide or self-harm,
progressive weakness, numbness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, unexplained
weight loss, or night sweats.
• Arrange follow-up in four weeks to assess response to treatment and consider
dose titration if required.

-- 1136 of 1282 --

1135
Learning Point from this Station
Fibromyalgia is a chronic pain disorder of unknown cause. It is more common in
women, who are affected around ten times more frequently than men. To diagnose
fibromyalgia, symptoms must have been present for at least three months, and there
should be no other condition that would otherwise explain the pain.
Fibromyalgia is associated with a range of symptoms, including widespread pain,
fatigue, headaches, morning stiffness, paraesthesia, and a sensation of swollen
joints despite no objective swelling on examination. Other common associated
features include problems with cognition, such as memory disturbance or difficulty
with word finding, as well as depression, anxiety, and sleep disturbance.
Management focuses on education and non-pharmacological approaches. This
includes advising patients to keep a symptom diary to identify triggers, using energy
management techniques such as pacing, referral for cognitive behavioural therapy,
and physiotherapy for graded exercise therapy.
Pharmacological management of pain may include antidepressants such as
duloxetine, amitriptyline, citalopram, fluoxetine, paroxetine, or sertraline. These
medications are used off-label in fibromyalgia to help manage pain and associated
symptoms.
The diagnosis of fibromyalgia can be made confidently in primary care. Referral to
rheumatology is only necessary if there are concerns about inflammatory arthritis or
if the patient does not improve despite appropriate management in primary care.
Further Reading
https://patient.info/doctor/rheumatology/fibromyalgia-pro
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/chronic-pain/

1138
Thank you Liam for explaining everything so clearly today. I can understand why
seeing blood when you vomited would be worrying, especially at your age.
From what you have told me, this episode is most likely due to repeated, forceful
vomiting after drinking a large amount of alcohol. This can cause a small tear in the
lining where the food pipe meets the stomach, called a Mallory–Weiss tear, or
irritation of the stomach lining. The good news is that these usually heal on their own
and, as the bleeding was very small and has not happened again, there are no signs
of anything serious at the moment. Does that make sense so far?
I would still like to see you face to face so I can examine your tummy and check your
observations, just to be safe. If you are not able to come in today, that is absolutely
fine and we can arrange this for tomorrow. Is that alright with you?
To help the lining of your stomach heal, I would like to start you on a medication
called omeprazole. You would take one tablet once a day for four weeks. This
reduces stomach acid and helps prevent further irritation. Are you happy to try that?
I also want to talk about alcohol, as this is an important part of what has happened.
Drinking at the levels you described can irritate the stomach and increase the risk of
vomiting and bleeding. The amount of alcohol you are currently drinking is also
above recommended limits, which can cause problems with the liver and lead to
other health issues over time. I would strongly advise cutting down your alcohol
intake. If you feel you might struggle with this, we can support you, including referral
to local alcohol services who can help you reduce safely and avoid withdrawal
symptoms. How do you feel about that?
Because of the amount of alcohol you are drinking, I would also recommend some
blood tests, including liver function tests, as alcohol can affect the liver. This would
be to make sure everything is okay. Are you happy for me to arrange this for you?
It is important that you know when to seek help. If you vomit blood again, if your
stools turn black, or if you develop dizziness, fainting, worsening tummy pain, or
heart racing, you should seek urgent medical advice straight away by contacting the
urgent GP service or NHS 111 if it is out of hours. If symptoms are severe, you
should attend A and E.
I would like to review you again in about four weeks to see how you are getting on.
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to assess the abdomen and check
observations. If he declines to attend today, this can be arranged for the next
day, as there is no immediate urgency.
• Offer blood tests, including liver function tests, to assess for alcohol-related
liver injury given his one-year history of heavy alcohol intake.

-- 1140 of 1282 --

1139
• Advise him that he needs to reduce his alcohol intake, as ongoing heavy
drinking is harmful.
• Offer referral to local alcohol support services to help him cut down safely and
reduce the risk of withdrawal symptoms.
• Offer omeprazole 20 mg once daily for four weeks to aid mucosal healing and
reduce gastric acid irritation.
• Safety-net clearly. Advise him to seek urgent medical advice by contacting the
urgent GP service or NHS 111 if out of hours if vomiting blood recurs, stools
become black, or he develops dizziness, abdominal pain, fainting, or
palpitations.
• Arrange follow-up in four weeks to review symptoms.
Learning Point from this Station
Mallory-Weiss syndrome (MWS) is a common but often overlooked cause of non-
variceal upper gastrointestinal bleeding. It results from longitudinal mucosal tears,
usually at the gastro-oesophageal junction, caused by sudden rises in intra-
abdominal pressure during vomiting, retching, or coughing.
The classic presentation is haematemesis following repeated retching or vomiting,
although a tear can occasionally occur after a single episode. Bleeding is usually
mild and self-limiting, meaning conservative medical management is sufficient in
most cases.
Alcohol-induced gastritis is another common cause of upper gastrointestinal bleeding
in people who binge drink and may coexist with a Mallory-Weiss tear. In patients who
are clinically stable with minimal, self-limited bleeding, management in primary care
maybe appropriate. However, if bleeding persists, stools become dark and tarry, or
the patient develops features of haemodynamic compromise such as dizziness,
palpitations, or syncope, urgent admission via A and E for immediate assessment
and resuscitation maybe required.
Further Reading
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538190/
https://patient.info/doctor/gastroenterology/mallory-weiss-syndrome-pro
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/alcohol-problem-drinking/

Ideas: You think this is gastritis because the nurse in A and E suggested this.
Concerns: You do not have any particular concerns.
Expectations: You would like the GP to prescribe medication for gastritis.
Say NO to any other questions/symptoms asked outside of the details already
provided in the scenario. Accept anything offered to you by the doctor.
Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the location, onset, and nature or character of the chest pain.
• Ask about aggravating and relieving factors for the pain (stable angina is
typically precipitated by physical exertion and relieved by rest).
• Ask whether the chest pain occurs only with exertion or also at rest (pain at
rest suggests acute coronary syndrome and requires urgent hospital
assessment).
• Ask whether the chest pain radiates anywhere, for example to the neck,
shoulders, jaw, or arms.
• Ask how long the pain lasts before resolving (stable angina pain usually lasts
less than 15 minutes).
• Ask whether she currently has chest pain.
• Ask about associated symptoms including shortness of breath, palpitations,
dizziness, nausea, or sweating.
• Ask about any past cardiac history or previous similar episodes.
• Ask about respiratory symptoms such as cough or recent flu-like illness.
• Ask about gastrointestinal symptoms including heartburn, vomiting,
regurgitation, and whether the pain is related to meals, to help rule out
gastritis as suggested by the nurse in A and E.
• Ask about social and lifestyle factors including smoking, alcohol use, illicit
drug use, physical activity level, occupation, and diet.
• Ask about family history of heart attack or stroke.
• Ask about the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a working diagnosis of stable angina.

-- 1143 of 1282 --

1142
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you, Emma, for explaining everything clearly today. I understand why you
were told this might be gastritis, and it is completely reasonable that you are asking
for treatment. However, after going through your symptoms carefully, I am
concerned that this chest pain is more likely to be coming from your heart rather than
your stomach. Are you following me so far?
The reason for my concern is that the pain comes on with exertion, such as walking
upstairs or carrying heavy shopping, and then settles when you rest. That pattern is
very typical of a condition called stable angina. Is this something you have heard of
before?
Angina happens when there is narrowing in the blood vessels that supply the heart.
Because of this narrowing, the heart muscle does not get enough blood and oxygen
during activity, which causes the chest pain you are experiencing. When you stop
and rest, the demand on the heart reduces and the pain settles. Does that make
sense to you?
Given your smoking history and family history of heart disease, it is important that we
take this seriously and investigate it properly.
What I would like to do next is arrange for you to be seen face to face so I can
examine your heart and repeat an ECG. I would also like to arrange some blood
tests, including cholesterol levels, as these help us assess your overall
cardiovascular risk. Does that sound okay?
I am also going to start you on two medications today. One is aspirin, which helps
reduce the risk of heart attacks, and the other is a spray called glyceryl trinitrate, or
GTN. If you get chest pain again, you should stop what you are doing and rest, then
use one spray under your tongue. If the pain has not eased after five minutes, you
should use a second spray. If the pain is still there five minutes after that, it is very
important that you call 999 for an ambulance. Does that make sense?
I will also refer you urgently to the rapid access chest pain clinic. You should be seen
within two weeks, and they will arrange further tests and scans to confirm the
diagnosis and decide on the best long-term treatment. Are you happy for me to make
this referral?
One very important thing we also need to talk about is smoking. Smoking
significantly increases the risk of heart disease, and stopping smoking is one of the
most effective ways to protect your heart. I appreciate that stopping is not easy, but
we can support you with this. If you are happy, I can refer you to our smoking
cessation service to help you quit safely and successfully.
If you develop chest pain at rest, worsening pain, pain lasting more than 15 minutes,
or symptoms such as breathlessness, dizziness, or collapse, please call 999 straight
away.

-- 1144 of 1282 --

1143
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment for a cardiovascular examination and repeat
ECG.
• Arrange blood tests including lipid profile, full blood count, urea and
electrolytes, and liver function tests.
• Start aspirin 75 mg once daily, unless contraindicated, and prescribe glyceryl
trinitrate (GTN) spray.
• Advise that if chest pain occurs, she should stop what she is doing and rest,
then use one spray of GTN. If the pain has not eased after five minutes, she
should use a second spray. If the pain has still not eased five minutes after
the second dose, she should call 999 for an ambulance.
• Offer an urgent referral to the rapid access chest pain clinic (RACPC). Explain
that she should be seen within two weeks and that further investigations, such
as scans and tests, will be carried out to confirm the diagnosis.
• Advise on smoking cessation and offer referral to smoking cessation services.
• Advise on maintaining a healthy lifestyle, including regular physical activity
within tolerance and a balanced diet.
• Safety-net clearly. Advise her to seek urgent medical help by calling 999 if she
develops chest pain at rest, worsening or more frequent pain, pain lasting
more than 15 minutes, or associated symptoms such as breathlessness,
dizziness, or collapse.
• Arrange follow-up after she has been seen in the rapid access chest pain
clinic to review the outcome and ongoing management.
Learning Point from this Station
Stable angina typically presents as chest pain or discomfort that:
• Occurs predictably with physical exertion or emotional stress
• Settles within minutes of rest or with sublingual glyceryl trinitrate (GTN)
• Lasts less than 15 minutes
• Follows a consistent pattern
• Suspected stable angina should referred urgently to a rapid access chest pain
clinic.
Unstable angina refers to chest pain that suggests an acute coronary syndrome.
This includes:
• Chest pain occurring at rest
• Chest pain lasting longer than 15 minutes
• Chest pain that is worsening in frequency, severity, or occurring with less
exertion than before

-- 1145 of 1282 --

1144
• Chest pain that does not reliably settle with rest or GTN
• Unstable angina requires urgent hospital admission.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/angina/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/chest-pain/
