Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Clarify the patient’s understanding of why a safeguarding referral was made
and what concerns were raised in A and E.
• Ask about the events leading to the child’s injury, including when it happened,
who was present, and what actions were taken afterwards.
• Ask about the child’s current condition, including whether the injury has
healed, whether the child is still in pain, and where the child is currently, for
example, at home or nursery.
• Ask about the child’s behaviour, including whether he appears withdrawn,
fearful, or less engaged than usual.
• Ask whether the child is eating and drinking normally, is up to date with
immunisations, and whether there are any concerns about his development.
• Ask if there has been any previous history of injuries, accidents, or hospital
attendances involving the child.
• Ask about the home situation, including who lives at home and whether there
are any other children in the household.

-- 1148 of 1282 --

1147
• Ask about the partner, including his role in the household, alcohol use,
smoking, or illicit drug use, and whether there has been any abusive
behaviour towards the patient or the child.
• Ask whether the patient feels safe at home.
• Ask about the patient herself, including occupation, financial situation,
smoking, alcohol use, and illicit drug use.
• Ask about her ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Consider safeguarding concerns relating to a child with an unexplained injury,
and potential domestic abuse risk.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Toni for explaining everything so openly today. I can hear how worried
and upset you are, and that is completely understandable. Anyone in your position
would feel anxious, especially when safeguarding is mentioned and your child is
involved.
I want to reassure you first that a safeguarding referral does not automatically mean
that a child will be taken away. Safeguarding is about making sure children are safe
and supported, and in most cases, the aim is to keep families together wherever it is
safe to do so. Does that make sense so far?
I also want to be honest with you about your medical records. Past histories of
cannabis and alcohol misuse cannot be removed from your records. As doctors, we
have an ethical and professional responsibility to keep records accurate and factual.
If you were to become unwell in the future and see another doctor, this information
could be important in helping them make safe decisions about your care. Does that
make sense?
I want to reassure you that safeguarding teams usually seek your consent before
accessing your medical records where possible. However, information may be
shared without consent if there are concerns about a child’s safety.
Toni, some of the things you have told me today do raise concerns about your safety
and your child’s safety. Because of that, it is important that I make a referral to the
safeguarding team today. This is to make sure that both you and your son are
properly supported and kept safe. How do you feel about that?
The children’s safeguarding team are likely to contact you within the next 24 to 48
hours. They will want to gather information and understand what has happened.
Their role is to assess the situation and offer support where needed, not to blame
you. I would strongly encourage you to engage openly with them and attend any
appointments they arrange.
If at any point you feel unsafe at home, or if you are worried about your partner’s
behaviour towards you or your son, it is really important that you seek help urgently.

-- 1149 of 1282 --

1148
That could mean calling the police on 999 if there is immediate danger, or seeking
urgent medical help if you are worried about your child.
I would like to arrange a follow-up call with you in the next 24 to 48 hours to check
whether safeguarding have been in touch and to see how you are coping. Does that
sound okay to you?
Management
• Inform her that past histories of cannabis and alcohol misuse cannot be
removed from her medical records, as medical records must be accurate,
factual, and reflect relevant past health information.
• Inform her that there is an ethical and professional responsibility to keep
medical records accurate. Explain that if she were to see a doctor in the
future, knowledge of her past medical history could be important in guiding
safe and appropriate care, and removing this information could affect the
management of her condition.
• Explain that safeguarding teams usually seek consent to access medical
records where possible. However, information may be shared without consent
if there are concerns about a child’s safety, in line with safeguarding
legislation.
• Offer to make an urgent referral to children’s safeguarding regardless of
whether a referral was made by the hospital. Note that safeguarding is
everyone’s responsibility, and hospital referrals can occasionally be delayed,
missed, or misdirected. Given that two weeks have passed since the incident
without contact, this raises a red flag that the safeguarding referral may have
been missed.
• Inform her that the child safeguarding team is likely to contact her within the
next 24 to 48 hours to gather further information and assess the situation.
Reassure her that their aim is to support families and keep children with their
parents wherever it is safe to do so.
• Encourage her to engage openly and honestly with safeguarding
professionals and to attend any appointments or assessments arranged.
• Safety-net clearly. Advise her to seek urgent help by calling 999 if she feels
unsafe at home or if she becomes concerned about her partner’s behaviour
towards her or her child.
• Advise her to seek urgent medical attention if she has any immediate
concerns about her child’s health or safety.
• Offer follow-up with a telephone review within 24 to 48 hours, to check
whether safeguarding services have made contact and to provide further
support if needed.

-- 1150 of 1282 --

1149
Learning Point from this Station
Safeguarding is everyone’s responsibility. Healthcare professionals have a legal,
ethical, and professional duty to act when there are concerns about the safety or
wellbeing of a child or vulnerable adult.
In this scenario, the mother has raised several concerns that require immediate
safeguarding action. Although she reports that the hospital made a referral, it is
important not to assume that this has been received or acted upon. Hospital referrals
can occasionally be delayed, missed, or misdirected. Given that two weeks have
passed since the incident without any contact, this raises a red flag that the
safeguarding referral may have been missed and should be followed up urgently.
According to the General Medical Council (GMC), confidential information can be
shared without consent if required by law or a court, or if sharing the information is
necessary to protect a child or young person. Doctors must balance the risk of harm
from not sharing information against the potential harm of breaching confidentiality.
Even if a parent or a child with capacity objects, information should usually be
shared when a child is at risk of abuse or neglect, as safeguarding the child’s welfare
takes priority.
If information is shared without consent, you should explain the reasons for doing so
to the people involved, unless doing this would place the child, young person, or
others at increased risk.
Medical records must remain accurate and factual and cannot be altered to remove
relevant past history.
Further Reading
https://www.gmc-uk.org/professional-standards/the-professional-
standards/protecting-children-and-young-people/about-this-guidance

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset, duration, and progression of the shortness of breath.
• Ask what triggers the symptoms and confirm whether they are situational, for
example occurring only when thinking about or attempting to leave the house.
• Ask about associated symptoms during episodes, including palpitations,
sweating, trembling, dizziness, a choking sensation, fear of losing control, fear
of dying, or avoidance of situations that may provoke breathlessness.
• Ask about the frequency of episodes and whether symptoms resolve
spontaneously.
• Ask about chest pain, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, wheeze,
cough, fever, recent flu-like symptoms, leg swelling, or reduced exercise
tolerance, to help exclude cardiac or respiratory disease.
• Ask whether there was any specific incident that triggered the onset of
symptoms, for example being attacked, robbed, or experiencing a distressing
event while outside, or whether the symptoms started spontaneously.
• Ask about mood, sleep, and appetite.
• Ask directly about thoughts of suicide or self-harm.
• Ask about common symptoms associated with cancer or cancer treatment,
including pain, constipation, or vomiting.
• Ask about the impact of symptoms on daily activities, independence, and
relationships.
• Ask about social and lifestyle history including smoking, alcohol use, illicit
drug use, and home situation.
• Ask about the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a working diagnosis of panic attacks with agoraphobia.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Mark for explaining everything so clearly today. I can see how frightening
and limiting these symptoms have been for you, especially as they are stopping you
from leaving the house and enjoying time with your wife and grandchildren.
From everything you have told me, and taking into account the normal assessments
carried out by the ambulance crews, your symptoms are most consistent with panic
attacks. Panic attacks can cause very real physical symptoms such as sudden

-- 1154 of 1282 --

1153
breathlessness, a racing heart, dizziness, and a strong sense of fear. Are you
following me so far?
An important part of what you have described is that these symptoms mainly happen
when you think about going outside or trying to leave the house. This pattern has a
name, and it is called agoraphobia. Agoraphobia means a fear of being in situations
where you feel you might not be able to escape or get help if symptoms come on. It
often develops alongside panic attacks, and it can gradually lead to avoiding going
out altogether. Does that make sense?
Although the symptoms feel very intense and frightening, they are not dangerous in
themselves. However, I completely understand how distressing they are and how
much they have affected your independence and quality of life.
The most effective treatment for panic attacks with agoraphobia is a talking therapy
called cognitive behavioural therapy, or CBT. CBT helps you understand how anxiety
affects the body and teaches techniques to manage panic symptoms. It also involves
gradual, supported exposure to situations you have started to avoid, helping you
rebuild confidence step by step. I would strongly recommend this. How would you
feel about being referred for talking therapy?
While you are waiting for therapy, there are some practical steps that can help.
Avoiding things that can worsen anxiety, such as caffeine, is important. Learning
simple breathing and grounding techniques can also help you manage symptoms
when they start.
We also have the option of medication while you are waiting for talking therapy, or if
you do not feel comfortable with talking therapy. If we do start medication, it is
important to know that symptoms can sometimes feel slightly worse in the first one to
two weeks before they begin to improve. We would monitor you closely and review
you after two weeks. Would you like to start medication to help with your symptoms?
It is also important that you know when to seek urgent help. If your symptoms
change in nature, start happening without anxiety triggers, are associated with chest
pain, collapse, other new worrying symptoms, or if you develop thoughts of harming
yourself, you should seek urgent medical attention by contacting the urgent GP line
or NHS 111 if out of hours, or calling 999 if symptoms are severe.
Management
• Acknowledge his symptoms and validate how distressing and limiting this has
been for him.
• Explain that his symptoms are most consistent with panic attacks, which can
cause very real physical symptoms such as breathlessness, palpitations,
dizziness, and a fear response.
• Offer referral for cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) as first-line treatment,
focusing on graded exposure and anxiety management techniques.

-- 1155 of 1282 --

1154
• Advise avoiding anxiety-provoking substances, for example caffeine, as these
can worsen panic symptoms.
• Reassure him that many people respond well to CBT. Explain that while he is
waiting for therapy, medication is an option if symptoms are significantly
affecting his quality of life.
• If he is open to medication, offer an SSRI such as sertraline, escitalopram, or
paroxetine, which are licensed treatments for panic disorder.
• If medication is started, explain that symptoms can sometimes worsen in the
first one to two weeks before improving, and advise close monitoring.
• Safety-net clearly. Advise him to seek urgent medical help if his symptoms
change in nature, occur without anxiety triggers, are associated with chest
pain, collapse, or other new concerning symptoms, or if he develops thoughts
of suicide or self-harm.
• Arrange follow-up in two weeks if medication is started, or in four weeks if
CBT alone is pursued.
Learning Point from this Station
Agoraphobia is a fear of being in situations where escape may feel difficult or where
help may not be available. These situations often trigger intense anxiety or panic
attacks. As a result, people may start avoiding places such as shops, public spaces,
or leaving the house altogether.
Agoraphobia is different from social anxiety disorder. Social anxiety disorder is the
fear of being judged, criticised, or embarrassed in social situations, such as speaking
in public or meeting new people. In agoraphobia, the fear is not about judgement, but
about feeling unsafe or unable to escape if symptoms occur.
Cancer can have a significant psychological impact. A cancer diagnosis is a major
life stressor and can trigger anxiety disorders, including panic attacks and
agoraphobia. Fear about health, uncertainty, and previous frightening experiences
can contribute to this.
Importantly, anxiety disorders such as agoraphobia can develop after successful
cancer treatment. This may be related to the emotional toll of the cancer journey,
fear of symptoms returning, loss of the "safety" of regular medical follow-ups, or fear
of experiencing panic symptoms away from help.
Further Reading
https://patient.info/doctor/mental-health/agoraphobia-pro

Marking Scheme

hallucinations (especially whether he is still hearing voices or seeing things),
or delusional beliefs.
• Confirm when his last relapse or psychotic episode occurred and whether it
was approximately one year ago.
• Ask directly about any current or recent thoughts of suicide, self-harm, or
harm to others.
• Ask about any safeguarding concerns, including whether he has felt bullied,
verbally abused, physically abused, or mistreated by his mental health
support workers or carers.
• Ask about his current employment, what his role involves, and why he wishes
to increase his working hours, including whether there are any financial
pressures.
• Ask about social and lifestyle factors, including where he lives, smoking
status, alcohol intake, and use of illicit drugs.

-- 1159 of 1282 --

1158
• Ask whether he has discussed his concerns or wishes with the Community
Mental Health Team.
• Ask about the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you, Jeffrey, for explaining how you are feeling today. I can understand why
you want more independence, more freedom to move around on your own, and to
increase your working hours, especially as you are feeling much better now.
I want to be open with you. I am not able to write a letter saying that you have
capacity to stop your support worker, stop your medication, or make changes to your
care plan. That is not because I do not trust you or think you are unwell, but because
decisions like this need to be made by your mental health team and your consultant
psychiatrist, who know your history in detail and are responsible for keeping you
safe. Are you following me so far?
It may help to explain how capacity works. We do not routinely write letters to
confirm capacity, because everyone is assumed to have capacity unless there is
evidence otherwise. Capacity is also decision-specific and time-specific. Most people
have capacity to make everyday decisions, such as what to wear or where to go.
However, bigger or higher-risk decisions, such as stopping medication or changing
levels of support, may need specialist assessment and guidance. This is not about
taking control away from you. It is about making sure any changes are made with
you, in a safe and supported way. How does that sound to you?
It is also important that you do not stop your medication suddenly. Even when people
feel well, stopping medication without specialist review can lead to a relapse, which
could put you at risk again. Does that make sense?
What I can do is contact your Community Mental Health Team today and let them
know how you are feeling. They can arrange a proper review to look at whether it is
safe to reduce supervision, review your medication, and discuss increasing your
working hours in a gradual and supported way. Are you happy for me to contact
them and update them on what we have discussed?
I hear that you want more independence, and that is a very reasonable goal. The
safest way to work towards that is step by step, with the mental health team
involved, rather than making sudden changes.
If at any point you notice your mood worsening, voices returning, or you have
thoughts of harming yourself or others, it is really important that you seek urgent help
straight away.
I will update you as soon as I hear back from your mental health team. How does
that sound to you?

-- 1160 of 1282 --

1159
Management
• Explain that the GP cannot provide a letter declaring capacity to stop
supervision, stop medication, or change care arrangements, as these
decisions sit with the Community Mental Health Team and Consultant
Psychiatrist.
• Explain that everyone is assumed to have capacity unless proven otherwise
and that capacity is decision-specific and time-specific. Everyday decisions
(such as what clothes to wear) are usually within capacity, but higher-risk
decisions such as stopping medication or reducing supervision require
specialist assessment to ensure decisions are made safely.
• Reassure him that this is not about taking control away from him, but about
ensuring changes are made with him in a safe and supported way.
• Advise strongly against stopping medication abruptly, explaining that sudden
discontinuation of antipsychotic or antidepressant medication significantly
increases the risk of relapse, with potential risk to himself and others.
• Offer to contact and inform the Community Mental Health Team about his
concerns. Explain that they may arrange a multidisciplinary review involving
his psychiatrist, mental health support workers, and care coordinators to
assess whether it is safe to reduce supervision, increase working hours, or
consider any changes to medication.
• Advise that any increase in working hours should be gradual and agreed with
the mental health team, rather than an immediate move to full-time work, as
increased stress can trigger a relapse.
• Safety-net clearly: advise him to seek urgent help from mental health
services, the local crisis team, or emergency services if he experiences a
return of hallucinations, suicidal thoughts, thoughts of harming others,
worsening mood, or loss of control.
• Reassure him that you will update him as soon as you hear back from the
Community Mental Health Team and arrange follow-up to review the
outcome.
Learning Point from this Station
Mental capacity is decision-specific and time-specific. This patient may have
capacity for day-to-day decisions such as what to wear, where to go locally, or
working limited hours. However, the decisions he is asking for stopping antipsychotic
medication, ending constant supervision, and increasing work to full time are high-
risk, complex decisions.

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask when the vomiting episodes started and how symptoms have progressed
over time
• Ask how many episodes of vomiting occur in a typical day and during a flare
• Ask about the contents of the vomit, specifically whether there is any blood or
bile
• Ask whether the patient is able to keep fluids down between episodes
• Ask about any known triggers, including emotional stress or anxiety,
infections, certain foods, prolonged periods without food, dehydration, hot
weather, menstrual periods, excessive exercise, or sleep deprivation
• Ask about associated symptoms such as nausea, abdominal pain, diarrhoea,
constipation, bloating, or unintentional weight loss
• Ask how the vomiting is affecting the patient’s day-to-day activities, work, and
quality of life
• Ask about symptoms of dehydration, including headache, dry mouth or lips,
dizziness, increased thirst, lethargy, and reduced urine output
• Ask about the date of her last menstrual period and acknowledge the recent
negative pregnancy test performed in the emergency department
• Ask about social and lifestyle history, including smoking, alcohol intake, illicit
drug use, particularly cannabis use, occupation, and home situation
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a diagnosis of cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Bella for explaining everything so clearly. From everything you have told
me, the most likely explanation for your symptoms is something called cannabis
induced vomiting.
This is a condition that can happen in people who use cannabis regularly. It can
cause repeated episodes of nausea and vomiting over weeks or months, and the
episodes can come on quite suddenly, just as you have described. Stress can often
make the symptoms worse, which fits with what you have noticed.
The most important part of treatment is stopping cannabis completely. Anti sickness
medication can help in the short term, but the vomiting usually continues if cannabis
use carries on. Does that make sense? I appreciate that this may be difficult, but I
want to reassure you that you are not on your own. We do have local services that
can support you if you would like help to stop, and we can talk through those
together. Is that something you would consider?

-- 1165 of 1282 --

1164
I would also like to see you face to face to examine your abdomen and arrange
some blood tests, just to make sure there is nothing else contributing and that your
salts and other blood results are normal. In the meantime, I can prescribe you some
anti sickness tablets to use when your symptoms occur.
It may also be helpful to keep a symptom diary, as this can help us identify any other
triggers aside from stress and monitor how your symptoms change over time.
You mentioned that stress plays a part in this. Are there any ways you feel you might
be able to reduce stress at the moment? Some people find breathing or relaxation
techniques helpful, others benefit from talking therapies, and some prefer increasing
physical activity such as walking, jogging, or running. Are any of these options
something you would like to try?
If at any point the vomiting becomes persistent, you are unable to keep fluids down,
you notice blood in your vomit or stools, you develop severe tummy pain,
unintentional weight loss, or symptoms of dehydration such as dizziness or racing of
your heart, please seek urgent medical attention by calling our urgent GP line or
NHS 111 if out of hours.
Management
• Offer a face to face appointment to perform an abdominal examination and
arrange blood tests, including urea and electrolytes, full blood count, liver
function tests, and HbA1c, to ensure there are no other contributing causes
and no electrolyte abnormalities
• Advise the patient to keep a symptom diary to help track the frequency and
severity of symptoms and to identify any additional triggers other than stress
that may help with prevention.
• Offer antiemetic treatment for symptomatic relief, for example
prochlorperazine 10 mg three times daily to be used when required
• Advise that stopping cannabis use completely is the most effective way to
prevent further episodes of vomiting.
• Explain that anti sickness medication may provide short term relief but will not
fully resolve symptoms if cannabis use continues.
• Offer support for stopping cannabis use and explain that local drug misuse
services are available if she would like additional help
• Discuss strategies to manage stress, including breathing and relaxation
exercises, regular physical activity, yoga, and talking therapies.
• Provide safety net advice to seek urgent medical review (by calling the GP
urgent line or 111 if out of hours) if vomiting becomes persistent, if she is
unable to keep fluids down, if there is blood in vomit or stools, if severe
abdominal pain develops, if there is unintentional weight loss, or if there are
signs of dehydration such as dizziness or palpitations.

-- 1166 of 1282 --

1165
Learning Point from this Station
Cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome is a condition associated with regular, long term
cannabis use and presents with recurrent or cyclical episodes of severe nausea and
vomiting. Cannabis contains several active compounds that act on receptors within
the brain and gastrointestinal system. While central effects are responsible for the
euphoric sensation experienced by users, peripheral effects alter gastric motility,
delay gastric emptying, and affect lower oesophageal sphincter function, leading to
persistent nausea and vomiting.
A key management point is that symptom resolution requires complete cessation of
cannabis use. Reducing cannabis intake is insufficient and symptoms typically
persist if use continues. Early recognition, non judgemental history taking, and clear
patient education are essential to achieving symptom resolution and preventing
recurrent presentations.
Standard antiemetic medication may be used to provide symptomatic relief. If there
is poor response to conventional antiemetics, topical capsaicin 0.1 percent cream
may be considered, with approximately 5 g applied to the abdomen.
Amitriptyline may also be considered for prevention of recurrent flares; however, the
evidence supporting its use is limited.
Further Reading
https://rcem.ac.uk/wp-
content/uploads/2024/02/RCEM_Cannabinoid_Hyperemesis_Syndrome_v5.0.pdf
https://patient.info/doctor/gastroenterology/cyclical-vomiting-syndrome-pro

-- 1167 of 1282 --

1168
• Ask about analgesic use and frequency to exclude medication overuse

headache)

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you, Catherine, for explaining everything so clearly. You mentioned that you
were worried this could be a migraine or something more serious. From everything
you have told me, your symptoms do not fit with migraine, and there are no
concerning features to suggest anything serious, especially as your examination
three weeks ago was normal and the headache pattern has not changed. Are you
following so far?
From what you have described, this fits best with what we call a tension-type
headache, along with an element coming from your neck and posture, which we call
a cervicogenic headache. When tension-type headaches become more frequent and
persistent over time, we describe them as chronic.
These types of headaches are very common and can be uncomfortable and
frustrating, but they are not dangerous.
The reassuring thing is that there are no red flag features and nothing to suggest
anything serious going on, so there is no need for scans at this stage. I understand
why you were worried, but based on the information we have, imaging would not be
helpful. Does that make sense?
Because these headaches have been happening frequently, using painkillers too
often can actually make headaches worse over time. To reduce that risk, I would
suggest starting a medication called amitriptyline, taken at night. It is not being used
as a painkiller here but to prevent the headaches from coming as often. We would
start at a low dose and see how you get on. Are you happy to try this?
Alongside this, I would recommend keeping a headache diary so we can track
triggers and see what helps. I will also refer you to physiotherapy, as working on
neck movement, strength, and posture often makes a big difference with this type of
headache.

-- 1170 of 1282 --

1169
If at any point you develop new symptoms such as weakness, speech problems,
vision changes, or a sudden severe headache, I would want you to seek urgent
medical advice straight away by calling our urgent GP line, or NHS 111 if it is out of
hours. Otherwise, we can review how things are going in four to six weeks and
adjust the plan if needed.
Management
• No need for a face-to-face review at present, as she has already been
examined and red flag features have been excluded
• Offer a headache diary to help monitor triggers, frequency, severity, and
response to treatment
• Offer amitriptyline 10 mg at night as preventive treatment, given the chronic
nature of her headaches and the risk of medication overuse headache
• Explain the risk of medication overuse headache and advise limiting simple
analgesia for acute attacks to fewer than 15 days per month
• Reassure her that brain imaging is not routinely indicated in the absence of
red flag symptoms or abnormal neurological findings
• Advise optimising posture and workplace ergonomics, including regular
breaks from prolonged sitting and screen use
• Offer a referral to physiotherapy to help improve neck range of motion,
strength, and posture.
• Provide clear safety-net advice to seek urgent medical review if she develops
new neurological symptoms, a sudden severe headache, visual disturbance,
or systemic symptoms
• Arrange follow-up in 4 to 6 weeks to review symptom control and response to
treatment
Learning Point from this Station
Chronic tension-type headache is defined as a tension-type headache occurring on
15 or more days per month for more than three months, in the absence of
medication overuse. It often presents as a persistent, bilateral, tight or pressing
headache and can significantly impact quality of life.
Chronic tension-type headache may coexist with secondary headache disorders,
such as cervicogenic headache, where pain arises from the cervical spine and is
commonly triggered by neck movement or prolonged poor posture.
The key aim in managing chronic tension-type headache is to reduce headache
frequency and prevent medication overuse headache. Preventive treatment, such as
low-dose amitriptyline, is recommended when headaches are frequent or persistent.
Cognitive behavioural therapy may also be beneficial, particularly where stress or
maladaptive coping strategies contribute.

-- 1171 of 1282 --

1170
Cervicogenic headache is managed primarily with non-pharmacological approaches,
including physiotherapy, targeted exercises to improve neck mobility and strength,
and ergonomic adjustments. Analgesia may be used cautiously, alongside education
and posture optimisation.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/headache-tension-type/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/headache-medication-overuse/

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you, Leo, for explaining everything so clearly. I can see why you are worried,
especially after what your colleagues said about asthma and heart attacks. From
what you have told me, your symptoms do not fit with asthma or a heart attack.
What this sounds most consistent with is anxiety-related chest tightness, sometimes
called panic or anxiety attacks. These can cause very real physical symptoms such
as chest tightness and a racing heart. Are you following so far?
Stress, lack of sleep, and energy drinks can all make this worse.
Moving forward, would you be happy for me to book you a face-to-face appointment
so I can examine your heart and chest, and arrange an ECG and some basic blood
tests, mainly to rule out any other contributing causes ?
I understand that you were hoping for medication to stop this straight away. At this
stage, the most effective treatment is actually addressing the triggers, rather than
starting tablets.
Cutting down on caffeine and energy drinks, improving your sleep routine, and using
breathing and relaxation techniques can make a big difference. Do you think this is
something you would be able to try?
We can also look at ways to help you balance your A-level revision with work, such
as setting realistic study goals and taking regular breaks, rather than pushing
yourself late into the night. Is there a way you feel you could schedule things to make
that more manageable?
Talking therapy is another option that can help you understand your symptoms better
and give you more control over managing them. Many people find it helpful. Is that
something you would be open to trying?
I do not expect this to happen, but if at any point you develop chest pain that does
not settle, ongoing shortness of breath, collapse, or worsening symptoms, you
should seek urgent medical help straight away by calling our urgent GP line, NHS
111 if it is out of hours, or 999 if the symptoms are severe.

-- 1175 of 1282 --

1174
Management
• Offer a face-to-face review for cardiovascular and respiratory examination
• Offer an ECG and baseline blood tests to exclude other abnormalities and
provide reassurance
• Advise reducing caffeine and energy drink intake, as these can contribute to
chest tightness and palpitations
• Advise establishing regular sleeping patterns and good sleep hygiene
• Discuss stress-relief strategies, including regular physical activity, relaxation
and breathing techniques, yoga, and talking therapies
• Discuss ways to balance A-level preparation with work commitments,
including taking regular breaks and setting realistic study schedules
• Offer referral to talking therapies to help manage anxiety-related symptoms
• Provide clear safety-net advice to seek urgent medical attention if he develops
chest pain, persistent shortness of breath, collapse, or worsening symptoms.
Advise contacting the urgent GP line, NHS 111 if out of hours, or 999 if
symptoms are severe
• Arrange follow-up in four weeks to review progress and symptom
improvement
Learning Point from this Station
This station highlights the importance of a thorough, structured assessment of chest
symptoms in young patients, where anxiety-related causes are common but serious
pathology must first be excluded. Careful history-taking to assess red flag cardiac
and respiratory features, alongside exploration of psychosocial stressors, is
essential.
Anxiety or panic-related chest tightness can present with real physical symptoms,
including chest tightness and palpitations, and is often exacerbated by stress, sleep
deprivation, and stimulant use, such as caffeine and energy drinks. Management
should focus on reassurance, lifestyle modification, and addressing underlying
stressors, with investigations used judiciously to provide reassurance and exclude
pathology.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/generalized-anxiety-disorder/

-- 1176 of 1282 --

your parents. There is no family history of bowel problems that you are aware of.
You are a university student and things are going well.

1176
Ideas: You are not sure what is causing your tiredness.
Concerns: You have no specific concerns.
Expectations: You would like the GP to identify the cause of your tiredness.

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask what the patient means by feeling tired all the time, whether it is physical
tiredness, mental tiredness, or both
• Ask how long it has been ongoing and whether it started gradually or
suddenly
• Ask if there is anything that makes the tiredness better or worse, for example
whether it improves with rest or worsens with minimal activity
• Ask about mood, sleep, and appetite to screen for psychological causes
• Ask about symptoms of obstructive sleep apnoea, including snoring,
witnessed apnoeic episodes, waking up gasping for breath, and daytime
sleepiness
• Ask about any recent flu like illness or infection
• Explore dietary history to assess for poor nutritional intake or vitamin
deficiencies such as vitamin B12, folate, and iron
• Ask about symptoms suggestive of vitamin deficiency, particularly in the
context of a vegan diet, including poor concentration, tingling, numbness, or
glossitis
• Ask about gastrointestinal symptoms including abdominal pain, change in
bowel habits such as diarrhoea, and any blood in the stool
• Ask about endocrine causes including diabetes with polyuria and polydipsia,
and thyroid disease with heat or cold intolerance and palpitations
• Screen for red flag symptoms of malignancy or tuberculosis, including weight
loss, night sweats, new lumps or masses, or a persistent cough, especially if
from or born in a TB endemic area
• Ask how the symptoms have affected the patient’s daily life, studies, and
functioning
• Explore social history including smoking, alcohol intake, illicit drug use, and
any recent stressors

-- 1178 of 1282 --

1177
• Ask about family history of medical conditions, particularly bowel conditions
such as coeliac disease
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a diagnosis of possible nutritional deficiency (vitamin B12 or folate) in
the context of a vegan diet, or coeliac disease or inflammatory bowel disease,
given intermittent diarrhoea episodes.
Management
• Offer a face-to-face examination to assess for lymphadenopathy and perform
an abdominal examination
• Offer blood tests including vitamin B12, folate, ferritin, vitamin D, coeliac
screen, thyroid function tests, full blood count, CRP, HbA1c, liver function
tests, and faecal calprotectin
• Advise the patient that if any vitamin deficiency is confirmed, this will be
treated with appropriate replacement, and if coeliac disease is suspected, he
will be referred to a specialist for confirmatory testing. Explain that
management of coeliac disease involves a gluten free diet
• Advise that in the meantime, he can optimise his vegan diet to ensure
adequate intake of essential nutrients, particularly vitamin B12. Suggest
options such as breakfast cereals fortified with vitamin B12, unsweetened
soya drinks fortified with vitamin B12, yeast extract such as Marmite, and
nutritional yeast flakes. Offer to send him written information
• Advise that for his tiredness, he can keep a symptom diary to understand his
energy levels, pace his activities, prioritise tasks, and plan rest periods to
conserve energy
• Offer follow up to review his results once available
• Provide safety netting that, although this is not expected, if he develops
worsening symptoms or new symptoms such as tingling in his arms or legs,
blurred vision, or confusion, he should seek urgent medical advice by
contacting the GP surgery urgently or NHS 111 if out of hours, as this may
indicate significant vitamin deficiency affecting the nerves.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Emeka for explaining all that, it is really helpful. From what you have told
me, there are a few possible reasons why you may be feeling tired. One of the
common causes, especially with a vegan diet, is low levels of certain vitamins such
as vitamin B12 or folate. This is because many people get vitamin B12 from animal
sources such as meat, fish, and dairy products. Sources for vegans are limited, and

-- 1179 of 1282 --

1178
it is therefore common for people following a vegan diet to develop vitamin B12
deficiency.
Also, your bowel symptoms such as diarrhoea and bloating make us think about
conditions like coeliac disease. Is this something you have heard of before? Coeliac
disease is a condition where the body reacts to foods containing gluten, such as
pasta, which you mentioned you eat quite a lot. Are you following so far?
Other bowel conditions, such as inflammatory bowel disease, could also explain your
symptoms. This is a condition where the body’s immune system, which normally
helps fight infection, for some unknown reason attacks the bowel, leading to
symptoms such as diarrhoea, sometimes with blood or mucus.
What we need to do next is arrange some blood tests to check your vitamin levels,
your thyroid function, your blood count, and to screen for coeliac disease. I would
also like to arrange a stool test to check for any inflammation in the bowel. In
addition, it would be helpful to see you face to face so I can examine you properly,
including checking for any lumps and examining your tummy. Are you happy for me
to arrange all these?
In the meantime, it would be important to make sure your diet contains enough
essential nutrients. With a vegan diet, vitamin B12 is particularly important. You can
get this from foods such as fortified breakfast cereals, unsweetened soya drinks,
yeast extract such as Marmite, and nutritional yeast flakes. I can also send you some
information to guide you further. Are you happy with that?
For your tiredness, it may also help to keep a simple diary of your symptoms to
understand your energy levels better. Try to pace your activities, prioritise important
tasks, and build in regular rest periods rather than pushing yourself until you feel
exhausted.
Once the results are back, we will review them together. If we find any vitamin
deficiencies, we will treat them. If there is any suggestion of coeliac disease, we will
refer you to a specialist for further tests, and the main treatment would be a gluten
free diet.
Although I do not expect this to happen, if you develop any new or worsening
symptoms such as tingling in your arms or legs, blurred vision, or confusion, please
seek urgent medical advice by contacting us immediately, or 111 if out of hours, as
this may mean your vitamin B12 levels are very low and affecting your nerves. Does
that all make sense, and do you have any questions or concerns?

-- 1180 of 1282 --

1179
Learning Point from this Station
This station focuses on the management of uncertainty and the structured
assessment of an undifferentiated presentation such as fatigue. An undifferentiated
presentation is when a patient presents with non-specific symptoms and a diagnosis
has not yet been established. Feeling tired all the time is a common undifferentiated
presentation in primary care and requires a structured and systematic approach.
Vitamin B12 deficiency is the most common and clinically significant deficiency in
individuals following a vegan diet, as it is found almost exclusively in animal products
such as meat, fish, and dairy products. Sources for vegans are limited, and a vitamin
B12 supplement may be required
This station also highlights the importance of linking associated symptoms to guide
diagnosis. In this case, gastrointestinal symptoms such as diarrhoea and bloating
raise the possibility of coeliac disease or other bowel pathology, which should be
appropriately investigated.
Further Reading
https://www.nhs.uk/live-well/eat-well/how-to-eat-a-balanced-diet/the-vegan-diet/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/anaemia-b12-folate-deficiency/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/coeliac-disease/

Patient’s Story (Role Player’s Brief)
Opening Statement: Hi doctor, the pharmacist would not let me have the new
medication the cardiologist prescribed for me. She asked me to speak to you.
If Asked to Explain Further: You had been experiencing palpitations and saw a
GP, who referred you to a cardiologist. The cardiologist carried out some heart tests
and prescribed a medication to help with the palpitations.
You are still experiencing palpitations. They have not worsened and occur once
every 2 to 3 days. You do not have any other symptoms.
ONLY SAY BELOW IF ASKED:
Social History: You do not smoke or drink alcohol. You live with your wife. You are
a businessman and own an Indian restaurant. You maintain a good diet and exercise
regularly.
Ideas: You are not sure why the pharmacist refused to dispense the medication.
Concerns: You are worried that the medication may be important for your safety.
Expectations: You would like the GP to explain why you cannot have the
medication.
If the doctor explains that you cannot take the medication and that it may be unsafe
with bisoprolol, you should ask whether the specialist made an error and whether
taking the medication could have been dangerous for you. You should become
angry and frustrated, and ask the doctor how to make a complaint, as you would like
to raise one.
Say NO to any other questions/symptoms asked outside of the details already
provided in the scenario. Accept anything offered to you by the doctor.
Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Start by acknowledging that you can see the patient has recently seen a
cardiologist for palpitations and ask him to explain in his own words what has
happened
• Ask about current palpitations, including whether he is still experiencing them
and whether they have worsened, improved, or remained the same
• Ask about associated symptoms including syncope, dizziness, shortness of
breath, chest pain, and exertional symptoms
• Ask whether he is still taking all his current medications, particularly bisoprolol
• Ask how the symptoms have affected his daily life

-- 1184 of 1282 --

1183
• Explore social history including smoking, alcohol intake, diet, and exercise
level
• Ask about family history of heart disease or sudden cardiac death
• Ask whether the pharmacist explained why the medication was not dispensed.
• Recognise a potential drug interaction between verapamil and bisoprolol,
which may increase the risk of bradycardia and heart block
Management
• Explain that verapamil and bisoprolol should generally not be taken together
as they can both slow the heart rate and may increase the risk of bradycardia
or heart block, which can be dangerous.
• Explain that this may be a prescribing oversight; however, you do not have
enough information at this stage to determine why it was prescribed in this
way, and you will clarify this with the cardiology team.
• Offer to contact the cardiology team to inform them about possible concerns
and interaction between verapamil and bisoprolol and to seek further advice.
• Advise the patient to continue his current medications, including bisoprolol,
until further specialist advice is obtained.
• If the patient wishes to make a complaint, advise that this can be done
through the hospital Patient Advice and Liaison Service (PALS), as the issue
relates to secondary care. The complaint can be made by writing (sending an
email or letter), or raising it verbally.
• Offer to follow up with the patient once you have heard back from the
cardiology team
• Provide safety netting that if he develops worsening palpitations, chest pain,
shortness of breath, dizziness, or syncope, he should seek urgent medical
attention by calling 999.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Vikram for explaining all that, I can understand why this situation would
be worrying for you.
From what you have told me, and from the letter from the cardiologist, the new
medication prescribed is verapamil. The reason the pharmacist has not given this to
you is because verapamil and one of your current medications, bisoprolol, should
generally not be taken together. Both of these medications can slow the heart rate
and can potentially be dangerous. Are you following me so far?
At this stage, it is difficult to say exactly why this was prescribed, as I do not have
enough information. It may be an oversight, but I cannot be certain. What we can do
now is contact the cardiology team, inform them about the potential interaction, and
seek further guidance from them. Are you following me so far?

-- 1185 of 1282 --

1184
In the meantime, you can continue taking your usual medications as prescribed, but
it is important that you do not start the verapamil until we have clarified this. What is
reassuring is that the pharmacist has picked this up, and you have not started the
medication, so there has been no harm to you. Does that make sense?
If you would like to raise a concern or make a complaint, you are absolutely entitled
to do so, and I can guide you on how to go about this. You would need to contact the
hospital’s Patient Advice and Liaison Service, known as PALS. You can write to
them, send an email, or raise your concern verbally, and they will investigate and get
back to you.
Although I do not expect this to happen, if you develop worsening palpitations, chest
pain, shortness of breath, dizziness, or if you faint, please seek urgent medical
attention by calling 999.
Learning Point from this Station
This station focuses on recognising and managing potentially dangerous drug
interactions.
When used together, bisoprolol and verapamil can increase the risk of bradycardia,
asystole, severe hypotension, and heart failure.
This station also assesses ethical and professional practice. The clinician should be
open and honest with the patient about the potentially dangerous drug interaction
while maintaining a non-accusatory approach. It is important not to prematurely
attribute blame to the specialist, as there may be clinical reasoning that is not yet
clear. The appropriate approach is to acknowledge the concern, ensure patient
safety by withholding the medication, and promptly seek clarification from the
cardiology team.
Further Reading
https://bnf.nice.org.uk/interactions/bisoprolol/
https://bnf.nice.org.uk/interactions/verapamil/

-- 1189 of 1282 --

1188
Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Start by acknowledging that you can see he was reviewed for urinary
symptoms and was sent for blood and urine tests
• Ask him to describe the symptoms that led to these investigations
• Ask about the onset of symptoms
• Ask about other lower urinary tract symptoms including urgency, straining,
intermittency, terminal dribbling (dribbling after passing urine), feeling of
incomplete bladder emptying, and poor stream
• Ask about symptoms of urinary retention including inability to pass urine,
abdominal pain, or suprapubic discomfort
• Ask about bowel symptoms such as constipation, which may worsen urinary
symptoms
• Ask about red flag symptoms for prostate cancer including new onset back
pain, bone pain, haematuria, weight loss, and night sweats
• Ask about family history of prostate cancer
• Ask whether the symptoms started after initiating amitriptyline and whether
the medication has helped his headaches
• Ask how the symptoms have affected his daily life and mood
• Ask about social history including smoking, alcohol intake, occupation, and
home situation
• Ask about intake of caffeine or caffeinated drinks
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a diagnosis of amitriptyline induced urinary symptoms
Management
• Explain to the patient that amitriptyline is the most likely cause of his urinary
symptoms due to its side effects
• Offer options including reducing the dose of amitriptyline to 10 mg. Explain
that urine symptoms may still persist, and his headache control may be less
effective. However, it is a reasonable option to try.
• Alternatively, offer switching to another migraine prophylaxis such as
topiramate 25 mg once daily, with gradual dose increase if needed, or
candesartan 16 mg once daily. Please note that candesartan (an angiotensin
receptor blocker - ARB) should not be offered in patients who are already
taking an angiotensin converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, as ACE inhibitors
and ARBs should not be used together.

-- 1190 of 1282 --

1189
• Inform the patient about potential side effects. Topiramate can cause
gastrointestinal upset and increase the risk of kidney stones, so advise
maintaining good fluid intake. Candesartan can cause dizziness and cough.
• Arrange follow up in 4 weeks to assess both migraine control and whether
urinary symptoms have improved
• Provide safety netting that if urinary symptoms persist, worsen, or if he
develops weight loss, haematuria or new back pain, he should seek medical
advice promptly. Advise seeking earlier review if his migraine symptoms
worsen.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Adam for explaining all that, it is really helpful. Your blood tests, including
your kidney function and the blood test to assess your prostate, have come back
normal. We also tested your urine for infection, and this was normal as well, which is
reassuring. Are you following so far?
From what you have told me, and looking at the timing of your symptoms, the most
likely cause is a side effect of amitriptyline. This medication can affect how the
bladder works, which explains the urinary symptoms you are experiencing. Does that
make sense?
We have a couple of options going forward. As you mentioned that amitriptyline has
helped with your headaches, one option is to reduce the dose to 10 mg. This may
help improve your urinary symptoms and may still provide some benefit for your
headaches. However, it is also possible that reducing the dose may not control your
headaches, and your urinary symptoms may persist, but it is a reasonable option to
try. Alternatively, we can switch to a different medication for migraine prevention
(such as topiramate or candesartan). What would you prefer? Are you happy to try
one of these options?
It would also help to reduce caffeine intake, especially in the evening, as this can
worsen urinary symptoms
I would like to review you again in about four weeks to see how your symptoms are
and whether your headaches remain controlled.
Although I do not expect this to happen, if you find that you are unable to pass urine,
develop severe tummy pain, notice any blood in your urine, or develop new back
pain, please seek medical advice urgently by calling us. Also, if your headaches
worsen significantly, please let us know sooner.

-- 1191 of 1282 --

1190
Learning Point from this Station
This station highlights the importance of recognising common medication side effects
in primary care. Amitriptyline has anticholinergic properties and can cause urinary
symptoms such as hesitancy, frequency, and incomplete bladder emptying,
particularly in older males and those with underlying prostatic enlargement, as seen
in this patient.
In patients with benign prostatic hyperplasia, anticholinergic medications can
increase the risk of urinary retention. Therefore, their use should be approached with
caution, especially in those with significant lower urinary tract symptoms.
Management should involve shared decision making, including discussing options
such as dose reduction, discontinuation, or switching to an alternative medication,
while balancing symptom control and side effects.
If dose reduction is offered, it is important to inform the patient that this may lead
to poorer headache control, that the urinary symptoms may still persist, and that
there remains a risk of urinary retention.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/luts-in-men/background-information/causes-of-luts/
https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdf/10.1002/psb.187#:~:text=to%20the%20use%2
0of%20amitripty%2D%20line%2C%20a,and%20other%20potential%20adverse%20
effects%20of%20amitriptyline.
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/migraine/

-- 1194 of 1282 --

• Ask about the pain, including onset, nature, and any relieving or aggravating

limb, and pallor (change in colour of the limb)
• Ask about other associated symptoms, including non-healing ulcers or
wounds on the legs and hair loss on the legs or feet
• Ask about swelling or redness in the legs to help exclude deep vein
thrombosis
• Ask about chest pain, shortness of breath, and palpitations to screen for co-
existing cardiovascular disease, particularly coronary artery disease, which is
common in patients with peripheral arterial disease
• Ask about the impact of symptoms on the patient’s life, including his
occupation
• Ask about smoking, alcohol intake, diet, and lifestyle
• Ask about the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a diagnosis of intermittent claudication secondary to peripheral arterial
disease, supported by reduced ABPI values
Management
• Explain to the patient that his symptoms and test results are consistent with
peripheral arterial disease, where there is reduced blood flow to the legs due
to narrowing of the arteries
• Offer blood tests to screen for associated conditions, including HbA1c,
cholesterol, lipid profile, liver function tests, renal function tests, and full blood
count
• Advise the patient that his blood pressure will be checked with the healthcare
assistant or nurse when his blood tests are taken, to ensure it is not raised
• Offer a statin such as atorvastatin 80 mg once daily for cardiovascular risk
reduction
• Offer antiplatelet therapy such as clopidogrel 75 mg once daily to reduce
cardiovascular risk.
• Offer referral to physiotherapy for a supervised exercise programme,
encouraging regular walking to the point of pain, resting, and then continuing,
as this helps improve symptoms over time
• Advise smoking cessation as this is the most important step to slow disease
progression. Offer referral to a smoking cessation service if the patient wishes

-- 1195 of 1282 --

1194
• Advise on lifestyle modification, including a healthy diet, weight management,
and regular physical activity.
• Offer support for work, as his condition is affecting his ability to carry out his
duties, including providing a fit note with amended duties to support him in the
workplace.
• Provide safety netting for signs of acute limb ischaemia, including sudden
worsening pain or rest pain, pallor, paraesthesia, paralysis, or a perishingly
cold leg, and advise him to seek urgent medical attention by calling 999
• Offer follow up in 4 to 6 weeks to review symptoms and response to treatment
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Jeremy for explaining all that, it is really helpful. From what you have told
me, and from the test results the podiatrist sent, this looks like a condition called
peripheral arterial disease. Is this something you have heard of before?
What this means is that the blood vessels supplying your legs have become
narrowed, so when you walk and your muscles need more blood, they are not
getting enough. That is why you get the cramping pain, and it improves when you
rest. Does that make sense so far?
The reassuring thing is that you are not getting pain at rest or at night, which tells me
this is at an earlier stage, and there is a lot we can do to manage it and improve your
symptoms.
The most important thing we need to focus on is improving your blood flow and
reducing your future risk of heart problems or stroke. One of the biggest factors here
is smoking. Stopping smoking is the most effective step you can take to prevent this
from getting worse, and it will also improve your overall health and reduce the risk of
serious heart and lung problems. Is this something you would be willing to consider?
I can support you with this and refer you to a stop smoking service if you are happy
with that.
We will also start you on medications to protect your blood vessels. This includes a
cholesterol lowering tablet called a statin and a blood thinning medication to reduce
the risk of clots. Are you happy for us to start these?
Another very important part of treatment is exercise. I know it may sound strange,
but regular walking actually helps improve the blood supply over time. I will refer you
to a supervised exercise programme where they will guide you on how to do this
safely.

-- 1196 of 1282 --

1195
We will also arrange to have your blood pressure checked and carry out some blood
tests to make sure there are no other contributing factors, such as diabetes or high
cholesterol, that we need to treat.
In terms of your work, are you happy for me to provide a fit note recommending
amended duties, so your employer is aware of your condition and can support you
by allowing more time or adjusting your responsibilities?
Although I do not expect this to happen, if you notice any sudden worsening of the
pain, pain at rest, your leg becoming very cold, pale, numb, or weak, it is important
that you seek urgent medical attention by calling 999.
Learning Point from this Station
Intermittent claudication typically presents as pain in the calves that occurs during
walking and is relieved by rest. The hallmark feature is pain that develops after
walking a predictable distance and usually resolves within about 10 minutes of rest.
The pain tends to recur when the patient walks a similar distance again.
Red flag features that indicate the need for urgent vascular referral or intervention
include pain at rest, which may be worse at night, pallor, pulselessness,
paraesthesia, a perishingly cold limb, and paralysis.
The Ankle Brachial Pressure Index is a key diagnostic tool in primary care and helps
confirm the diagnosis and assess severity. Interpretation is as follows:
• Less than 0.5 suggests chronic limb threatening ischaemia and requires
urgent referral for specialist vascular assessment
• 0.9 or less suggests the presence of peripheral arterial disease
• Between 0.91 and 0.99 indicates that peripheral arterial disease may be
present, and further investigation is required if there is strong clinical
suspicion
• Between 1.0 and 1.4 is considered normal
• Greater than 1.4 may suggest arterial calcification, which can be seen in
conditions such as diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, or systemic vasculitis
Management focuses not only on symptom control but also on reducing
cardiovascular risk through smoking cessation, antiplatelet therapy, statins, and
supervised exercise programmes for 3 months. If symptoms do not improve with a
supervised exercise programme, referral to a vascular specialist would be warranted
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/peripheral-arterial-disease/

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Acknowledge that you have reviewed the spirometry results and ask the
patient to explain, in his own words, the symptoms he has been experiencing
• Ask about the shortness of breath, including onset, progression, and severity
• Ask about functional limitation, including how far he can walk and what
activities bring on symptoms
• Ask whether he is breathless at rest or only on exertion
• Ask about associated respiratory symptoms including cough, sputum
production, and wheeze
• Ask about features suggestive of heart failure, including orthopnoea
(shortness of breath when lying flat), paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea (waking
at night feeling short of breath), and leg swelling
• Ask about chest pain and palpitations to help exclude angina
• Ask about red flag symptoms to rule out malignancy, including weight loss,
night sweats, and haemoptysis

-- 1200 of 1282 --

1199
• Ask about past respiratory infections and whether he has required steroids,
antibiotics, or hospital admission due to chest symptoms in the last year, as
this helps classify disease severity and guide management
• Ask about social history including smoking, alcohol intake, home situation,
occupation, diet, and the impact of his condition on daily functioning
• Ask about the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a diagnosis of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
Management
• Offer to start a LAMA + LABA combination inhaler (for example, Anoro Ellipta
or Ultibro Breezhaler), as the patient is in Group B
• Note: Patients who are newly diagnosed are classified into Group A, B, or E
based on exacerbation history and level of breathlessness. Group A and B
patients have 0 or 1 exacerbation per year without requiring hospital
admission. Group A patients are not troubled by breathlessness except
during strenuous exercise (mMRC scale 0–1), whereas Group B patients
have an mMRC score of 2 or more, meaning they are short of breath when
hurrying or walking up a slight hill or on minimal exertion. Group E patients
have 2 or more exacerbations within a year or at least one hospital
admission due to COPD or chest infection
• Offer a short-acting bronchodilator (SABA such as salbutamol) with a spacer
device for relief of breakthrough symptoms if required
• Advise smoking cessation as this is the most important step to slow disease
progression. Offer referral to a smoking cessation service and discuss support
options if he is willing
• Offer referral to pulmonary rehabilitation to improve breathlessness, exercise
tolerance, and quality of life
• Offer vaccinations including annual influenza vaccine and pneumococcal
vaccine
• Offer baseline blood tests including full blood count to check for anaemia or
polycythaemia
• Offer referral to a COPD nurse, who will provide inhaler technique training and
help develop a personalised COPD self-management plan to support
understanding, monitoring, and management of his condition
• Provide safety netting that if he develops worsening breathlessness, chest
pain, haemoptysis, or becomes systemically unwell, he should seek urgent
medical attention by contacting the GP urgently or calling NHS 111 if out of
hours
• Offer follow up in 4 to 6 weeks to review symptoms, inhaler technique, and
response to treatment

1200
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Peter for explaining all that, it is really helpful. From what you have told
me, and from the breathing test results, this is consistent with a condition called
chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, also known as COPD. Is this something you
have heard of before?
COPD is a long term condition where the airways become narrowed and inflamed,
which makes it harder for air to move in and out of the lungs. This is why you are
experiencing breathlessness and a persistent cough. It can also flare up from time to
time, especially with infections. Does that make sense so far?
The main cause of this is smoking, and stopping smoking is the most important step
we can take to slow the condition down and prevent it from getting worse. I know this
can be difficult, but we can support you with this. Is this something you would be
willing to consider?
In terms of treatment, we will start you on a regular inhaler that helps to open up your
airways and improve your breathing. This is a combination inhaler called Anoro
Ellipta, which you will use every day. We will also give you another inhaler (blue
inhaler called salbutamol) to use only when you feel more breathless. Are you happy
to try this?
I will arrange for our COPD nurse to go through how to use the inhalers with you,
and we can also send you a video and leaflet so you can read more about them at
home. The COPD nurse will also provide you with a personalised COPD plan to help
you understand, monitor, and manage your condition, and to know when to seek
help. Does that sound okay?
You mentioned that your breathlessness is affecting how you play with your
grandchildren. I would also like to refer you to a pulmonary rehabilitation programme.
This is a structured programme that includes exercise and education delivered by
specialist physiotherapists, and it has been shown to significantly improve
breathlessness and quality of life. Would you like me to arrange this for you?
We will also arrange some blood tests and ensure your vaccinations, such as flu and
pneumonia vaccines, are up to date to help reduce the risk of infections.
Although I do not expect this to happen, if you develop worsening breathlessness,
chest pain, coughing up blood, or feel generally unwell, please seek urgent medical
attention by contacting us or NHS 111, or calling 999 if it is severe.
Does that all make sense, and do you have any questions or concerns?

-- 1202 of 1282 --

1201
Learning Point from this Station
COPD is a common chronic lung condition and should be suspected in people aged
over 35 years who have a risk factor, such as smoking or occupational or
environmental exposure, and present with symptoms including exertional
breathlessness, chronic or recurrent cough, or regular sputum production
A post bronchodilator ratio of forced expiratory volume in 1 second to forced vital
capacity (FEV₁/FVC) of less than 0.7 on spirometry confirms persistent airflow
obstruction and supports the diagnosis
This station emphasises the updated GOLD classification adopted by NICE, where
patients are grouped based on symptom burden and exacerbation history to guide
management
Management should be holistic and include smoking cessation, pulmonary
rehabilitation, vaccinations, inhaler technique education, and a personalised self
management plan. Smoking cessation remains the most effective intervention to
slow disease progression
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/chronic-obstructive-pulmonary-disease/

Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the rash, including onset, duration, location, and progression
(whether it is getting worse, better, or staying the same)
• Ask about the nature of the rash, including redness, crusting, discharge,

-- 1205 of 1282 --

1204
• Ask about scalp symptoms, including itching, rash, and whether any lice or
eggs have been seen. Note that a live louse must be found to confirm active
head lice infestation, and an itchy scalp alone is not sufficient to make the
diagnosis
• Ask about contact with others who may have head lice
• Ask about previous eczema flares and frequency of flares per month
• Ask what treatments have been used and whether they have been effective
• Ask about symptoms to help exclude safeguarding concerns, including

the body
• Ask about the impact of the rash on sleep and daily activities
• Ask about social history, including nursery attendance and home situation
• Ask about the parent’s ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a diagnosis of possible Secondary bacterial infection (impetigo) due to
itching from head lice/ eczema
Management
• Explain to the mother that it is possible that head lice, on a background of
eczema, has led to increased itching, causing breaks in the skin and resulting
in a secondary infection, which explains the yellowish crusting.
• Offer a face-to-face assessment to have a closer look at the scalp and the
back of the neck. If this is not possible, advise sending a clear photograph for
review.
• Advise that if the appearance suggests infection, treatment can be started
with a topical antiseptic such as hydrogen peroxide 1% cream or a topical
antibiotic such as fusidic acid 2%, applied three times daily for 5 days.
• Advise keeping the child’s nails short and avoiding scratching to reduce
further skin damage and the risk of infection.
• Advise continued regular use of emollients such as E45 cream multiple times
daily to keep the skin well moisturised. Advise avoiding topical steroids on the
affected infected area as this may worsen the infection.
• Reassure her that as she did not find any head lice, it is possible that the wet
combing carried out by her ex-husband’s wife was effective. However, if she
remains concerned, she can repeat wet combing, as it is an effective method
for both diagnosing and treating head lice.
• Advise that the child can continue to attend nursery if this is due to head lice
alone. However, as impetigo is suspected, advise that she should remain off
nursery until 48 hours after starting treatment, as it can be contagious. Inform
her that once the pictures are reviewed, you will provide further guidance on
nursery attendance.

-- 1206 of 1282 --

1205
• Reassure the mother that head lice are not related to poor hygiene. Explain
that there is no evidence that head lice prefer clean or dirty hair, and they can
occur in clean hair. They spread through direct head to head contact rather
than poor hygiene.
• Provide safety netting that if the child develops fever, worsening redness,
spreading rash, pain, or becomes systemically unwell, she should seek urgent
medical attention by contacting the GP or calling NHS 111 if out of hours.
Example of Explanation to the Parent (Mum)
Thank you Helen for explaining all that, it is really helpful. I understand you were
wondering whether this could be head lice, especially as her symptoms started while
she was staying with her father and his partner mentioned finding lice.
It is possible that your daughter may have had head lice. With her background of
eczema, the itching can cause small breaks in the skin. These breaks can then allow
bacteria to enter and lead to a skin infection called impetigo. This would explain the
yellow crusting you have noticed at the back of her neck. Does that make sense so
far?
Another important clue here is that the steroid cream is not helping and may even be
making things worse. When the skin becomes infected, steroid creams on their own
do not treat the infection and can sometimes allow it to worsen. This pattern strongly
points towards a skin infection like impetigo rather than eczema alone. Does that
make sense?
In terms of what we can do next, it would be really helpful to see the rash more
closely. Ideally, I would like to examine her in person, but I understand you are not
able to come in today or tomorrow. If you are happy to do so, you could send us a
clear photograph of the affected areas, and I can review it and guide you further.
If it does look like a skin infection, we can start treatment with an antiseptic cream
called hydrogen peroxide, which you would apply three times a day for 5 days. I can
see from her notes that she does not have any allergies, is that still correct? Perfect.
I would advise that you avoid using the steroid cream on that area for now, as it may
make things worse. You can continue using her moisturising creams regularly to
keep the skin well hydrated, and try to keep her nails short to reduce scratching and
irritation. Are you happy with that?
As you have not seen any live lice, it is possible that the wet combing may have
already taken care of it. However, if you are still concerned about head lice, wet
combing is a very effective method for both detecting and treating them, so you can
repeat this to check and manage it if needed.
Regarding nursery, if this were head lice alone, she would be able to attend as
normal. However, as we are concerned about a possible skin infection, it would be

-- 1207 of 1282 --

1206
best for her to stay off nursery until 48 hours after starting treatment, as it can be
contagious. Once I have reviewed the pictures, I will give you clearer advice about
nursery attendance. Is that alright?
I also want to reassure you that head lice are not related to poor hygiene. They can
occur in clean hair, and they spread through direct head-to-head contact rather than
how often hair is washed. So this would not be due to anything you or her father
have done wrong.
Although I do not expect this to happen, if she develops a fever, if the rash becomes
more red, spreads, becomes painful, or if she seems generally unwell, please seek
urgent medical advice by contacting us or calling NHS 111 if it is out of hours.
Does that all make sense, and do you have any questions or concerns?
Learning Point from this Station
This station highlights the importance of recognising secondary bacterial skin
infection. It is important to note that constant scratching, for example from head lice ,
can lead to breaks in the skin and subsequent infection such as impetigo.
It is also important to note that the use of topical steroids on impetigo can worsen the
infection. Steroids suppress the local immune response, allowing bacterial infections
such as Staphylococcus or Streptococcus to spread, while potentially masking
symptoms and hiding the true severity of the infection, a phenomenon known as
impetigo incognito.
Head lice should be diagnosed by identifying a live louse. An itchy scalp alone is not
sufficient to make the diagnosis
Wet combing is both a diagnostic and treatment method. The Bug Buster® kit is a
commonly used wet combing method available on the NHS and has been evaluated
in randomised controlled trials. It contains specialised combs designed to detect and
remove lice and eggs
If head lice is confirmed, the recommended treatment involves repeated wet
combing sessions over 2 weeks, typically on days 1, 5, 9, and 13. It is also important
to treat all affected household members on the same day to prevent reinfection
This station also reinforces the importance of clear parental education, addressing
misconceptions about hygiene, and providing practical advice to support effective
management
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/head-lice/
