• Ask about sleep pattern, mood and symptoms of anxiety.
• Ask whether an ECG and a full set of observations have been performed, and
request the results.
• Ask about social history, including smoking, alcohol intake, recreational drug
use, occupation, and home situation.
• Ask about the paramedic’s ideas, concerns, and expectations, as well as
those of the patient.
• Make a diagnosis of likely benign palpitations related to excess caffeine intake
and stress.
Management
• Thank the paramedic for the detailed assessment.
• Advise that there are no red flag features to suggest an immediate life-
threatening cause, so hospital conveyance is not required at this stage.
• Explain that the likely cause of his palpitations is related to excessive caffeine
intake and stress.
• Inform the paramedic to advise the patient to significantly reduce caffeine
intake, ideally limiting it to no more than one to two cups per day, and to avoid
energy drinks or other stimulants.
• Encourage stress management strategies, including adequate rest, regular
sleep, and relaxation techniques. Advise that he may also discuss with his
employer to explore ways of reducing work-related stress.
• Advise the patient to keep a symptom diary, noting the timing, duration, and
any triggers of the palpitations.
• Offer to organise baseline blood tests, including thyroid function tests, full
blood count, HbA1c, urea and electrolytes, liver function tests, and lipid
profile. Inform the paramedic that these will be arranged with the healthcare
assistant or practice nurse, and confirmation will be sent to the patient via text
message.
• Advise that if his palpitations persist, worsen, or do not improve, he should
seek review, and further investigations such as a 3-day Holter monitor will be
considered.
• Ask the paramedic to provide clear safety-net advice to the patient to seek
urgent medical attention by calling 999 if symptoms change, including chest
pain, syncope, severe dizziness, worsening shortness of breath, or prolonged
episodes.
• Inform the paramedic that you will also send the patient a text message
summarising everything discussed, including the management plan, red flag
symptoms to watch out for, and how to seek urgent medical help.
• Advise that if any abnormalities are identified in the blood tests, he will be
offered a follow-up consultation.

-- 1211 of 1282 --

1210
Example of Explanation to the Paramedic
Thank you Sally for explaining everything so clearly and for the assessment you
have done for Graham, that is really helpful. From what you have described, he is
currently stable, his observations are within normal limits, and his ECG shows a
normal sinus rhythm, which is reassuring.
Based on the history, this sounds most in keeping with benign palpitations, likely
related to his high caffeine intake and the stress from his new role. It is reassuring
that there are no red flag features such as chest pain, collapse, or exertional
symptoms to suggest a more serious cause at this stage.
I think, given how stable he is at the moment, we could safely manage him in the
community rather than taking him to hospital, as long as you are comfortable with
that as well.
It would be helpful if we could encourage him to gradually reduce his caffeine intake
to no more than one to two cups per day and avoid energy drinks or other stimulants.
Supporting him with stress management, such as improving sleep and considering
ways to reduce work-related pressure would also be beneficial. For example, he
could speak with his employer to explore whether any adjustments can be made to
help him cope better. What do you think?
I can arrange some blood tests for him, including thyroid function, blood sugar, full
blood count, liver function, kidney function, and cholesterol levels, just to ensure
there are no other contributing factors, if he is happy to proceed. I will organise an
appointment with the nurse and send him a confirmation.
Please let him know that if his symptoms worsen or do not improve, he should
contact us, and we can consider further investigations such as a 24-hour ECG or a
3-day Holter monitor.
Meanwhile, it would also be helpful for him to keep a symptom diary to identify any
triggers and patterns related to his palpitations.
Would you be happy to advise him on the red flag symptoms to watch out for, such
as chest pain, worsening shortness of breath, severe dizziness, or fainting or
collapse, and that he should seek urgent medical attention by calling 999 if any of
these occur?
I will send him a text message summarising everything we have discussed, including
what to do next and the warning signs to look out for.

-- 1212 of 1282 --

1211
Learning Point from This Station
Palpitations are a common presentation, and while many cases are benign, it is
important to systematically exclude red flag features such as chest pain, syncope,
exertional symptoms, and a family history of sudden cardiac death, which may
indicate a serious underlying cardiac condition.
General practice involves reasonable risk. In a patient who is clinically stable with no
red flag features, it is safe to advise on possible triggers such as caffeine and stress,
while carrying out basic investigations including thyroid function tests, HbA1c, lipid
profile, urea and electrolytes, and liver function tests to exclude any underlying
abnormalities.
A watch and wait approach can then be taken to see if symptoms improve with
lifestyle modification before proceeding to further investigations such as a 24-hour
ECG or a 3-day Holter monitor, with appropriate safety-netting in place.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/palpitations/

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Katie for explaining everything so clearly, and I can completely
understand why you are concerned about Sandra, especially with the long wait to be
seen by CAMHS. You have done the right thing by seeking help early and exploring
other options.
From what you have told me, it does sound like Sandra is experiencing symptoms of
depression, and I understand why the private psychiatrist has suggested starting
medication. Fluoxetine is in fact the first-line antidepressant used in young people
when medication is needed.
However, in the UK, we usually start this type of medication in children and young
people under specialist guidance, such as through CAMHS. This is because they
need closer monitoring and support when starting treatment. So at this stage, it
would be safest for us to involve CAMHS before initiating medication. Does that
make sense so far?
In the meantime, there are still very effective things we can do to support Sandra.
Talking therapies, particularly cognitive behavioural therapy, can be very helpful. It
works by helping Sandra identify, understand, and change negative thought patterns,
behaviours, and emotions, and many people find it very beneficial. I can provide you
with some information to read, and you can go through it together with Sandra as
well. What do you think about that?
In regards to getting her seen by CAMHS, we can let them know that her symptoms
are ongoing and see if there is any possibility of expediting her appointment. At
home, simple measures can also make a difference, such as maintaining a regular
routine, encouraging good sleep, gentle physical activity, and keeping
communication open so she feels supported. It may also help to involve the school, if
you are comfortable with that, so they can provide additional support if needed.
Does that sound like something you would be happy to try for now?
In addition to all these, it would also be helpful for us to check for any physical
causes that can sometimes present with similar symptoms to low mood. This
includes checking vitamin levels such as B12, folate, and iron, as well as thyroid
function, which can affect energy levels. How would you feel about us arranging
these tests?
If at any point you notice that she is talking about harming herself, expressing
suicidal thoughts, or if her mood significantly worsens, it is important to seek urgent
help. You can contact NHS 111, local mental health crisis services, for which I will
provide you with the contact details, or the GP practice during working hours.
I would also like to arrange a follow-up in about 4 to 6 weeks, ideally with Sandra
present, so we can review how she is getting on and decide on the next steps
together.

-- 1217 of 1282 --

1216
Learning Point from This Station
Depression in children is a condition that GPs must be confident in recognising and
assessing. General practice plays a key role in early identification, risk assessment,
and ongoing support. The key symptoms of depression include persistent sadness or
low mood, which may present as irritability in young people. In younger children, this
may present with somatic symptoms such as abdominal pain or headaches. Loss of
interest or pleasure in usual activities is also a core feature.
During assessment, it is important to consider and exclude other conditions that can
mimic depressive symptoms, such as anaemia, including iron, B12 or folate
deficiency, hypothyroidism, and viral illnesses.
Management primarily involves psychological interventions, particularly cognitive
behavioural therapy, alongside referral to CAMHS for specialist input. GPs do not
usually initiate antidepressants in children under the age of 18, as this requires
specialist involvement and close monitoring.
This case also highlights an important medico-legal principle when prescribing based
on the recommendation of another clinician, including private specialists. According
to the General Medical Council guidance in Good Medical Practice (2024), doctors
must recognise and work within the limits of their competence. If prescribing based
on another healthcare professional’s recommendation, the GP must be satisfied that
the prescription is appropriate, safe, and that they have sufficient information to
proceed. In this scenario, initiating antidepressant medication in a young person
without specialist involvement would not be within the GP’s scope of competence
and should therefore be avoided.
Further Reading
https://www.gmc-uk.org/professional-standards/the-professional-standards/good-
practice-in-prescribing-and-managing-medicines-and-devices/about-this-guidance
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/depression-in-children/

• If the doctor suggests referral to a specialist, ask what the specialist is likely to

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask why the CT scan was carried out in the first place.
• Ask if she has any respiratory symptoms such as cough, shortness of breath,
reduced exercise tolerance, wheeze, or chest pain.
• Ask whether she has noticed any constitutional symptoms such as weight
loss, fatigue, fevers, or night sweats.
• Ask about any history of tuberculosis, pneumonia, or recurrent chest
infections, as fibrosis can develop after severe or repeated infections.
• Ask about symptoms suggestive of connective tissue disease, including joint
pains, rashes, dry eyes, dry mouth, Raynaud’s phenomenon, or muscle
weakness.
• Ask about social history, including smoking, alcohol use, and home situation.
• Ask about the patient’s occupation and work history. Risk occupations include
livestock farming or bird keeping (exposure to organic dusts), hairdressers
(prolonged exposure to sprays or dyes), construction workers, plumbers,
miners, or demolition workers (possible exposure to asbestos, coal dust, or
silica)
• Ask about any family history of lung disease.
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a diagnosis of pulmonary fibrosis and consider nitrofurantoin-induced
lung toxicity as an important possible cause.
Management
• Offer an urgent referral to a respiratory specialist. Explain that they may
arrange further tests such as spirometry and, in some cases, more detailed
investigations such as a lung biopsy if needed.
• Inform her that pulmonary fibrosis is a rare side effect of long-term use of
nitrofurantoin.
• Advise the patient to stop nitrofurantoin and discuss alternative ways of
managing her recurrent urinary tract infections, including lifestyle measures
such as maintaining adequate hydration, avoiding delaying urination including
after sexual activity, and wiping vulval and perineal areas from front to back

-- 1221 of 1282 --

1220
as first-line. If her symptoms improve, we can continue with this approach,
and if they recur, we can consider other options such as trimethoprim.
• Explain that if this is caused by the medication, stopping nitrofurantoin can
prevent the condition from worsening and may lead to some improvement,
especially as it has been identified early. However, any scarring that has
already developed in the lungs may not completely reverse.
• Offer baseline blood tests, including ESR, CRP, full blood count, and an
autoimmune screen, to exclude other possible causes.
• Provide safety-netting by advising her to seek medical attention by contacting
the GP practice if she develops symptoms such as shortness of breath,
persistent cough, chest pain, or reduced exercise tolerance.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Alice for explaining everything, and I can understand why this has come
as a surprise, especially as you are feeling completely well. You have done the right
thing by following this up.
From the scan results, there are some early changes in the lungs that we call
pulmonary fibrosis. Have you heard of this before?
This is a condition where there is some scarring in the lungs. Sometimes this can
happen on its own without a clear cause, and sometimes it can occur after previous
chest infections. In your case, one possible cause is the medication nitrofurantoin,
especially as you have been taking it for a longer period.
Does that make sense so far?
In terms of your question about whether this is curable, if this is related to the
medication, stopping it can prevent things from getting worse and may even lead to
some improvement, particularly as we have picked this up early. However, any
scarring that has already formed may not completely reverse. The important thing is
that we act early to prevent progression.
You also asked what might have caused this and why this was not discussed before.
I am sorry if this was not explained to you at the time. Side effects like this are quite
rare, and nitrofurantoin is generally a safe and commonly used medication. However,
in rare cases, particularly with long-term use, it can affect the lungs.
The next step would be to involve a respiratory specialist who can assess this in
more detail. They may arrange further tests such as breathing tests to assess your
lung function, and in some cases, additional scans or other investigations if needed.
For now, I would advise that we stop the nitrofurantoin and look at alternative ways
of managing your urinary infections.

-- 1222 of 1282 --

1221
We can start with some simple measures, such as drinking plenty of fluids, avoiding
tight or non-breathable underwear, and not using products like douching which can
irritate the area. It is also helpful to wipe from front to back after using the toilet and
to pass urine regularly, including after sexual activity, to help reduce the risk of
infections.
I can also send you a leaflet which outlines these measures in more detail and ways
to reduce the risk of urinary tract infections. If your infections return, we can consider
other medication options. Does that sound okay?
I would also like to arrange some blood tests to check for other possible causes and
to give us a baseline.
If you notice any symptoms such as shortness of breath, a persistent cough, chest
discomfort, or reduced exercise tolerance, please let us know promptly so we can
review you sooner.
Learning Point from This Station
Pulmonary fibrosis is a long-term condition in which the lung tissue becomes
thickened, stiff, and scarred. This scarring makes it harder for the lungs to expand
and take in oxygen effectively.
Nitrofurantoin is one of the medications known to be associated with adverse
pulmonary reactions, particularly with long-term use. It is therefore important to
always review medication history when new lung findings are identified.
Management involves prompt cessation of the suspected offending agent, early
referral to a respiratory specialist for further assessment, and baseline investigations
to exclude alternative causes such as autoimmune disease
Further Reading
https://patient.info/doctor/respiratory-medicine/pulmonary-fibrosis
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2408600/

ONLY SAY BELOW IF ASKED:

usually comes from. You usually get at least one episode every 2 weeks, but it is
becoming more frequent, now around 2 episodes every 2 weeks.
You do not have any other symptoms apart from the nosebleeds. When they occur,
you lean backwards and pinch the hard part of your nose to try to stop the bleeding.
Please demonstrate this during the video consultation if asked.
You have hypertension and osteoarthritis, and you take your prescribed medications
regularly. For your osteoarthritis, you have been using over-the-counter ibuprofen
tablets, which you started around 6 to 7 months ago as they helped with your knee
pain. You do not take co-codamol regularly because you find that ibuprofen works

Social History: You do not smoke, drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. You work as a
store manager in a large retail supermarket and live with your husband.

pinch the hard part of my nose. Is that right?

provided in the scenario. Accept anything offered to you by the doctor.
Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset and progression of the nosebleeds.
• Ask if she is currently having an episode and when the last episode occurred.
• Ask whether the bleeding is from one nostril or both. Note that bleeding from
one nostril is more suggestive of an anterior bleed, which is usually easier to

-- 1225 of 1282 --

1224
control, whereas bleeding from both nostrils may suggest a posterior source,
which can be more difficult to manage.
• Ask about the severity of the bleeding and whether there are any clots.
• Ask what she does during an episode to stop the bleeding.
• Ask about local nasal causes such as nose picking, trauma, recent upper
respiratory tract infection, chronic rhinosinusitis, nasal dryness, or use of
nasal sprays.
• Ask about symptoms of anaemia such as fatigue, dizziness, palpitations,
chest pain, or shortness of breath.
• Ask if she has experienced bleeding from other sites, such as gum bleeding,
easy bruising, or blood in the stool or urine, to exclude a bleeding disorder.
• Ask about red flag symptoms that may suggest malignancy, including weight
loss, night sweats, unilateral nasal obstruction, persistent nasal discharge,
facial pain, hearing loss, persistent lymphadenopathy, facial numbness, or
double vision.
• Ask about medication use, including anticoagulants and over-the-counter
medications such as aspirin or NSAIDs.
• Ask about any family history of bleeding disorders.
• Ask about smoking, alcohol use, occupation, and home situation.
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a diagnosis of recurrent epistaxis, likely worsened by regular use of
over-the-counter ibuprofen tablets.
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to examine the nostrils and look for any local
causes such as polyps or masses that may be contributing.
• Offer blood tests, including full blood count to check for anaemia, liver function
tests, and a coagulation profile to assess for clotting abnormalities.
• Advise that she can continue using Naseptin and increase application to four
times daily.
• Advise discontinuation of ibuprofen tablets, as these can contribute to
bleeding and worsen nosebleeds.
• Offer alternative pain relief such as paracetamol with or without codeine or
topical ibuprofen gel. If symptoms are not controlled, arrange a follow-up to
discuss further options for osteoarthritis management, including physiotherapy
or joint injections.
• Advise avoiding nose blowing or picking to reduce further trauma.
• Reassure her that her blood pressure was well controlled at her recent A and
E visit and is unlikely to be the cause of her nosebleeds. Offer to recheck her
blood pressure at the face-to-face appointment.

-- 1226 of 1282 --

1225
• Reinforce correct first aid for epistaxis by advising her to sit upright, lean
forward, and pinch the soft part of the nose firmly for 10 to 15 minutes without
releasing pressure.
• Provide safety-netting by advising her to seek urgent medical attention by
contacting the GP urgently, NHS 111, or attending Accident and Emergency if
bleeding lasts longer than 20 minutes despite applying pressure, becomes
heavy, or is associated with symptoms such as dizziness, shortness of breath,
palpitations, or weakness.
• Offer follow-up to discuss the blood test results once they are available.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Juliana for explaining everything so clearly. I can understand why this is
worrying you, especially as the nosebleeds are becoming more frequent.
From what you have told me, this sounds like what we call epistaxis, which is just a
medical term for a nosebleed. Nosebleeds are quite common and can sometimes
occur without a specific cause. However, one thing that may be contributing in your
case is the use of ibuprofen tablets, as these can increase the risk of bleeding. Does
that make sense so far?
In terms of what to do next, would it be okay if I arrange a face-to-face appointment
so I can examine your nose properly and rule out any other more serious causes
such as any lumps? We can also arrange some blood tests to make sure there is no
underlying issue such as anaemia or a clotting problem. Is that all okay with you?
For now, I would advise that you stop taking ibuprofen tablets. Instead, you can use
paracetamol, or paracetamol with codeine such as co-codamol when needed, or the
ibuprofen gel for your knee pain. If your pain is not well controlled, we can arrange
another appointment to explore other options to help you. How does that sound to
you? Would you be happy to try this?
Just to let you know, co-codamol can sometimes cause constipation, so if you do
take it, we would advise increasing your fluid intake and fibre in your diet, and we
can also consider a laxative if needed.
You can continue using the Naseptin cream, and it would be helpful to apply it up to
four times a day to support healing. It is also important to avoid picking or blowing
your nose, as this can trigger further bleeding.
In terms of your concern about your blood pressure, it was well controlled when it
was checked recently in hospital, so it is unlikely to be the cause. We can recheck it
when you come in just to be sure.

-- 1227 of 1282 --

1226
When you do get a nosebleed, the best way to manage it is to sit upright, lean
forward, and pinch the soft part of your nose firmly for 10 to 15 minutes without
letting go. Leaning backwards can actually make things worse, so it is best to avoid
that.
If the bleeding lasts longer than 20 minutes despite doing this, becomes very heavy,
or if you feel unwell with symptoms like dizziness or shortness of breath, you should
seek urgent medical attention by contacting the GP urgently, NHS 111, or going to
Accident and Emergency.
I will arrange a follow-up with you to go through your blood test results once they are
back.
Learning Point from This Station
Most cases of epistaxis are self-limiting and benign. Common causes include local
trauma such as nose picking, nasal fractures, septal irritation or perforation, foreign
bodies, or blunt injury. Inflammatory causes include infections such as chronic
sinusitis, allergic rhinitis, or nasal polyps.
Medications are an important and often overlooked cause. Topical agents such as
decongestants or corticosteroids, as well as substances like cocaine, can contribute.
Systemic medications including anticoagulants, antiplatelets, and non-steroidal anti-
inflammatory drugs can impair clotting and increase the risk of bleeding.
Lifestyle factors such as excessive alcohol intake, cigarette smoking, and poorly
controlled hypertension can also contribute.
It is important to consider haematological conditions affecting clotting, including
thrombocytopenia, platelet dysfunction, von Willebrand disease, leukaemia, and
haemophilia. Although less common, tumours must also be considered, including
benign causes such as angiofibroma and malignant conditions such as squamous
cell carcinoma, particularly in older patients or those with red flag symptoms.
A structured assessment helps identify the underlying cause, guides management,
and ensures that serious pathology is not missed.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/epistaxis-nosebleeds/

• Ask about social history, including smoking, alcohol use, illicit drug use,

• Explain that women who have sex with women can still get and pass on

are cuts or sores in the mouth, and contact with bodily fluids.

• Address her misconception regarding cervical screening and explain that HPV
can be transmitted between women, so cervical screening remains important
regardless of sexual orientation. Explain that most cases of cervical cancer
are linked to HPV, which is spread through skin-to-skin contact during any

• Strongly encourage her to book a cervical smear and explain its role in
preventing cervical cancer.
• Discuss safer sex practices, including using condoms on sex toys, avoiding
sharing them or cleaning them thoroughly between use, (washed with soap
and water) and considering the use of dental dams for oral sex.
• Advise that sex toys should be washed with soap and water between uses.
• Offer written information or leaflets about STI risk and safer sex practices.
• Provide safety-netting by advising her to seek medical attention if she
develops symptoms such as abnormal vaginal discharge, painful urination,
lower abdominal pain, genital ulcers, or any other concerns.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Victoria for explaining everything so clearly. I can understand why reading
that article has made you worried, and it is really good that you have come to
discuss this.
Just to clarify, women who have sex with women can still get and pass on sexually
transmitted infections. This can happen through things like sharing sex toys, oral
sex, especially if there are any cuts or sores, and contact with bodily fluids. So
although the risk may be different compared to sexual relationships with men, it is
not zero. Does that make sense so far?
In terms of your situation, as your last sexual contact was about 3 months ago, it
would be appropriate to offer you a full sexual health screen. This would include

-- 1231 of 1282 --

1230
tests for infections such as HIV, chlamydia, gonorrhoea, and syphilis. We can
arrange this through a sexual health clinic, where they specialise in this, or if you
would prefer, we can organise the tests here in the practice. How would you feel
about that?
You also mentioned cervical screening. It is a common misconception that it is only
needed for women who have sex with men, but that is not the case. The virus that
causes cervical cancer, called HPV, is passed on through skin-to-skin contact during
sexual activity, not just through penetration. This means it can be transmitted
between women as well. So it is still very important that you attend for cervical
screening. Does that make sense?
In terms of reducing your risk going forward, simple measures can help, such as
avoiding sharing sex toys or using condoms on them. If they are shared, they should
be cleaned thoroughly with soap and water between use. You can also consider
using protection such as dental dams during oral sex. How do you feel about these
options? Would you be happy to try some of these?
You have asked what percentage of lesbians have STIs, and that is a really good
question. I am not sure of an exact percentage, as it can vary depending on
individual factors rather than sexual orientation alone. What is important to know is
that there can still be a risk, depending on the type of sexual contact, so it is always
best to think about your individual situation and how we can reduce that risk going
forward. Does that make sense?
You have asked about the risk of HIV in women who have sex with women, and that
is a really good question. While the risk is generally low, HIV can still be transmitted
between women. This can happen in certain situations, for example if there is
contact with blood or vaginal fluids, especially if there are any cuts or sores. Does
that make sense?
If you notice any symptoms such as unusual discharge, pain when passing urine,
lower abdominal pain, or any sores, please let us know.
Learning Point from This Station
Women who have sex with women can still acquire and transmit sexually transmitted
infections. Risk depends on sexual practices rather than sexual orientation alone.
Infections such as chlamydia can be transmitted through the exchange of bodily
fluids, while herpes and genital warts can be passed through direct skin-to-skin
contact, including during oral sex or through shared contact between partners.
Sharing sex toys without protection or cleaning can also increase the risk of
transmission.

-- 1232 of 1282 --

1231
Human papillomavirus is particularly important, as nearly all cases of cervical cancer
are associated with this virus. HPV is transmitted through skin-to-skin contact during
sexual activity, including between women. This means that women who do not have
sex with men are still at risk of HPV infection and therefore cervical cancer.
Cervical screening remains essential regardless of sexual orientation, as it detects
early changes in cervical cells before they develop into cancer.
This station also highlights the importance of addressing misconceptions in a non-
judgemental and patient-centred manner, providing clear education on risk, offering
appropriate sexual health screening, and promoting safer sex practices such as the
use of condoms on sex toys, cleaning them between use, and considering barrier
methods such as dental dams.

bisexual-women/cervical-screening-for-lesbian-and-bisexual-women#cervical-
screening-is-not-just-for-heterosexual-women
https://www.nhs.uk/live-well/sexual-health/sexual-health-for-lesbian-and-bisexual-
women/

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset of PR bleeding, including duration, frequency, and
whether it is increasing or resolving.
• Ask about the colour of the blood, including whether it is dark red or fresh
bright red.
• Ask whether the blood is mixed with the stool, on the surface of the stool, or
only seen on wiping. (PR bleeding with haemorrhoids is usually seen on
wiping and not mixed with the stool. If mixed, consider other causes such as
malignancy).
• Ask about any associated anal pain during or after passing stool
(haemorrhoids are usually painless unless thrombosed; if painful, consider
anal fissures).
• Ask about associated abdominal pain, constipation, or diarrhoea.
• Ask about symptoms suggestive of colorectal cancer, including weight loss,
fatigue, loss of appetite, or night sweats.
• Ask about symptoms of anaemia such as dizziness, palpitations, chest pain,
or shortness of breath.

-- 1235 of 1282 --

1234
• Ask about any history of haemorrhoids, anal fissures, or previous similar
episodes.
• Ask about recent sexual history, including anal intercourse, any trauma, pain,
or bleeding associated with it, and use of protection.
• Ask about risk of sexually transmitted infections, including discharge, ulcers,
or rectal pain.
• Ask about medication use, including anticoagulants, antiplatelets, or NSAIDs.
• Ask about family history of bowel cancer and clarify the age of diagnosis.
• Ask about social history, including smoking, alcohol use, and occupation.
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.

Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment for examination, including abdominal
examination and digital rectal examination to confirm the diagnosis.
• Advise on conservative management, including increasing dietary fibre intake,
maintaining adequate hydration, and avoiding straining during bowel
movements.
• Advise on the importance of good anal hygiene. The anal area should be kept
clean and dry to promote healing and reduce irritation and itching.
• Advise avoiding prolonged sitting on the toilet.

as creams or suppositories, for example Anusol cream, to be used morning
and night and after bowel movements for up to 7 days.
• Reassure him that his symptoms are more likely due to haemorrhoids and are
less likely to be due to cancer or his sexual orientation. Encourage him to

• Provide safety-netting by advising him to seek medical attention if bleeding
becomes heavy, persists, is mixed with stool, or if he develops weight loss, a
change in bowel habit, or symptoms of anaemia such as dizziness,
palpitations, or shortness of breath.
• Offer follow-up in 4 weeks to review symptoms and ensure improvement.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Nick for explaining everything so clearly. I can understand why you are
worried, especially with your family history, as bleeding from the back passage can
sometimes be associated with cancer.
However, reassuringly, you do not have symptoms such as weight loss, change in
bowel habits, or blood mixed with the stool. From what you have described,

-- 1236 of 1282 --

1235
especially the blood being seen on wiping and not mixed with the stool, this is most
likely due to haemorrhoids. Is that something you have heard of before?
I can also see from your previous consultation that my colleague examined you and
found haemorrhoids at that time, which further supports this being the likely cause.
Haemorrhoids are swellings of blood vessels in or around the back passage, and
they can cause bleeding. They are common and usually settle on their own, although
we can help manage them with simple measures.
The next step would be to arrange a face-to-face appointment so I can examine you
properly, with a chaperone present, and confirm the diagnosis. Would you be happy
for me to arrange this?
In the meantime, there are a few things that can help. Increasing fibre in your diet,
drinking plenty of fluids, and avoiding straining when opening your bowels can
reduce irritation. It is also important not to sit on the toilet for long periods, and
keeping the area clean and dry will help with healing. Is that something you would be
happy to try?
You can also try over-the-counter treatments such as haemorrhoid creams like
Anusol, which can be used for about 7 days, morning and evening, and after opening
your bowels to help with symptoms.
In terms of your concern about whether this could be related to your sexual activity,
haemorrhoids are not caused by sexual orientation. However, it is still important to
continue practising safe sex.
If you notice that the bleeding becomes heavy, continues, starts mixing with the
stool, or if you develop symptoms such as weight loss, change in bowel habit,
dizziness, or shortness of breath, please seek medical attention promptly.
I will arrange a follow-up in a few weeks to see how you are getting on.
Learning Point from This Station
This station reinforces the importance of making a logical and safe clinical diagnosis
in primary care. It highlights the need to balance not overinvestigating and not
underinvestigating patients.
Fresh red blood seen on wiping and not mixed with stool is most commonly due to
haemorrhoids, whereas blood mixed with stool or associated with systemic
symptoms should raise concern for more serious pathology such as colorectal
cancer.
In this case, the patient has no red flag symptoms suggestive of colorectal cancer,
and the history of fresh red blood on wiping, along with a previous examination
confirming haemorrhoids, makes a benign cause the most likely diagnosis.

-- 1237 of 1282 --

1236
A safe primary care approach is to arrange a face-to-face examination to confirm the
diagnosis, provide appropriate conservative management, and offer clear safety-
netting with follow-up to ensure symptoms improve.
It is equally important to recognise when further investigation is required. If
symptoms do not improve, persist, or if red flag features develop, then appropriate
escalation, including a FIT test and or referral for further investigations, is warranted.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/haemorrhoids/

• Ask about the onset and progression of his tiredness, including whether it is

• Ask if there are any factors that make the tiredness better or worse.

in stool, or vomiting blood.

difficulty swallowing, heart burn, change in bowel habit, or any abdominal or

Management
• Offer further investigations, including a FIT test and coeliac screening, to rule
out other causes of iron and folate deficiency.
• Start iron supplementation with ferrous sulfate 200 mg once daily for 3
months, and folic acid 5 mg daily for 4 months.
• Advise dietary modification, including increasing intake of iron-rich foods such
as red meat, green leafy vegetables, and fortified cereals, as well as folate-
rich foods such as broccoli and peas.
• If meal preparation is a struggle, offer referral to a social prescriber who can
support access to local community meal services such as “Meals on Wheels”
• Explain that if any investigations return abnormal results, further referral for
specialist assessment, including possible endoscopy, may be required.
• Arrange repeat blood tests, including full blood count, in 4 weeks to assess
response to treatment.

unintentional weight loss, or worsening symptoms of anaemia such as

Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Patrick for taking to discuss your results. I can see from your blood tests
that your iron levels and folate levels are a bit low, and this is likely contributing to
why you have been feeling tired. Does that make sense so far?
The next step is to treat this and also understand why it has happened. In your case,
your diet may be playing a role, especially as you mentioned it has been difficult to
prepare meals since your wife passed away. I am sorry to hear about your loss.
To help with this, I would like to start you on iron tablets and folic acid supplements
to bring your levels back up.
The iron tablets can sometimes cause side effects such as an upset stomach,
constipation, or diarrhoea, but these usually settle on their own. If they become
troublesome, please let us know and we can review things.
Would you be happy to start this?
While it is likely that your diet is contributing to the low iron and folate levels, it is
important that we do not miss any other possible causes.
I would like to arrange some further tests, including a stool test to check for any
hidden blood in your stools. This can sometimes be a sign of more serious
conditions such as bowel cancer. I am not saying that you have cancer, but at your
age, it is something we routinely check to be safe. Is that okay with you?
We will also arrange a blood test to check for conditions such as coeliac disease. If
any of these tests come back abnormal, we may need to refer you for further
investigations, such as a camera test to look at the stomach or bowel. How do you
feel about that plan?
We will repeat your full blood count in about 4 weeks to make sure things are
improving.
If you notice any new symptoms such as difficulty swallowing, change in bowel
habits, blood in your stool, weight loss, or if you feel more dizzy or unwell, please let
us know straight away.
Learning Point from This Station
Non-anaemic iron deficiency is a common finding in primary care. This occurs when
iron stores are low, for example low ferritin, but haemoglobin remains within the
normal range. Iron deficiency develops in stages, beginning with depletion of iron

-- 1242 of 1282 --

1241
stores before progressing to anaemia, making haemoglobin a late marker of
deficiency.
Guidance from the British Society of Gastroenterology highlights that the overall risk
of significant gastrointestinal pathology, including malignancy, is low in non-anaemic
iron deficiency. However, the threshold for investigation should be low in men,
postmenopausal women, and those with gastrointestinal symptoms or a family
history of gastrointestinal disease.
For this patient, his poor diet and coexisting folate deficiency alongside low iron
make a nutritional cause likely. However, it is important not to assume this without
excluding other causes. Further investigations such as a coeliac screen and FIT test
are necessary to rule out underlying pathology.
It is also important to note that in men, even non-anaemic iron deficiency warrants a
low threshold for further investigation, and this may include bidirectional endoscopy
(upper gastrointestinal endoscopy and colonoscopy), similar to the approach in
confirmed iron deficiency anaemia. However, in the absence of red flag symptoms, a
pragmatic and stepwise approach can be adopted in primary care, starting with initial
investigations and monitoring response to treatment.
It is also important to recognise that when both iron and folate deficiency are
present, this can mask the typical microcytosis seen in iron deficiency, resulting in a
normal mean cell volume. In addition, haemoglobin is a late indicator of iron
deficiency. Therefore, repeating the full blood count in 4 weeks is important not only
to monitor response to treatment, but also to check for the development of anaemia
and to ensure that no other concerning features are missed.
Further Reading
https://gut.bmj.com/content/70/11/2030
https://patient.info/doctor/haematology/non-anaemic-iron-deficiency
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/anaemia-b12-folate-deficiency/
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/anaemia-iron-deficiency/

1244
• Ask about the impact on quality of life, including school attendance, sports,

• Make a working diagnosis of primary dysmenorrhoea with menorrhagia.
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to assess the patient, including observations
and abdominal examination. Note: As the patient has never been sexually
active, a bimanual or speculum examination is not indicated at this stage. The
likelihood of pelvic inflammatory disease or cervical pathology is low, and
findings are unlikely to alter management. Additionally, these examinations
can be uncomfortable and invasive in this group. If there is concern regarding
underlying pathology such as fibroids, a transabdominal pelvic ultrasound
scan should be considered as a first-line, non-invasive investigation.
• Offer blood tests, including full blood count, to check for anaemia.
• Offer medical management, including tranexamic acid with or without
mefenamic acid or other NSAIDs during periods.
• Discuss hormonal options if symptoms are not controlled, including the
combined oral contraceptive pill, explaining that this is commonly used for
regulating periods and reducing pain and bleeding, not just for contraception.
• Offer written information or leaflets for both the patient and her stepmother to
review before the appointment, so they can consider which options may suit
her best.
• Advise lifestyle measures, including maintaining a healthy diet, adequate
hydration, and regular exercise.
• Provide safety-netting by advising that if she develops symptoms such as
dizziness, palpitations, shortness of breath, or extreme tiredness, she should
contact the GP urgently or NHS 111 if out of hours, as this may indicate
significant blood loss.
Example of Explanation to the Patient’s Step Mother
Thank you Lisa for explaining everything so clearly, and I can understand why you
are concerned about Samantha, especially as this has been going on for some time
and is affecting her school.
From what you have described, Samantha’s symptoms sound like what we call
heavy and painful periods. This is quite common in young people, especially in the
first few years after starting periods, and is usually due to how the body produces
certain hormones during the menstrual cycle. Does that make sense so far?

-- 1246 of 1282 --

1245
The next step would be for me to see Samantha in a face-to-face appointment so I
can assess her properly and also speak with her directly. We would usually carry out
a general and abdominal examination at this stage.
We can also arrange a blood test to check if she may be anaemic, as heavy periods
can sometimes lead to low blood levels, which can make her feel tired. Would you be
happy for me to arrange this?
In terms of treatment, there are medications that can really help. There are tablets
such as tranexamic acid, which can reduce the amount of bleeding, and anti-
inflammatory medications like mefenamic acid, which can help with both pain and
bleeding. These are taken during her periods.
We also have the option of hormonal treatments such as the contraceptive pill, which
is taken daily and can help regulate and control periods. However, we would need to
assess whether this would be suitable for Samantha.
I can also provide some information for both of you to read before the appointment,
so you can think about which options might suit her best, and we can discuss this
further when we see her. Does that sound okay?
If at any point Samantha becomes very tired, dizzy, short of breath, or develops
palpitations, it is important to seek medical attention urgently, as this could mean she
has lost a significant amount of blood.
Learning Point from This Station
This station highlights the importance of recognising and managing heavy menstrual
bleeding and dysmenorrhoea in adolescents. Primary dysmenorrhoea is common in
young people and is usually due to prostaglandin-mediated uterine contractions,
often presenting with painful and heavy periods soon after menarche.
Primary dysmenorrhoea is the most likely diagnosis when menstrual pain starts
within 6 to 12 months after menarche, once cycles become regular. The pain is
typically cramping in nature, felt in the lower abdomen, and may radiate to the back
or inner thighs. It usually begins shortly before or at the onset of menstruation and
lasts up to 72 hours, improving as the period progresses.
Secondary dysmenorrhoea should be considered when symptoms do not follow this
typical pattern. It is more likely when pain starts after several years of previously
painless periods, is not limited to menstruation, or persists beyond the menstrual
phase. It may also be associated with other gynaecological symptoms such as
dyspareunia, abnormal vaginal discharge, heavy menstrual bleeding, intermenstrual
bleeding, or postcoital bleeding.

1246

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the history of heavy periods, including onset, duration, and whether
symptoms are worsening.
• Ask about her menstrual history, including last menstrual period, cycle length,
duration of bleeding, any intermenstrual bleeding, and possibility of
pregnancy.
• Ask about how heavy the flow is, including number of pads or tampons used
and presence of clots.
• Ask if she is using any contraception.
• Ask about sexual history sensitively, including whether she is sexually active,
any new partners, number of partners, history of sexually transmitted
infections, and whether there is any postcoital bleeding.
• Ask about associated lower abdominal or pelvic pain.
• Ask about any abnormal vaginal discharge or urinary symptoms.
• Ask about symptoms suggestive of perimenopause, including hot flushes,
night sweats, or mood changes.
• Ask about red flag symptoms, including unintentional weight loss, loss of
appetite, and night sweats, to exclude possible malignancy.
• Ask about symptoms of anaemia, including dizziness, palpitations, shortness
of breath, or tiredness.
• Ask about social history, including smoking, alcohol use, illicit drug use, and
home situation.
• Ask about family history of heavy periods, bleeding disorders, or
gynaecological cancers.
• Ask about the impact on quality of life, including work, social activities, and
daily functioning.
• Ask what she has tried so far to manage her symptoms and response to
treatment.
• Explore her ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a working diagnosis of heavy menstrual bleeding, likely dysfunctional
uterine bleeding.

-- 1251 of 1282 --

1250
Management
• Offer repeat blood tests, including full blood count, to reassess for anaemia
given ongoing bleeding.
• Offer short-term treatment with norethisterone 5 mg three times a day for 10
days to help stop the bleeding. Explain that it may take up to 48 hours to
reduce the bleeding.
• Inform her about the rare risk of blood clots with norethisterone. Advise her to
stay well hydrated and keep mobile, especially during travel, to reduce this
risk.
• Advise her on red flag symptoms of blood clots, including leg pain or swelling,
chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, or coughing up blood, and to
seek urgent medical attention if these occur.
• Explain that once she returns from the wedding, it would be important to
review long-term management options, including the levonorgestrel
intrauterine system such as the Mirena coil, which can help control heavy
periods.
• Provide safety-netting by advising her to seek medical attention by contacting
GP practice urgently or NHS 111 if she develops symptoms such as
dizziness, palpitations, shortness of breath, or extreme tiredness, which may
indicate significant blood loss.
• Offer follow-up after she returns from her trip to review symptoms and discuss
ongoing management options.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Ashling for explaining everything so clearly. I can understand why this is
particularly stressful for you, especially with your friend’s wedding coming up and
your travel plans.
From what you have told me, it sounds like your heavy periods are not being well
controlled with the current medications, which can happen sometimes.
In terms of helping you in the short term, especially with the wedding in a few days,
there is a medication we can use called norethisterone. This is a hormone tablet that
can help reduce or temporarily stop the bleeding. It is taken three times a day for a
short course, and it may take up to 48 hours to start reducing your bleeding. How do
you feel about trying this?
As with any medication, there are some risks to be aware of. Very rarely, it can
increase the risk of blood clots. To reduce this risk, it is important to stay well
hydrated and keep moving, especially during your travel. If you notice any symptoms

-- 1252 of 1282 --

1251
such as leg swelling or pain, chest pain, shortness of breath, or coughing up blood,
you should seek urgent medical attention. Does that make sense?
Because you have been bleeding heavily, I would also like to repeat your blood test
to check your blood levels, just to make sure you have not become anaemic.
Once you are back from your trip, it would be a good idea for us to review things
properly and discuss longer-term options to manage your periods. One option is a
hormonal coil, such as the Mirena, which can be very effective in reducing heavy
bleeding. I will provide you with a leaflet to read more about the Mirena coil, so you
can consider whether this might be a suitable option for you.
If at any point you feel very dizzy, short of breath, have a racing heartbeat, or feel
extremely tired, please seek medical help urgently by contacting the GP surgery or
NHS 111 if out of hours, as this could be a sign of significant blood loss.
Learning Point from This Station
Menorrhagia is a common presentation in primary care, particularly in women of
reproductive age. In women around the age of 45, it is important to consider
perimenopause as a potential cause, as this can present with heavier, longer, or
more irregular periods, often alongside symptoms such as hot flushes, irritability, and
difficulty with concentration.
Management of heavy menstrual bleeding involves both short-term/ urgent reduction
of ongoing bleeding and long-term strategies. For acute reduction of bleeding,
options include norethisterone or tranexamic acid, with or without an NSAID such as
mefenamic acid.
For long-term management, options include the levonorgestrel intrauterine system
such as the Mirena coil, the combined oral contraceptive pill if suitable,
norethisterone, or the use of tranexamic acid during menstruation.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/menorrhagia-heavy-menstrual-bleeding/

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask if the symptoms are affecting one breast (unilateral) or both breasts
(bilateral).
• Ask about the onset and progression of breast pain, including whether it is
getting better, worse, or staying the same.
• Ask her to characterise the pain and specifically ask about deep burning pain
(deep burning pain may suggest ductal infection and may require milk
culture).
• Ask about the location of the pain and whether it is localised to one area or
affecting the whole breast.
• Ask about associated symptoms such as redness, warmth, swelling, areas of
hardness, or any lumps.
• Ask about nipple discharge or nipple changes, including cracks or bleeding.
• Ask about systemic symptoms, including fever, chills, or feeling generally
unwell.

feeding from one breast only, or mixed feeding.

she is coping at home, how the baby is doing, whether the baby is feeding

-- 1255 of 1282 --

1254
well and growing well, any fever in the baby, and any whitish coating on the
baby’s tongue suggestive of oral thrush.
• Explore her ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a diagnosis of lactational mastitis.
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to assess the breast and rule out an abscess
that may require drainage.
• Advise her that as her symptoms have worsened over the last 24 hours,
antibiotics are indicated. Prescribe flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 10
days. Reassure her that this is safe during breastfeeding and check for any
allergies.
• Advise that she can use ibuprofen for pain relief if there are no
contraindications.
• Advise her to continue breastfeeding from the affected breast if she is able, as
this helps with recovery. If breastfeeding is too painful or the baby refuses to
feed, advise her to hand express milk to maintain supply until she can resume
breastfeeding.
• If the nipple is cracked or painful, advise expressing milk from that breast until
it heals.
• Reassure her that it is safe to continue breastfeeding. The infection does not
pass to the baby through breast milk, and breast milk contains protective
factors that help the baby fight infections.
• Advise her to rest and wear a well-fitting, supportive bra.
• Offer to send leaflets and videos on breastfeeding techniques. If she
continues to have difficulty, offer referral to a midwife or breastfeeding
specialist for further support.
• Provide safety-netting by advising her to seek urgent medical attention by
contacting the GP surgery or NHS 111 out of hours if symptoms worsen, if
she develops rigors, chills, a fast heart rate, feels increasingly unwell, or if
there is no improvement within 48 hours of starting antibiotics.
• Follow up in 10 days time after completion of antibiotics
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Olympia for explaining everything so clearly. I can understand that this
must be quite uncomfortable for you, especially so soon after giving birth.
From what you have described, this sounds like something we call mastitis, which is
an infection or inflammation of the breast that can happen during breastfeeding. It
often causes pain, redness, warmth, and sometimes fever, just as you have
described. Are you with me so far?

-- 1256 of 1282 --

1255
What I would like to do next is arrange a face-to-face appointment today so I can
examine your breast in the presence of a chaperone. This is important because, in
some cases, this condition can lead to a collection of fluid called an abscess, which
may need drainage. So it is important that we rule this out. Would you be happy for
me to arrange this?
Because you have had a fever and your symptoms are getting worse, I would
recommend starting antibiotics to treat the infection. The antibiotic is called
flucloxacillin, and you would take it four times a day for 10 days. I can see from your
records that you do not have any allergies. Is that still correct?
This antibiotic is safe to take while breastfeeding. The important thing to know is that
it is safe to continue breastfeeding, even from the affected breast. In fact, continuing
to feed or express milk helps clear the infection more quickly. If it feels too painful or
your baby does not want to feed from that side, you can express milk instead until
things improve. How do you feel about that?
You mentioned that you are worried about passing the infection to your baby if you
continue breastfeeding. I would like to reassure you that it is safe to continue
breastfeeding. The infection does not pass to the baby through breast milk, and in
fact, breast milk contains protective factors that help your baby fight infections. Does
that make sense?
In the meantime, there are some simple things that can help. You can take regular
pain relief such as paracetamol or ibuprofen if suitable. Applying cold compresses for
about 10 minutes can help reduce pain and swelling. It is also important to rest and
stay well hydrated.
If you notice that the pain, redness, or swelling is getting worse, if your heart is
racing, if you develop shivering or feel very unwell, or if you have any concerns,
please contact us urgently or NHS 111 if out of hours.
I will arrange a follow-up in about 10 days to check that your symptoms have
improved.
Learning Point from This Station
Lactational mastitis is a common complication in the early postpartum period,
typically presenting with unilateral breast pain, redness, warmth, and systemic
symptoms such as fever. It is important to recognise that it can occasionally be
bilateral, although this is less common. In lactating women, milk stasis is usually the
primary underlying cause, where accumulated milk leads to an inflammatory
response that may or may not progress to infection.
The most common organism associated with infective mastitis is Staphylococcus
aureus.

-- 1257 of 1282 --

1256
Guidance from National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) advises that
a breast milk culture should be considered if
• Symptoms do not improve after 48 hours of first-line treatment,
• Mastitis is recurrent or atypical,
• A hospital-acquired infection is suspected, or
• if there is severe deep burning breast pain suggestive of ductal infection.
A key learning point is addressing common misconceptions. Many women worry that
they should stop breastfeeding due to the risk of passing infection to the baby.
However, the World Health Organization (WHO) advises that it is safe to continue
breastfeeding, even in the presence of infection such as Staphylococcus aureus.
Continued breastfeeding or expressing milk is important, as it helps resolve milk
stasis and supports recovery.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/mastitis-breast-abscess/
https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/WHO-FCH-CAH-00.13
https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/15613-mastitis

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Clarify whether she has consent to discuss her mother’s medical issues or
has lasting power of attorney.
• Acknowledge the daughter’s concern and explain confidentiality, including that
you may not be able to share specific medical details without her mum’s
consent.
• Ask what she already understands about her mother’s condition.
• Ask what she has noticed about her mother’s symptoms, including any
changes in vision, eye pain, itching, swelling, photophobia, redness,
headache, or difficulty with carrying out daily activities.
• Ask if she knows the reason her mother stopped using the eye drops.
• Ask about any side effects from the eye drops, such as stinging, dry eyes, or
eye colour changes.
• Ask about any concerns regarding her mother’s memory or changes in
behaviour that may affect her ability to manage medications independently
• Ask about the mother’s social history, including home situation, who she lives
with, level of independence, smoking, alcohol use, and whether she is
currently driving.
• Explore the daughter’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
Management
• Acknowledge the daughter’s concerns and thank her for raising them.
• Explain that as she does not have consent from her mother, you are unable to
disclose the diagnosis or specific reasons for the prescribed eye drops.
• Provide general information, explaining that eye drops like these are important
in managing eye conditions and preventing long-term complications, and that
stopping them without an alternative treatment may lead to worsening of the
condition.
• Encourage her to speak with her mother to book an appointment with the GP
to discuss her concerns and side effects. Explain that you can liaise with the
ophthalmology team through advice and guidance if needed.
• Reassure her that if her mother is experiencing side effects, there may be
alternative treatments available, and this can be reviewed.

-- 1261 of 1282 --

1260
• Inform the daughter that, if she is happy for you to do so, you can contact her
mother directly to share the concerns she has raised and speak with her
about this.
• Provide safety-netting by advising her to inform her mother that if she
develops symptoms such as red eye, headache, or blurred vision, she should
seek urgent medical attention by contacting the GP or NHS 111 if out of
hours.
Example of Explanation to the Patient (Daughter)
Thank you Laura for sharing your concerns with me. I can see that you are worried
about your mum, especially as she has stopped using her eye drops.
Before we go further, I just need to explain that because of confidentiality, I am not
able to share specific details about your mum’s diagnosis or treatment without her
permission. I hope you understand this.
What I can say in general is that eye drops like the ones your mum has been
prescribed are often very important in managing eye conditions and preventing long-
term problems with vision. Stopping them without an alternative can sometimes lead
to worsening of the condition. Does that make sense so far?
It is quite common for people to experience side effects such as stinging when they
first start using eye drops, and in many cases this settles with time. If it does not,
there are often alternative options that can be considered.
The best next step would be for your mum to speak directly with us so we can
understand what is going on and review her treatment. It would be really helpful if
you could encourage her to book an appointment with us, so we can support her
properly. How do you feel about that?
If you are happy for me to do so, I can contact your mum directly, let her know that
you are concerned about her, and invite her to speak with us. What do you think
about that?
In the meantime, if you notice that she develops any new symptoms such as red
eye, headache, or blurred vision, please encourage her to seek medical attention
urgently by contacting the GP or NHS 111 if it is out of hours.

-- 1262 of 1282 --

Learning Point from This Station
This station highlights the importance of maintaining confidentiality when speaking to
third parties, even when concerns are genuine and well intentioned. Unless the
patient has given consent or the caller has legal authority such as lasting power of
attorney, specific clinical information must not be disclosed.
It also emphasises the role of the GP in balancing confidentiality with patient safety.
While detailed information cannot be shared, general advice can be given, and
concerns can be acknowledged.
Further Reading
https://www.gmc-uk.org/professional-standards/the-professional-
standards/confidentiality

Station 243: Video Consultation

Your last menstrual period was 1 week ago.

which is stressful but enjoyable. You do not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs.

Concerns: You are worried about sinusitis.

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset, location, character, and progression of the headache.
• Ask about associated symptoms including nausea, vomiting, and
photophobia.
• Ask about symptoms of sinusitis, including nasal congestion, facial pain or
pressure, reduced sense of smell, fever, recent flu-like illness, or purulent
nasal discharge.
• Ask about triggers for the headache, including recent stress, caffeine intake,
missed meals, or dehydration.
• Ask about red flag symptoms, including visual changes, neck stiffness,
confusion, rash, recent head trauma, or focal neurological symptoms such as
weakness, tingling, or slurred speech.
• Ask about medication use, including analgesia taken, frequency, and
response.
• Ask about menstrual history and any relation of headaches to the menstrual
cycle.
• Ask about any past history of similar headaches.
• Ask about severity of pain and impact on daily activities.
• Ask about social history, including alcohol use, smoking, and illicit drug use.
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a working diagnosis of tension-type headache.
Management
• Acknowledge her symptoms and explain that her symptoms are not typical of
sinusitis and are more in keeping with a tension-type headache.
• Advise her to keep a symptom diary to help identify potential triggers.
• Advise that she can take ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily for pain relief,
which can be taken alongside paracetamol if needed. Inform her that it is

-- 1265 of 1282 --

1264
available over the counter, and advise her about possible side effects such as
stomach upset, recommending that she takes it with or after food.
• Discuss the role of stress as a contributing factor and advise strategies to
manage this, such as regular exercise, yoga, and mindfulness.
• Advise reducing screen time where possible, as eye strain can contribute to
headaches.
• Encourage her to speak with her workplace about workload and support, and
discuss the possibility of adjustments or time off if needed.
• Provide safety-netting by advising her to seek medical attention by contacting
the GP surgery or NHS 111 if out of hours if headaches worsen, become

vomiting, neurological symptoms, or fever.
• Arrange follow-up in 4 weeks to review her symptoms and progress.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Hannah for explaining everything so clearly. I can see why you are
concerned, especially as your friend had similar symptoms and was given antibiotics.
From what you have described, your symptoms are not typical of sinusitis. Sinusitis
usually presents with symptoms such as facial pain, blocked nose, fever, or thick
nasal discharge. Your headache sounds more like what we call a tension-type
headache, which is very common and often linked to stress or lifestyle factors. Does
that make sense so far?
Because of this, antibiotics would not help in your case, and we would avoid
prescribing them as they can cause side effects and are not effective for this type of
headache.
In terms of managing your symptoms, you can take ibuprofen 400 mg up to three
times a day for pain relief, and you can take this alongside paracetamol if needed.
You can get ibuprofen over the counter. It can sometimes cause stomach upset, so it
is best taken with or after food. Are you happy to try this?
It would also be helpful to keep a symptom diary to see if there are any triggers for
your headaches, such as stress, missed meals, dehydration, or caffeine. From what
you have told me, starting a new job can be stressful, and this may be contributing.
Would you be able to speak with your manager at work to see if a reduction in your
workload, some additional support, or even a short period of time off might help
reduce your stress?
Activities such as regular exercise, mindfulness, yoga, or other relaxation techniques
can also be helpful in managing stress. Does this sound like something you would be
willing to try?

-- 1266 of 1282 --

1265
Simple measures such as regular meals, staying well hydrated, reducing screen
time, and taking short breaks during work can help
If your headaches get worse, become persistent, or if you develop symptoms such
as visual changes, vomiting, or weakness in any part of your body, please seek
medical attention urgently by contacting us or NHS 111 if it is out of hours.
I will arrange a follow-up in about 4 weeks to see how you are getting on.
Learning Point from This Station
This station highlights the importance of differentiating between common primary
headache disorders and avoiding unnecessary antibiotic prescribing. Tension-type
headache is a common cause of headache in primary care and typically presents
with a bilateral, band-like pressure without associated features such as nausea,
photophobia, or focal neurological symptoms.
It is important to distinguish this from sinusitis, which usually presents with facial
pain, nasal congestion, fever, and purulent nasal discharge. In the absence of these
features, antibiotics are not indicated, and overprescribing should be avoided in line
with antimicrobial stewardship principles.
Management of tension-type headache is centred on simple analgesia and
addressing contributing factors such as stress, dehydration, poor sleep, and
excessive screen time. Non-pharmacological strategies, including relaxation
techniques and lifestyle modification, play a key role.
Further Reading
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/headache-tension-type/

-- 1267 of 1282 --

Station 244: Telephone Consultation
Patient’s Name: Richard Brentwood

Patient’s Story (Role Player’s Brief)
You are Richard Brentwood, a 56-year-old male.
Opening Statement: Hello doctor, have you had a look at the picture of the rash on
my body that I sent?
If Asked to Explain Further: You noticed the rash started 2 days ago. It began on
your tummy and has spread to your neck, both upper limbs and back. The rash is
very itchy and is affecting your sleep.
ONLY SAY BELOW IF ASKED:
The rashes are flat and red.
You have not had contact with anyone with a similar rash. You do not have a fever
and there are no rashes on your palms, mouth, face, genitals, or lower limbs.
You do not have any shortness of breath or facial swelling.
You are still taking the allopurinol and took it again this morning.
You are not taking any other medications.
Social History: You are a non-smoker, do not drink alcohol, and do not use illicit
drugs. You work as a solicitor and live with your wife.
Ideas: You think the rash may be caused by allopurinol, as you read this in the
leaflet.
Concerns: The rash is very itchy and is affecting your sleep.

Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset and progression of the rash, including when it started and
how quickly it has spread.
• Ask about the appearance of the rash, including whether it is raised, flat,
blotchy, or blistering.
• Ask if the skin is dry and whether there is any discharge from the rash.
• Ask about associated symptoms such as itching, pain, or burning.
• Ask about symptoms suggestive of anaphylaxis or angioedema, including
facial swelling, lip or tongue swelling, shortness of breath, or wheeze.

-- 1269 of 1282 --

1268
• Ask about symptoms suggestive of Stevens–Johnson syndrome, including
mouth ulcers, genital sores, blisters, skin peeling, fever, palpitations or feeling
generally unwell.
• Ask whether the rash involves the palms or soles.
• Ask about any recent flu-like illness, sore throat, or diarrhoea (to rule out viral
rash e.g. measles).
• Ask if the rash started after initiating allopurinol and whether he is taking any
over-the-counter medications.
• Ask about any new soaps, creams, detergents, foods, or other possible
allergens.
• Ask about contact with anyone with a similar rash.
• Ask about the impact on sleep and daily activities.
• Ask about social history, including smoking, alcohol use, and illicit drug use.
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a working diagnosis of likely allopurinol-induced maculopapular drug
rash.
Management
• Advise the patient to stop allopurinol immediately.
• Offer a non-sedating antihistamine such as cetirizine. Inform him that although
it is generally non-drowsy, it can occasionally cause mild drowsiness, and
advise him not to drive or operate machinery if he feels drowsy.
• Offer a topical emollient to soothe the skin (for example, Epimax or Oilatum
cream for moist skin, or Cetraben or Diprobase ointment for dry skin).
• Consider adding a topical steroid, such as a mild to moderate potency steroid,
if symptoms are not controlled with emollients alone.
• Advise that once the rash has settled, alternative urate-lowering therapy can
be considered, such as febuxostat.
• Inform him that you will document allopurinol as a drug allergy in his records
and advise him to avoid it in the future.
• Provide safety-netting and advise that if the rash worsens, or if he develops
blistering, fever, mouth ulcers, facial or tongue swelling, or difficulty breathing,
he should call 999 or attend A&E urgently.
• Arrange follow-up in 2 weeks to ensure the rash has settled and to discuss
alternative urate-lowering therapy.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Richard for explaining everything so clearly, and I have had a look at the
picture you sent.

-- 1270 of 1282 --

1269
From what you have described and the timing of the rash, this looks most likely like a
reaction to the allopurinol. This type of rash can sometimes occur shortly after
starting the medication, and allopurinol is known to cause this. Are you following so
far?
The most important thing we need to do now is to stop the allopurinol straight away,
as continuing it could make the rash worse. Once the rash has settled, we can look
at alternative options to manage your gout, such as a medication called febuxostat.
To help with the itching, I would recommend starting an antihistamine such as
cetirizine taken once a day. This is usually non-drowsy, but in some people it can
cause a bit of drowsiness, so I would advise not driving or operating machinery if you
feel affected. Are you happy to try this?
You can also use a moisturising cream, to help soothe the skin. These are available
over the counter at pharmacies or supermarkets, such as Oilatum or similar
products, and can be used regularly.
If the rash remains bothersome, we can consider prescribing a mild steroid cream.
However, most people improve once the medication is stopped, and emollients are
often enough to settle the symptoms.
It is really important that you watch out for any warning signs. If the rash worsens, or
if you develop symptoms such as blisters, skin peeling, mouth ulcers, fever, swelling
of the face or lips, or difficulty breathing, you should seek urgent medical attention by
calling 999 or going to A&E. Does that make sense?
I will arrange to follow you up in a couple of weeks to make sure the rash has settled
and to discuss alternative treatment for your gout.
Learning Point from This Station
Allopurinol is a well-established first-line treatment for gout prevention. Common
early side effects include skin rashes, diarrhoea, and nausea. Importantly, a rash
may be the first sign of a hypersensitivity reaction, and clinicians must be alert to the
possibility of severe cutaneous adverse reactions such as Stevens–Johnson
syndrome.
The most common presentation of a drug-related rash is a morbilliform drug
eruption, which typically begins on the trunk and spreads to the limbs and neck. The
distribution is usually bilateral and symmetrical, with lesions appearing as pink to red
macules or papules.
Certain populations, particularly individuals of Southeast Asian origin such as Han
Chinese, Indian, and Korean patients, should be considered for HLA-B*5801

-- 1271 of 1282 --

1270
screening before starting allopurinol, as this allele is strongly associated with severe
hypersensitivity reactions.
Management requires prompt cessation of the offending drug. Symptomatic
treatment includes antihistamines for itching, along with emollients and soothing
measures such as cool showers. Topical corticosteroids can be added in more
severe cases or if symptoms do not improve with emollients alone.
A stepwise approach to itch control is important. Non-sedating antihistamines can be
used during the day, and if symptoms are severe and affecting sleep, a sedating
antihistamine such as chlorphenamine may be added at night, with appropriate
counselling regarding drowsiness.
Further Reading
https://www.pcds.org.uk/clinical-guidance/adverse-cutaneous-drug-reactions-mild-to-
moderate
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/gout/prescribing-information/allopurinol/
https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2025/november/breaking-out-the-wild-world-of-
diagnosing-and-mana
https://dermnetnz.org/topics/morbilliform-drug-reaction
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10042238/

ONLY SAY BELOW IF ASKED:
Your mood has been low, and your mind is often preoccupied with thoughts about
dying. This makes you feel anxious and sometimes affects your sleep. These
feelings started about 4 weeks ago after your last discussion with the oncologist.
What worries you most about dying is the loss of control, particularly the fear of
going to sleep and not waking up.
You are still managing to eat and drink. You do have generalised bone pain, but the
Oramorph helps control this. Your bowels are regular, and you are passing urine

You are not struggling to look after yourself, you have no financial difficulties, and
