Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Acknowledge her concerns and explore her mood, including onset, duration,
and progression of low mood.

-- 1274 of 1282 --

1273
• Ask about symptoms of depression, including poor sleep, low energy, reduced
appetite, loss of interest, or reduced concentration.
• Ask about symptoms of anxiety, including constant worry, restlessness, panic
episodes, or physical symptoms such as palpitations.
• Explore her thoughts about dying, including what specifically worries her and
any fears about the process of dying. Note: Some people may have fears
about loss of control, dying suddenly in their sleep, experiencing pain,
becoming a burden to others, anxiety about leaving loved ones behind or
uncertainty about what happens during the dying process.
• Ask sensitively about suicidal thoughts or thoughts of self-harm.
• Ask about the impact of symptoms on daily functioning and quality of life.
• Ask about other common palliative symptoms, including pain, constipation,
nausea, vomiting, difficulty breathing, dry mouth, or headache.
• Ask about support systems, including her husband, family, and community or
palliative care team involvement.
• Ask about social history, including home situation, finance, support at home,
smoking, alcohol use, and illicit drug use.
• Ask about any previous history of mental health problems.
• Explore her ideas, concerns, and expectations
• Make a working diagnosis of adjustment disorder with low mood and anxiety
Management
• Acknowledge her feelings with empathy and validate her fears about dying.
• Reassure her that what she is experiencing is understandable given her
situation.
• Offer clear explanation of the dying process to reduce uncertainty and fear.
Inform her that as her condition progresses, people typically become
gradually more tired and sleepy, and that this is usually a gentle and
progressive process rather than something sudden or frightening
• Explain that to help her feel more in control, we can carry out advance care
planning, which allows her to document her wishes and preferences for future
care in case she is unable to make decisions later, ensuring that all care
provided is in line with her preferences
• Offer referral for talking therapy with a psychologist experienced in palliative
care. Explain that this can help her explore her thoughts, feel more in control,
and manage her anxiety and low mood.
• Explain that many people do well with talking therapy alone, but if her
symptoms begin to significantly affect her, medication can be considered.
Options include mirtazapine, which may help with sleep and mood, or an
SSRI such as sertraline.

-- 1275 of 1282 --

1274
• Offer another telephone consultation or home visit to discuss advance care
planning. Also explain that we can prescribe “just in case” medications, which
are used to manage common symptoms that may arise as her condition
progresses.
• Inform her that she may be eligible for additional support, including benefits.
Explain that an SR1 form can be completed, and offer to liaise with a social
prescriber to assist with this.
• Provide her with the local palliative care emergency contact number for easy
access to the district nurse or GP in case she has any concerns or worsening
symptoms.
• Provide safety-netting by advising her to seek help if her mood worsens, she
develops thoughts of self-harm, or if her symptoms become overwhelming.
• Arrange follow-up in 2 weeks to ensure she has appropriate support in place
and to review any ongoing concerns.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Kirsty for sharing that with me. I can hear how difficult this has been for
you, and it is completely understandable to feel this way given everything you have
been going through.
From what you have told me, it sounds like your mood has been low and you have
been feeling quite anxious, particularly with thoughts about what lies ahead. Many
people in your situation feel this way, and you are not alone in this.
It can really help to talk through these fears.
You mentioned that you are worried about not having control and the fear of not
waking up. That is something many people feel, and it is completely understandable.
Sometimes, the fear of dying comes from not knowing what to expect. What I can
reassure you is that, as your condition progresses, our main focus will always be on
keeping you comfortable and free from distress. We have very good ways of
managing symptoms such as pain, breathlessness, and anxiety, and we will support
you closely through every stage. You will not be left to cope on your own. Are you
with me so far?
In terms of control, one of the things we can do together is make a clear plan for your
care. This includes advance care planning, where you can tell us your wishes about
how you would like to be looked after. That way, even if you are not able to speak for
yourself later, we will follow what you have told us. This often helps people feel more
in control.

-- 1276 of 1282 --

1275
With regard to the fear of not waking up, it can help to understand that as people
become more unwell, the body usually becomes gradually more tired, and people
tend to become more sleepy over time rather than anything sudden or frightening.
Our focus is always to keep you comfortable, calm, and free from distress.
How does that sound to you? Do you have any questions or anything you would like
me to go over again?
One of the most helpful things we can offer is talking therapy with a specialist
psychologist who works in palliative care. They can support you in processing these
thoughts and help you find ways to feel more in control and at peace with things.
Some people find that talking therapy on its own is enough, but if your sleep or mood
becomes more difficult, we can also consider medication to support you. For
example, there are medications such as mirtazapine, which can help with both sleep
and mood if needed.
Would you prefer to try this now, or would you rather wait and see how you get on
with talking therapy first?
You are not alone in this, and there is support available. I can also give you a direct
number to access the district nurses or the GP practice, so if you ever feel worried or
your symptoms change, you can get help quickly.
If at any point you feel your mood is getting worse or you have any thoughts of
harming yourself, please let us know straight away. We are here to support you.
I will check in with you again in a couple of weeks to see how you are getting on, but
please do reach out sooner if you need to.
Learning Point from This Station
This station highlights the importance of recognising and managing psychological
distress in palliative care, particularly anxiety related to dying and loss of control. In
patients with terminal illness, fear of dying is common and may relate to pain, fear of
the unknown, loss of control, becoming a burden, leaving loved ones behind, or
concerns about dying suddenly or what the dying process involves.
These feelings of fear and low mood are often part of an adjustment reaction rather
than clinical depression. However, it is essential to actively screen for depression
and suicidal ideation to ensure patient safety.
Management should take a holistic and patient-centred approach. Effective
communication is key, including acknowledging fears, exploring specific concerns,
and gently explaining what to expect during the natural dying process to reduce
uncertainty and anxiety.

-- 1277 of 1282 --

1276
Non-pharmacological support is first-line, including counselling or cognitive
behavioural therapy delivered by a palliative care psychologist, which can help
patients regain a sense of control. Medications may be considered if symptoms
persist or significantly impact quality of life, or if this aligns with the patient’s
preferences.
Further Reading
https://patient.info/doctor/oncology/prescribing-in-palliative-care#common-problems
https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/palliative-care-general-issues/management/terminal-
phase/

provided in the scenario. Accept anything offered to you by the doctor.
Marking Scheme
Data Gathering and Diagnosis
• Ask about the onset, location, duration, character, and progression of the
pain, including whether it is getting better, worse, or staying the same.
• Ask if the pain radiates anywhere.
• Ask about triggers such as touching the face, brushing teeth, chewing,
speaking, or exposure to cold air.
• Ask about any relieving factors.
• Ask about associated neurological symptoms such as facial numbness,
weakness, visual changes, or hearing problems.
• Ask about sinus-related symptoms such as nasal congestion, runny nose,
nasal discharge, facial pressure, or fever.
• Ask about symptoms of migraine, including nausea, vomiting, photophobia, or
aura.
• Ask about dental symptoms or recent dental procedures.
• Ask about any history of trauma or infection affecting the face.
• Ask about red flag symptoms such as unintentional weight loss, night sweats,
or loss of appetite to rule out malignancy.
• Ask about social history, including smoking, alcohol use, and illicit drug use.
• Ask about the impact of the pain on his daily activities
• Explore the patient’s ideas, concerns, and expectations.
• Make a working diagnosis of trigeminal neuralgia.
Management
• Offer a face-to-face appointment to examine the cranial nerves of the face.
• Offer treatment with carbamazepine, starting at 100 mg twice daily and
titrating up as needed for symptom control.
• Explain that carbamazepine is a medication commonly used for seizures but
is also effective in treating nerve-related pain such as trigeminal neuralgia.
• Arrange baseline blood tests before starting treatment, including full blood
count and liver function tests.

-- 1280 of 1282 --

1279
• Inform the patient about common side effects such as dizziness, drowsiness,
dry mouth, and gastrointestinal upset.
• Explain rare but important side effects, including a drop in white blood cells
(agranulocytosis). Advise the patient that if they develop symptoms such as a
sore throat, fever, or feel generally unwell, they should seek medical attention
promptly.
• Provide safety-netting by advising the patient to seek urgent help by
contacting the GP surgery or NHS 111 if symptoms worsen or if they develop
red flag symptoms such as visual disturbance, hearing changes, or facial
weakness.
• Arrange follow-up in 2 weeks to assess symptom control and response to
treatment.
Example of Explanation to the Patient
Thank you Henry for explaining everything so clearly. You mentioned that you
thought this might be migraine. You are right that migraines can cause severe
headaches, however, your symptoms are not typical of migraine.
From what you have described, the pattern of your pain sounds more like a condition
called trigeminal neuralgia. Have you heard of this before?
This is a condition that affects one of the nerves in the face, called the trigeminal
nerve, and it can cause very sharp, sudden, electric shock-like pain. It is often
triggered by things like touching the face, brushing your teeth, or chewing, just as
you have described. Are you following me so far?
Before we start any treatment, I would like to see you face to face so I can examine
the nerves in your face, rule out other causes, and confirm the diagnosis. Would you
be happy for me to arrange that?
The good news is that we do have effective treatments for this. The main medication
we use is called carbamazepine. It is commonly used for conditions such as
seizures, but it also works very well for this type of nerve pain. We would start at a
low dose and gradually increase it depending on how you respond.
How do you feel about trying this?
Before starting the medication, we would arrange some blood tests to make sure it is
safe for you, including checking your blood count and liver function.
Like all medications, it can have some side effects. The more common ones include
feeling a bit drowsy, dizzy, or having an upset stomach, especially when you first
start it. There are also some rare but important side effects, including a drop in white
blood cells. So if you develop symptoms such as a sore throat, fever, or start to feel
generally unwell, it is important that you let us know straight away.

-- 1281 of 1282 --

1280
If your symptoms get worse, or if you notice any new symptoms such as weakness
in your face, changes in your hearing or vision, please seek medical attention
urgently by contacting us or NHS 111 if it is out of hours.
I will arrange a follow-up in about 2 weeks to see how you are getting on with the
treatment.
Learning Point from This Station
Trigeminal neuralgia is a neuropathic pain condition affecting one or more branches
of the fifth cranial nerve. It is characterised by severe, sharp, electric shock-like pain
that is typically unilateral and only rarely bilateral. Each episode is short-lived,
usually lasting from a few seconds up to 2 minutes, but attacks can occur multiple
times a day with pain-free intervals in between.
The pain is often triggered by light stimuli, such as touching the face, brushing teeth,
chewing, speaking, or exposure to cold air. Some patients may also report a
lingering dull ache between attacks.
Although primarily a pain condition, some patients may have associated autonomic
features such as tearing, eye redness, nasal congestion, or facial sweating, which
can mimic conditions like cluster headache.
A key clinical point is that carbamazepine is the first-line and only licensed
medication with strong evidence for treating trigeminal neuralgia, and early
recognition allows prompt and effective symptom control.
Further Reading
